topics.title: Introduction: Computer Forensic Investigations, Forensics Investigation Process, Preparing for Computer Investigations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT602 - CYBER FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction: Computer Forensic Investigations, Forensics Investigation Process, Preparing for Computer Investigations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Computer Forensics — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Computer Forensics** (also called **Digital Forensics** or **Cyber Forensics**) is the lawful, scientific process of identifying, preserving, analyzing, and presenting digital evidence retrieved from computer systems, storage media, networks, and electronic devices in a manner that is legally admissible in a court of law.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context (PCCCT602), computer forensics is the cornerstone subject that bridges the gap between **cybersecurity incidents** and the **judicial system**. The discipline mandates that every byte of digital evidence must follow strict procedural protocols so that it remains **authentic, reliable, and admissible**.

| Forensic Sub-Discipline | Primary Focus |
|---|---|
| **Computer Forensics** | PCs, laptops, servers, OS artifacts |
| **Mobile Forensics** | Smartphones, tablets, SIM cards |
| **Network Forensics** | Traffic logs, IDS/IPS data, packet captures |
| **Memory Forensics** | RAM dumps, live system state |
| **Cloud Forensics** | SaaS, IaaS, virtualized environments |
| **Database Forensics** | DBMS logs, SQL injection trails |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Crime Scene"

> [!NOTE]  
> **Intuition for Students:**  
> Imagine a traditional police officer arriving at a *physical* crime scene — say, a robbed bank. Before touching anything, the officer **cordons off the area**, **photographs the scene**, **collects fingerprints and DNA**, and **logs every item** in a sealed evidence bag with a documented chain of custody. The fingerprints and DNA are then sent to a forensic lab for analysis.  

> Now translate that exact workflow into the digital world: the **"crime scene"** is a hard drive, a USB stick, or a server. The **"yellow tape"** is a *bit-stream image* (a forensic clone). The **"fingerprint"** is a **cryptographic hash** (MD5/SHA-1/SHA-256). The **"evidence bag"** is a *write-blocked forensic container* (e.g., E01/AD1). The **"lab analysis"** is what tools like **EnCase, FTK, Autopsy, or X-Ways** perform.

If a forensic investigator casually boots the suspect's laptop to "look around," he has just **destroyed volatile evidence** (RAM, network state, running processes, temp files). This is the digital equivalent of a policeman allowing 500 tourists to walk through the bank lobby *before* fingerprinting it.

## 1.3 Why Computer Forensics Matters in the KTU Curriculum

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — CO1):**  
> *“Illustrate the fundamental concepts of computer forensics, digital evidence, and the legal framework governing cyber investigations.”*

The three pillars that the KTU board examiner expects a student to internalize are:

1. **Identification** — Know *where* the evidence lives (sectors, clusters, file slack, unallocated space, RAM, cloud logs).
2. **Preservation** — Ensure evidence is *not altered* (write-blockers, hashing, chain of custody).
3. **Presentation** — Communicate findings in **legally understandable language** (reports admissible under **Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act, 1872 / Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023**).

## 1.4 Key Terminology Seed List (For 2-Mark KTU Questions)

| Term | One-Line Definition |
|---|---|
| **Digital Evidence** | Any information of probative value stored or transmitted in digital form. |
| **Bit-Stream Image** | A sector-by-sector, bit-for-bit copy of a storage device. |
| **Hash Value** | A fixed-length alphanumeric fingerprint of data (MD5 = 128-bit, SHA-1 = 160-bit, SHA-256 = 256-bit). |
| **Chain of Custody** | The documented, chronological, and unbroken trail of evidence handling. |
| **Write-Blocker** | A hardware/software device that prevents any write operations to the source media. |
| **Live Acquisition** | Capturing volatile data (RAM, network connections) from a *running* system. |
| **Dead Acquisition** | Capturing non-volatile data (HDD, USB) from a *powered-off* system. |
| **E01 / AD1** | Forensic container formats (Expert Witness Format / AccessData Custom Content). |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Phases of the Forensics Investigation Process

> [!NOTE]  
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly requires mastery of the **Forensics Investigation Process**. While there are several competing models (NIST SP 800-86, RFC 3227, the **Kruse & Heiser** model), the **5-phase model** is the de-facto industry standard and the most-asked framework in KTU examinations.

### Phase 1 — **Pre-Investigation / Preparation**

The investigator prepares the legal, technical, and operational groundwork **before** touching any device.

- **Legal authorization** — Search warrant, written consent, or corporate AUP (Acceptable Use Policy).
- **Tool validation** — Verify forensic software against known test images (e.g., NIST CFTT — Computer Forensic Tool Testing).
- **Hardware readiness** — Sterilized forensic workstations, blank media, write-blockers, cables.
- **Knowledge refresh** — OS version specifics (e.g., Windows 10 vs 11 Registry hives differ).

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Pitfall:** Most students lose marks by *jumping* to "acquisition" without describing preparation. The board examiner allocates **at least 2–3 marks** to legal authorization and tool validation.

### Phase 2 — **Identification / Survey**

The investigator identifies the **scope, jurisdiction, and physical layout** of digital evidence.

- Locate *powered-on* and *powered-off* devices.
- Document: device model, serial number, MAC address, OS, time zone, screen state.
- Photograph the workspace (cables, peripherals, sticky notes on monitors).
- Identify **volatile** vs **non-volatile** sources.

### Phase 3 — **Preservation / Acquisition**

This is the most **technically sensitive** phase. Two sub-strategies exist:

| Strategy | When Used | Tools |
|---|---|---|
| **Dead Acquisition** | Suspect machine is OFF | `dd`, `dcfldd`, `FTK Imager`, hardware write-blockers |
| **Live Acquisition** | Suspect machine is ON (RAM, network state needed) | `Magnet RAM Capture`, `WinPmem`, `Velociraptor` |

The **Order of Volatility (RFC 3227)** is the gold-standard priority list:

$$\text{CPU Registers} \rightarrow \text{Cache} \rightarrow \text{RAM} \rightarrow \text{Network State} \rightarrow \text{Running Processes} \rightarrow \text{Disk} \rightarrow \text{Logs} \rightarrow \text{Physical Configuration} \rightarrow \text{Archival Media}$$

### Phase 4 — **Analysis / Examination**

The forensic analyst correlates artifacts to reconstruct events. Common artifact categories:

- **File System Artifacts** — MFT ($MFT), $LogFile, $UsnJrnl (NTFS); EXT4 journals (Linux).
- **OS Artifacts** — Registry (UserAssist, ShimCache, AmCache), Prefetch, Event Logs.
- **Application Artifacts** — Browser history, email headers, chat logs.
- **Network Artifacts** — PCAP, firewall logs, DHCP leases, ARP tables.
- **User Artifacts** — Recent documents, LNK files, Jump Lists, thumbnail cache.

### Phase 5 — **Presentation / Reporting**

A forensic report must be **peer-reviewable, reproducible, and courtroom-defensible**. KTU-mandated components:

1. **Executive Summary** — Plain-language synopsis for the judge/jury.
2. **Acquisition Details** — Tool versions, hash values, image format.
3. **Chain of Custody Log** — Every transfer, with date/time/handler.
4. **Findings** — Correlated evidence with timestamps (in UTC and local time).
5. **Conclusion** — Investigative opinion, limitations, alternative hypotheses.

## 2.2 Preparing for a Computer Investigation — Detailed Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> KTU frequently asks: *"List the steps to prepare for a computer forensic investigation"* — usually for **7 marks** in Part B. Use this structured checklist as your model answer.

### A. Legal & Administrative Preparation
1. **Verify jurisdiction** — Is the offense under IT Act 2000, IPC, CrPC, or company policy?
2. **Obtain authorization** — Warrant under **Section 165 CrPC** (India) or corporate policy.
3. **Define scope** — Which systems, accounts, time window?
4. **Engage legal counsel** — Pre-emptive advice on admissibility.

### B. Technical Preparation
1. **Forensic Workstation Setup**
   - Clean OS (no Internet, no auto-mount).
   - Write-blockers (Tableau, WiebeTech).
   - Validated tools (EnCase, FTK, X-Ways, Autopsy).
2. **Storage Media Preparation**
   - Sterilize target drives (use `wipe -k` or `DBAN`).
   - Verify with hash = `0000…000` (after sanitization).
3. **Tool Validation**
   - Test acquisition on a **known reference image** (e.g., the *Digital Forensic Tool Testing Images* from NIST).
4. **Chain of Custody Forms** — Pre-printed, sequentially numbered.

### C. Operational Preparation
1. **Two-Person Rule (NITF / Daubert Standard)** — Never work alone.
2. **Contingency Plan** — Power failure, dead device, encrypted volumes (use **Plain View Doctrine** carefully).
3. **Communication Protocol** — Secure channel, no email about case details.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]  
> Although Cyber Forensics is procedure-heavy, KTU examiners love to test the following **quantitative formulas**. Memorize them.

| # | Formula / Quantity | Symbol | Description | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hash Collision Resistance** | $2^{n/2}$ | Expected number of trials for collision in an $n$-bit hash | MD5: $\approx 2^{64}$ trials |
| 2 | **Storage Capacity** | $C = S \times T$ | $C$ = capacity (bytes), $S$ = sectors, $T$ = sector size | HDD: 512 B / 4096 B |
| 3 | **Data Transfer Time** | $t = \dfrac{C}{R}$ | $t$ = time, $R$ = read rate (B/s) | USB 3.0 ≈ 100 MB/s |
| 4 | **Data Acquisition Time** | $t_a = \dfrac{C \cdot (1 + \alpha)}{R}$ | $\alpha$ = overhead factor (0.1 – 0.3) | Typical $\alpha = 0.2$ |
| 5 | **Slack Space** | $S_{slack} = CL - F_L$ | $CL$ = cluster size, $F_L$ = file length | FAT32 cluster: 4 KB – 64 KB |
| 6 | **Raid Parity** | $P = \bigoplus_{i=1}^{n} D_i$ | XOR parity for $n$ data disks | RAID-5: 1 parity disk |
| 7 | **Entropy** | $H = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Randomness of data (0 to 8 bits/byte) | Encrypted: $H \geq 7.9$ |
| 8 | **Drive Geometry** | $C = H \times S \times T$ | Heads, Sectors, Tracks | Modern LBA: $\text{LBA} = \frac{C}{T}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Never use** the pipe character `\vert` for absolute value inside markdown tables — it breaks the table parser. Use `\lvert x \rvert` or just `x` (with context).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Incident Response (IR) Teams** — Use the same 5-phase model during ransomware outbreaks.
- **e-Discovery (Litigation Support)** — Corporate legal teams deploy forensic methodologies for civil cases.
- **Insider Threat Hunting** — UEBA (User & Entity Behavior Analytics) pipelines depend on forensic artifact reconstruction.
- **Malware Analysis Labs** — Sandbox images and memory dumps are analyzed using forensic Phase 4 tools.
- **Smart Forensics (IoT)** — Investigators extend the model to firmware extraction, JTAG, and chip-off analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]  
> Cyber Forensics is **not a calculus-heavy** subject, but KTU examiners love derivations in the following areas: (a) **forensic image integrity verification**, (b) **acquisition time estimation**, and (c) **cluster slack-space computation**. We derive all three rigorously.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Verifying the Integrity of a Forensic Image (Hash-Based)

**Problem Setup.**  
A forensic investigator acquires a 1 TB (Tera-byte) hard disk into a `.E01` image. The **source media** has a **SHA-256 hash** value. The **destination image** must yield the *exact same* hash. Derive the mathematical relationship that guarantees integrity.

**Step 1 — Define the Hash Function.**  
A cryptographic hash function $H$ takes an input of arbitrary length and produces a fixed-size output of $n$ bits:

$$H : \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^{n}$$

For **SHA-256**, $n = 256$ bits, so the output space has cardinality:

$$\lvert \text{Output} \rvert = 2^{256}$$

**Step 2 — Original Media Hash.**  
Let the source media be $M$ (a bit-string of length $L$ bits). Its hash is:

$$h_M = H(M)$$

**Step 3 — Forensic Image.**  
Let the forensic image be $M'$ (a bit-for-bit copy). For acquisition to be *forensically sound*:

$$M' = M \quad \text{(bit-exact equality)}$$

**Step 4 — Image Hash.**  
The image hash is:

$$h_{M'} = H(M')$$

**Step 5 — Integrity Condition.**  
By the **deterministic property** of cryptographic hash functions:

$$M' = M \implies H(M') = H(M)$$

$$\therefore \quad h_{M'} = h_M$$

**Step 6 — Collision Probability.**  
By the **birthday paradox**, the probability of an *accidental* collision after $q$ random hashes is approximately:

$$P(\text{collision}) \approx 1 - e^{-q^2 / (2 \cdot 2^{256})}$$

For any realistic $q < 2^{80}$, this probability is **astronomically negligible** (≈ 0).

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Conclusion:** A matching SHA-256 hash provides **$2^{256}$ security** against accidental or intentional tampering. This is the *mathematical foundation* of forensic integrity.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Estimating Forensic Acquisition Time

**Problem Setup.**  
A forensic workstation must image a 4 TB HDD using a USB 3.0 write-blocker. The read rate of the source is $R = 100 \text{ MB/s}$. The forensic tool's overhead factor is $\alpha = 0.20$ (20 % — due to bad-sector retries, hash computation, containerization). Derive the *minimum* acquisition time.

**Step 1 — Capacity in MB.**

$$C = 4 \text{ TB} \times \frac{10^{6} \text{ MB}}{1 \text{ TB}} = 4 \times 10^{6} \text{ MB}$$

*(Note: KTU may use the binary convention $1 \text{ TiB} = 2^{40}$ bytes; we present both forms for clarity.)*

Binary equivalent:

$$C_{\text{bin}} = 4 \times 2^{40} \text{ bytes} = 4 \times 1{,}099{,}511{,}627{,}776 \text{ B} \approx 4.39 \times 10^{6} \text{ MB}$$

**Step 2 — Effective Throughput.**  
The effective throughput $R_{\text{eff}}$ accounts for the overhead factor:

$$R_{\text{eff}} = R \times (1 - \alpha) = 100 \text{ MB/s} \times 0.80 = 80 \text{ MB/s}$$

**Step 3 — Acquisition Time (Decimal TB).**

$$t_a = \frac{C}{R_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{6} \text{ MB}}{80 \text{ MB/s}} = 50{,}000 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Hours.**

$$t_a = \frac{50{,}000 \text{ s}}{3600 \text{ s/h}} \approx 13.89 \text{ h}$$

**Step 5 — Acquisition Time (Binary TiB).**

$$t_a^{(\text{bin})} = \frac{4 \times 2^{40} \text{ B}}{80 \times 10^{6} \text{ B/s}} = \frac{4.398 \times 10^{12}}{8.0 \times 10^{7}} \approx 54{,}976 \text{ s} \approx 15.27 \text{ h}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Always budget **time + 30 % buffer** for forensic acquisition. For a 4 TB drive, plan for **≈ 18 hours** of unattended imaging.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Computing Cluster Slack Space (File System Forensics)

**Problem Setup.**  
A FAT32 file system uses a **cluster size** of $CL = 32 \text{ KB}$. A suspect file `secret.docx` has a **logical size** of $F_L = 18 \text{ KB}$. Derive the **slack space** $S_{slack}$ available for hidden data.

**Step 1 — Number of Allocated Clusters.**  
The number of clusters occupied by the file is:

$$N = \left\lceil \frac{F_L}{CL} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{18}{32} \right\rceil = 1 \text{ cluster}$$

**Step 2 — Allocated Space.**

$$S_{\text{alloc}} = N \times CL = 1 \times 32 \text{ KB} = 32 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 3 — Slack Space.**

$$S_{slack} = S_{\text{alloc}} - F_L = 32 \text{ KB} - 18 \text{ KB} = 14 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 4 — Sub-Classification of Slack.**  
The 14 KB breaks down into two forensic-relevant zones:

$$S_{slack} = S_{slack}^{ram} + S_{slack}^{file}$$

- **RAM Slack (Volume Slack):** Always equal to $CL - \text{file system's sector size}$. For a 512 B sector:

$$S_{slack}^{ram} = 32 \text{ KB} - 512 \text{ B} = 32{,}768 \text{ B} - 512 \text{ B} = 32{,}256 \text{ B} = 31.5 \text{ KB}$$

- **File Slack:** The remainder of the last sector.

$$S_{slack}^{file} = S_{slack} - S_{slack}^{ram} = 14 \text{ KB} - 31.5 \text{ KB} = \text{(not applicable here, file fits in 1 sector)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Anti-Forensics Insight:** Tools like `slacker.exe` or `bmap` deliberately write data into slack space to **covertly exfiltrate** information. A skilled forensic analyst **always examines slack** in KTU practical examinations.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Acquisition Strategies

| Criterion | Dead Acquisition | Live Acquisition |
|---|---|---|
| **System State** | Powered OFF | Powered ON |
| **Volatile Data (RAM, Net)** | ❌ Lost | ✅ Captured |
| **File System Consistency** | ✅ Guaranteed | ⚠️ May be inconsistent |
| **Anti-Forensic Risk** | Low | High (rootkits, self-destruct) |
| **Time Required** | Slow (full disk) | Fast (RAM only) |
| **Legal Admissibility (India)** | High (BSB §63) | Conditional (BSB §65B certificate) |
| **KTU Marks Weightage** | 7 marks (definitions) | 7 marks (order of volatility) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]  
> All diagrams below are **Mermaid** renderings. They satisfy the KTU 2024 Scheme expectation that students be able to **sketch process flows** in their answer booklets. Mermaid's text-based syntax also helps when a student has to **re-draw** them by hand in an exam.

## 4.1 The 5-Phase Forensics Investigation Process (Top-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Incident Detected]) --> phase1
    phase1[Phase 1 Preparation and Legal] --> phase2
    phase2[Phase 2 Identification and Survey] --> phase3
    phase3[Phase 3 Preservation and Acquisition] --> phase4
    phase4[Phase 4 Analysis and Examination] --> phase5
    phase5[Phase 5 Presentation and Reporting] --> court[Courtroom Admissible Evidence]
    phase3 --> hash{Hash Match Source and Image}
    hash -->|Yes| phase4
    hash -->|No| redo[Reacquire and Document]
    redo --> phase3
    phase5 --> coc[Chain of Custody Updated]
    coc --> court
```

## 4.2 Chain of Custody — Item Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Evidence Located] --> B[Photographed In Situ]
    B --> C[Tagged with Unique ID]
    C --> D[Sealed in Tamper-Evident Bag]
    D --> E[Logged in CoC Register]
    E --> F[Transferred to Forensic Lab]
    F --> G[Acquisition with Write-Blocker]
    G --> H[Hash Verified MD5 and SHA256]
    H --> I[Stored in Evidence Locker]
    I --> J[Presented in Court]
    J --> K[Returned or Destroyed]
```

## 4.3 Order of Volatility (RFC 3227) — Hierarchical Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Most Volatile] --> L1[CPU Registers and Cache]
    L1 --> L2[Routing Table and ARP Cache]
    L2 --> L3[Process Memory RAM]
    L3 --> L4[Network Connections and Temp Filesystems]
    L4 --> L5[Disk File System]
    L5 --> L6[Remote Logging and Monitoring Data]
    L6 --> L7[Physical Configuration and Archival Media]
    L7 --> L8[Least Volatile]
```

## 4.4 Preparation Phase — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    prep[Preparation Phase Entry] --> legal[Legal Authorization Layer]
    prep --> tech[Technical Readiness Layer]
    prep --> ops[Operational Readiness Layer]
    legal --> L1[Search Warrant or Consent]
    legal --> L2[Jurisdiction Check]
    legal --> L3[Scope Definition]
    tech --> T1[Forensic Workstation Sterilized]
    tech --> T2[Write-Blocker Tested]
    tech --> T3[Tools Validated Against NIST CFTT]
    tech --> T4[Blank Media Sanitized]
    ops --> O1[Two-Person Rule Enforced]
    ops --> O2[CoC Forms Pre-Printed]
    ops --> O3[Contingency Plan for Encrypted Volumes]
    ops --> O4[Secure Communication Channel]
    L1 --> ready[Ready for Identification]
    L2 --> ready
    L3 --> ready
    T1 --> ready
    T2 --> ready
    T3 --> ready
    T4 --> ready
    O1 --> ready
    O2 --> ready
    O3 --> ready
    O4 --> ready
```

## 4.5 Forensics vs. Incident Response — Comparative Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FORENSICS[Computer Forensics Post-Mortem]
        F1[After the fact] --> F2[Legal Admissibility Focus]
        F2 --> F3[Bit-Stream Imaging]
        F3 --> F4[Courtroom Report]
    end
    subgraph IR[Incident Response Live]
        I1[Real-time] --> I2[Containment Focus]
        I2 --> I3[Triage and Live Acquisition]
        I3 --> I4[Lessons Learned Report]
    end
    FORENSICS -.Evidence Handover.-> IR
    IR -.Memory Dumps.-> FORENSICS
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]  
> All questions are modeled on **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** patterns. Mark distribution: Part A (3 marks × 2 = 6 marks) and Part B (14 marks × 1 with internal choice = 14 marks). Total = 20 marks per question-paper slot.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]`  
**Define Computer Forensics. Differentiate between Dead Acquisition and Live Acquisition.** (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**  
Computer Forensics is the disciplined, scientific procedure of **identifying, preserving, analyzing, and presenting** digital evidence in a legally admissible manner. It applies investigative techniques to computers, storage media, and networks while maintaining the **integrity** of evidence and an unbroken **chain of custody**.

**Comparison (1.5 marks):**

| Aspect | Dead Acquisition | Live Acquisition |
|---|---|---|
| **System State** | Powered OFF | Powered ON |
| **Data Captured** | Non-volatile (disk) | Volatile (RAM, network) + non-volatile |
| **Tools** | `dd`, FTK Imager | `WinPmem`, Velociraptor |

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`  
**What is a forensic hash? Why is SHA-256 preferred over MD5 in modern investigations?** (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**  
A **forensic hash** is a **fixed-length cryptographic digest** of digital evidence (a "digital fingerprint"). Any single-bit modification changes the hash completely (the **avalanche effect**).

- **MD5** produces a 128-bit hash and is **cryptographically broken** (collision attacks exist).
- **SHA-256** produces a 256-bit hash and is **collision-resistant** ($2^{256}$ security), aligning with **NIST SP 800-22** and **FIPS 180-4** standards, making it **courtroom-defensible** in 2024+ jurisdictions.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) Explain in detail the five phases of the Computer Forensics Investigation Process. (7 marks)**  
**(b) Describe the steps an investigator must take to *prepare* for a computer investigation, with emphasis on legal and technical readiness. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The five-phase model, drawn from **NIST SP 800-86** and the **Kruse & Heiser** textbook, is the industry standard adopted in KTU evaluations.

**Phase 1 — Preparation (2 marks):**  
- Legal authorization (warrant, consent, corporate AUP).  
- Forensic workstation setup (clean OS, no Internet).  
- Tool validation against NIST CFTT reference images.  
- Chain of Custody forms prepared.

**Phase 2 — Identification (1 mark):**  
- Catalog all devices, OS versions, network connections.  
- Document volatile vs non-volatile data sources.  
- Photograph workspace and peripherals.

**Phase 3 — Preservation / Acquisition (2 marks):**  
- Choose **Dead** or **Live** acquisition per scenario.  
- Use **write-blockers** to prevent accidental writes.  
- Generate **bit-stream image** (e.g., `.E01` format).  
- Compute **MD5 + SHA-256** hashes.

**Phase 4 — Analysis (1 mark):**  
- Examine file system artifacts (MFT, $LogFile, $UsnJrnl).  
- Correlate OS artifacts (Registry, Prefetch, Event Logs).  
- Reconstruct timeline in **UTC** using tools like **Autopsy** or **X-Ways**.

**Phase 5 — Presentation (1 mark):**  
- Draft report with **executive summary, findings, conclusions**.  
- Maintain **chain of custody documentation**.  
- Provide expert testimony in **plain language** for the court.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**1. Legal Readiness (3 marks):**
- **Obtain warrant** under **Section 165 CrPC** (search by police) or **Section 94** (search-warrant issuance).
- Verify **jurisdiction** (cybercrime falls under IT Act 2000 + State/Central cyber cells).
- **Define scope** — which accounts, systems, time window.
- **Engage legal counsel** for admissibility review (Section 65B / BSB 2023).

**2. Technical Readiness (3 marks):**
- **Forensic workstation** — clean install, no auto-mount, no network.
- **Write-blockers** — hardware (Tableau T35u) or software (USB WriteBlocker).
- **Tool validation** — EnCase v8, FTK v7, Autopsy v4, X-Ways v20.
- **Storage media** — sterilize target drives (verify by re-hash).
- **Hash library** — maintain a database of known hash values (NSRL, custom).

**3. Operational Readiness (1 mark):**
- **Two-person rule** (chain-of-custody integrity).
- **Pre-numbered forms** for chain-of-custody.
- **Contingency for encryption** — coordinate with key-holder or use Plain View Doctrine.

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question A:**  
> Students frequently **omit the legal section** and jump straight to "boot the machine and image it." This loses **at least 3 marks** because the *legal authorization* is the single most-emphasized component in the KTU 2024 evaluation rubric (aligned with **CO1** of the syllabus).

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) What is a Chain of Custody? Explain its components and why it is critical for legal admissibility in Indian courts. (7 marks)**  
**(b) With a neat diagram, explain the Order of Volatility as defined in RFC 3227. Why must volatile data be acquired first? (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition (2 marks):**  
A **Chain of Custody (CoC)** is the **chronological, documented, unbroken trail** that records the **seizure, control, transfer, analysis, and disposition** of digital evidence — from the crime scene to the courtroom.

**Components of a CoC Form (4 marks):**

| # | Field | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Case ID & Exhibit Number** | Unique identifier (e.g., `EX-2024-0142-A`) |
| 2 | **Description of Item** | Make, model, serial, capacity, color |
| 3 | **Date & Time of Seizure** | With timezone (IST / UTC) |
| 4 | **Location of Seizure** | GPS / physical address |
| 5 | **Seizing Officer** | Name, rank, badge number |
| 6 | **Witness Signatures** | Two witnesses minimum |
| 7 | **Each Transfer Event** | From → To, date/time, reason |
| 8 | **Storage Location** | Locker ID, temperature, access logs |
| 9 | **Hash Values at Each Stage** | MD5 + SHA-256 |
| 10 | **Final Disposition** | Returned to owner / retained / destroyed |

**Why CoC is Critical (1 mark):**  
- **Admissibility** — Courts reject evidence if CoC has gaps (Daubert Standard, BSB §65B).
- **Authenticity** — Proves the evidence presented is *exactly* what was seized.
- **Integrity** — Detects tampering or accidental modification.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Order of Volatility — RFC 3227 (4 marks):**

| Priority | Data Source | Approx. Lifespan |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | CPU Registers, Cache | Nanoseconds |
| 2 | Routing Table, ARP Cache | Seconds |
| 3 | Process Memory (RAM) | Minutes |
| 4 | Temp File Systems, Network State | Minutes–Hours |
| 5 | Disk File System | Days–Years |
| 6 | Remote Logging Data | Days–Years |
| 7 | Physical Configuration, Archival Media | Years |

**Diagram (2 marks):**  
(Student should sketch the hierarchy: Most Volatile → Least Volatile as shown in Section 4.3 of these notes.)

**Why Volatile First (1 mark):**  
- RAM contents are **lost when power is cut** (cold-boot attacks aside, data decays in seconds).  
- A correct forensic sequence **acquires volatile data while the system is still ON**, *then* powers off for dead acquisition.  
- Missing volatile evidence = **irrecoverable loss** that may break a case (e.g., missing encryption keys in RAM).

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question B:**  
> Students often **list items in random order** without referencing **RFC 3227**. Always **name the RFC** in the answer — examiners allocate **1 mark** specifically for the citation. Also, students forget to state **"the system is powered on"** during volatile acquisition; this costs another mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1**

- ✅ **Computer Forensics =** Identify → Preserve → Analyze → Present **digital evidence** in a *legally admissible* form.
- ✅ **5 Phases:** Preparation → Identification → Preservation/Acquisition → Analysis → Presentation.
- ✅ **Order of Volatility (RFC 3227):** CPU/Registers → Cache → RAM → Network → Disk → Logs → Archival.
- ✅ **Dead Acquisition** = powered OFF (clean, slow); **Live Acquisition** = powered ON (volatile, risky).
- ✅ **Bit-stream image** = sector-by-sector, bit-for-bit copy (NOT file-copy).
- ✅ **Hashes** for integrity: MD5 (legacy) + SHA-256 (modern, courtroom-defensible). $n$-bit hash has $2^{n/2}$ birthday-bound collisions.
- ✅ **Chain of Custody** must be **continuous, documented, and witnessed**. Gaps = inadmissible.
- ✅ **Write-blockers** are *mandatory* for source media during acquisition.
- ✅ **Slack space** = allocated-but-unused portion of the last cluster. $S_{slack} = CL - F_L$. Hidden data lurks here.
- ✅ **Indian Legal Backing:** IT Act 2000, BSB 2023 (formerly IEA 1872 §65B), CrPC §165/§94.
- ✅ **Tools:** EnCase, FTK, Autopsy, X-Ways, Magnet RAM Capture, Velociraptor.
- ✅ **Time estimate for acquisition:** $t_a = C \cdot (1 + \alpha) / R$. Always **add 30 % buffer**.
- ✅ **Two-person rule** = NITF / Daubert standard compliance.
- ✅ **Examiner loves:** RFC citations, ISO 27037 references, NIST SP 800-86 mentions, and **Indian law clauses**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Understanding Public and Private Investigations, Data Acquisition, Storage Formats for Digital Evidence
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT602 - CYBER FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Understanding Public and Private Investigations, Data Acquisition, Storage Formats for Digital Evidence

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITIONS & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Understanding Public Investigations

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Public Investigation** in cyber forensics refers to a digital forensic investigation conducted by **government law enforcement agencies** (e.g., local police, CBI, NIA, INTERPOL, FBI) where the primary objective is to gather, preserve, and analyze digital evidence to support **criminal prosecution** under statutory law. The investigation is governed by constitutional protections, search warrants, jurisdictional boundaries, and rules of evidence (Indian Evidence Act, 1872 / IT Act, 2000 in the Indian KTU context).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Public investigations must strictly comply with the **Fourth Amendment equivalent protections** (in India, Article 20(3) of the Constitution + Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act for digital evidence admissibility). Any deviation can render the evidence *inadmissible* in court.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a public investigator like a **Government-appointed Crime Scene Officer (CSO)** at a murder scene. They cannot enter the house, dust for fingerprints, or collect blood samples without a **warrant signed by a Magistrate**. They are bound by procedure, must record everything, and the evidence they collect will be used to **punish the accused** in a court of law. If they break procedure, the criminal walks free (technicality). Similarly, a public cyber investigator cannot seize a server, image a hard disk, or sniff network traffic without proper legal authorization.

### Key Characteristics of Public Investigations
- Conducted by **law enforcement officers** (police, cybercrime cells).
- Driven by **criminal statutes** (IPC, IT Act, CrPC, BNSS).
- Requires **search warrants, court orders, or consent**.
- Subject to **strict chain of custody** and forensic admissibility rules.
- Results are used in **criminal courts** for prosecution.
- Heavy emphasis on **mirrored bit-stream images** and **write-blocked acquisitions**.

---

## 1.2 Understanding Private Investigations

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Private Investigation** in cyber forensics refers to a digital forensic examination conducted by **corporate entities, private forensic consultants, or internal incident response teams** to address civil matters, internal policy violations, employee misconduct, data breaches, or to support civil litigation. It is governed primarily by **corporate policies, civil procedure codes, and contractual obligations** rather than criminal law.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction:** Private investigations can be initiated by a **corporate HR manager or a private attorney** without a court warrant, *provided* the scope is limited to corporate-owned assets and is consistent with the organization's acceptable-use policy (AUP) and the employee's signed consent.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **private building security head** investigating a theft within a corporate office. The head does not need a Magistrate's warrant to check the office CCTV or the employee's official laptop—because the company **owns** the device and the employee consented to monitoring via the employment contract. However, the evidence collected is used for **internal disciplinary action or civil court** (e.g., terminating the employee, suing for damages), *not* for sending someone to jail. If criminal acts are uncovered, the evidence is typically **handed over to law enforcement** to convert the matter into a public investigation.

### Key Characteristics of Private Investigations
- Conducted by **corporate CSIRTs, MSSPs, or private DFIR firms**.
- Driven by **corporate policy, AUP, and civil law**.
- Requires **employee consent or ownership of asset** (no warrant needed).
- Focuses on **business continuity, attribution, and damage assessment**.
- Evidence is used in **HR tribunals, civil courts, or insurance claims**.
- May be escalated to a public investigation if criminality is discovered.

---

## 1.3 Comparative Overview: Public vs. Private Investigations

| Parameter | Public Investigation | Private Investigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Authority** | State / Government | Corporate / Private Firm |
| **Legal Basis** | Search Warrant, CrPC/BNSS | Employment Contract, AUP |
| **Goal** | Criminal Prosecution | Internal Action / Civil Suit |
| **Scope** | Cross-jurisdictional, may involve ISP records | Limited to corporate assets |
| **Admissibility** | Strict (Section 65B, IT Act) | Less strict (internal use) |
| **Seizure** | Whole device, ISP data, server logs | Targeted data, mailbox, endpoint |
| **Reporting To** | Magistrate, Court | Management, Civil Attorney |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** KTU students often confuse *private* and *public* investigations. Remember: **Public = Criminal Court = Warrants**. **Private = Civil/HR Court = Contracts**.

---

## 1.4 Data Acquisition — Formal Definition

**Data Acquisition** is the **forensic process of creating a verified, bit-for-bit copy of digital storage media** (hard disks, SSDs, volatile memory, mobile devices) while preserving the integrity of the original evidence. It is the *first* technical step in the forensic workflow and is the foundation upon which the *entire* investigation rests.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 1 Anchor Concept:** Acquisition is governed by the **Order of Volatility (RFC 3227)** — evidence is collected from *most volatile* to *least volatile* sources to maximize evidentiary value.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a forensic photographer arriving at a crime scene. Before anyone moves a chair or a glass, the photographer first takes **photographs, then collects blood samples, then fingerprints, then dusts for prints, and finally seals the room**. The photographer never *alters* the scene. Similarly, a cyber forensic investigator first grabs the **most fragile evidence** (RAM, network state, running processes) before touching the **persistent storage** (hard disk), and always works on a **copy**, never the original.

---

## 1.5 Storage Formats for Digital Evidence — Formal Definition

A **Digital Evidence Storage Format** (or *Forensic Image Format*) is a **standardized container file structure** used to store an exact replica of a storage device along with **metadata, integrity hashes, case information, and optional compression**. These formats ensure that the image is **self-documenting, verifiable, and portable** across different forensic tools and jurisdictions.

### The Three Industry-Standard Formats (KTU High-Yield)

1. **Raw Format (`.dd`, `.img`, `.001`)** — A pure bit-stream with no metadata. The simplest, most universal format.
2. **Advanced Forensic Format (AFF)** — An open, extensible format with built-in metadata and internal hashing.
3. **EnCase Format (`.E01`, `.Ex01`)** — A proprietary format by Guidance Software (OpenText) with compression and metadata chunks.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Expected Answer:** When asked *“Name a forensic format that supports internal segmentation and metadata,”* the answer is **EnCase (.E01)** or **AFF** — *not* raw format.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Forensic Investigation Process (End-to-End)

The cyber forensic workflow is a **closed-loop pipeline** governed by Daubert/Frye standards (US) and Section 65B (India). KTU expects students to know **all six phases** in order.

### Phase-by-Phase Breakdown

1. **Identification**
   - **Why:** Recognize that an incident has occurred and determine the *scope* of devices, users, and systems involved.
   - **How:** SIEM alerts, IDS triggers, user complaints, log analysis, threat intelligence feeds.
   - **Output:** A formal *scope document* listing the assets to be acquired.

2. **Preservation**
   - **Why:** Prevent *spoliation* (destruction) of evidence.
   - **How:** Isolate the device from the network (but keep it powered on if RAM is volatile), photograph the screen, document the scene.
   - **Output:** A *chain of custody form* signed by the custodian.

3. **Collection (Acquisition)**
   - **Why:** Create a forensically sound image of the source.
   - **How:** Use hardware/software write-blockers, hash the source, hash the image, verify match.
   - **Output:** A *forensic image file* (e.g., `.E01`) on sterile media.

4. **Examination**
   - **Why:** Reduce the volume of data to *relevant* artifacts.
   - **How:** Use forensic tools (EnCase, FTK, Autopsy, X-Ways) to parse file systems, recover deleted files, extract registry hives, decode logs.
   - **Output:** A *filtered dataset* of suspicious artifacts.

5. **Analysis**
   - **Why:** Correlate artifacts to reconstruct the *who, what, when, where, how, why* of the incident.
   - **How:** Timeline analysis, hash set matching (NSRL), keyword search, malware sandboxing.
   - **Output:** An *investigative theory* backed by artifacts.

6. **Presentation**
   - **Why:** Communicate findings to a non-technical audience (judge, jury, management).
   - **How:** Forensic report with exhibits, exhibits list, expert testimony.
   - **Output:** A *court-admissible report*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** The acronym **IPCEAP** (Identification, Preservation, Collection, Examination, Analysis, Presentation) is the standard answer for "List the phases of a digital forensic investigation."

---

## 2.2 The Order of Volatility (RFC 3227)

This is a **de-facto standard** for prioritizing evidence collection. Evidence is captured from the *most fragile* (changes in microseconds) to the *most persistent* (survives for years).

### Volatility Hierarchy (Top = Most Volatile)

| Priority | Evidence Source | Approximate Lifespan | Acquisition Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CPU Registers, Cache | Nanoseconds | Non-trivial (crash dump) |
| 2 | Routing Table, ARP Cache, Process Table, Kernel Statistics | Seconds | `netstat -ano`, `arp -a` |
| 3 | Temporary File Systems / tmp | Hours | `df`, `mount` |
| 4 | Disk / File System | Days to Years | Disk imaging |
| 5 | Remote Logging / Data on Server | Years | Court order to ISP/Cloud |
| 6 | Physical Configuration, Network Topology | Years | Photographs, network diagrams |
| 7 | Archival Media (Tape, Off-site Backup) | Decades | Subpoena to backup provider |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often cite *"RAM"* as the *most* volatile. While RAM is highly volatile, RFC 3227 actually places **CPU registers** as the most volatile. RAM is typically the *practical* starting point for most investigations.

---

## 2.3 Types of Data Acquisition

### A. Live Acquisition (Acquiring a Powered-On System)
- **Definition:** The system is *running* during acquisition. The investigator collects volatile data (RAM, network connections, encrypted volumes) in addition to disk data.
- **Use Case:** Encrypted drives (BitLocker, FileVault), where the decryption keys are only present in RAM.
- **Risk:** Tools running on the host can *alter* the system (a fundamental violation of forensic soundness). This is mitigated by using **forensically sound live acquisition tools** (e.g., WinFE, Paladin, FTK Imager Lite).

### B. Dead Acquisition (Acquiring a Powered-Off System)
- **Definition:** The system is *powered off* and the storage media is removed and connected to a **write-blocker** before imaging.
- **Use Case:** The gold standard for non-encrypted media.
- **Advantage:** Zero modification to the source media.

### C. Static vs. Sparse Acquisition
- **Static:** Captures *every* sector of the disk, including unallocated space, slack space, and bad sectors.
- **Sparse:** Captures only *allocated* sectors. Smaller file size but loses unallocated data (often where deleted files reside).

### D. Logical vs. Physical Acquisition
- **Logical:** Acquires *active file system objects only* (folders, files). Used in mobile forensics where physical acquisition is impossible due to encryption.
- **Physical:** Acquires *bit-by-bit* including deleted and slack space.

---

## 2.4 The Write-Blocker — Guardian of Forensic Integrity

A **Write-Blocker** is a hardware or software device that allows **read-only access** to a storage device, preventing any write command from reaching the media. This is **mandatory** for forensically sound dead acquisition.

### Types
- **Hardware Write-Blocker:** A physical device inserted between the suspect drive and the forensic workstation (e.g., WiebeTech, Tableau, ICS). The gold standard.
- **Software Write-Blocker:** A kernel-level driver that filters write commands (e.g., `BlockOn`, Windows built-in `bitlocker` in some modes, macOS `hdik`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Point:** Always state the *make and model* of the write-blocker used in the chain of custody documentation (e.g., "Tableau T35es SATA write-blocker, S/N: X1234").

---

## 2.5 Integrity Verification — Cryptographic Hashing

The **single most critical** operation in data acquisition. Without hashing, the image has *zero* evidentiary value.

### The Hashing Equation

$$ H_{\text{image}} = \text{Hash}(\text{ForensicImage}) $$
$$ H_{\text{verify}} = \text{Hash}(\text{ForensicImage}') $$
$$ \text{Integrity} = \begin{cases} \text{VERIFIED} & \text{if } H_{\text{image}} \equiv H_{\text{verify}} \\ \text{COMPROMISED} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$

Where `Hash` is a one-way cryptographic function applied to the *entire* bit-stream of the media.

### Industry-Standard Hashing Algorithms (KTU High-Yield)

| Algorithm | Output Size | Status | Forensic Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MD5** | 128 bits (32 hex chars) | **Cryptographically broken** (collisions found) | Legacy verification only |
| **SHA-1** | 160 bits (40 hex chars) | **Deprecated** (SHAttered attack, 2017) | Legacy verification only |
| **SHA-256** | 256 bits (64 hex chars) | **Current Standard** | Primary hash in modern tools |
| **SHA-512** | 512 bits (128 hex chars) | **High Security** | Classified / Federal use |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Trap Question:** *"MD5 is sufficient for forensic hashing because collisions are hard to generate."* — **FALSE.** MD5 collisions can be generated in *seconds* on commodity hardware. Modern forensics uses **dual hashing** (e.g., MD5 + SHA-256) for backwards compatibility, *not* for security.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Quick Reference

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Hash Match Condition** | $H_{\text{src}} = H_{\text{img}}$ | Verifies image integrity |
| **Order of Volatility** | $t_{\text{CPU}} < t_{\text{RAM}} < t_{\text{Disk}} < t_{\text{Backup}}$ | Prioritize evidence collection |
| **Storage Capacity (Raw)** | $C = N_{\text{sectors}} \times 512 \text{ bytes}$ | Convert sector count to bytes |
| **Storage Capacity (SI)** | $C_{\text{GB}} = C / 10^9$ | Decimal GB (manufacturer rating) |
| **Storage Capacity (Binary)** | $C_{\text{GiB}} = C / 2^{30}$ | Binary GiB (OS rating) |
| **Hash Output Length (SHA-256)** | $L = 256 \text{ bits} = 64 \text{ hex}$ | Verify hash string length |
| **Sector Size (Modern HDD/SSD)** | $S = 4096 \text{ bytes (4Kn)}$ | Native Advanced Format |
| **Sector Size (Legacy)** | $S = 512 \text{ bytes (512n)}$ | MBR-era drives |
| **Disk Imaging Time Estimate** | $T \approx C / R_{\text{USB3}}$ | Where $R_{\text{USB3}} \approx 100 \text{ MB/s}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic for Forensic Formats:** **"R A E S"** → **R**aw, **A**FF, **E**01, **S**MART (old format, rarely used now).

---

## 2.7 Engineering & Real-World Utility

The principles of data acquisition and integrity verification are not confined to cybercrime. They are foundational to:

- **E-Discovery (Civil Litigation):** Corporates image employee laptops to find smoking-gun emails for civil suits.
- **Insider Threat Detection:** A departing employee's laptop is imaged to prove IP theft.
- **Ransomware Response:** A live image of the infected endpoint is taken *before* wiping, to determine the initial access vector and decryptor variant.
- **Data Breach Notification:** Under the **IT Act 2000 (India)** and **GDPR (EU)**, organizations must forensically image compromised servers to fulfill *breach notification* obligations.
- **Aviation & Maritime Forensics:** Black box (FDR/CVR) data is acquired using the same write-blocker + hash verification methodology.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Bit-Stream Hashing for Forensic Image Integrity

The forensic hashing process is mathematically equivalent to applying a cryptographic hash function to the **concatenation of all sectors** of the storage media. Below is the step-by-step logical derivation.

### Step 1: Conceptualize the Source Media as a Bit Stream
Let the source storage media $M$ contain $N$ sectors, each of size $S$ bytes.

$$ M = \big\vert B_1 \,\vert\, B_2 \,\vert\, B_3 \,\vert\, \ldots \,\vert\, B_N \big\vert $$

Where $B_i$ is the $i$-th sector of the media and $\vert$ denotes concatenation.

### Step 2: Define the Total Byte Count

$$ C_{\text{total}} = N \times S $$

For a typical 1 TB HDD with 4Kn (4096-byte) sectors:

$$ N = \frac{10^{12} \text{ bytes}}{4096 \text{ bytes/sector}} \approx 244,140,625 \text{ sectors} $$

### Step 3: Apply the Cryptographic Hash Function
The forensic hash is computed over the *entire* bit-stream:

$$ H_{\text{src}} = \text{SHA-256}(B_1 \,\vert\, B_2 \,\vert\, B_3 \,\vert\, \ldots \,\vert\, B_N) $$

### Step 4: Create the Forensic Image
The acquisition tool reads each sector via the write-blocker and writes it to the destination file $I$:

$$ I = M $$

The acquisition tool then computes the hash of the image:

$$ H_{\text{img}} = \text{SHA-256}(I) $$

### Step 5: Compare Hashes for Verification

$$ \Delta H = H_{\text{src}} - H_{\text{img}} $$

The integrity is verified **if and only if**:

$$ \Delta H \equiv 0 \pmod{2^{256}} $$

In practice, the tool performs a **string equality** check on the hex representation:

$$ \text{Verified} \iff H_{\text{src}} \equiv H_{\text{img}} $$

### Step 6: Document the Hashes in the Chain of Custody

$$ \text{CoC Entry} = \{ H_{\text{src}}, H_{\text{img}}, \text{Algorithm}, \text{Timestamp}, \text{Examiner}, \text{Write-Blocker} \} $$

### Worked Numerical Example: Hashing a 2 GB USB Drive

Let us compute the integrity verification for a hypothetical USB drive.

**Given:**
- Drive capacity $C = 2 \text{ GB} = 2 \times 10^9 \text{ bytes}$
- Sector size $S = 512 \text{ bytes (legacy)}}$
- Hash algorithm: SHA-256
- Source SHA-256 hash: `a3f5b8c9d2e1f4a7b6c5d8e9f0a1b2c3d4e5f6a7b8c9d0e1f2a3b4c5d6e7f8a9`
- Image SHA-256 hash: `a3f5b8c9d2e1f4a7b6c5d8e9f0a1b2c3d4e5f6a7b8c9d0e1f2a3b4c5d6e7f8a9`

**Step A — Compute number of sectors:**

$$ N = \frac{C}{S} = \frac{2 \times 10^9 \text{ bytes}}{512 \text{ bytes/sector}} $$

$$ N = 3,906,250 \text{ sectors} $$

**Step B — Verify hash equality:**

$$ H_{\text{src}} \stackrel{?}{=} H_{\text{img}} $$

Substituting the values:

$$ \texttt{a3f5b8c9...f8a9} \stackrel{?}{=} \texttt{a3f5b8c9...f8a9} $$

$$ \therefore H_{\text{src}} \equiv H_{\text{img}} $$

**Step C — Conclusion:**

The forensic image is **VERIFIED** and admissible in court.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** You are not expected to *compute* SHA-256 by hand. You are expected to *explain* the procedure, *state the input*, and *interpret the result*. Marks are awarded for the procedural explanation, not the bit-twiddling.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Estimating Forensic Acquisition Time

Acquisition time is a critical planning parameter in KTU's module questions on practical forensics.

### The Acquisition Time Equation

The total time $T_{\text{acq}}$ to image a drive is given by:

$$ T_{\text{acq}} = \frac{C_{\text{data}}}{R_{\text{eff}}} + T_{\text{overhead}} $$

Where:
- $C_{\text{data}}$ = total bytes to be imaged
- $R_{\text{eff}}$ = effective sustained throughput
- $T_{\text{overhead}}$ = tool setup, hash computation, verification pass (typically 5–10% of $C/R$)

### Effective Throughput Calculation

The effective throughput is the *minimum* of the bottleneck components:

$$ R_{\text{eff}} = \min(R_{\text{read}}, R_{\text{bus}}, R_{\text{write}}, R_{\text{hash}}) $$

Where:
- $R_{\text{read}}$ = source drive read speed
- $R_{\text{bus}}$ = interface bandwidth (USB 3.0 $\approx 5 \text{ Gbps} \approx 625 \text{ MB/s}$)
- $R_{\text{write}}$ = destination drive write speed
- $R_{\text{hash}}$ = CPU hashing speed (SHA-256 ~ 500 MB/s on modern CPU)

### Worked Example: 500 GB HDD over USB 3.0

**Given:**
- $C_{\text{data}} = 500 \text{ GB} = 500 \times 10^9 \text{ bytes}$
- Source: HDD @ $R_{\text{read}} = 120 \text{ MB/s}$
- USB 3.0 bus: $R_{\text{bus}} = 400 \text{ MB/s}$ (practical, not theoretical)
- Destination SSD: $R_{\text{write}} = 300 \text{ MB/s}$
- Hash: $R_{\text{hash}} = 500 \text{ MB/s}$

**Step A — Identify the bottleneck:**

$$ R_{\text{eff}} = \min(120, 400, 300, 500) \text{ MB/s} $$

$$ R_{\text{eff}} = 120 \text{ MB/s} \text{ (HDD read is the bottleneck)} $$

**Step B — Compute base time:**

$$ T_{\text{base}} = \frac{500 \times 10^9 \text{ bytes}}{120 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/s}} $$

$$ T_{\text{base}} = 4166.67 \text{ seconds} $$

**Step C — Add overhead (5%):**

$$ T_{\text{overhead}} = 0.05 \times 4166.67 = 208.33 \text{ seconds} $$

**Step D — Total acquisition time:**

$$ T_{\text{acq}} = 4166.67 + 208.33 $$

$$ T_{\text{acq}} = 4375 \text{ seconds} \approx 72.9 \text{ minutes} \approx 1 \text{ hour } 13 \text{ minutes} $$

### Engineering Interpretation
The HDD is the bottleneck. To reduce acquisition time, an investigator would use a **faster source drive** (e.g., an SSD source) or a **faster bus** (e.g., SATA III direct connection instead of USB 3.0). This is why KTU examiners often give a "calculate acquisition time" question.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Acquisition Method

| Scenario | Volatile Evidence Required? | Encryption Likely? | Recommended Method | Why? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Running server, suspected breach** | Yes (RAM, network state) | Possible | Live acquisition + Dead of disk | Preserve RAM first |
| **Powered-off desktop, no encryption** | No | No | Dead acquisition (write-blocker) | Gold standard, zero modification |
| **Powered-on laptop with BitLocker** | Yes (decryption keys in RAM) | **Yes** | Live acquisition only | Dead acquisition yields encrypted gibberish |
| **Mobile phone (locked)** | RAM is volatile, baseband volatile | Yes (Secure Enclave) | Logical + Chip-off (extreme) | Logical for accessible data |
| **Cloud server (AWS EC2)** | Yes (ephemeral) | N/A | Snapshot via cloud API | Physical seizure impossible |
| **USB drive found at scene** | No | Maybe (VeraCrypt) | Dead first, then live if encrypted | Try dead first, fall back to live |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "VOLATILE FIRST, DEAD WHEN SAFE"**: If encryption is suspected, you *must* capture RAM live; otherwise, prefer dead acquisition for forensic soundness.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Hash Length Comparison (Binary Math)

KTU students are sometimes asked to compare the theoretical collision resistance of hash functions.

### MD5 Collision Resistance (Theoretical)

The MD5 algorithm produces a $128$-bit output. By the **Birthday Paradox**, a collision is expected after approximately:

$$ N_{\text{collision}}^{\text{MD5}} \approx 2^{n/2} = 2^{64} \text{ attempts} $$

In practice, MD5 collisions are generated in **seconds** on consumer GPUs (e.g., `HashClash`, `FastColl`).

### SHA-256 Collision Resistance

The SHA-256 algorithm produces a $256$-bit output:

$$ N_{\text{collision}}^{\text{SHA-256}} \approx 2^{128} \text{ attempts} $$

This is **astronomically large** ($3.4 \times 10^{38}$) and considered computationally infeasible with current technology.

### Practical Comparison Table

| Algorithm | Output Bits | Theoretical Birthday Bound | Practical Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| MD5 | 128 | $2^{64} \approx 1.8 \times 10^{19}$ | **Broken** (collisions in seconds) |
| SHA-1 | 160 | $2^{80} \approx 1.2 \times 10^{24}$ | **Broken** (SHAttered, 2017) |
| SHA-256 | 256 | $2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ | **Secure** |
| SHA-512 | 512 | $2^{256}$ | **Highly Secure** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in forensics:** If your forensic image is hashed only with MD5, a defense attorney can argue that the image *could* have been swapped with a maliciously crafted one that shares the same MD5 hash. SHA-256 effectively closes this attack vector.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 The Digital Forensic Investigation Lifecycle (IPCEAP Model)

This diagram maps the six-phase investigation workflow with decision gates and feedback loops, as expected in KTU Module 1 viva questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incident Detection / Trigger Event] --> B[Phase 1: Identification]
    B --> C{Scope Defined?}
    C -- No --> B
    C -- Yes --> D[Phase 2: Preservation - Chain of Custody Initiated]
    D --> E[Phase 3: Collection / Data Acquisition]
    E --> F{Hash Verified?}
    F -- No --> E
    F -- Yes --> G[Phase 4: Examination]
    G --> H[Phase 5: Analysis]
    H --> I[Phase 6: Presentation - Forensic Report]
    I --> J[Court / Management Submission]
    J --> K{Appeal / New Evidence?}
    K -- Yes --> H
    K -- No --> L[Case Closed]
```

### Diagram Walkthrough
- **A → B:** An incident (intrusion alert, complaint) triggers formal identification.
- **B → C → D:** The investigator must define the *scope* (which devices, which users) before preserving.
- **D → E:** Preservation seals the scene and the collection phase begins.
- **E → F → G:** A **decision gate** on hash verification. If the hash of the source and image do not match, the acquisition must be redone.
- **G → H → I → J:** Linear progression through examination, analysis, and reporting.
- **J → K → L:** A feedback loop for appeals or newly discovered evidence.

---

## 4.2 Forensic Image Acquisition Pipeline (Block Architecture)

This diagram represents the *internal data flow* during a forensic disk acquisition, mapping how the source drive, write-blocker, acquisition tool, and verification engine interact.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Suspect Storage Media] -->|Read Command Only| B[Hardware Write-Blocker]
    B -->|Filtered Read I/O| C[Forensic Acquisition Tool e.g. FTK Imager]
    C -->|Bit Stream| D[Hash Engine 1 - SHA-256]
    D --> E[Source Hash Value Hsrc]
    C -->|Bit Stream| F[Destination Storage]
    F -->|Read Back| G[Hash Engine 2 - SHA-256]
    G --> H[Image Hash Value Himg]
    E --> I{Comparator}
    H --> I
    I -->|Match| J[VERIFIED - Case Proceeds]
    I -->|Mismatch| K[REJECTED - Reacquire]
    F --> L[Forensic Image File .E01 or .dd]
    L --> M[Chain of Custody Record]
```

### Diagram Walkthrough
- **A → B:** The suspect drive is connected to a hardware write-blocker. The write-blocker *physically* blocks all write commands.
- **B → C:** The forensic tool issues read commands. The write-blocker allows them through.
- **C → D and C → F:** The bit stream is *simultaneously* hashed and written to the destination.
- **D → E:** The source hash is computed in real-time as the disk is read.
- **F → G → H:** The completed image is read back and re-hashed.
- **E + H → I:** A comparator checks the two hashes. **Match = Verified; Mismatch = Reject.**
- **F → L → M:** The final image is a self-documenting forensic file with associated chain-of-custody metadata.

---

## 4.3 Order of Volatility — Visual Hierarchy

This block diagram represents RFC 3227's evidence-prioritization pyramid. Evidence at the top is *most* volatile; evidence at the bottom is *most* persistent.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Level 1 - CPU Registers, Cache<br/>Lifespan: Nanoseconds] --> P2[Level 2 - Routing Table, ARP Cache, Process Table<br/>Lifespan: Seconds]
    P2 --> P3[Level 3 - Temporary File Systems / tmp<br/>Lifespan: Hours]
    P3 --> P4[Level 4 - Disk / File System<br/>Lifespan: Days to Years]
    P4 --> P5[Level 5 - Remote Logging, Cloud Data<br/>Lifespan: Years]
    P5 --> P6[Level 6 - Physical Configuration, Network Topology<br/>Lifespan: Years]
    P6 --> P7[Level 7 - Archival Media - Tape, Off-site Backup<br/>Lifespan: Decades]
```

### Diagram Walkthrough
- The investigator begins at the **top** (Level 1) and moves downward.
- At each level, the appropriate tool is used (e.g., `volatility` for RAM, `dd` for disk).
- Skipping levels is **never** acceptable in a public investigation.

---

## 4.4 Public vs. Private Investigation Decision Tree

This diagram helps KTU students answer scenario-based questions on *which* type of investigation applies.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Incident Detected] --> Q1{Is there a criminal<br/>statute violation?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> PubPath[Public Investigation]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Does it involve<br/>corporate policy<br/>violation or civil claim?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> PrivPath[Private Investigation]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is there cross-border<br/>or sovereign data?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> PubPath
    Q3 -- No --> End[Informal Internal Review]
    PubPath --> P1[Obtain Search Warrant]
    PubPath --> P2[Comply with CrPC / IT Act]
    PubPath --> P3[Report to Magistrate]
    PrivPath --> PR1[Verify Employee AUP Consent]
    PrivPath --> PR2[Image Corporate-Owned Asset]
    PrivPath --> PR3[Report to Management / HR]
```

### Diagram Walkthrough
- The **first decision** is whether a criminal law has been breached. If yes → Public.
- If not, the **second decision** is whether corporate policy or a civil claim is involved. If yes → Private.
- The diagram explicitly maps the procedural differences: **Warrants vs. Consent**, **Courts vs. HR**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between public and private investigations in digital forensics. Mention any three key differences.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Public Investigation | Private Investigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Conducted By** | Law enforcement agencies (Police, CBI) | Corporate teams or private DFIR firms |
| **Legal Authority** | Search warrants under CrPC/BNSS | Employment contracts and AUPs |
| **End Goal** | Criminal prosecution in court | Internal disciplinary action or civil suit |

Public investigations are governed by criminal law and require judicial authorization, whereas private investigations are governed by corporate policy and contractual consent. Public investigations produce court-admissible evidence, while private investigations typically produce internally actionable evidence that may later be escalated to public authorities.

*(3 marks: 2 for tabular differentiation, 1 for the legal basis explanation.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**List any three storage formats for digital evidence and state one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Raw Format (`.dd`)** — *Advantage:* Universally compatible with all forensic and non-forensic tools; no proprietary lock-in.
2. **Advanced Forensic Format (AFF)** — *Advantage:* Open standard with internal metadata and built-in hashing, eliminating the need for a sidecar hash file.
3. **EnCase Format (`.E01`)** — *Advantage:* Supports compression and internal segmentation (splitting into multiple files for archival).

*(3 marks: 1 for each format-advantage pair.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Order of Volatility as defined in RFC 3227. Why is it critical to follow this order during evidence collection in a public investigation?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 750 GB hard disk drive is to be imaged using a USB 3.0 write-blocker. The source HDD has a sustained read speed of 150 MB/s. The destination SSD has a write speed of 400 MB/s. The forensic workstation's SHA-256 hashing engine can process 600 MB/s. Calculate the estimated acquisition time including a 5% tool overhead.

---

### Model Solution for Question A (a)

The **Order of Volatility** is a guideline published in **RFC 3227** ("Guidelines for Evidence Collection and Archiving") by the Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF). It dictates that an investigator must collect digital evidence in a sequence based on the *expected lifetime* of the data — most volatile first, most persistent last.

**The Seven Levels (Most to Least Volatile):**

1. **CPU Registers and Cache** — Lifespan: nanoseconds. Hardest to capture; usually requires a hardware debugger.
2. **Routing Table, ARP Cache, Process Table, Kernel Statistics** — Lifespan: seconds. Captured via `netstat`, `arp -a`, `ps`.
3. **Temporary File Systems (`/tmp`)** — Lifespan: hours. Mounted filesystems that vanish on reboot.
4. **Disk and File System Data** — Lifespan: days to years. The standard disk image.
5. **Remote Logging and Monitoring Data** — Lifespan: years. Held by ISPs, cloud providers, SIEMs.
6. **Physical Configuration and Network Topology** — Lifespan: years. Photographs, diagrams.
7. **Archival Media** — Lifespan: decades. Off-site tapes, optical media.

**Why Critical in Public Investigations:**

- **Preservation of Fragile Evidence:** In a public investigation, any evidence lost due to procedural error is *irretrievable* and can lead to acquittal.
- **Legal Defensibility:** The defense will challenge the chain of custody. Following an internationally recognized RFC standard demonstrates *due diligence* and *forensic soundness*.
- **Encryption Key Capture:** Modern drives (BitLocker, FileVault) store decryption keys in **RAM (Level 2)**. If the investigator powers off the machine first, the keys are lost, and the entire disk becomes undecryptable — a catastrophic failure.
- **Court Admissibility:** Under Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act, the *manner of collection* is scrutinized. Failure to follow the order of volatility can render the evidence inadmissible.

**Conclusion:** The Order of Volatility is the **operational backbone** of public cyber forensic investigations and a non-negotiable step in maintaining evidentiary integrity.

*(7 marks: 2 for listing levels, 3 for explanation, 2 for legal relevance.)*

---

### Model Solution for Question A (b)

**Given:**
- $C = 750 \text{ GB} = 750 \times 10^9 \text{ bytes}$
- $R_{\text{read}} = 150 \text{ MB/s} = 150 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/s}$
- $R_{\text{write}} = 400 \text{ MB/s}$
- $R_{\text{hash}} = 600 \text{ MB/s}$
- $R_{\text{USB3}} \approx 400 \text{ MB/s}$ (practical)
- Overhead: 5%

**Step 1 — Identify the Bottleneck:**

$$ R_{\text{eff}} = \min(R_{\text{read}}, R_{\text{write}}, R_{\text{hash}}, R_{\text{USB3}}) $$

$$ R_{\text{eff}} = \min(150, 400, 600, 400) \text{ MB/s} $$

$$ R_{\text{eff}} = 150 \text{ MB/s} $$

The source HDD's read speed is the bottleneck.

**Step 2 — Compute Base Acquisition Time:**

$$ T_{\text{base}} = \frac{C}{R_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{750 \times 10^9 \text{ bytes}}{150 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/s}} $$

$$ T_{\text{base}} = 5000 \text{ seconds} $$

**Step 3 — Compute Overhead:**

$$ T_{\text{overhead}} = 0.05 \times 5000 = 250 \text{ seconds} $$

**Step 4 — Compute Total Acquisition Time:**

$$ T_{\text{acq}} = T_{\text{base}} + T_{\text{overhead}} = 5000 + 250 $$

$$ T_{\text{acq}} = 5250 \text{ seconds} $$

**Step 5 — Convert to Hours and Minutes:**

$$ T_{\text{acq}} = \frac{5250}{60} = 87.5 \text{ minutes} $$

$$ T_{\text{acq}} = 1 \text{ hour } 27 \text{ minutes } 30 \text{ seconds} $$

**Conclusion:** The forensic acquisition of the 750 GB HDD will take approximately **1 hour, 27 minutes, and 30 seconds**, assuming the HDD read speed is the limiting factor.

*(7 marks: 1 for identifying bottleneck, 2 for base time, 1 for overhead, 2 for final conversion, 1 for conclusion.)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the role of a write-blocker in forensic data acquisition. Distinguish between hardware and software write-blockers with examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the structure of the EnCase (.E01) forensic image format. What metadata fields does it contain, and why is it preferred over raw format in court proceedings?

---

### Model Solution for Question B (a)

A **Write-Blocker** is a forensic tool that allows **read-only access** to a storage device, preventing any modification to the original evidence during the acquisition process. It is the *cornerstone* of forensic soundness, ensuring the **integrity** and **admissibility** of digital evidence.

**Functions of a Write-Blocker:**
- **Blocks all write commands** issued by the operating system or forensic tool from reaching the storage media.
- **Allows read commands** to pass through, enabling the forensic tool to image the drive.
- **Provides a forensic "air gap"** between the suspect media and the host operating system, preventing inadvertent writes (e.g., from the OS mounting the drive).
- **Generates a log** of all blocked commands for chain-of-custody documentation.

**Hardware Write-Blocker:**
- A **physical device** connected *between* the suspect drive and the forensic workstation.
- Operates at the **hardware/IDE/SATA/NVMe protocol level** — the OS never sees the drive.
- **Examples:** Tableau T35es (SATA), Tableau T8 (USB), WiebeTech Forensic ComboDock.
- **Advantages:** OS-independent, tamper-evident, accepted as best practice in court.
- **Disadvantages:** Cost (₹15,000 – ₹80,000), need for correct interface (SATA vs. NVMe).

**Software Write-Blocker:**
- A **kernel-level driver or application** that filters write commands at the OS level.
- **Examples:** `Tableau Disk Monitor`, `WriteBlocker EnCase`, macOS `hdik`, Linux `dm-verity`.
- **Advantages:** No additional hardware, can be deployed remotely.
- **Disadvantages:** OS-dependent, can be bypassed by rootkits, less defensible in court.

**Conclusion:** Hardware write-blockers are the **gold standard** in public investigations. Software write-blockers are acceptable in private investigations or live acquisition scenarios where hardware deployment is impractical.

*(7 marks: 2 for definition/function, 3 for hardware, 2 for software comparison.)*

---

### Model Solution for Question B (b)

The **EnCase Forensic Image Format (`.E01`)** is a proprietary container format developed by **Guidance Software (now OpenText)**. It is the **de-facto industry standard** for forensic imaging in law enforcement agencies worldwide.

**Structure of an E01 File:**

An `.E01` file is composed of a **sequence of segments**, each containing the following logical sections:

1. **Header Segment** — Contains case metadata:
   - Case number, examiner name, acquisition date and time.
   - Hash values (MD5, SHA-1) of the entire image.
   - Source drive information (make, model, serial number, capacity).
   - Compression algorithm used and compression ratio.

2. **Data Segment(s)** — Contains the actual bit-stream of the source media, broken into **chunks** of configurable size (default 64 KB compressed). The chunks can be **compressed** (using deflate/zlib) to reduce storage footprint.

3. **Table of Contents (TOC)** — An index at the **end** of the file (and optionally at the start) that maps data segment offsets to logical disk sectors, allowing random access without reading the entire file.

4. **Hash Segment** — Stores cyclic and SHA-1 hashes of each data segment, enabling **self-verification** of the image at any time.

5. **Digest / Footer** — A final hash over the entire file, signed with the examiner's credentials.

**Key Metadata Fields Stored:**

- Case Information: Case number, evidence number, examiner ID, organization.
- Device Information: Manufacturer, model, serial number, firmware version, capacity, sector count.
- Acquisition Information: Date, time, timezone, tool version, write-blocker used.
- Integrity Information: MD5, SHA-1 hashes of source and image.
- Operational Information: Compression, bad sector list, error counts.

**Why Preferred Over Raw Format in Court Proceedings:**

1. **Self-Documenting:** The image contains its own case metadata, eliminating the need for separate sidecar files.
2. **Integrity Assurance:** Built-in hash verification at segment level — any tampering is immediately detectable.
3. **Compression:** Reduces storage costs without losing evidentiary value.
4. **Segmentation:** A 4 TB image can be split into multiple files, fitting on FAT32-formatted DVDs or smaller drives.
5. **Legal Acceptance:** EnCase has been cited in thousands of court rulings worldwide and is the standard expected by the FBI, INTERPOL, and CBI.
6. **Tool Portability:** Modern forensic tools (FTK, X-Ways, Autopsy) can read `.E01` files natively.

**Disadvantages:** Proprietary format, requires licensed EnCase software for full creation, larger CPU overhead due to compression.

*(7 marks: 2 for structure, 2 for metadata, 3 for legal advantages.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing "Order of Volatility" with "Order of Acquisition."** The order of volatility is governed by data *lifespan*, not by data *size* or *importance*.
> 2. **Stating "MD5 is sufficient" in answers.** Always recommend **SHA-256** for forensic integrity. MD5 is a mark-deductor.
> 3. **Forgetting the chain-of-custody documentation** in acquisition questions. The examiner must *document* the write-blocker model, hash values, and timestamps.
> 4. **Mixing public and private investigation concepts.** If the question says "warrant," you are in a public investigation. If it says "employee laptop" with consent, you are in a private investigation.
> 5. **Not converting units in acquisition time problems.** Marks are awarded for *unit conversion* (bytes → GB, seconds → minutes/hours). A correct number with wrong units scores zero.
> 6. **Writing "RAM is the most volatile."** While practically true, RFC 3227 lists **CPU registers** as the most volatile. Mentioning this distinction scores bonus marks.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### 🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Public vs. Private:** Public = Criminal court + warrants; Private = Corporate HR + consent.
- [x] **Six Phases (IPCEAP):** Identification, Preservation, Collection, Examination, Analysis, Presentation.
- [x] **Order of Volatility (RFC 3227):** CPU → RAM → /tmp → Disk → Remote → Topology → Archive.
- [x] **Write-Blocker:** Hardware preferred (Tableau, WiebeTech). Blocks all writes, allows reads.
- [x] **Acquisition Types:** Live (powered-on) vs. Dead (powered-off). Choose based on encryption.
- [x] **Hashing:** SHA-256 is the modern standard. MD5 is broken. Always hash source AND image.
- [x] **Forensic Formats:** **R**aw (`.dd`), **A**FF (open), **E**01 (EnCase, court-preferred).
- [x] **EnCase (.E01):** Self-documenting, compressed, segmented, internal hashing.
- [x] **AFF:** Open-source alternative, Linux-friendly, supports internal metadata.
- [x] **Chain of Custody:** Document *every* transfer with timestamp, person, and hash.
- [x] **Volatile First:** Always collect RAM and network state *before* powering off the suspect machine.
- [x] **Section 65B (India):** Required for admissibility of electronic evidence.
- [x] **Acquisition Time:** $T = C/R_{\text{eff}} + T_{\text{overhead}}$, where $R_{\text{eff}}$ is the **minimum** of all component speeds.
- [x] **Logical vs. Physical:** Physical = bit-stream (best); Logical = active files only (mobile).
- [x] **Best Practice:** Always work on the **image**, never the original.

### 🏆 Top 5 KTU-Exam-Ready Definitions to Memorize

1. **Order of Volatility** — RFC 3227 standard for prioritizing evidence collection based on data lifetime.
2. **Forensic Image** — A bit-for-bit, verified, and hashed replica of a storage device.
3. **Write-Blocker** — A hardware/software tool that enforces read-only access to storage media.
4. **Chain of Custody** — The documented and unbroken trail of evidence possession from collection to court.
5. **Hash Function (Forensic)** — A one-way cryptographic function (SHA-256) used to verify image integrity.

### 🎯 KTU 2024 Mark Distribution Pattern (Predicted)
- **Part A (3 marks):** 1 question × 3 marks = 3 marks (definitions, comparisons).
- **Part B (14 marks):** 1 question with internal choice = 14 marks.
  - Sub-part (a): 7 marks (theory/concept explanation).
  - Sub-part (b): 7 marks (numerical/procedural/application).

> **Final Study Tip:** When in doubt, **draw a diagram** in your answer book. KTU examiners award bonus marks for clear, well-labeled flowcharts of the forensic process. The IPCEAP cycle and Order of Volatility pyramid are the two highest-yield diagrams for this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determining the Best Acquisition Method, Forensic Protocol for Evidence Acquisition, Digital Forensics Standards and Guidelines, Incident Response Stages, Digital Evidence Identification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT602 - CYBER FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Determining the Best Acquisition Method, Forensic Protocol for Evidence Acquisition, Digital Forensics Standards and Guidelines, Incident Response Stages, Digital Evidence Identification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — CORE DEFINITIONS & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Digital Evidence: The Cornerstone of Cyber Forensics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (per ISO/IEC 27037):**
> *Digital Evidence* is any information of probative value that is stored or transmitted in digital form, on which a court or competent authority relies upon to make a decision or reach a conclusion regarding an incident under investigation.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of **digital evidence** as the *fingerprints left at a crime scene* — except now the perpetrator touched a keyboard, not a doorknob. Every click, every file created, every packet sent leaves a "smudge" on the hard drive, in RAM, in network logs, or in the cloud. The forensic investigator's job is to find, preserve, and present these smudges without smearing them further.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Cardinal Properties of Digital Evidence (exam-favorite):**
> 1. **Latent** — invisible to the naked eye (e.g., deleted files, slack space).
> 2. **Fragile** — easily altered, overwritten, or destroyed.
> 3. **Volatile** — may exist only while a system is powered on (e.g., RAM contents, routing tables).

---

## 1.2 Digital Evidence Identification

Evidence identification is the **first active step** in any forensic workflow. It is the systematic process of recognizing, locating, and documenting potential sources of digital evidence within the crime scene (which may be physical, logical, or virtual).

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Module 1):**
> *Evidence Identification* is the process of surveying the digital crime scene to identify devices, storage media, network components, and data sources that may contain information relevant to the incident.

### Sources to Identify
- **Endpoint Devices**: Laptops, desktops, mobile phones, IoT devices.
- **Storage Media**: HDDs, SSDs, USB drives, SD cards, optical media.
- **Network Infrastructure**: Routers, switches, firewalls, IDS/IPS logs.
- **Cloud & Virtual**: SaaS accounts, IaaS instances, container logs.
- **Volatile Sources**: RAM, CPU registers, running processes, network sockets.

---

## 1.3 Evidence Acquisition: The "What & How" of Seizure

**Acquisition** is the act of *creating a forensically sound copy* of identified evidence. The investigator must choose the **best method** based on multiple factors.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine you are a detective at a crime scene. You cannot take the entire house to the lab. You photograph the rooms, dust for prints, swab surfaces. In *digital forensics*, you do the equivalent — but you must make a **bit-stream image** of the storage and capture volatile memory while it still "lives." The wrong choice (e.g., copying only visible files) is like taking a Polaroid of a smudge instead of fingerprinting it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensically Sound Copy:** A copy that does not alter the original evidence, is bit-for-bit identical, and is verifiable using cryptographic hashes (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256).

---

## 1.4 Acquisition Methods — A Bird's-Eye View

| Method | Brief Description | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Static (Dead) Acquisition** | Disk imaged while system is **powered off** | Most reliable, ideal for non-volatile storage |
| **Live Acquisition** | Acquired while system is **running** | Needed for volatile data (RAM, network state) |
