| **Disk-to-Disk (Clone)** | Bit-stream copy onto another physical disk | Large drives, lab environment |
| **Disk-to-Image (File)** | Bit-stream copy into a single forensic file (.E01, .dd, .AFF) | Standard practice, supports compression |
| **Logical Acquisition** | Copies only files/folders visible to the OS | Mobile devices, when bit-stream not possible |
| **Sparse Acquisition** | Captures only allocated + unallocated clusters | Time-constrained cases |
| **Remote/Network Acquisition** | Acquisition over a network connection | Enterprise incidents, geographically spread assets |

---

## 1.5 Determining the Best Acquisition Method — Key Decision Factors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Decision Matrix Inputs:**
> 1. **Volatility of Data** (RAM vs HDD)
> 2. **Power State** of the device (On/Off/Sleep)
> 3. **Type of Storage Media** (HDD, SSD, encrypted, RAID, virtual)
> 4. **Suspected Offense** (Child exploitation, malware, insider threat)
> 5. **Legal Jurisdiction** (warrant scope, cross-border rules)
> 6. **Time & Resource Constraints**
> 7. **Integrity & Admissibility Requirements**

### Intuition
Choosing an acquisition method is like choosing a **medical imaging technique**: an X-ray for bones, an MRI for soft tissue, a CT scan for a comprehensive view. You don't do an MRI to find a fracture, and you don't do a static disk image to capture RAM. The pathology (case type) dictates the modality (acquisition method).

---

## 1.6 Digital Forensics Standards & Guidelines

> [!NOTE]
> **Major Standards (Board-Exam Essential):**
> - **ISO/IEC 27037:2012** — *Guidelines for identification, collection, acquisition, and preservation of digital evidence.*
> - **ISO/IEC 27042:2015** — *Guidelines for the analysis and interpretation of digital evidence.*
> - **NIST SP 800-86** — *Guide to Integrating Forensic Techniques into Incident Response.*
> - **RFC 3227** — *Guidelines for Evidence Collection and Archiving* (defines Order of Volatility).
> - **ACPO (UK) Principles** — 4 cardinal principles for digital evidence handling.
> - **IOCE** — *International Organization on Digital Evidence* (16 principles).
> - **SWGDE & SWGIT** — Scientific working groups (US).

---

## 1.7 Incident Response Stages

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NIST SP 800-61 (Revision 2) — Four-Stage Model:**
> 1. **Preparation**
> 2. **Detection & Analysis**
> 3. **Containment, Eradication & Recovery**
> 4. **Post-Incident Activity**

### Analogy
Incident response is like a **fire brigade**:
- *Preparation* = drills, equipment checks, training.
- *Detection & Analysis* = fire alarm rings; assess severity.
- *Containment, Eradication & Recovery* = stop the fire, remove the fuel, rebuild.
- *Post-Incident Activity* = fire report, lessons learned, code revisions.

---

## 1.8 Forensic Protocol for Evidence Acquisition — High-Level Steps

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Protocol (ISO/IEC 27037 aligned):**
> 1. **Document the scene** (photograph, sketch, timestamp).
> 2. **Identify potential evidence sources.**
> 3. **Acquire evidence** using a forensically sound method.
> 4. **Preserve integrity** via cryptographic hashing.
> 5. **Maintain chain of custody.**
> 6. **Package, label, and transport** securely.
> 7. **Store in a controlled environment.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Order of Volatility (RFC 3227) — The "Volatility Pyramid"

The order of volatility dictates **what should be captured first** before it disappears.

| Layer | Data Source | Typical Lifespan |
|---|---|---|
| L1 | CPU Registers, Cache | Nanoseconds |
| L2 | Routing Table, ARP Cache, Process Table | Seconds |
| L3 | Kernel Statistics, Memory (RAM) | Minutes |
| L4 | Temporary File Systems | Hours |
| L5 | Disk Files | Days–Years |
| L6 | Remote Logging & Monitoring Data | Years |
| L7 | Physical Configuration, Network Topology | Permanent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acquisition Rule of Thumb:** Capture the most volatile data **first**, working down the pyramid until time/resources are exhausted.

---

## 2.2 ACPO (Association of Chief Police Officers) — 4 Cardinal Principles

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory Memorization (4-Mark Board Favorite):**
> 1. No action taken by law enforcement agencies or their agents should change data held on a computer or storage media which may subsequently be relied upon in court.
> 2. In exceptional circumstances, where a person finds it necessary to access original data held on a computer or storage media, that person must be competent to do so and be able to give evidence explaining the relevance and the implications of their actions.
> 3. An audit trail or other record of all processes applied to digital evidence should be created and preserved. An independent third party should be able to examine those processes and achieve the same result.
> 4. The officer in charge of the investigation is responsible for ensuring that these principles are followed and for ensuring that the law and these principles are adhered to.

---

## 2.3 ISO/IEC 27037 — The Foundational Standard

ISO/IEC 27037 is **the** international standard for **digital evidence identification, collection, acquisition, and preservation**. It defines:

- **Digital Evidence First Responder (DEFR)** — the on-scene personnel.
- **Digital Evidence Specialist (DES)** — the trained forensic expert.
- **Digital Evidence Recovered Object (DERO)** — the logical entity containing potential evidence.

### The 4 Sub-Processes of ISO/IEC 27037
1. **Identification** — recognize and document potential evidence.
2. **Collection** — label, package, and physically/logically collect.
3. **Acquisition** — create a forensically sound copy.
4. **Preservation** — protect integrity and chain of custody.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Standard Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification Formulas (Cryptographic Hashing):**

$$
H_{\text{MD5}} = \text{MD5}(\text{image file}) \rightarrow 128\text{-bit hash}
$$

$$
H_{\text{SHA1}} = \text{SHA1}(\text{image file}) \rightarrow 160\text{-bit hash}
$$

$$
H_{\text{SHA256}} = \text{SHA256}(\text{image file}) \rightarrow 256\text{-bit hash}
$$

$$
\text{Integrity Condition: } H(\text{original}) = H(\text{copy}) = H(\text{working copy})
$$

| Concept | Symbol / Formula | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Order of Volatility | $\text{L1} \rightarrow \text{L7}$ | Determines capture priority |
| Bit-Stream Image | $I = \{0,1\}^n$ where $n = \text{sector count} \times 512$ | Complete copy of every bit |
| Hash Verification | $H(I) \equiv H(I')$ | Proves image integrity |
| Slack Space Size | $S = C - (F \bmod C)$ where $C$ = cluster size, $F$ = file size | Holds residual evidence |
| Slack Time Bound | $T_{\text{slack}} \leq \Delta t_{\text{case}}$ | Ensures evidence is still valid |
| Chain of Custody Transfer | $C_n = \text{sign}(\text{prev hash} + \text{operator} + \text{time})$ | Tamper-evident log |

> [!NOTE]
> For all exam problems, replace $\vert$ with $\mid$ or $\vert$ notation in any LaTeX expression to preserve markdown integrity.

---

## 2.5 Incident Response — NIST SP 800-61 Deep Dive

### Stage 1: Preparation
- Establish policies, procedures, and communication plans.
- Deploy host/network monitoring tools.
- Conduct training and tabletop exercises.
- Pre-stage forensic kits (write-blockers, imaging tools, blank drives).

### Stage 2: Detection & Analysis
- Monitor for indicators (alerts from SIEM, IDS, anti-virus).
- **Incident Triage**: profile the attacker's TTPs (Tactics, Techniques, Procedures).
- Validate the alert (true positive vs false positive).
- Document initial scope.

### Stage 3: Containment, Eradication & Recovery
- **Short-term containment**: isolate affected host (network disconnect).
- **Long-term containment**: patching, temporary firewall rules.
- **Eradication**: remove malware, attacker accounts, backdoors.
- **Recovery**: restore from clean backups, validate system integrity.

### Stage 4: Post-Incident Activity
- **Lessons learned meeting** (within 2 weeks of incident closure).
- Generate final forensic report.
- Update IR plan, signatures, playbooks.
- Evidence preservation or legal transfer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NIST SP 800-61 Rev. 2 explicitly recommends a parallel forensic track** running alongside containment. Evidence is collected at **every** stage without delaying containment.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise SOCs** use these standards for **legal admissibility**.
- **Law enforcement** relies on ACPO/ISO for **court-accepted evidence**.
- **Cloud providers** (AWS, Azure) implement ISO 27037-aligned APIs for **customer-driven forensic export**.
- **Insider-threat cases** hinge on **live RAM acquisition** to recover encryption keys.

---

## 2.7 Determining the Best Acquisition Method — Decision Logic

The selection is governed by a **weighted decision function**:

$$
M_{\text{select}} = \arg\max_{m \in \mathcal{M}} \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i \cdot f_i(m)
$$

where $\mathcal{M}$ is the set of candidate methods, $w_i$ are weights for criteria (volatility, time, legal scope, media type), and $f_i(m)$ is the suitability score.

**Practical Heuristics (must-know for KTU):**
- **Powered-off machine + HDD** → **Static disk-to-image** is best.
- **Running server with suspected RAM-resident malware** → **Live acquisition** mandatory.
- **Mobile device, locked** → **Logical acquisition** or **chip-off/jtag** (specialist).
- **Encrypted disk** → **Live acquisition** to capture keys *before* imaging.
- **RAID/Storage array** → **Logical over iSCSI/NFS** or **remote acquisition**.
- **Cloud workload** → **API-based snapshot + memory dump** of instance.

# STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS, PROTOCOLS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 End-to-End Forensic Protocol for Evidence Acquisition (ISO 27037-Aligned)

Below is the **exhaustive**, step-by-step protocol expected in a 14-mark KTU answer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Procedure ID: PCCCT602-M1-P01**

### Step 1 — Scene Documentation
- Photograph the scene from multiple angles.
- Record: device make/model, serial numbers, cable connections, screen state.
- Generate a **Scene Sketch** and a **Digital Evidence Log (DEL)**.
- Record timestamps (local + UTC) and operator name.

### Step 2 — Evidence Source Identification
Survey and list:
- Computing devices (desktop, laptop, server, mobile).
- Removable media (USB, optical, magnetic tape).
- Networked devices (NAS, SAN, cloud endpoints).
- Volatile sources (RAM, cache, network connections).

> Each source is **tagged** with a unique Evidence ID (EVID-001, EVID-002, …).

### Step 3 — Volatility Assessment
Apply the **Order of Volatility** (RFC 3227). For each source, decide:
- Will the evidence survive power-off? → Defer acquisition.
- Will the evidence disappear in minutes? → Acquire **immediately**.

### Step 4 — Tool Selection & Sterilization
- Select forensically validated tools (e.g., FTK Imager, EnCase, dd, Guymager).
- Verify tool hashes against vendor-published values.
- Wipe destination media; verify zero-fill.
- Sanitize write-blockers (USB/IDE/SATA).

### Step 5 — Live Acquisition (If Required)
$$
I_{\text{live}} = \text{capture}(\text{RAM}) \cup \text{capture}(\text{process list}) \cup \text{capture}(\text{network state})
$$
- Use tools: `WinPmem`, `FTK Imager Lite`, `LiME` (Linux).
- Hash the RAM image immediately:
$$
H_{\text{RAM}} = \text{SHA256}(I_{\text{live}})
$$
- Store in a **read-only evidence container** with metadata.

### Step 6 — Static Acquisition
- Power down the system (if policy permits).
- Connect source disk to a **write-blocker**.
- Image the entire disk (or specific partitions) using `dd`, `dcfldd`, or `ewf` (EnCase format).
- Compute the hash:
$$
H_{\text{disk}} = \text{SHA256}(I_{\text{static}})
$$
- Generate an MD5 cross-verification:
$$
H_{\text{disk}}^{\text{MD5}} = \text{MD5}(I_{\text{static}})
$$

### Step 7 — Integrity Verification
- Compare hash of source (if obtainable) vs hash of image.
- For source-hash, use **non-invasive read** (write-blocked).
- Verify:
$$
H(\text{source}) \overset{?}{=} H(\text{image})
$$

### Step 8 — Chain of Custody Initiation
- Generate a **Chain of Custody Form** with:
  - Evidence ID
  - Date/time of each transfer
  - Source → Destination
  - Operator signature + witness signature
  - Purpose of transfer
- A cryptographic notarization can be added:
$$
C_0 = \text{SHA256}(E_{\text{ID}} \,\vert\vert\, T_0 \,\vert\vert\, O_0)
$$
where $E_{\text{ID}}$ is the evidence identifier, $T_0$ is the timestamp, $O_0$ is the originating operator.

### Step 9 — Packaging & Labeling
- Use **anti-static bags** for media.
- Use **tamper-evident seals** on bags and ports.
- Label with: Case ID, Evidence ID, Hash, Date, Custodian.
- Maintain temperature/humidity if magnetic/optical media.

### Step 10 — Secure Storage & Transfer
- Store in a **locked safe** with access logging.
- During transport, use a **tamper-evident courier bag**.
- Maintain a **continuity log** at every checkpoint.

### Step 11 — Documentation Handover
- Create a **Forensic Acquisition Report** with:
  - Tools used (with version + hash).
  - Acquisition start/end time.
  - Hash values (MD5, SHA1, SHA256).
  - Anomalies encountered.
  - Operator and witness signatures.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Acquisition Method vs Scenario

| Scenario | Best Method | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Desktop PC, powered off, HDD | **Static disk-to-image** | Highest integrity, low complexity |
| Running server, suspected rootkit | **Live RAM + static disk** | Rootkit hides in memory; static alone insufficient |
| Encrypted laptop (BitLocker) | **Live acquisition first** | Capture keys in memory; static image un-readable without keys |
| Smartphone, locked | **Logical via Cellebrite / chip-off** | Logical preferred; chip-off destructive but bypasses locks |
| Cloud VM, suspected compromise | **API-based snapshot + memory dump** | Vendor tools are forensically sound and court-tested |
| NAS with RAID-5 | **Logical acquisition over network** | RAID reconstruction is complex; logical captures user data |
| USB drive, small capacity | **Static disk-to-image** | Quick, simple, full bit-stream |

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation — Hash-Based Integrity Proof

We now derive why **cryptographic hashing** ensures forensic integrity.

**Goal:** Show that a *single-bit change* in the image produces a *completely different* hash (avalanche effect).

**Setup:** Original image $I$, tampered image $I' = I \oplus e_k$ where $e_k$ is a unit vector flipping bit $k$.

**Step 1:** Original hash
$$
H(I) = h_0
$$

**Step 2:** Tampered hash
$$
H(I') = H(I \oplus e_k) = h_0'
$$

**Step 3:** Avalanche property of SHA-256
$$
\mathbb{P}(h_0 = h_0') \approx 2^{-256} \approx 8.6 \times 10^{-78}
$$

**Step 4:** Practical implication — if $H(I) \neq H(I')$, **tampering is detected with near-certainty**.

**Step 5:** Chain of custody notarization
$$
C_{n+1} = \text{SHA256}(C_n \,\vert\vert\, T_{n+1} \,\vert\vert\, O_{n+1} \,\vert\vert\, A_{n+1})
$$
where $A_{n+1}$ is the action performed (e.g., "transferred to lab freezer"). Any modification to $C_n$ breaks the chain at $C_{n+1}$.

---

## 3.4 Incident Response — Per-Stage Forensic Actions

| Stage | IR Action | Forensic Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Preparation | Build IR plan, train team | Stock forensic kits, validate tool hashes |
| 2. Detection & Analysis | Confirm incident, scope it | Acquire volatile data (RAM, network) |
| 3. Containment | Isolate host | Acquire disk image **before** wipe/reimage |
| 4. Eradication | Remove malware, patch | Forensic analysis of malware sample |
| 5. Recovery | Restore from clean backup | Verify integrity of restored system |
| 6. Post-Incident | Lessons learned | Final report, evidence archival, legal handoff |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic acquisitions MUST occur in the Detection & Containment stages.** Waiting until Recovery is too late — evidence may be lost or destroyed by reimaging.

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 1]**
**List and explain any three principles of the ACPO guidelines for handling digital evidence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Association of Chief Police Officers (ACPO)** defines four cardinal principles governing digital evidence:

1. **Principle 1 — No Alteration:** No action taken should change data on a computer or storage media which may be relied upon in court. Investigators must use write-blockers, hash verification, and validated tools to ensure the original is untouched.

2. **Principle 2 — Competence:** Where access to original data is unavoidable, the person must be competent to do so and able to give evidence explaining the relevance and implications of their actions. This ensures accountability in front of the court.

3. **Principle 3 — Audit Trail:** An audit trail of all processes applied to digital evidence must be created and preserved. An independent third party should be able to reproduce the same results — this is the **reproducibility** requirement.

*(Any 3 principles = 3 marks; 1 mark per principle with brief explanation.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**Define the "Order of Volatility" as per RFC 3227. Give two examples of highly volatile and two examples of non-volatile data sources.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Order of Volatility (RFC 3227):** A priority list prescribing which data should be collected first based on how long it will persist in its original form. The more fleeting the data, the higher the priority.

**Highly Volatile (collect first):**
- CPU registers and cache (nanoseconds).
- Routing tables, ARP cache, kernel statistics (seconds to minutes).

**Non-Volatile (collect last):**
- Files on a hard disk (years).
- Remote logs, backups, archived emails (permanent with proper retention).

*(Definition 1 mark + 2 volatile examples 1 mark + 2 non-volatile examples 1 mark = 3 marks.)*

> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B:**
> - Students often **omit the Standard citation** (e.g., writing "live acquisition" without mentioning *why* — cite RFC 3227 / NIST 800-86).
> - Students describe a method but **forget the hash verification step**, which is a 2-mark loss.
> - Students list IR stages but **miss the parallel forensic track** running through them (lose 2 marks).
> - Always close with a **chain-of-custody statement** in any acquisition answer.

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Series I]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the various factors that determine the **best acquisition method** in digital forensics, with at least five decision criteria and their justifications. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the **forensic protocol for evidence acquisition** as per ISO/IEC 27037, highlighting the role of the Digital Evidence First Responder (DEFR) and Digital Evidence Specialist (DES). (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Acquisition Method Selection (7 marks):**

The selection of the best acquisition method is governed by a **multi-criteria decision** involving technical, legal, and operational inputs.

1. **Volatility of Data (most critical):** Per **RFC 3227**, RAM and cache must be captured via **live acquisition** before the system is powered off. Conversely, data on a powered-off HDD is best captured by **static acquisition** for integrity.

2. **Power State of the Device:** A powered-off system enables the safest, most forensically sound **static disk-to-image** method using a hardware write-blocker. A running system demands live tools (FTK Imager Lite, WinPmem).

3. **Storage Media Type:** HDDs tolerate static imaging easily. **SSDs** suffer from wear-leveling and TRIM, so logical or live acquisition may be needed. **Encrypted disks** require live acquisition to capture the decryption key.

4. **Type of Suspected Offense:** For **malware incidents**, RAM + network state are critical. For **child exploitation**, disk files and deleted data are key. This dictates the data sources to capture.

5. **Legal Jurisdiction & Scope:** A warrant may restrict the investigator to specific files (logical) or authorize a full disk image (bit-stream). **Cross-border** cases may forbid certain methods.

6. **Time & Resource Constraints:** Bit-stream imaging of a 4 TB drive may take 8+ hours. Sparse acquisition is faster but less complete.

7. **Integrity & Admissibility Requirements:** Court cases demand **cryptographic hashing** (SHA-256); only methods supporting hash verification are admissible.

$$
M_{\text{select}} = \arg\max_{m \in \mathcal{M}} \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i \cdot f_i(m)
$$

where $M_{\text{select}}$ is the chosen method, $\mathcal{M}$ is the candidate set, and $f_i(m)$ is the suitability score for criterion $i$.

*(Citing standards + formula = full 7 marks.)*

---

**Part (b) — ISO/IEC 27037 Protocol with DEFR / DES Roles (7 marks):**

**ISO/IEC 27037** defines four sub-processes: **Identification, Collection, Acquisition, Preservation.** The personnel are split into two roles:

**Digital Evidence First Responder (DEFR):**
- The **first trained person** on the scene.
- Performs **Identification** and **Collection** (labeling, packaging, on-scene preservation).
- Cannot perform deep technical acquisition.

**Digital Evidence Specialist (DES):**
- A higher-skilled expert.
- Performs **Acquisition** (creating forensically sound copies) and **Preservation** (long-term integrity).
- Uses validated hardware/software (write-blockers, FTK, EnCase).

**Step-by-step Protocol:**

1. **Identification** — DEFR surveys the scene, lists devices, tags each with an Evidence ID.
2. **Collection** — DEFR labels, photographs, and packages items using anti-static bags and tamper-evident seals.
3. **Acquisition** — DES connects the source media to a write-blocker, performs **bit-stream imaging**:
   $$
   I = \text{dd}(\text{if}=\text{source}, \text{of}=\text{image.dd}, \text{bs}=512, \text{hash=sha256})
   $$
4. **Hashing & Verification**:
   $$
   H_{\text{SHA256}}(I) \overset{?}{=} H_{\text{SHA256}}(I_{\text{working}})
   $$
5. **Chain of Custody Initiation** — DES signs the CoC form; subsequent transfers are logged with cryptographic notarization:
   $$
   C_{n+1} = \text{SHA256}(C_n \,\vert\vert\, T_{n+1} \,\vert\vert\, O_{n+1} \,\vert\vert\, A_{n+1})
   $$
6. **Preservation** — Stored in a tamper-evident safe with controlled access; hash re-verified periodically.

*(Role distinction 2 marks + 4 sub-processes 3 marks + step-by-step protocol 2 marks = 7 marks.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **four stages of incident response** as per **NIST SP 800-61**, and describe the **forensic activities** that occur in each stage. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the major **digital forensics standards and guidelines** — ISO/IEC 27037, ISO/IEC 27042, NIST SP 800-86, RFC 3227, and ACPO — highlighting their scope and significance. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — NIST SP 800-61 Four-Stage IR Model with Forensic Activities (7 marks):**

**Stage 1 — Preparation:**
- Build IR policy, communication plan, escalation matrix.
- Pre-stage forensic kits (write-blockers, blank media, tool CDs).
- Validate forensic tool hashes; train staff.
- **Forensic activity:** Tool inventory, evidence locker setup, role assignment.

**Stage 2 — Detection and Analysis:**
- Monitor alerts from SIEM, IDS, EDR.
- Triage the alert: false positive vs. true positive.
- Determine attack vector, scope, and severity.
- **Forensic activity:** **Live acquisition** of volatile data (RAM, running processes, network connections) from suspect systems.

**Stage 3 — Containment, Eradication, and Recovery:**
- Short-term containment: network isolation.
- Long-term containment: patching, firewall rules.
- Eradication: malware removal, account revocation.
- Recovery: restore from clean backup; validate.
- **Forensic activity:** **Static disk-to-image acquisition** of the affected system **before reimaging**; preserve malware samples for analysis.

**Stage 4 — Post-Incident Activity:**
- Conduct a **lessons-learned** meeting within 2 weeks.
- Generate the final **forensic report** with timeline, evidence list, findings, and recommendations.
- Update the IR plan, signatures, and playbooks.
- Archive evidence or transfer to legal.
- **Forensic activity:** Evidence archival, expert testimony preparation, chain-of-custody closure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Exam Point:** Forensic activity runs **in parallel** with the IR stages — it is not a separate, post-incident phase. Evidence is collected at every stage to ensure nothing is lost during containment/eradication.

*(4 stages × 1.25 marks each + parallel forensic track 1 mark + standards citation 0.75 mark = 7 marks.)*

---

**Part (b) — Digital Forensics Standards & Guidelines (7 marks):**

| Standard | Full Name | Scope | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ISO/IEC 27037** | Guidelines for identification, collection, acquisition, preservation of digital evidence | Pre-analysis phase | Defines DEFR/DES roles, DERO concept, the foundational international standard |
| **ISO/IEC 27042** | Guidelines for analysis and interpretation of digital evidence | Post-acquisition analysis | Provides methodology for examination, ensuring reproducibility and admissibility |
| **NIST SP 800-86** | Guide to Integrating Forensic Techniques into Incident Response | IR-forensic integration | Bridges the gap between incident handling and forensic procedures |
| **RFC 3227** | Guidelines for Evidence Collection and Archiving | Volatile data priority | Defines the **Order of Volatility** — the volatility pyramid |
| **ACPO Principles** | Association of Chief Police Officers (UK) | 4 cardinal rules | The de-facto best-practice doctrine, legally referenced in UK courts |

**Key Insights:**
- ISO 27037 + 27042 = **complete forensic lifecycle** (acquisition + analysis).
- NIST 800-86 = **practical bridge** between IR and forensics.
- RFC 3227 = **technical priority** for volatile data.
- ACPO = **legal-philosophical** doctrine anchoring digital evidence in court.

*(5 standards × 1 mark each + 2 marks for synthesis/relationship explanation = 7 marks.)*

## 5.1 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must-Know for KTU Board Exam):**

- **Digital Evidence** = latent, fragile, volatile, admissible in court.
- **Identification** → **Collection** → **Acquisition** → **Preservation** (ISO 27037 cycle).
- **Order of Volatility (RFC 3227)**: CPU → Routing Table → RAM → Temp FS → Disk → Logs → Topology.
- **Best Acquisition Method** depends on: volatility, power state, media type, encryption, offense type, legal scope, time, integrity needs.
- **Static (Dead)** = powered off, gold standard for HDD; **Live** = mandatory for RAM/encrypted drives.
- **Hashing (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256)** is the integrity proof — avalanche property ensures tamper detection.
- **Write-blockers** prevent accidental modification during acquisition.
- **Chain of Custody** = signed, timestamped, tamper-evident log; cryptographic notarization strengthens it.
- **ACPO 4 Principles** = No alteration, Competence, Audit trail, Officer accountability.
- **ISO/IEC 27037** = DEFR + DES roles; **ISO/IEC 27042** = analysis methodology.
- **NIST SP 800-86** = forensic + IR integration; **NIST SP 800-61** = 4-stage IR model.
- **IR Stages** = Preparation → Detection & Analysis → Containment/Eradication/Recovery → Post-Incident.
- **Forensic track** runs **parallel** to IR stages, not after.
- **Logical acquisition** = visible files only; **Bit-stream** = every bit (sectors, slack, unallocated).
- **Common tools**: FTK Imager, EnCase, X-Ways, dd, dcfldd, Guymager, Cellebrite (mobile).
- **Common pitfalls**: skipping hash, missing CoC, imaging encrypted drive while powered off, using un-validated tools.
- **Exam Mantra**: Always cite the **standard** (RFC/ISO/NIST) for every method you mention.
- **Mobile forensics** uses logical or chip-off; **Cloud** uses vendor APIs (e.g., AWS Snapshot).
- **Post-incident activity** includes the **lessons-learned** meeting and **forensic report** — both are examinable.
- **Court admissibility** = forensically sound copy + unbroken chain of custody + competent witness.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Board Warning:** In any 14-mark answer, always structure as: *Definition (Standard) → Method → Steps → Hash Verification → Chain of Custody → Documentation*. Skipping any of these 6 elements costs 2+ marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Collection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT602 - CYBER FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Collection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Evidence Collection (Digital Forensics Context):** Collection is the first and most critical phase of the digital forensics investigation lifecycle. It refers to the systematic, legally-defensible, and forensically-sound process of **identifying, recognizing, preserving, acquiring, documenting, and packaging** digital evidence from electronic devices, networks, and storage media in a manner that maintains the **integrity**, **authenticity**, and **admissibility** of the evidence for judicial proceedings.

The KTU 2024 Scheme PCCCT602 syllabus defines Collection as the procedure encompassing:
1. **Data Acquisition** – the physical or logical capture of digital artifacts.
2. **Chain of Custody** – the unbroken, documented, and chronological trail of evidence possession.
3. **Order of Volatility** – the prioritization framework (RFC 3227) dictating which data sources must be captured first based on how rapidly their state changes.
4. **Integrity Verification** – the use of cryptographic hash functions to prove the acquired data is bit-for-bit identical to the source.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The collection phase is governed by the principle of **"minimization of alteration"** — an investigator must extract evidence without modifying the original data. This is often legally tested in court (e.g., Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act, 1872, as amended by the Information Technology Act, 2000).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **crime scene investigator (CSI)** arriving at a burglary. Before touching anything, the CSI:
1. **Photographs** the scene (analogous to *bit-stream imaging* of a hard disk).
2. **Dons gloves** to avoid leaving fingerprints (analogous to using a *write-blocker* so the forensic tool doesn't alter the suspect's drive).
3. **Bags and tags** every piece of evidence with a unique label and a signed slip (analogous to *evidence labeling and chain-of-custody documentation*).
4. **Logs** the time, location, and the person who handled each item at every step (analogous to a *chain of custody form*).

In **Cyber Forensics**, the "crime scene" is a hard drive, a smartphone, a cloud account, or a network packet stream. The investigator cannot literally "freeze" electrons, but can:
- **Snapshot volatile memory** (RAM) before it vanishes on shutdown.
- **Bit-stream copy** the entire disk (including deleted files, slack space, and metadata).
- **Hash** the original and the copy to mathematically prove they are identical.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** Think of collection as the "**P.A.I.D.**" process — **P**reserve, **A**cquire, **I**dentify, **D**ocument. If you remember P.A.I.D., you will never miss a step in the KTU viva.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value / Specification |
|---|---|
| **Minimum MD5 Hash Length** | **128 bits (16 bytes)** |
| **SHA-1 Hash Length** | **160 bits (20 bytes)** |
| **SHA-256 Hash Length** | **256 bits (32 bytes)** |
| **Typical forensic image file format** | **E01 (EnCase), DD (raw), AFF (Advanced Forensic Format)** |
| **Court-recognized integrity standard (India)** | **Section 65B, Indian Evidence Act** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Forensic Hash Algorithms (NIST approved):** **MD5**, **SHA-1**, and **SHA-256**. For modern KTU-aligned investigations, **SHA-256** is preferred due to collision resistance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 5 Sub-Phases of Evidence Collection

The collection process is **not a single action** but a sequence of carefully orchestrated sub-phases. KTU examiners frequently test each sub-phase independently.

### Phase A — Identification & Preparation
- Determine the **scope** of the investigation (which devices, accounts, time window).
- Obtain **legal authorization** (search warrant, court order, written consent).
- Prepare the **forensic workstation** with sterile media, write-blockers, hashing tools, and documentation forms.
- **Why this matters:** Failure to secure proper authorization renders the entire collection **inadmissible** in court.

### Phase B — Securing & Preserving the Scene
- **Isolate** the device from the network (pull the Ethernet cable; disable Wi-Fi) to prevent remote wiping.
- **Photograph** the physical state: screen contents, connected cables, indicator LEDs.
- **Do NOT power off** running systems prematurely — volatile memory (RAM) must be captured first.
- **Why this matters:** Encrypted drives (e.g., BitLocker, FileVault) may become permanently inaccessible if powered off without first capturing the live decryption keys from RAM.

### Phase C — Acquisition (Data Capture)
Two principal acquisition strategies exist:

| Strategy | Description | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Live Acquisition** | Captures volatile data (RAM, network state, running processes) from a powered-on system | Servers, encrypted devices, cloud sessions |
| **Dead / Static Acquisition** | Captures non-volatile data (disks, USB) from a powered-off system using a write-blocker | Standard desktops, laptops, mobile devices in airplane mode |

> [!NOTE]
> **Volatile Data Order (Most → Least Volatile):**
> 1. **CPU Registers & Cache** (nanoseconds)
> 2. **Routing Table, ARP Cache, Process Table, Kernel Memory** (seconds)
> 3. **RAM (Raw Memory Dump)** (seconds to minutes)
> 4. **Temporary File Systems (/tmp, swap)** (minutes to hours)
> 5. **Disk File System** (persistent)
> 6. **Remote Logging / Syslog Servers** (persistent but remote)
> 7. **Physical Configuration & Network Topology** (archival)
> 8. **Archival Media (Tapes, Offsite Backups)** (most stable)

### Phase D — Integrity Verification (Hashing)
- Compute the cryptographic hash of the **source media**.
- Compute the cryptographic hash of the **forensic image**.
- Compare — they **must match bit-for-bit**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A one-bit difference invalidates the entire evidence chain.** The hash is the mathematical "fingerprint" of the data.

### Phase E — Documentation & Chain of Custody
Every transfer, every access, every storage event **must be recorded** in the Chain of Custody (CoC) form.

---

## 2.2 Chain of Custody (CoC) — The Legal Backbone

The CoC is a **chronological written record** proving that the evidence was continuously controlled from the moment of collection until its presentation in court.

**Mandatory CoC Fields (KTU-mandated):**

| # | Field | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Case Number / Unique Identifier** | Ties evidence to a specific investigation |
| 2 | **Investigator's Full Name & Agency** | Accountability |
| 3 | **Date & Time of Collection (with timezone)** | Temporal precision |
| 4 | **Location of Collection (GPS + address)** | Jurisdictional clarity |
| 5 | **Description of Evidence (make, model, S/N, capacity)** | Unambiguous identification |
| 6 | **Reason / Authority for Collection** | Legal basis (warrant, consent) |
| 7 | **Method of Acquisition (live / dead / logical)** | Reproducibility |
| 8 | **Hash Value (MD5 / SHA-256)** | Integrity proof |
| 9 | **Signature of Collecting Officer** | Authentication |
| 10 | **Transfer Log (every subsequent handler)** | Continuous accountability |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students describe the CoC as "a form that is filled once." This is **incorrect**. The CoC is a **living document** — it is appended every single time the evidence changes hands.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` (NOT `|`) for absolute value in tables below to comply with the markdown isolation rule.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Hash comparison (pass condition)** | $H_{\text{source}} = H_{\text{image}}$ | Both must match **exactly** |
| 2 | **MD5 output size** | $L_{\text{MD5}} = 128 \text{ bits} = 16 \text{ bytes}$ | Hex digest length = **32 chars** |
| 3 | **SHA-1 output size** | $L_{\text{SHA-1}} = 160 \text{ bits} = 20 \text{ bytes}$ | Hex digest length = **40 chars** |
| 4 | **SHA-256 output size** | $L_{\text{SHA-256}} = 256 \text{ bits} = 32 \text{ bytes}$ | Hex digest length = **64 chars** |
| 5 | **Bit-stream image size** | $S_{\text{image}} = N_{\text{sectors}} \times 512 \text{ bytes}$ | bytes (sector size standard = **512 B**) |
| 6 | **Bit-stream image size (Advanced Format)** | $S_{\text{image}} = N_{\text{sectors}} \times 4096 \text{ bytes}$ | bytes (4Kn drives, **4 KiB** sector) |
| 7 | **Write-blocker condition (mathematical)** | $\forall \, w \in W_{\text{write}} : \text{blocked}(w) = \text{TRUE}$ | All write operations from host to source are rejected |
| 8 | **Time-of-check-to-time-of-use (TOCTOU) safeguard** | $\vert T_{\text{hash}} - T_{\text{acquire}} \vert \leq \Delta t_{\text{tolerance}}$ | Time gap must be logged |
| 9 | **Forensic duplication ratio** | $R_{\text{acq}} = \dfrac{S_{\text{image}}}{S_{\text{source}}}$ | Ideal value = **1.000** (lossless) |
| 10 | **Slack space (per cluster)** | $S_{\text{slack}} = C_{\text{cluster}} - (L_{\text{file}} \bmod C_{\text{cluster}})$ | bytes, with $C_{\text{cluster}}$ = cluster size |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Law Enforcement (CBI, NIA, Interpol):** Cybercrime prosecution (e.g., phishing, ransomware, terrorism financing).
- **Corporate Incident Response (IR):** Insider threat investigation, data exfiltration, IP theft — typically governed by ISO/IEC 27037.
- **e-Discovery (Litigation):** Identifying, collecting, and producing electronically stored information (ESI) for civil suits.
- **Military & Intelligence (CERT-In, NCIIPC):** Nation-state APT (Advanced Persistent Threat) attribution.
- **Banking & Financial Forensics:** RBI-mandated cyber fraud investigation (KYC, card cloning, UPI fraud).
- **Healthcare (HIPAA-equivalent, DISHA Bill India):** Patient data breach investigation.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Viva One-Liner:** "Collection is the gateway phase of forensics — if evidence is collected poorly, even the most sophisticated analysis is rendered inadmissible."

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Bit-Stream Image Size Calculation

**Problem:** A forensic investigator needs to create a bit-stream image of a 1 TB hard disk. The disk uses the legacy 512-byte sector format. Compute the forensic image size in bytes, MiB, and GiB.

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** Source drive size $S_{\text{source}} = 1 \text{ TB}$, sector size $B_{\text{sector}} = 512 \text{ bytes}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the number of sectors.**

A 1 TB drive (using the decimal TB convention used by drive manufacturers) contains:

$$S_{\text{source}} = 1 \text{ TB} = 1 \times 10^{12} \text{ bytes}$$

The number of 512-byte sectors is:

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{sectors}} &= \frac{S_{\text{source}}}{B_{\text{sector}}} \\
&= \frac{1 \times 10^{12} \text{ bytes}}{512 \text{ bytes/sector}} \\
&= 1{,}953{,}125{,}000 \text{ sectors}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the image size in bytes.**

A bit-stream image is a sector-by-sector copy, so:

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{image}} &= N_{\text{sectors}} \times B_{\text{sector}} \\
&= 1{,}953{,}125{,}000 \times 512 \\
&= 1 \times 10^{12} \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to MiB (mebibytes, $1 \text{ MiB} = 2^{20}$ bytes).**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{image, MiB}} &= \frac{1 \times 10^{12}}{2^{20}} \\
&= \frac{1 \times 10^{12}}{1{,}048{,}576} \\
&\approx 953{,}674.32 \text{ MiB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to GiB (gibibytes, $1 \text{ GiB} = 2^{30}$ bytes).**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{image, GiB}} &= \frac{1 \times 10^{12}}{2^{30}} \\
&= \frac{1 \times 10^{12}}{1{,}073{,}741{,}824} \\
&\approx 931.32 \text{ GiB}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Verify the acquisition ratio.**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{acq}} &= \frac{S_{\text{image}}}{S_{\text{source}}} \\
&= \frac{1 \times 10^{12}}{1 \times 10^{12}} \\
&= 1.000
\end{aligned}$$

Since $R_{\text{acq}} = 1.000$, the acquisition is **lossless** — every byte, including slack space, deleted file remnants, and unallocated clusters, has been captured.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the image is smaller than 1 TiB:** The decimal "1 TB" ($10^{12}$ bytes) is approximately 0.909 TiB ($2^{40}$ bytes). This is the famous "Terabyte myth" — drive manufacturers use decimal SI prefixes, while operating systems report in binary IEC prefixes.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Hash Integrity Verification

**Problem:** An investigator creates a forensic image of a USB drive. The MD5 hash of the source is `5d41402abc4b2a76b9719d911017c592`, and the MD5 hash of the image is `5d41402abc4b2a76b9719d911017c592`. Determine whether the image is admissible, and compute the collision probability.

**Step 1 — Compare the hashes.**

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{source}} &= \texttt{5d41402abc4b2a76b9719d911017c592} \\
H_{\text{image}} &= \texttt{5d41402abc4b2a76b9719d911017c592}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the pass condition.**

$$H_{\text{source}} = H_{\text{image}} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{PASS}$$

The image is **bit-for-bit identical** to the source. The evidence is admissible from an integrity standpoint.

**Step 3 — Compute the theoretical collision probability for MD5 (Birthday Paradox approximation).**

The MD5 hash space is $2^{128}$. By the Birthday Paradox, the probability of an unintended collision becomes significant (≈ 50%) after approximately:

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{collision, 50\%}} &\approx 1.177 \times \sqrt{2^{128}} \\
&\approx 1.177 \times 2^{64} \\
&\approx 2.18 \times 10^{19} \text{ inputs}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** While the theoretical 50% collision point is astronomically large, **practical MD5 collisions have been demonstrated** (Wang et al., 2004). For KTU 2024 answers, always state that **SHA-256 is preferred** for new investigations.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Slack Space Recovery

**Problem:** A 5,000-byte file is stored on a FAT32 file system with a 4,096-byte cluster size. Compute the slack space (RAM slack + file slack) created.

**Step 1 — Identify cluster size and file size.**

$$C_{\text{cluster}} = 4{,}096 \text{ bytes}, \quad L_{\text{file}} = 5{,}000 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the number of clusters consumed.**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{clusters}} &= \left\lceil \frac{L_{\text{file}}}{C_{\text{cluster}}} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil \frac{5{,}000}{4{,}096} \right\rceil \\
&= \lceil 1.2207 \rceil \\
&= 2 \text{ clusters}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the total slack space.**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{slack, total}} &= (N_{\text{clusters}} \times C_{\text{cluster}}) - L_{\text{file}} \\
&= (2 \times 4{,}096) - 5{,}000 \\
&= 8{,}192 - 5{,}000 \\
&= 3{,}192 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Forensic Significance:** Slack space is a **goldmine** for investigators — it often contains fragments of previously deleted files, metadata, and even passwords. A bit-stream image captures slack space; a simple file copy does NOT.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Evidence Collection Lifecycle (Mermaid Flowchart)

The following diagram illustrates the complete evidence collection workflow as mandated by ISO/IEC 27037 and the KTU 2024 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Step 1: Legal Authorization] --> node2[Step 2: Prepare Forensic Workstation]
    node2 --> node3[Step 3: Isolate and Photograph Scene]
    node3 --> node4{System Powered On?}
    node4 -- Yes --> node5[Step 4a: Capture Volatile Data RAM Dump Network State Processes]
    node4 -- No --> node6[Step 4b: Connect Write Blocker]
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7[Step 5: Bit Stream Imaging]
    node7 --> node8[Step 6: Compute Hash Source SHA-256]
    node7 --> node9[Step 6: Compute Hash Image SHA-256]
    node8 --> node10{Hashes Match?}
    node9 --> node10
    node10 -- Yes --> node11[Step 7: Label and Package Evidence]
    node10 -- No --> node12[STOP Acquisition Failed]
    node11 --> node13[Step 8: Initialize Chain of Custody Form]
    node13 --> node14[Step 9: Secure Storage Locked Cabinet]
    node14 --> node15[Step 10: Transport to Forensic Lab]
    node15 --> node16[Analysis Phase]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The diamond `node4` represents a decision branch — if the system is live, volatile data (RAM) must be captured **before** applying the write-blocker. Powering off a live system without first dumping RAM can destroy critical evidence (encryption keys, active network connections).

---

## 4.2 Order of Volatility Pyramid (RFC 3227)

The pyramid below visually represents the Order of Volatility — data sources at the top vanish in nanoseconds, while data sources at the bottom persist for years.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    peakA[Top - CPU Registers and Cache Nanoseconds] --> peakB[Routing Table ARP Cache Process Table Seconds]
    peakB --> peakC[RAM Raw Memory Dump Seconds to Minutes]
    peakC --> peakD[Temporary File Systems Minutes to Hours]
    peakD --> peakE[Disk File System Persistent]
    peakE --> peakF[Remote Logging and Syslog Servers Persistent but Remote]
    peakF --> peakG[Physical Configuration and Topology Archival]
    peakG --> peakH[Bottom - Archival Media Tapes Offsite Backups Most Stable]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic (Top → Bottom):** **"C-R-P-T-D-R-P-A"** → **C**PU, **R**outing, **P**rocess/RAM, **T**emp FS, **D**isk, **R**emote log, **P**hysical config, **A**rchival.

---

## 4.3 Chain of Custody Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

This block diagram maps the documentary flow of evidence from scene to courtroom.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockA[Investigator at Scene] --> blockB[Collect Evidence and Tag]
    blockB --> blockC[Sign Initial CoC Entry]
    blockC --> blockD[Sealed Bag with Tamper Proof Tape]
    blockD --> blockE[Transport Vehicle with GPS Log]
    blockE --> blockF[Forensic Lab Receiving Officer]
    blockF --> blockG[Sign Transfer Entry in CoC]
    blockG --> blockH[Evidence Vault Biometric Access]
    blockH --> blockI[Analyst Signs Out for Examination]
    blockI --> blockJ[Examination Performed in Lab]
    blockJ --> blockK[Analyst Signs In Upon Return]
    blockK --> blockL[Court Presentation with CoC Folder]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every rectangular block in the diagram above represents a mandatory signature event in the Chain of Custody.** A missing signature at **any** block breaks the chain and renders the evidence **inadmissible** under Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**Define the term "Order of Volatility" as per RFC 3227. List at least five data sources in the correct order, from most volatile to least volatile.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> The **Order of Volatility**, as defined in **IETF RFC 3227**, is a guideline that prescribes the sequence in which digital evidence should be collected based on **how rapidly the data's state changes**. Investigators must capture the most volatile data first because it may be lost within seconds.
>
> Order of Volatility (Most → Least Volatile):
> 1. **CPU Registers and Cache** (nanoseconds)
> 2. **Routing Table, ARP Cache, Process Table, Kernel Statistics** (seconds)
> 3. **RAM (Random Access Memory)** (seconds to minutes)
> 4. **Temporary File Systems (e.g., `/tmp`, swap)** (minutes to hours)
> 5. **Disk File Systems** (persistent)
> 6. **Remote Logging and Monitoring Servers** (persistent but remote)
> 7. **Physical Configuration and Network Topology** (archival)
> 8. **Archival Media (tapes, optical media, offsite backups)** (most stable)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**What is a Chain of Custody (CoC)? List any four mandatory fields that a CoC form must contain.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> The **Chain of Custody (CoC)** is a **chronologically maintained, legally admissible document** that records the **unbroken sequence of possession, transfer, analysis, and storage** of digital evidence from the moment of its collection at the crime scene to its final presentation in court.
>
> Four Mandatory CoC Fields:
> 1. **Case Number / Unique Evidence Identifier** — links the evidence to a specific investigation.
> 2. **Investigator's Full Name, Agency, and Signature** — establishes accountability.
> 3. **Date, Time, and Location of Collection (with timezone and GPS)** — establishes temporal and spatial precision.
> 4. **Cryptographic Hash Value (MD5 / SHA-256)** — provides mathematical proof of integrity.
>
> *(Other valid fields: Description of evidence, reason for collection, transfer log, signature of receiving officer.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the five sub-phases of evidence collection in digital forensics. Describe the role of a write-blocker in dead acquisition.**

> **Model Answer (7 Marks):**
> The five sub-phases of evidence collection are:
>
> **1. Identification & Preparation (1 Mark):** The investigator identifies the scope of the case, obtains legal authorization (search warrant, court order, or written consent), and prepares the forensic workstation with sterile media, validated hashing tools (e.g., FTK Imager, EnCase), and documentation forms.
>
> **2. Securing & Preserving the Scene (1 Mark):** The crime scene (computer, server, mobile device) is **isolated from the network** to prevent remote wiping. The physical state is **photographed** — screen contents, connected cables, and indicator LEDs are documented. The system is left **powered on** to preserve volatile data.
>
> **3. Acquisition (2 Marks):** Two strategies are used:
>    - **Live Acquisition** — captures RAM, network state, running processes, and encryption keys from a powered-on system.
>    - **Dead Acquisition** — captures non-volatile storage from a powered-off system using a **hardware or software write-blocker**.
>
> **4. Integrity Verification (1.5 Marks):** Cryptographic hashes (MD5, SHA-256) are computed for **both the source media and the forensic image**. The hashes must match **exactly**. This is the mathematical proof that the image is a true copy of the source.
>
> **5. Documentation & Chain of Custody (1.5 Marks):** Every transfer, access, and storage event is recorded in the **Chain of Custody form**, signed by every handler. The evidence is sealed in a tamper-evident bag and stored in a secured vault.
>
> **Role of a Write-Blocker (Included in 7-Mark allocation):**
> A **write-blocker** is a hardware or software device that **intercepts and rejects all write commands** issued by the host operating system to the source storage media. It allows **read-only access** to the suspect's drive, ensuring that the forensic examination process does **not modify, delete, or alter** any data on the source. This is essential for maintaining the **"minimization of alteration"** principle and for admissibility under Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Differentiate between Live Acquisition and Dead Acquisition. Under what circumstances should each be preferred?**

> **Model Answer (7 Marks):**
>
> | Parameter | Live Acquisition | Dead Acquisition |
> |---|---|---|
> | **System State** | Powered ON | Powered OFF |
> | **Volatile Data Captured** | Yes (RAM, network state, processes) | No (volatile data is lost) |
> | **Tool** | Memory forensic tools (e.g., FTK Imager Live, WinPmem, LiME) | Hardware write-blocker + imaging tool |
> | **Risk of Evidence Alteration** | Higher (system is running) | **Lower** (write-blocker ensures read-only) |
> | **Encryption Keys** | Capturable from RAM | Often lost (BitLocker, FileVault keys vanish) |
> | **Hibernation / Sleep Risk** | Avoids accidental data loss | Risk of unsaved data loss on shutdown |
> | **Time Required** | Longer (memory dump is slow) | Faster for disk imaging |
>
> **Preferred Circumstances (3 Marks):**
> - **Prefer Live Acquisition** when:
>   1. The drive is **encrypted** (BitLocker, FileVault, VeraCrypt) and the decryption keys are stored only in RAM.
>   2. The investigation requires **active network state** (open sockets, VPN sessions, mounted shares).
>   3. The device is a **server that cannot be shut down** without significant business disruption.
>   4. The system has **open encrypted chat sessions** (e.g., Signal, WhatsApp Web) that are accessible only while running.
>
> - **Prefer Dead Acquisition** when:
>   1. The device is a **standard desktop / laptop** with no active encryption concerns.
>   2. The investigator needs a **forensically pristine, court-defensible image** with minimum alteration risk.
>   3. The storage media is **physically failing** (live acquisition may trigger write retries that destroy data).
>   4. **Time and resource constraints** make live memory dump impractical.

---

### Question B `[14 Marks — Alternative Choice] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the concept of Chain of Custody in detail. Why is it considered the legal backbone of digital evidence?**

> **Model Answer (7 Marks):**
>
> **Definition (1 Mark):** The **Chain of Custody (CoC)** is a **chronologically maintained, legally admissible document** that records every instance of possession, transfer, analysis, and storage of digital evidence from the moment of its collection at the crime scene to its final presentation in court.
>
> **Essential Elements of CoC (3 Marks):**
> 1. **Case Identification** — unique case number and evidence tag number.
> 2. **Investigator Identity** — full name, rank, agency, signature.
> 3. **Date, Time, and Location** — precise timestamp (with timezone) and physical location (with GPS coordinates).
> 4. **Evidence Description** — make, model, serial number, capacity, condition.
> 5. **Authority / Reason** — search warrant number, court order, or written consent.
> 6. **Acquisition Method** — live or dead, tools used, hash values.
> 7. **Transfer Log** — every handler signs in and signs out, with date, time, and purpose.
> 8. **Storage Location** — vault ID, access control logs.
>
> **Why CoC is the Legal Backbone (3 Marks):**
> 1. **Admissibility:** Under **Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act (as amended by the IT Act, 2000)**, electronic records are admissible only if a proper certificate accompanies them, and that certificate is essentially a summary of the CoC.
> 2. **Authentication:** The CoC proves that the evidence presented in court is **the same evidence** that was collected at the scene — no substitution, no tampering.
> 3. **Integrity:** A continuous, unbroken chain demonstrates that the evidence was **never exposed to unauthorized modification**.
> 4. **Credibility:** Defense counsel routinely attacks the CoC. A complete CoC **defeats defense challenges** and reinforces the investigator's credibility.
> 5. **Reproducibility:** The CoC enables another forensic expert to **independently verify** the chain by tracing the documentation.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Describe the role of cryptographic hashing in evidence collection. Compute the MD5 and SHA-256 hash space sizes in bits, and explain why SHA-256 is preferred over MD5 in modern forensic practice.**

> **Model Answer (7 Marks):**
>
> **Role of Cryptographic Hashing (3 Marks):**
> A **cryptographic hash function** is a one-way mathematical function that maps data of arbitrary size to a fixed-size bit string (the "digest"). In evidence collection, hashing serves three purposes:
> 1. **Integrity Verification:** Hashing the source and the forensic image, and confirming that the digests match, mathematically proves the image is a **bit-for-bit identical** copy.
> 2. **Tamper Detection:** Any subsequent modification of the evidence (intentional or accidental) **changes the hash value**, immediately revealing tampering.
> 3. **Authentication:** The hash is recorded in the CoC form, providing a **permanent mathematical fingerprint** of the evidence at the time of collection.
>
> **Hash Space Computation (2 Marks):**
> - **MD5** produces a 128-bit digest. Hash space size = $2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ possible digests.
> - **SHA-256** produces a 256-bit digest. Hash space size = $2^{256} \approx 1.16 \times 10^{77}$ possible digests.
>
> **Why SHA-256 is Preferred (2 Marks):**
> 1. **Collision Resistance:** MD5 has **demonstrated collision vulnerabilities** (Wang et al., 2004 — two different inputs producing the same MD5 hash). SHA-256, as of the KTU 2024 curriculum, has no known practical collisions.
> 2. **Larger Hash Space:** SHA-256's $2^{256}$ space is astronomically larger than MD5's $2^{128}$, providing a far stronger guarantee against accidental collisions.
> 3. **NIST & Judicial Acceptance:** SHA-256 is the current **NIST-recommended** standard (FIPS 180-4) and is increasingly mandated by Indian courts under CERT-In guidelines.
> 4. **Future-Proofing:** MD5 is deprecated for security-sensitive applications; SHA-256 is the modern default.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in the 'Collection' Topic:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "Order of Volatility" with "Order of Collection":** They are not the same. Order of Volatility is a *prioritization* guideline — you don't *always* collect CPU registers first because you often **cannot** (the tool runs on the OS, not the CPU directly). Be precise.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the Write-Blocker:** Any answer about dead acquisition that **does not mention a write-blocker** will lose at least 1–2 marks. It is non-negotiable.
>
> 3. **Stating MD5 alone is sufficient:** Always mention **both** MD5 and SHA-256 in modern answers, and explain that **SHA-256 is preferred** for new investigations.
>
> 4. **Treating the CoC as a one-time form:** Examiners **will deduct marks** if you describe the CoC as a single form filled at the crime scene. It is a **living document** updated at every transfer.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the legal dimension:** Cyber forensics is **not just technical** — it is also **legal**. Always anchor your answer to **Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act, 1872 (as amended by the IT Act, 2000)** and the **IT Act, 2000 / 2008 amendments**.
>
> 6. **Unit Confusion:** Mixing up TB (decimal, $10^{12}$) with TiB (binary, $2^{40}$) is a common calculation error. State the convention explicitly in your answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This!)**
>
> - **Collection = the FIRST phase of digital forensics** — preceded only by legal authorization.
> - **P.A.I.D. = Preserve, Acquire, Identify, Document** — the four-action mental model.
> - **Order of Volatility (Top → Bottom): CPU → Routing/ARP → RAM → Temp FS → Disk → Remote Log → Physical Config → Archival Media.**
> - **Live Acquisition** = powered ON, captures RAM and volatile state.
> - **Dead Acquisition** = powered OFF, uses **write-blocker** (mandatory).
> - **Bit-Stream Image** = sector-by-sector copy; captures **slack space** and **deleted file remnants**; size = sectors × 512 bytes (or × 4096 for 4Kn drives).
> - **Hashing** = mathematical fingerprint; **MD5 (128-bit)**, **SHA-1 (160-bit)**, **SHA-256 (256-bit, preferred)**.
> - **Hash Pass Condition:** $H_{\text{source}} = H_{\text{image}}$ — must match **exactly**.
> - **Chain of Custody = living document**, signed at **every** transfer event.
> - **Mandatory CoC Fields:** Case #, Investigator ID, Date/Time/Location, Evidence Description, Authority, Method, Hash, Signature, Transfer Log.
> - **Legal Anchor:** Section 65B, Indian Evidence Act, 1872 (as amended by IT Act 2000).
> - **Standard:** ISO/IEC 27037 (Guidelines for identification, collection, acquisition, and preservation of digital evidence).
> - **Hashing Tools (Industry Standard):** FTK Imager, EnCase, X-Ways Forensics, Autopsy, The Sleuth Kit.
> - **Write-Blockers (Industry Standard):** Tableau (T8, T35), WiebeTech, CRU Acquisitions, ICS (Image Masster).
> - **1 TB (decimal) ≈ 0.909 TiB (binary)** — always state the convention used in calculations.
> - **Acquisition Ratio $R_{\text{acq}} = 1.000$** for a lossless, court-defensible image.
> - **Slack space calculation:** $S_{\text{slack}} = \lceil L_{\text{file}} / C_{\text{cluster}} \rceil \times C_{\text{cluster}} - L_{\text{file}}$.
> - **MD5 has known collisions (Wang, 2004) — never use MD5 alone for high-stakes cases.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT602 - CYBER FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Processing** in Cyber Forensics is the disciplined, methodologically rigorous phase of the digital investigation lifecycle that occurs **immediately after evidence identification and collection**. It encompasses the systematic preparation, authentication, duplication, and structuring of seized digital artifacts to render them suitable for forensic examination and analysis while maintaining strict **evidentiary integrity**, **admissibility**, and an unbroken **chain of custody**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework, Processing is treated as the critical bridge between the *raw seized evidence* (which is volatile, fragile, and legally sensitive) and the *analytical stages* (where investigators extract investigative intelligence). The forensic investigator does **NOT** perform analytical reasoning on the original evidence; instead, processing creates a **forensically sound working copy** upon which all subsequent operations are executed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Processing is one of the four canonical phases of the Digital Forensics Process Model (alongside **Identification, Collection, Examination, and Analysis**). Examiners frequently frame these as: *Identify → Collect → **Process** → Examine → Analyze → Report.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Crime Scene Photograph" Metaphor

Imagine a police detective arriving at a physical crime scene (a broken glass window with muddy footprints).

- **Identification** is spotting the scene.
- **Collection** is bagging the muddy shoe-print cast into an evidence bag and sealing it.
- **Processing** is what the forensic lab does next — they make a **3D plaster replica** of the footprint, photograph it under controlled lighting, weigh it, and assign it an **Exhibit ID** so that any court can trust the replica is a *true likeness* of the original.
- The detective then studies the **replica** to draw conclusions — never the original cast, because handling it would risk damage.

In digital forensics:
- The **original evidence** = a suspect's hard drive, mobile phone, USB stick, or memory dump.
- **Processing** = creating a **forensically sound bit-stream image**, computing its **cryptographic hash**, sealing the original in a faraday bag / evidence locker, and registering a working copy in the case file.

### 1.3 Key Metrics & Constants (KTU Board-Relevant)

| Metric | Value | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **NIST Recommended Hash (current)** | **SHA-256** | Default integrity algorithm for federal/forensic use |
| **Legacy Hash (deprecated)** | **MD5 (128-bit)** | Still seen, but cryptographically broken |
| **Standard Bit-stream image formats** | **E01 (EnCase), DD (raw), AFF (Advanced Forensic Format)** | Container formats carrying hash + metadata |
| **Minimum write-blocker certification** | **NIST CFTT (Computer Forensic Tool Testing)** | Validates hardware/software write-blockers |
| **Order of Volatility (RFC 3227)** | **CPU registers → RAM → Disk → Remote logs → Offsite backups** | Defines the order in which evidence should be captured |
| **Common sector size** | **512 bytes (legacy), 4096 bytes (Advanced Format / 4Kn)** | Granularity of disk imaging |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The term **"Forensically Sound"** in KTU valuation scripts means: *(a)* the original evidence is **unchanged bit-for-bit**, *(b)* the working copy is a **complete, verified duplicate**, and *(c)* every action is **documented, repeatable, and defensible in court**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Sub-Phases of Forensic Processing

The processing stage decomposes into the following sequential (and often iterative) sub-operations:

1. **Evidence Logging & Chain-of-Custody Initialization**
   - Each piece of evidence is assigned a unique **Evidence ID / Exhibit Number**.
   - The investigator records: *date/time, location, collector, device serial number, make/model, condition, hash value*.
   - Every subsequent transfer of the evidence is appended to the chain-of-custody document.

2. **Volatility Assessment & Live-System Triage (Pre-Processing)**
   - If the device is **powered on**, volatile evidence is captured first per the **Order of Volatility**:
     - CPU registers & cache → Kernel statistics → Routing table / ARP cache → Process list → Memory (RAM) → Temporary file systems → Disk → Remote logging data → Physical documentation.
   - The system is then **powered down in a forensically defensible manner** (typically by pulling the power cord, NOT via OS shutdown, to avoid log/file-system mutation).

3. **Bit-Stream Imaging (Disk-to-Image / Disk-to-Disk)**
   - A **forensic image** is a **sector-by-sector, bit-for-bit copy** of the source media — including **file slack, unallocated space, partition tables, and bad sectors**.
   - A **forensic duplicate** is a bit-for-bit copy onto an equally-sized destination medium.
   - Imaging is performed through a **write-blocker** (hardware or software) to ensure **no write operations** reach the source.

4. **Hash Verification & Authentication**
   - The cryptographic hash of both the source and the image is computed **before and after** every processing step.
   - If the two hashes match across all transitions, the image is **authenticated** as a faithful replica.

5. **Restoration, Indexing & Case-File Archival**
   - The working copy is mounted **read-only**, indexed (file names, headers, metadata, deleted entries), and registered in the forensic case management software (e.g., EnCase, FTK, Autopsy).
   - The original evidence is sealed, returned to secure storage, and the case file is locked for review.

### 2.2 Hashing — The Mathematical Heart of Processing

A **cryptographic hash function** $H$ maps an arbitrary-length input $M$ (the media contents) to a fixed-length output $h$ (the digest):

$$h = H(M)$$

Forensic-critical properties a hash function must satisfy:

- **Determinism:** $H(M)$ is identical for identical $M$.
- **Avalanche Effect:** A 1-bit change in $M$ produces a drastically different $h$.
- **Collision Resistance (practical):** Finding $M_1 \neq M_2$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$ must be computationally infeasible.
- **Pre-image Resistance:** Given $h$, recovering $M$ must be infeasible.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **high-yield formulas** examiners expect candidates to reproduce verbatim in the Cyber Forensics paper.

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h = H(M)$ | Cryptographic hash of message $M$ | Integrity verification |
| 2 | $M_{\text{image}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} S_i$ | Bit-stream image = sum of all sectors $S_i$ | Disk imaging |
| 3 | $N = \dfrac{C_{\text{drive}}}{S_{\text{sector}}}$ | Number of sectors $N$ = drive capacity / sector size | Capacity math |
| 4 | $C_{\text{image}} \geq C_{\text{source}} \cdot (1 + \varepsilon)$ | Image storage $\geq$ source size (with overhead $\varepsilon$) | Storage planning |
| 5 | $t_{\text{image}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{drive}}}{R_{\text{read}}}$ | Imaging time = capacity / read rate | Time estimation |
| 6 | $V_{\text{ratio}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{user}}}{V_{\text{total}}}$ | Volatility ratio (user-addressable / total addressable) | Memory forensics |
| 7 | $H_{\text{verify}} = H_{\text{original}} \stackrel{?}{=} H_{\text{copy}}$ | Hash equality check | Authentication |
| 8 | $P_{\text{collision}} \approx \dfrac{k^2}{2 \cdot 2^{n}}$ | Birthday-attack collision probability for $k$ samples, $n$-bit hash | MD5 deprecation math |

> **Units used:** $C$ = bytes, $S$ = bytes/sector, $N$ = dimensionless count, $R$ = bytes/second, $t$ = seconds, $V$ = bytes, $H$ = hex digest (bits/4), $n$ = hash output length in bits.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The processing phase is the **single most legally consequential step** in any forensic investigation. A flawed processing pipeline renders the entire downstream analysis **inadmissible** under the *Daubert Standard* (US) and the Indian *Section 65B Indian Evidence Act* (which mandates a *properly authenticated electronic record*). In industry, processing pipelines are deployed in:
- **Incident Response (IR) teams** for ransomware and APT investigations.
- **e-Discovery workflows** in litigation support.
- **Lawful Interception** for telecom and ISP-level forensics.
- **Cloud Forensics** (where "imaging" becomes a *snapshot + API-mediated export*).
- **IoT and Automotive Forensics** (ECU data extraction via OBD-II).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Sector Count of a Storage Device

**Given:** A hard disk drive with a manufacturer-rated capacity $C_{\text{drive}}$ and a sector size $S_{\text{sector}}$.

**To Find:** The total number of addressable sectors $N$.

**Step 1 — Establish the relationship.**
The total capacity is the sum of all sectors:

$$C_{\text{drive}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} S_i$$

**Step 2 — Assume uniform sector size** (the standard assumption for modern storage):

$$S_i = S_{\text{sector}} \quad \forall i \in \lbrace 1, 2, \dots, N \rbrace$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify.**

$$C_{\text{drive}} = N \cdot S_{\text{sector}}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $N$.**

$$N = \dfrac{C_{\text{drive}}}{S_{\text{sector}}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example (KTU-style).**
A 1 TB hard drive uses 4 KB (4096-byte) Advanced Format sectors. Convert capacity to bytes:

$$C_{\text{drive}} = 1 \text{ TB} = 1 \times 10^{12} \text{ bytes}$$

$$N = \dfrac{1 \times 10^{12}}{4096}$$

$$N = \dfrac{1 \times 10^{12}}{4.096 \times 10^{3}}$$

$$N = 2.44140625 \times 10^{8}$$

$$N = 244{,}140{,}625 \text{ sectors}$$

**Interpretation:** A 1 TB 4Kn drive contains approximately **244.14 million sectors**, each of which must be copied in order to produce a forensically complete image.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Imaging Time Estimation

**Given:** Drive capacity $C_{\text{drive}}$ and the sustained read rate $R_{\text{read}}$ of the imaging hardware (e.g., USB 3.0 write-blocker throughput).

**To Find:** The minimum imaging time $t_{\text{image}}$.

**Step 1 — Define imaging as a sequential read operation.**

$$t_{\text{image}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{drive}}}{R_{\text{read}}}$$

**Step 2 — Insert units check.**

$$t_{\text{image}} \;\; [\text{s}] = \dfrac{C_{\text{drive}} \;\; [\text{B}]}{R_{\text{read}} \;\; [\text{B/s}]}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Example.**
For the 1 TB drive above imaged at a sustained 100 MB/s (typical USB 3.0):

$$R_{\text{read}} = 100 \text{ MB/s} = 1.0 \times 10^{8} \text{ bytes/s}$$

$$t_{\text{image}} = \dfrac{1 \times 10^{12}}{1.0 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$t_{\text{image}} = 1.0 \times 10^{4} \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to hours.**

$$t_{\text{image}} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{3600} \approx 2.78 \text{ hours}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Real-world imaging time is **always longer** than this theoretical value due to bad-sector retries, hash computation overhead, and bus contention. Add a safety margin of **$\varepsilon \approx 20\%$** when planning case timelines.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Hash Collision Probability (Birthday Bound)

**Given:** $k$ random forensic files, each hashed with an $n$-bit hash (e.g., MD5 ⇒ $n = 128$).

**To Find:** The approximate probability that **at least two files share the same hash** (a *collision*).

**Step 1 — Apply the classical Birthday Paradox approximation.**

$$P_{\text{collision}} \approx 1 - e^{-k(k-1) / (2 \cdot 2^{n})}$$

**Step 2 — For small probabilities, the linear approximation holds.**

$$P_{\text{collision}} \approx \dfrac{k^{2}}{2 \cdot 2^{n}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Example (MD5, $n = 128$).**
Suppose $k = 10{,}000$ evidence files are hashed. Compute:

$$P_{\text{collision}} \approx \dfrac{(10^{4})^{2}}{2 \cdot 2^{128}}$$

$$P_{\text{collision}} \approx \dfrac{10^{8}}{2 \cdot 3.4028 \times 10^{38}}$$

$$P_{\text{collision}} \approx \dfrac{10^{8}}{6.8056 \times 10^{38}}$$

$$P_{\text{collision}} \approx 1.47 \times 10^{-31}$$

**Interpretation:** Even with 10,000 files, the MD5 collision probability is astronomically small **in theory**. However, MD5 is considered broken because **targeted collisions** can be engineered (the *chosen-prefix* and *identical-prefix* attacks), which is why KTU examiners expect you to recommend **SHA-256** for new investigations.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Processing Tools & Their Use-Cases

| Tool | Type | Image Format | Strength | KTU Exam-Relevant Point |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **EnCase** | Commercial (Opentext) | **E01 / Ex01** | Court-defensible, integrated case mgmt | Industry gold standard for law enforcement |
| **FTK (Forensic Toolkit)** | Commercial (Exterro) | **DD / E01** | Fast indexing, e-discovery focused | Strong email-parsing capabilities |
| **Autopsy / The Sleuth Kit** | Open Source | **DD / raw, AFF** | Free, GUI on top of TSK CLI | KTU labs often use this; cite it for academic projects |
| **dcfldd** | Open Source (DoD) | **DD** | Multi-destination imaging, on-the-fly hashing | Scriptable, ideal for batch processing |
| **Guymager** | Open Source | **DD / E01** | User-friendly, parallel hashing | Common in academic forensic labs |
| **X-Ways Forensics** | Commercial | **DD / E01** | Lightweight, fast, advanced carving | Mentioned in advanced KTU modules |
| **CAINE** | Linux Distro | Multiple | Bundled forensic environment | Often referenced in KTU viva questions |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Forensic Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Evidence Identification] --> B[Evidence Collection]
    B --> C[Chain of Custody Initiated]
    C --> D{Device Powered ON?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Volatile Data Capture - RAM, Network, Processes]
    D -- No --> F[Proceed to Imaging]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Apply Hardware Write Blocker]
    G --> H[Connect Source to Forensic Workstation]
    H --> I[Generate Bit Stream Image - Sector by Sector]
    I --> J[Compute Hash of Source Media]
    I --> K[Compute Hash of Generated Image]
    J --> L{Hash Match?}
    K --> L
    L -- Yes --> M[Image Authenticated - Forensically Sound]
    L -- No --> N[Discard Image - Repeat Process]
    M --> O[Mount Image as Read Only]
    O --> P[Index Files Metadata Slack Space]
    P --> Q[Archive Original Evidence in Secure Locker]
    P --> R[Generate Forensic Case File]
    R --> S[Hand Over to Examination and Analysis Phase]
    Q --> S

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#b45309,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#b45309,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#4d7c0f,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#4d7c0f,color:#ffffff
    style L fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    style M fill:#4d7c0f,color:#ffffff
    style N fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    style O fill:#6d28d9,color:#ffffff
    style P fill:#6d28d9,color:#ffffff
    style Q fill:#4d7c0f,color:#ffffff
    style R fill:#6d28d9,color:#ffffff
    style S fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
```

**Legend / Reading Guide:**
- **Blue** = Identification & Collection phases (predecessors).
- **Teal** = Core processing operations (imaging, write-blocking).
- **Amber** = Volatility decision branch.
- **Green** = Integrity verification & secure storage.
- **Red** = Failure / re-do branch.
- **Purple** = Indexing & case-file preparation for downstream analysis.

### 4.2 Schematic: Hash-Based Authentication Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Media M] -->|H function| B[Hash h1]
    A -->|Bit stream copy| C[Forensic Image M prime]
    C -->|H function| D[Hash h2]
    B --> E{Compare h1 vs h2}
    D --> E
    E -- Equal --> F[Verified Authentic]
    E -- Different --> G[Integrity Failure]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#4d7c0f,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#4d7c0f,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#b45309,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#4d7c0f,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Order-of-Volatility Reference (RFC 3227 Compliant)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU Registers Cache] --> B[Kernel Routing Tables]
    B --> C[Process Memory RAM]
    C --> D[Network State ARP Connections]
    D --> E[File System Tempfiles]
    E --> F[Disk Storage Local]
    F --> G[Remote Logs Servers]
    G --> H[Offsite Backups Cloud Archives]
    H --> I[Physical Notes Photographs]

    style A fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#b45309,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#b45309,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#4d7c0f,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#4d7c0f,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#6d28d9,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#6d28d9,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> The red→amber nodes (top of the chain) are the **most volatile** and must be captured **first**. KTU board questions frequently test whether you can list these in the correct sequence.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**Q1. Define forensic processing. List its main sub-phases.**
*(Cognitive Level: Remember / Understand | CO Mapping: Understand the forensic process model)*

**Model Answer:**
