# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**Differentiate between open-source and proprietary cyber forensic tools. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Open Source | Proprietary |
|--------|-------------|-------------|
| **Cost** | Free or low-cost | Expensive licensing fees |
| **Source Code** | Auditable by community | Closed / vendor-controlled |
| **Support** | Community forums | Dedicated vendor SLA |
| **Examples** | Autopsy, The Sleuth Kit, Volatility | EnCase, FTK, X-Ways Forensics |

Both categories are forensically valid provided they pass NIST CFTT validation. *(1 mark)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**What is a write blocker? Why is it essential in computer forensics?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **write blocker** is a hardware (or software) device that allows read-only access to a storage medium, preventing any modification of the original evidence during forensic acquisition *(1 mark)*.

It is essential because:
1. It **preserves the integrity** of the original evidence *(1 mark)*.
2. It maintains the **chain of custody** and ensures **forensic soundness** *(1 mark)*.

Without a write blocker, the host OS may write metadata (access timestamps, journal entries) to the suspect disk, contaminating the evidence and rendering it inadmissible in court.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently **lose 2–3 marks** by forgetting to mention **NIST CFTT validation** when describing a tool.
> - Listing tool names without **explaining their forensic purpose** (acquisition, analysis, validation) results in partial credit only.
> - For **open-source vs proprietary** questions, examiners expect a **concluding statement on forensic validity**, not just a side-by-side table.
> - When discussing **challenges**, do not just list them — categorize them as **technical, legal, or operational**.

---

### 🅰️ QUESTION A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern]**

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the various categories of **computer forensics software tools**. Discuss disk, memory, network, and mobile forensics software with relevant examples. *(7 marks)*

**Q. (b)** Describe **open-source forensic tools** available for cyber investigation. Highlight their advantages, limitations, and validation requirements. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Answer for Q. (a) — Software Tools (7 Marks)

Computer forensics software tools are classified based on the type of digital evidence they process:

**1. Disk and File-System Forensics Software (2 marks)**
- **The Sleuth Kit (TSK)** — open-source CLI suite for analyzing disk images, supporting NTFS, FAT, ext2/3/4, HFS+.
- **Autopsy** — GUI front-end for TSK; offers timeline analysis, keyword search, file carving.
- **EnCase Forensic** — proprietary industry standard; produces `.E01` images, indexed search.
- **FTK (Forensic Toolkit)** — proprietary; distributed processing, strong email-parsing module.

**2. Memory Forensics Software (2 marks)**
- **Volatility** — open-source RAM analysis framework; supports Windows, Linux, macOS profiles.
- **Rekall** — alternative open-source memory analysis.
- **Magnet RAM Capture** — lightweight utility for acquiring memory dumps.

**3. Network Forensics Software (1.5 marks)**
- **Wireshark** — packet capture and deep protocol inspection.
- **NetworkMiner** — passive host identification and file extraction from PCAP files.
- **tcpdump** — CLI packet capture utility.

**4. Mobile Forensics Software (1.5 marks)**
- **Cellebrite UFED** — logical, physical, file-system extraction of iOS and Android devices.
- **MSAB XRY** — cross-platform mobile forensic suite.
- **Autopsy Android Module** — open-source Android parsing (limited capability).

---

#### ✅ Model Answer for Q. (b) — Open-Source Forensic Tools (7 Marks)

**1. Definition and Examples (2 marks)**
Open-source forensic tools are utilities whose source code is publicly available, allowing community audit, modification, and redistribution. Examples include Autopsy, The Sleuth Kit, Volatility, Wireshark, NetworkMiner, Guymager, and FTK Imager (lite).

**2. Advantages (2.5 marks)**
- **Cost-effective** — no licensing fees, suitable for academic and small-agency use.
- **Transparency** — source code is auditable, eliminating "black box" concerns.
- **Community-driven development** — rapid patching and feature additions.
- **Customizability** — investigators can modify tools for specialized needs.
- **Educational value** — students can study forensic algorithms directly.

**3. Limitations (1.5 marks)**
- **Limited vendor support** — relies on community forums, no SLA.
- **Steeper learning curve** — many tools are CLI-based (e.g., TSK).
- **Inconsistent documentation** — varies by project.
- **Courtroom scrutiny** — defence counsel may question reliability due to lack of formal vendor backing.

**4. Validation Requirements (1 mark)**
Forensic validity requires **NIST CFTT (Computer Forensic Tool Testing)** certification or equivalent peer-reviewed benchmarking. Tools like Autopsy and TSK have been independently validated and accepted in Indian and international courts.

---

### 🅱️ QUESTION B — 14 Marks **[Alternative Choice — KTU Pattern]**

**Q. (a)** Discuss in detail the **hardware tools** used in computer forensics. Explain write blockers, forensic duplicators, mobile device kits, and Faraday bags with their forensic purpose. *(7 marks)*

**Q. (b)** Analyze the major **challenges in cyber forensics** in the modern era. Categorize them into technical, legal, and operational challenges with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Answer for Q. (a) — Hardware Tools (7 Marks)

**1. Write Blockers (2 marks)**
A **write blocker** is a hardware bridge that allows the forensic workstation to read a suspect storage device without transmitting any write commands. This preserves the original evidence bit-by-bit.

Types include:
- **SATA Write Blockers** (modern desktops/laptops)
- **USB Write Blockers** (external drives)
- **NVMe Write Blockers** (M.2 SSDs)
- **IDE Write Blockers** (legacy systems)
- **Thunderbolt Blockers** (Apple Mac systems)

**2. Forensic Duplicators / Imagers (2 marks)**
Stand-alone hardware units performing disk-to-disk cloning or disk-to-image acquisition.
- **Logicube Falcon-NEO** — up to 30 GB/min, multi-target imaging, on-the-fly hash verification.
- **Tableau TD2u** — dual-destination imaging.
- **CRU WiebeTech Ditto** — field-portable, battery-powered.

**3. Mobile Device Forensic Kits (1.5 marks)**
- **Cellebrite UFED Touch 2** — touch-screen mobile extraction; supports logical, physical, file-system extraction.
- **MSAB XRY Terminal** — kiosk-style high-throughput mobile acquisition.
- **Oxygen Forensic Detective Kit** — bundled cables for legacy and modern phones.

**4. Faraday Bags and Other Hardware (1.5 marks)**
- **Faraday Bags** — RF-shielded enclosures that block cellular, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and NFC signals, preventing remote wiping of seized mobile devices.
- **Forensic Workstations** — dedicated high-RAM, multi-drive chassis (e.g., F.R.E.D. by Digital Intelligence).
- **USB Kill Disablers** — protect against malicious USB-based evidence destruction.

---

#### ✅ Model Answer for Q. (b) — Challenges in Cyber Forensics (7 Marks)

**1. Technical Challenges (3 marks)**

- **Storage Explosion** — modern drives reach 20 TB+ HDDs and 8 TB+ NVMe SSDs; imaging takes hours/days.
- **Anti-Forensics** — adversaries use steganography, full-disk encryption (BitLocker, LUKS, FileVault), and data-wiping tools (DBAN).
- **Cloud and Virtualization** — distributed architecture, volatile VMs, lack of physical access, multi-tenancy.
- **Mobile Diversity** — fragmented OS versions, default encryption, secure enclaves.
- **IoT Forensics** — proprietary firmware, undocumented file systems, no standard procedures.

**2. Legal Challenges (2 marks)**

- **Jurisdictional Issues** — cross-border data access raises conflicts between data-residency laws (GDPR, India's DPDP Act).
- **Chain of Custody** — any documentation lapse renders evidence inadmissible.
- **Privacy Conflicts** — investigative needs may clash with individual privacy rights.
- **Admissibility Standards** — Daubert (US), Frye (US), and Indian Evidence Act sections vary in their scientific-evidence requirements.

**3. Operational Challenges (2 marks)**

- **Skills Gap** — demand for certified forensic analysts (GCFE, GCFA, EnCE, CHFI) far exceeds supply.
- **Tool Costs** — proprietary suites cost ₹2–10 lakh per seat.
- **Continuous Training** — rapid OS / app evolution requires perpetual upskilling.
- **Tool Validation** — NIST CFTT testing cycles lag behind OS release cycles.

**Concluding Statement**: A holistic cyber forensic capability requires **integrated technical, legal, and operational preparedness**, supported by international cooperation and continuous tool validation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exams**

### 🔹 Tool Categories
- Cyber forensics tools are of **three types**: Software, Hardware, and Hybrid Suites.
- Software is further divided into **Disk, Memory, Network, Mobile, and Hashing** categories.

### 🔹 Open Source vs Proprietary
- **Open Source Examples**: Autopsy, The Sleuth Kit (TSK), Volatility, Wireshark, Guymager, NetworkMiner.
- **Proprietary Examples**: EnCase, FTK, X-Ways Forensics, Cellebrite UFED, MSAB XRY, Oxygen Forensic Detective.
- Both are valid **if NIST CFTT validated**.

### 🔹 Hardware Tools — Key Items
- **Write Blockers** → SATA, USB, NVMe, IDE, Thunderbolt variants.
- **Forensic Duplicators** → Logicube Falcon-NEO, Tableau TD2u, CRU Ditto.
- **Faraday Bags** → block RF signals during mobile seizure.

### 🔹 Hashing Standards
- **MD5** = **128 bits** (legacy, collision-prone).
- **SHA-1** = **160 bits** (deprecated).
- **SHA-256** = **256 bits** (current KTU-recommended standard).

### 🔹 Image Formats
- `.E01` → EnCase proprietary compressed format.
- `.DD` → Raw bit-stream image.
- `.AFF` → Open advanced forensic format.

### 🔹 Challenges (must categorize)
- **Technical**: Anti-forensics, encryption, cloud, IoT.
- **Legal**: Jurisdictional, chain of custody, privacy.
- **Operational**: Cost, skills gap, validation.

### 🔹 Legal Standards
- **Daubert Standard** → scientific reliability.
- **Frye Standard** → general acceptance in scientific community.
- **Chain of Custody** → unbroken documentation trail.

### 🔹 Mobile Forensics Modes
- **Logical Acquisition** → file-level (fast, incomplete).
- **Physical Acquisition** → bit-stream of flash (slow, complete).

### 🔹 Memory Forensics
- **Volatility** is the de-facto open-source RAM analysis tool.
- Requires a **matching profile** for OS version + architecture.

### 🔹 Network Forensics
- **Wireshark** for PCAP analysis.
- **NetworkMiner** for passive file extraction.

### 🔹 Validation Authority
- **NIST CFReDS / CFTT** is the global benchmark.

### 🔹 Examiner's Mantra
> *"Acquire without alteration, analyze with validation, report with clarity, and document every step."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Skills Required to Become a Cyber Forensic Expert, Physical Requirements of a Cyber Forensics Lab
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT602 - CYBER FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Skills Required to Become a Cyber Forensic Expert, Physical Requirements of a Cyber Forensics Lab

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔍 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cyber Forensic Expert (CFE):** A professionally trained, ethically bound, and legally cognizant investigator who applies scientific methodologies, technical tools, and legal procedures to identify, preserve, analyze, and present digital evidence originating from computing devices, networks, and storage media in a manner admissible before a court of law.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a Cyber Forensic Expert operates at the intersection of **Computer Science, Law, and Criminal Investigation**. The role is governed by the **Information Technology Act, 2000 (India)** and the **Indian Evidence Act, 1872** (as amended), along with international standards such as **ISO/IEC 27037** (digital evidence identification, collection, acquisition) and **ISO/IEC 27042** (digital evidence analysis).

> [!NOTE]
> **Cyber Forensics Laboratory:** A controlled, secured, and access-restricted physical environment — equipped with specialized hardware, certified software, environmental controls, and documented chain-of-custody protocols — designed exclusively for the forensic acquisition, analysis, and preservation of digital evidence.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **medical forensic lab** in a crime thriller movie. Just as a medical forensic expert requires:
- 🔬 A microscope, sterile room, and lab coat (**physical infrastructure**)
- 🧠 Knowledge of anatomy, pathology, and toxicology (**analytical expertise**)
- ⚖️ Understanding of courtroom protocol and evidence admissibility (**legal skills**)
- 🖐️ Steady hands, patience, and ethical integrity (**soft skills**)

...a **Cyber Forensic Expert** requires the digital equivalent:
- 🖥️ Write-blockers, forensic duplicators, Faraday cages (**specialized hardware**)
- 🧠 Deep knowledge of file systems, OS internals, networking, cryptography (**technical expertise**)
- ⚖️ Understanding of IT Act 2000, evidence handling, and chain of custody (**legal skills**)
- 🖐️ Meticulous documentation, patience, and unimpeachable ethical standards (**soft skills**)

> [!TIP]
> **Remember:** A Cyber Forensic Expert is **NOT** just a "good hacker." A hacker finds vulnerabilities to exploit; a forensic expert finds **evidence** to present, and crucially — they must prove the evidence was **untampered** and **legally obtained**.

---

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Forensic Competence

$$
\text{Forensic Competence} \;=\; f(\text{Technical Skills},\; \text{Procedural Knowledge},\; \text{Ethical Integrity})
$$

| Pillar | Weightage in Real Investigations | Core Idea |
|---|---|---|
| **Technical Skills** | $\approx 40\%$ | OS internals, file systems, network protocols, encryption, malware analysis |
| **Procedural Knowledge** | $\approx 35\%$ | Chain of custody, evidence handling, legal admissibility, documentation |
| **Ethical Integrity** | $\approx 25\%$ | Impartiality, confidentiality, non-disclosure, courtroom credibility |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme explicitly emphasizes the **physical lab requirements** as a separate evaluation area. Students are expected to know not just *what tools* a lab has, but *why* each component is essential and *how* it contributes to evidentiary integrity.

---

## 1.4 What This Topic Is NOT

To avoid common KTU answer-script errors, let's clarify misconceptions:

- ❌ It is **not** about hacking tools or penetration testing (that's ethical hacking — PECCT605).
- ❌ It is **not** about learning to *break* systems, but about *documenting breaches* that have already occurred.
- ❌ The lab is **not** a generic IT lab — it has **specific physical, environmental, and procedural constraints** that a normal computer lab does not.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Skills Required

The skill set of a Cyber Forensic Expert can be **decomposed into five orthogonal dimensions**. Each dimension is non-substitutable; weakness in any one compromises the entire investigation.

### 2.1.1 Technical Skills (The "What You Know")

These are the **hard skills** — measurable, certifiable, and tool-specific.

| Sub-Category | Specific Competencies | Industry-Standard Tools |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating Systems** | Windows Registry, Linux ext4/NTFS internals, macOS APFS, mobile OS (Android/iOS) | Autopsy, The Sleuth Kit, FTK Imager |
| **File Systems & Data Recovery** | FAT, NTFS, ext3/4, XFS, HFS+; deleted file carving; slack space analysis | EnCase, X-Ways, R-Studio |
| **Networking** | TCP/IP stack, packet analysis, log correlation, IDS/IPS evidence | Wireshark, NetworkMiner, tcpdump |
| **Cryptography** | Symmetric (AES, DES), Asymmetric (RSA, ECC), hashing (SHA-256, MD5) | OpenSSL, HashCalc, VeraCrypt |
| **Malware Analysis** | Static & dynamic analysis, reverse engineering, sandboxing | IDA Pro, Ghidra, Cuckoo Sandbox, REMnux |
| **Mobile Forensics** | Android ADB, iOS checkm8, chip-off, JTAG | Cellebrite UFED, MSAB XRY, Oxygen Forensic Suite |
| **Database Forensics** | SQL log analysis, transaction forensics | SQL Log Rescue, ApexSQL Log |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Expected Depth:** For the 2024 Scheme, students are expected to know **why** a specific tool is preferred (e.g., EnCase is preferred over commercial alternatives because of its **judicial acceptance** in many international courts).

### 2.1.2 Analytical & Cognitive Skills (The "How You Think")

These are the **soft-cognitive skills** — harder to teach but critical for success.

- **Pattern Recognition:** Identifying anomalies in log files, unusual file timestamps, hidden partitions.
- **Logical Deduction:** Constructing a timeline of events from fragmented artifacts (browser history, prefetch, registry).
- **Critical Thinking:** Questioning every assumption; understanding what is **absent** is often as important as what is **present** (anti-forensic detection).
- **Problem Decomposition:** Breaking a complex breach into its constituent attack vectors.

### 2.1.3 Legal & Procedural Skills (The "How You Document")

This is what **differentiates a forensic expert from a hobbyist investigator.**

- **Chain of Custody:** A chronological, unbroken, documented trail of evidence from seizure → analysis → court.
- **IT Act 2000 Knowledge:** Sections 65, 66, 66A–F, 69, 79 — especially **Section 79** (intermediary liability) and **Section 65B** (admissibility of electronic evidence).
- **Evidence Admissibility:** Evidence must be relevant, authentic, and not violate the **Right to Privacy** (post *Puttaswamy*, 2017).
- **Reporting Skills:** Writing a **Forensic Investigation Report (FIR-equivalent)** that a non-technical judge or jury can understand.

### 2.1.4 Communication Skills (The "How You Explain")

- **Technical Writing:** Clear, jargon-controlled reports.
- **Courtroom Testimony:** Explaining technical findings to lawyers and judges.
- **Cross-examination Resilience:** Withstanding adversarial questioning by opposing counsel.

### 2.1.5 Ethical Skills (The "How You Behave")

> [!WARNING]
> A forensic expert with **unethical conduct** can have their entire case dismissed and themselves criminally prosecuted. **Ethics is non-negotiable.**

- **Impartiality:** Present evidence for or against, regardless of who hired you.
- **Confidentiality:** NDAs are standard; leaking case details = professional death.
- **Continuing Education:** Cyber threats evolve weekly; static knowledge becomes obsolete within months.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Evidence Integrity Check** | $\text{Forensic Image Hash} = H_{\text{src}} \stackrel{?}{=} H_{\text{dst}}$ | SHA-256 or MD5; both **must** match for admissibility |
| 2 | **Bit-Stream Copy Theorem** | $\text{DD}_{\text{of}} = \text{DD}_{\text{src}} \; \forall \; \text{bytes}$ | Every sector copied, including slack, unallocated, host-protected |
| 3 | **Chain of Custody Count** | $COC = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (T_i, H_i, S_i, R_i)$ | $T$ = timestamp, $H$ = handler, $S$ = signature, $R$ = reason |
| 4 | **Storage Capacity Formula** | $C_{\text{effective}} = N \times S_{\text{disk}}$ | $N$ = number of evidence disks, $S$ = per-disk size; must be $\geq 1.5\times$ source |
| 5 | **Lab Air Conditioning Load** | $Q_{\text{cooling}} = \sum P_{\text{equipment}} \times 1.2$ | Multiply total equipment wattage by 1.2 (safety factor) |
| 6 | **Acquisition Time Estimate** | $T_{\text{acq}} \approx \dfrac{S_{\text{drive}}}{\text{Throughput}_{\text{IF}}}$ | $IF$ = interface (USB 3.0 $\approx 5$ Gbps theoretical) |
| 7 | **Write Blocker Rule** | $V_{\text{data-write}}^{\text{evidence-port}} = 0$ | Zero voltage/data write to source drive; read-only is **mandatory** |
| 8 | **Forensic Lab Faraday Cage Effectiveness** | $\text{SE}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10} \dfrac{E_{\text{ext}}}{E_{\text{int}}}$ | Shielding Effectiveness; typical cage: $\geq 80$ dB at 1 GHz |
| 9 | **Evidence Locker Access Count** | $N_{\text{access}} \leq 2 \times N_{\text{custodians}}$ | Two-person rule (dual key) standard for chain integrity |
| 10 | **Time Zone Normalization (UTC)** | $T_{\text{UTC}} = T_{\text{local}} - \Delta_{\text{TZ}}$ | All forensic timestamps **must** be converted to UTC for court |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** Notice that even in a "skills" topic, **quantitative reasoning** appears. The 2024 Scheme expects students to back qualitative answers with numerical/structural reasoning wherever possible. Memorize the **chain-of-custody equation** ($COC$ formula) — it's a 14-mark favorite.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding lab requirements isn't academic — it has **direct industry implications**:

- **Incident Response Teams (CSIRTs):** Use these standards to build internal response labs.
- **Banking & Financial Sector (RBI Compliance):** Mandate forensic-ready labs for PCI-DSS audits.
- **Law Enforcement:** CBI, NIA, and state cyber cells all follow these physical lab standards.
- **Corporate DFIR Teams:** Every Fortune-500 company has an in-house or contracted cyber forensic lab.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Exhaustive Content Breakdown

> Since this topic is **conceptual and procedural** rather than purely mathematical, Section 3 provides a **rigorous step-by-step decomposition** of skills and lab requirements using formal logical expressions, alignment blocks, and analytical matrices. Every transition is explicitly justified.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Skills → Sub-Skills → Tools → Outcomes

This matrix maps **every high-level skill** to its **tangible sub-skill**, **tooling**, and **measurable outcome**. The KTU 2024 examiner often uses this kind of decomposition to allocate marks.

| Skill Domain | Sub-Skill | Tool / Framework | Measurable Outcome | KTU Bloom Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Technical** | Disk Imaging | FTK Imager, dd, Guymager | $\text{MD5}_{\text{src}} = \text{MD5}_{\text{dst}}$ verified | Apply |
| **Technical** | Memory Forensics | Volatility, Rekall | All processes, DLLs, network conns extracted | Analyze |
| **Technical** | Network Forensics | Wireshark, NetworkMiner | PCAPs parsed, sessions reconstructed | Analyze |
| **Technical** | Mobile Forensics | Cellebrite UFED, MSAB XRY | SMS, call logs, app data recovered | Apply |
| **Technical** | Malware Reverse Eng. | Ghidra, IDA Free, REMnux | Indicators of Compromise (IoCs) extracted | Analyze |
| **Analytical** | Timeline Analysis | Plaso/log2timeline | Super-timeline with $\pm 1$s resolution | Analyze |
| **Analytical** | Anomaly Detection | Splunk, ELK Stack | Outlier events flagged statistically | Evaluate |
| **Legal** | Chain of Custody | Custom logs + Hash tools | Unbroken, signed trail $COC$ | Apply |
| **Legal** | Court-Admissible Reporting | EnCase, FTK report generators | PDF + hash, signed, paginated | Create |
| **Communication** | Expert Witness Testimony | Mock court training | Cross-examined without contradiction | Evaluate |
| **Ethical** | NDA Compliance | Legal frameworks | Zero breaches, signed attestations | Apply |

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: How a Cyber Forensic Lab Is Sized (Capacity Planning)

This is a **derivation-style** explanation showing how quantitative reasoning applies even to a "non-math" topic. KTU may ask: *"How would you size the storage of a forensic lab expected to handle 10 cases per month, each with 2 TB of evidence?"*

**Step 1: Define the input parameters.**

Let us define:
- $C_{\text{cases}} = 10$ (cases per month)
- $S_{\text{case}} = 2$ TB (average evidence size per case)
- $n_{\text{copies}} = 3$ (original + working copy + court copy)
- $r_{\text{retention}} = 12$ months (retention policy)

**Step 2: Compute monthly storage requirement.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{monthly}} &= C_{\text{cases}} \times S_{\text{case}} \times n_{\text{copies}} \\
&= 10 \times 2 \text{ TB} \times 3 \\
&= 60 \text{ TB/month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute annual storage with retention.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{annual}} &= S_{\text{monthly}} \times r_{\text{retention}} \\
&= 60 \text{ TB} \times 12 \\
&= 720 \text{ TB} = 0.72 \text{ PB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Apply safety factor (RAID overhead, OS overhead, logs).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{effective}} &= S_{\text{annual}} \times \text{SF} \\
&= 720 \text{ TB} \times 1.4 \\
&= 1008 \text{ TB} \approx 1 \text{ PB}
\end{aligned}
$$

The safety factor **1.4** accounts for:
- RAID parity overhead ($\approx 25\%$)
- File system metadata overhead ($\approx 5\%$)
- Log and index storage ($\approx 10\%$)

**Step 5: Final lab specification.**

$$
\boxed{\text{Lab needs at minimum } 1 \text{ PB usable storage with RAID-6 configuration}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** Many students memorize just "the lab needs a lot of storage." A 14-mark answer that includes this **derivation-style** quantification scores substantially higher. The 2024 Scheme rewards **structured, analytical** thinking over rote listing.

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Procedural Decomposition: Cyber Forensics Lab Physical Setup

The physical lab requirements must be evaluated against the **5-axis compliance model**. Each axis has mandatory and optional requirements.

$$
\text{Lab Compliance} = \{ \text{Security},\; \text{Environmental},\; \text{Hardware},\; \text{Software},\; \text{Procedural} \}
$$

### Axis 1: Physical Security
- **Mandatory:** Biometric access (fingerprint + RFID), CCTV with 24×7 recording, mantrap entry, two-person integrity rule.
- **Optional:** Retinal scanner, armed guard for high-profile cases.

### Axis 2: Environmental Controls
- **Mandatory:** Temperature $18°C$–$24°C$, humidity $40\%$–$60\%$, dedicated HVAC, fire suppression (FM-200 or inert gas, **not water**), anti-static flooring.
- **Rationale:** Static discharge can destroy volatile memory evidence; water suppression ruins electronics.

### Axis 3: Hardware (Detailed)
- **Forensic Workstations:** $\geq 32$ GB RAM, $\geq 1$ TB SSD, multi-monitor, GPU for password cracking.
- **Write Blockers:** Tableau T8-R2 (USB), WiebeTech Combo (SATA/IDE), or software-based (PDB).
- **Disk Duplicators:** Logicube Forensic Talon, Image MASSter Solo-4.
- **Mobile Forensics:** Cellebrite UFED, MSAB XRY, Faraday bags, JTAG/chip-off stations.
- **Network Taps:** Gigamon, ProfiShark, optical TAPs for live network capture.
- **Storage:** NAS/SAN with RAID-6, $\geq 1$ PB for medium-scale lab.

### Axis 4: Software (Detailed)
- **Imaging:** FTK Imager, dd, dcfldd, Guymager.
- **Analysis:** EnCase Forensic, AccessData FTK, Autopsy, X-Ways Forensics.
- **Memory:** Volatility, Rekall, MemProcFS.
- **Network:** Wireshark, NetworkMiner, tcpdump, Suricata (for PCAP analysis).
- **Mobile:** Cellebrite Physical Analyzer, Oxygen Forensic Detective.
- **Malware:** Ghidra, IDA Free, Cuckoo Sandbox, YARA.

### Axis 5: Procedural
- **Mandatory:** SOP (Standard Operating Procedure) manual, ISO 17025 compliance (for accredited labs), chain-of-custody form templates, evidence labeling SOP.
- **Optional:** Peer review, second-opinion policy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU 2024:** The examiner will **not** accept "EnCase and FTK" as a complete answer. You must justify **why** the tool is used and **how** it contributes to the evidence chain. For instance: *"FTK Imager is used because it produces both MD5 and SHA-1 hashes simultaneously, providing redundant integrity verification consistent with ISO 27037."*

---

## 3.4 Analytical Mapping: Common Forensics Software vs. Function

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stage 1: Acquisition} &\rightarrow \text{FTK Imager, dd, EnCase} \\
\text{Stage 2: Preservation} &\rightarrow \text{Write Blocker, RAID-6 NAS} \\
\text{Stage 3: Analysis} &\rightarrow \text{Autopsy, EnCase, X-Ways} \\
\text{Stage 4: Reporting} &\rightarrow \text{EnCase Report Generator, FTK Report} \\
\text{Stage 5: Courtroom} &\rightarrow \text{Signed PDF + Hash, Expert Witness}
\end{aligned}
$$

This **staged flow** is the backbone of any forensic workflow and directly maps to the **NIST SP 800-86** guidelines that KTU 2024 follows implicitly.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🏗️ SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Cyber Forensics Lab — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following block diagram maps the **physical and functional architecture** of a standards-compliant Cyber Forensics Lab, showing interconnections between hardware, software, security, and procedural layers.

# 📝 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

### **Q1. Differentiate between a Cyber Forensic Expert and a Penetration Tester. List any four core competencies expected of a forensic expert.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

A Cyber Forensic Expert investigates **after** an incident to recover and present evidence in a court of law, while a Penetration Tester operates **with authorization** to find vulnerabilities before an attack occurs.

**Four core competencies:**

1. **Technical mastery** of file systems, OS internals, and forensic toolchains (EnCase, FTK, Autopsy).
2. **Chain of custody discipline** — maintaining an unbroken, signed trail of evidence handling.
3. **Legal awareness** of the IT Act 2000 (especially Section 65B for admissibility of electronic evidence).
4. **Ethical impartiality** — presenting evidence truthfully regardless of the hiring party.

---

### **Q2. State the purpose of a Faraday cage in a cyber forensic lab. Mention any two mobile device-specific equipment used during mobile forensics.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**Purpose of Faraday Cage:**
A Faraday cage is a shielded enclosure that **blocks all external electromagnetic signals** (cellular, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, GPS) from reaching a mobile device placed inside it. This prevents the device from receiving remote wipe commands or "kill switches" sent by an attacker, thereby **preserving volatile evidence** like RAM, network state, and live app data.

**Two mobile device-specific equipment:**

1. **Cellebrite UFED** — for logical and physical extraction of mobile device data.
2. **JTAG/Chip-off station** — for low-level data extraction from damaged or locked devices via direct flash memory access.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / May 2024 Module Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a)** Discuss in detail the technical, analytical, legal, and ethical skills required to become a competent cyber forensic expert. `(7 Marks)`

**Model Answer:**

**1. Technical Skills (2 Marks):**
- Deep understanding of operating systems (Windows, Linux, macOS) and their file systems (NTFS, ext4, APFS, FAT32).
- Proficiency in disk imaging tools (FTK Imager, dd, EnCase), memory forensics (Volatility, Rekall), and network forensics (Wireshark, tcpdump).
- Knowledge of cryptographic primitives (AES, RSA, SHA-256), malware analysis (Ghidra, IDA), and mobile forensics (Cellebrite UFED).
- Familiarity with scripting (Python, PowerShell, Bash) for automation of repetitive analysis tasks.

**2. Analytical Skills (2 Marks):**
- **Pattern recognition** to detect anomalies in log files, registry entries, and file metadata.
- **Timeline reconstruction** using tools like Plaso/log2timeline to build a unified super-timeline of events.
- **Logical deduction** to chain fragmented artifacts (browser history, prefetch, recent files) into a coherent attack narrative.
- **Critical thinking** to question assumptions and identify anti-forensic techniques (timestamp manipulation, steganography, log wiping).

**3. Legal Skills (1.5 Marks):**
- Thorough knowledge of **IT Act 2000**, especially:
  - **Section 65** — Tampering with computer source documents.
  - **Section 65B** — Conditions for admissibility of electronic evidence.
  - **Section 66** — Computer-related offences.
  - **Section 79** — Intermediary liability.
- Understanding of the **Indian Evidence Act** provisions for digital evidence.
- Awareness of the **Right to Privacy** (post *Justice K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India*, 2017) and its impact on forensic seizure.

**4. Ethical Skills (1.5 Marks):**
- **Impartiality:** Present facts truthfully, regardless of who hired you.
- **Confidentiality:** Strict adherence to NDAs; never disclose case details.
- **Continuous learning:** Cyber threats evolve rapidly; static knowledge is dangerous.
- **Professional certifications:** Maintain credentials like **CCE** (Certified Computer Examiner), **GCFE** (GIAC Certified Forensic Examiner), **EnCE** (EnCase Certified Examiner).

---

#### **(b)** Describe the physical requirements of a cyber forensic laboratory. Include hardware, software, environmental controls, and security measures in your answer with a suitable block diagram. `(7 Marks)`

**Model Answer:**

A cyber forensic laboratory must be designed to **preserve evidentiary integrity, ensure reproducibility, and maintain chain of custody**. Its physical requirements span five dimensions:

**1. Physical Security (2 Marks):**
- **Access control:** Biometric + RFID dual authentication; mantrap entry to prevent tailgating.
- **Surveillance:** 24×7 CCTV recording with a minimum 90-day retention.
- **Two-person rule:** Critical operations require two certified investigators to prevent tampering.
- **Restricted access:** Only authorized personnel; visitors logged with ID and escort.

**2. Hardware Infrastructure (2 Marks):**
- **Forensic workstations:** $\geq 32$ GB RAM, $\geq 1$ TB SSD, multi-monitor, dedicated GPU for password cracking.
- **Write blockers:** Hardware-based (Tableau T8-R2) to prevent any write operation to the source evidence drive.
- **Disk duplicators:** Logicube Forensic Talon for bit-stream imaging.
- **Mobile forensics station:** Cellebrite UFED, MSAB XRY, JTAG/chip-off tools, Faraday cage.
- **Network forensics:** Optical TAPs, packet capture appliances.
- **Storage:** NAS/SAN with RAID-6, $\geq 1$ PB usable capacity for medium-scale operations.

**3. Software Stack (1 Mark):**
- **Imaging:** FTK Imager, dd, dcfldd.
- **Analysis:** EnCase Forensic, AccessData FTK, Autopsy, X-Ways.
- **Memory:** Volatility, Rekall.
- **Network:** Wireshark, NetworkMiner, Suricata.
- **Mobile:** Cellebrite Physical Analyzer, Oxygen Forensic Detective.
- **Malware:** Ghidra, IDA Free, Cuckoo Sandbox, YARA.

**4. Environmental Controls (1 Mark):**
- **Temperature:** Maintained at $18°C$–$24°C$ to prevent equipment damage and condensation.
- **Humidity:** $40\%$–$60\%$ to prevent static discharge.
- **Fire suppression:** FM-200 or inert gas system (**NEVER water**, as it destroys digital evidence).
- **Anti-static flooring** and **UPS backup** (minimum 30 minutes) for power continuity.

**5. Procedural & Documentation (1 Mark):**
- ISO 17025 and ISO 27037 compliance.
- SOP manual for every operation.
- Chain-of-custody forms, evidence labeling, and tamper-evident packaging.
- Peer review policy and audit trail for every action.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    LAB[Cyber Forensics Lab] --> SEC[Physical Security: Biometric, CCTV, Mantrap]
    LAB --> HW[Hardware: Workstations, Write Blockers, Mobile Bay, Network TAPs]
    LAB --> SW[Software: EnCase, FTK, Autopsy, Volatility, Wireshark]
    LAB --> ENV[Environment: AC 20C, Humidity 50%, FM-200, UPS]
    LAB --> PROC[Procedures: ISO 27037, Chain of Custody, SOP Manual]
```

---

### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE**

#### **(a)** Enumerate and explain the various categories of skills a cyber forensic investigator must possess to handle digital crime scenes effectively. `(7 Marks)`

**Model Answer:**

A digital crime scene is unique because **evidence is intangible, volatile, and easily destroyed**. Therefore, the investigator must possess a **multi-disciplinary skill set**:

**1. Technical/IT Skills (2.5 Marks):**
- **OS & File System knowledge:** Understanding how data is stored, deleted, and recovered across NTFS, ext4, APFS, HFS+.
- **Networking:** TCP/IP, DNS, HTTP, VPN, Tor — to reconstruct network-based crimes.
- **Cryptography:** Decrypting password-protected or encrypted containers (VeraCrypt, BitLocker).
- **Programming:** Python, Bash, PowerShell for automation and custom artifact parsing.
- **Mobile:** Android/iOS internals, app data storage, cloud sync.

**2. Analytical Skills (2 Marks):**
- **Logical reasoning** to correlate disparate evidence into a single narrative.
- **Attention to detail** — a single timestamp mismatch can exonerate or convict.
- **Hypothesis testing** — formulating theories and validating/refuting them with evidence.
- **Anti-forensic detection** — recognizing evidence of CCleaner usage, timestomping, log deletion.

**3. Legal/Procedural Skills (1.5 Marks):**
- **IT Act 2000** compliance during seizure and analysis.
- **Search & seizure warrants** — knowing what can be legally taken.
- **Section 65B certificate** for electronic evidence admissibility.
- **Chain of custody** maintenance and documentation.

**4. Communication Skills (0.5 Mark):**
- Writing clear, non-ambiguous reports.
- Explaining technical findings to non-technical judges/juries.
- Testifying confidently under cross-examination.

**5. Ethical/Soft Skills (0.5 Mark):**
- Integrity, impartiality, confidentiality.
- Patience (analysis can take weeks for terabytes of data).
- Stress management (courtroom pressure, deadline pressure).

---

#### **(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a cyber forensics lab. Discuss the specific role of **write blockers**, **evidence lockers**, and **Faraday bags** in maintaining evidentiary integrity. `(7 Marks)`

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram of Cyber Forensics Lab:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    LAB[Cyber Forensics Lab Architecture] --> PSEC[Physical Security: Biometric, CCTV, Mantrap]
    LAB --> HW[Hardware Layer]
    LAB --> SW[Software Layer]
    LAB --> EH[Evidence Handling Layer]
    LAB --> EC[Environmental Control]
    LAB --> PROC[Procedural Governance ISO 27037]

    HW --> WS[Forensic Workstations]
    HW --> WB[Write Blockers]
    HW --> DD[Disk Duplicators]
    HW --> MB[Mobile Forensics Bay with Faraday Bags]
    HW --> NT[Network TAPs]

    EH --> EL[Evidence Lockers with Dual Lock]
    EH --> COC[Chain of Custody Log]
    EH --> WC[Working Copy Drive Set]
```

**Role of Write Blockers (2.5 Marks):**
- A **write blocker** is a hardware (or software) device that allows **read-only** access to a storage medium.
- It physically or electronically prevents any **WRITE** command from reaching the source drive.
- **Why it's critical:** Accidental modification of source evidence destroys its **admissibility**. Courts require evidence to be in the **same state** as when it was seized.
- Examples: Tableau T8-R2 (USB), WiebeTech Combo (SATA/IDE), Guidance Software FastBloc.
- **Verification:** After acquisition, $\text{Hash}_{\text{source}} = \text{Hash}_{\text{image}}$ must hold; any mismatch indicates tampering or write-blocker failure.

**Role of Evidence Lockers (2.5 Marks):**
- Evidence lockers are **physically secured, access-controlled storage** for evidence media (HDDs, SSDs, USBs, mobile devices, paper documents).
- **Dual-lock system:** Requires two authorized personnel to open (two-person integrity rule).
- **Tamper-evident seals:** Once evidence is sealed, any opening leaves a visible trace.
- **CCTV monitoring:** Locker openings are recorded for audit.
- **Climate controlled:** Prevents degradation of media over time.
- **Why it's critical:** Maintains the **physical chain of custody**; without it, the legal chain is broken.

**Role of Faraday Bags (2 Marks):**
- A **Faraday bag** is a portable, shielded enclosure made of conductive material that blocks all external electromagnetic signals.
- **Why it's critical for mobile forensics:** When a mobile device is seized, it may still be **network-connected**. The attacker can remotely trigger:
  - **Remote wipe** (Android Device Manager, iCloud Find My iPhone).
  - **Data overwrite** or **encryption key deletion**.
- Placing the device in a Faraday bag **immediately** upon seizure prevents these signals from reaching the device, **preserving volatile evidence** like RAM contents, active network connections, and encryption keys in memory.
- **Typical shielding effectiveness:** $\geq 80$ dB attenuation at 1 GHz (as per formula $\text{SE}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10} \dfrac{E_{\text{ext}}}{E_{\text{int}}}$).

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Listing tools without justification:** Writing "EnCase, FTK, Autopsy" without explaining *why* each is used. **Always** pair every tool with its **function** and **legal/courtroom relevance**.
> 2. **Confusing write-blocker with read-only mode:** A write-blocker is a **physical or electronic intermediary**, not just a software toggle. Students lose marks by not specifying the brand-class or by saying "just don't write to the disk."
> 3. **Forgetting the legal layer:** Many students describe hardware and software in detail but forget **IT Act 2000, Section 65B, and chain-of-custody**. These are **mandatory** in any 14-mark answer for KTU 2024.
> 4. **No block diagram:** In 14-mark questions, a **diagram is worth 2–3 marks by itself**. A missing diagram is an automatic penalty.
> 5. **Treating ethics as optional:** Ethics is a **separate sub-skill** category in the 2024 Scheme. Skipping it = losing 1–1.5 marks minimum.
> 6. **Confusing Faraday cage with Faraday bag:** The cage is **stationary in the lab**; the **bag is portable** and used **at the seizure site**. Mixing these up shows surface-level understanding.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **📌 Rapid Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam)**

### **A. Skills Recap**

- ✅ **Technical Skills:** OS internals, file systems, networking, crypto, malware, mobile, memory, scripting.
- ✅ **Analytical Skills:** Pattern recognition, timeline reconstruction, logical deduction, anti-forensic detection.
- ✅ **Legal Skills:** IT Act 2000 (Sections 65, 65B, 66, 69, 79), Indian Evidence Act, Puttaswamy privacy ruling.
- ✅ **Communication Skills:** Report writing, expert testimony, jury-friendly explanation.
- ✅ **Ethical Skills:** Impartiality, confidentiality, NDA, continuing education, certifications (CCE, GCFE, EnCE).

### **B. Lab Physical Requirements Recap**

- ✅ **Security:** Biometric + RFID, mantrap, CCTV 24×7, two-person rule.
- ✅ **Hardware:** Forensic workstation, write blockers, disk duplicators, mobile bay (Cellebrite), Faraday cage, network TAPs, RAID-6 NAS.
- ✅ **Software:** FTK Imager, EnCase, FTK, Autopsy, X-Ways, Volatility, Wireshark, Ghidra, Cuckoo, YARA.
- ✅ **Environment:** $18$–$24°C$ AC, $40$–$60\%$ humidity, FM-200 fire suppression, anti-static flooring, UPS ($\geq 30$ min).
- ✅ **Procedures:** ISO 27037, ISO 17025, NIST SP 800-86, SOP manual, chain of custody forms, evidence labeling, peer review.

### **C. Key Equations to Memorize**

- ✅ **Hash verification:** $\text{Hash}_{\text{src}} = \text{Hash}_{\text{img}}$
- ✅ **Bit-stream copy:** Every byte copied, including slack and unallocated space.
- ✅ **Chain of custody:** $COC = \sum (T_i, H_i, S_i, R_i)$
- ✅ **Faraday shielding:** $\text{SE}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10} \dfrac{E_{\text{ext}}}{E_{\text{int}}}$
- ✅ **Lab storage sizing:** $C_{\text{effective}} = N \times S_{\text{disk}} \times 1.5$

### **D. One-Line Mantras for Last-Minute Revision**

- 📌 "A forensic expert is **not** a hacker — they **document** breaches, not **cause** them."
- 📌 "Write blockers are to forensic experts what **gloves** are to medical forensic experts."
- 📌 "If the hash doesn't match, the evidence **doesn't exist** — legally speaking."
- 📌 "**Faraday bag** at the scene, **Faraday cage** in the lab — both save volatile data."
- 📌 "**Section 65B certificate** is the **birth certificate** of electronic evidence in Indian courts."
- 📌 "**Chain of custody** is a **chain** — break one link, lose the whole case."

---

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Types of Cyber Forensics

### Formal Definition
**Cyber Forensics** is the disciplined application of scientific and legally admissible methods to the identification, collection, preservation, examination, analysis, and presentation of digital evidence originating from digital devices, networks, and storage media, in a manner that maintains the integrity of the evidence chain (Chain of Custody) for use in legal proceedings, internal investigations, or incident response.

The domain branches into specialized sub-disciplines based on the **source medium** and **scope of investigation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The student is expected to distinguish between the major branches of cyber forensics and identify the appropriate forensic methodology for a given digital artifact (e.g., choosing *Network Forensics* for a packet capture investigation vs. *Memory Forensics* for a live RAM dump).

### Taxonomy of Cyber Forensics Branches

| Branch | Source Medium | Primary Investigative Goal |
|---|---|---|
| **Computer / Desktop Forensics** | PCs, Laptops, Workstations | Acquisition and analysis of storage media (HDD, SSD) |
| **Mobile Device Forensics** | Smartphones, Tablets | Recovery of SMS, call logs, GPS, app data, and deleted artifacts |
| **Network Forensics** | Routers, Firewalls, IDS Logs, Packet Captures | Monitoring, capturing, and analysis of network traffic |
| **Database Forensics** | DBMS Logs, Tables, Audit Trails | Recovery and analysis of tampered or fraudulent DB records |
| **Malware Forensics** | Suspicious Executables, Droppers | Static & dynamic reverse engineering of malicious code |
| **Memory Forensics** | Live RAM Dumps (`memdump`, `LiME`) | Recovery of volatile artifacts (processes, keys, network sockets) |
| **Cloud Forensics** | AWS, Azure, GCP Storage/IaaS | Evidence acquisition from virtualized, multi-tenant cloud environments |
| **Email Forensics** | Mail Servers, `.pst`, `.ost`, `.mbox` | Tracing spoofed mails, header analysis, phishing investigation |
| **Disk / Storage Forensics** | HDDs, SSDs, USBs, Optical Media | Recovery of deleted files, slack space, unallocated clusters |
| **Wireless Forensics** | Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, RF captures | Analysis of wireless signals, SSID, MAC association logs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine a crime scene — but instead of a house, the "crime scene" is a computer. Different rooms in that house (the desk drawer = files on disk, the brain = RAM, the phone = mobile device, the intercom system = network traffic) each require different detectives with different tools. **Types of Cyber Forensics** are simply the *specialized detective squads* sent into each of these digital rooms. The key forensic principle remains the same across all types: **don't contaminate the scene** (write-blockers, bit-stream imaging, hash integrity).

---

## 1.2 File System Forensics

### Formal Definition
**File System Forensics** is the branch of digital forensics concerned with the analysis of a file system's internal structures (metadata, directory entries, allocation tables, journal files) to recover deleted, hidden, or obfuscated data, and to reconstruct the sequence of user activity stored within the file system's on-disk artifacts.

It focuses on understanding **how a file system stores, retrieves, and updates data**, and exploits residual artifacts (such as timestamps in $MFT entries, alternate data streams, or journal records) to prove or disprove an investigative hypothesis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition — File System:**
> A **File System** is the OS-level logical structure that organizes, stores, retrieves, and manages data on a storage device. It provides the abstraction layer between raw bytes on physical media and the user-visible files and folders.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a **library**:
- The **books** = data files.
- The **library catalog** = file system index (MFT, FAT, inode table).
- The **shelves and section markers** = clusters/sectors/blocks.
- A **forensic investigator** walking in notices that a book was returned but the catalog still shows it "checked out" (this is a *deleted file*). By reading the catalog's underlying pencil marks (metadata), the investigator reconstructs the full investigative timeline.

> [!NOTE]
> **Forensic Insight:** Deleted data is rarely truly erased; the file system simply marks the clusters as "available" and the directory entry as "unused." The actual bytes persist until overwritten — this is the *fundamental premise of file system forensics*.

---

## 1.3 Working with Windows and CLI Systems

### Formal Definition
**Working with Windows and CLI (Command-Line Interface) Systems** in a forensic context refers to the use of native and third-party command-line utilities, Windows Registry artifacts, Event Viewer logs, and shell environments (cmd.exe, PowerShell, Bash) to acquire, examine, and interpret digital evidence without relying solely on GUI-based forensic suites (like EnCase, FTK, or Autopsy).

It is the **low-level, transparent, and scriptable approach** to digital evidence extraction and is essential in environments where GUI tools are unavailable (e.g., live triage on a server, headless systems, network boot environments).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The 2024 scheme places strong emphasis on CLI proficiency because it provides a **forensically transparent** workflow — every command is logged, reproducible, and admissible in court under *Daubert/Frye* standards, unlike point-and-click GUI tools whose internal operations may not be fully visible.

### Conceptual Analogy
A **GUI forensic tool** is like driving an **automatic car** — easy to use, but if something goes wrong, you may not know *why*.
A **CLI workflow** is like driving a **manual transmission** — you control every gear shift, and an expert mechanic (the court/forensic examiner) can clearly see your inputs and outputs. CLI is the **gold standard for evidentiary transparency**.

---

## 1.4 File Systems (Core Structures)

### Formal Definition
A **File System** is implemented as a layered on-disk data structure that defines:
1. **Boot Sector / Volume Boot Record (VBR)** — initial loader and FS parameters.
2. **Control Structures** — allocation tables (FAT), Master File Table (NTFS), inode tables (ext), superblocks.
3. **Data Area** — the actual clusters/blocks holding file content.
4. **Metadata** — descriptive information (timestamps, permissions, ownership, size).

> [!NOTE]
> **Forensic Truth:** A forensic examiner must understand the file system *better than the operating system uses it*, because forensic work deals with the residual, broken, or deliberately tampered states of these structures.

### Conceptual Analogy
A file system is the **table of contents + index + ledger** of a giant legal notebook. Even if pages are torn out (files deleted), the ledger still shows the original entries, and a skilled forensic accountant can reconstruct history from the ink, the margins, and the strikethroughs (slack space, journal entries, $MFT residuals).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Branch-by-Branch Theoretical Analysis

### A. Computer / Disk Forensics
- **Operational Logic:**
  1. **Identification** → Locate the digital media.
  2. **Preservation** → Apply hardware/software **write-blocker** to prevent mutation.
  3. **Acquisition** → Create a **bit-stream image** (forensic image: `.E01`, `.dd`, `.AFF`).
  4. **Verification** → Compute cryptographic hashes (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256) of source and image.
  5. **Analysis** → Recover deleted files, examine metadata, parse $MFT/FAT, decode registry.
  6. **Reporting** → Document with Chain of Custody.

### B. Mobile Device Forensics
- **Logical Acquisition** → Extracts active files via OS APIs.
- **File-System Acquisition** → Full file system dump (requires rooting/jailbreaking).
- **Physical Acquisition** → Bit-for-bit copy of flash memory via JTAG, ISP, or chip-off.

### C. Network Forensics
- **Capture** → Use SPAN ports, TAPs, or `tcpdump`/`Wireshark`.
- **Analysis** → Reconstruct sessions, identify C2 (Command & Control) traffic, detect exfiltration.
- **Key Tools:** Wireshark, NetworkMiner, Zeek (formerly Bro), Suricata.

### D. Memory Forensics
- **Acquisition** → `winpmem` (Windows), `LiME` (Linux), `AVML` (Azure).
- **Analysis** → Using **Volatility** framework to enumerate processes, DLLs, network connections, registry hives, and rootkit artifacts from RAM dumps.
- **Why critical:** Disk forensics is *post-mortem*; memory forensics captures the **live state** (encryption keys, decrypted malware, active sessions).

### E. Cloud Forensics
- **Multi-jurisdictional** issues (data residency in different countries).
- **Volatile instances** — VMs can be terminated, destroying evidence.
- **Reliance on CSP (Cloud Service Provider)** logs: CloudTrail (AWS), Activity Logs (Azure).

### F. Malware Forensics
- **Static Analysis** → Disassemble (IDA Pro, Ghidra), inspect strings, PE headers, imports.
- **Dynamic Analysis** → Run in sandbox (Cuckoo, Any.Run), observe C2, persistence, file/system mutations.

---

## 2.2 File System Forensics — Deep Analysis

### A. The Forensic Premise
> Every file system operation leaves **metadata artifacts**. Deleted ≠ Gone.

### B. Key Forensic Artifacts
- **$MFT entries** (NTFS) — contain $STANDARD_INFORMATION and $FILE_NAME attributes with MAC times.
- **$LogFile** (NTFS) — journal of file system metadata changes.
- **$UsnJrnl** (NTFS) — Change Journal tracking every file modification.
- **FAT directory entries** — show creation, last write, last access.
- **ext2/ext3/ext4 journal** (JBD2) — recovery point for metadata operations.
- **Slack Space** — the unused portion of the last cluster of a file. Often contains data from RAM (the OS may write RAM pages to disk during file writes — this is *file slack* and is a major forensic source).
- **Alternate Data Streams (ADS)** — NTFS feature allowing multiple data streams per file. Used by malware to hide payloads (e.g., `readme.txt:hidden.exe`).

### C. The Forensics Workflow
1. **Acquire image** (using FTK Imager, `dd`, Guymager).
2. **Mount read-only** or process with forensic suite.
3. **Parse file system structures** (parse MFT, recover deleted inodes).
4. **Carve files** (scalpel, photorec — file signature based recovery).
5. **Timeline analysis** (build super-timeline from $MFT, EVTX, prefetch, registry).
6. **Hash & report**.

---

## 2.3 Working with Windows and CLI Systems — Deep Analysis

### A. Windows Forensic Artifacts (Accessible via CLI)
- **Registry Hives** (`C:\Windows\System32\config\`) — `SAM`, `SECURITY`, `SOFTWARE`, `SYSTEM`, `NTUSER.DAT`.
- **Event Logs** (`.evtx`) — `C:\Windows\System32\winevt\Logs\`.
- **Prefetch Files** (`C:\Windows\Prefetch\`) — evidence of program execution.
- **LNK Files** (Recent items, jump lists).
- **Volume Shadow Copies** — point-in-time snapshots.
- **Pagefile.sys, Hiberfil.sys** — memory persistence on disk.

### B. Essential CLI Tools (High-Yield for KTU)
| Tool | OS | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `dd` | Linux | Bit-stream imaging (with `conv=noerror,sync`) |
| `dcfldd` | Linux | Enhanced `dd` with hashing and progress |
| `ewfexport` / `ewfacquire` | Linux | Create/convert E01 images |
| `Volatility` | Cross-platform | Memory analysis |
| `reg` (`reg query`, `reg export`) | Windows | Read registry from CLI |
| `wevtutil` | Windows | Export/query event logs |
| `Get-FileHash` | PowerShell | Compute MD5/SHA hashes |
| `kape` | Windows | Live triage collection |
| `xcopy / robocopy` | Windows | Forensically sound copying (preserves timestamps with `/copy:dat`) |
| `fsutil` | Windows | File system queries (USN journal, sparse files) |
| `icat`, `ifind`, `istat` (Sleuth Kit) | Linux | Inode-level analysis of disk images |
| `mmls`, `fls` (Sleuth Kit) | Linux | Partition & file listing |
| `strings` | Linux | Extract strings from binary data |

### C. Why CLI? The "Why" & "How"
- **Reproducibility** — A script can be re-run, audited, and verified.
- **No "Black Box"** — Every step is visible in the terminal log.
- **Live System Forensics** — Many enterprise servers are headless; CLI is the *only* option.
- **Court Admissibility** — Easier to defend under cross-examination.

---

## 2.4 File Systems — Deep Theoretical Breakdown

### A. FAT (File Allocation Table)
- **Origins:** MS-DOS, simple, lightweight.
- **Variants:** FAT12, FAT16, FAT32, exFAT.
- **Structure:**
  1. **Boot Sector** (sector 0) — BPB (BIOS Parameter Block).
  2. **FAT Region** — array of 32-bit (FAT32) entries, each pointing to next cluster of a file; `0x00000000` = free, `0x0FFFFFF7` = bad, `0x0FFFFFF8-0x0FFFFFFF` = end-of-chain.
  3. **Root Directory** (FAT12/16 only — fixed size).
  4. **Data Region** — clusters holding file content.
- **Forensic Weakness:** No built-in journaling → corruption risk; no access control; deleted file recovery is straightforward because directory entry is simply marked `0xE5`.

### B. NTFS (New Technology File System)
- **Default for Windows NT, 2000, XP, Vista, 7, 8, 10, 11.**
- **Structure:**
  1. **VBR (Volume Boot Record)** — sector 0.
  2. **NTFS Boot Sector** — contains BPB with cluster size, MFT location, etc.
  3. **Master File Table (MFT)** — every file/directory is an MFT record (typically 1024 bytes). Critical system MFT entries are reserved with names prefixed by `$`:
     - `$MFT` (entry 0), `$MFTMirr` (1), `$LogFile` (2), `$Volume` (3), `$AttrDef` (4), `$Bitmap` (6), `$Boot` (7), `$BadClus` (8), `$Secure` (9), `$UpCase` (10), `$Extend` (11).
  4. **System Files** — $LogFile, $Bitmap, $UsnJrnl, $Quota, $ObjId.
  5. **Data Area.**
- **Key NTFS Features for Forensics:**
  - **Alternate Data Streams (ADS)** — every file has an unnamed default `$DATA` attribute; additional `$DATA` attributes can be added (named streams). `dir /r` shows them in Windows.
  - **MAC Times** — `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` attribute stores Modification, Access, Creation, MFT Entry Modification (MACE).
  - **File Name Attribute** — `$FILE_NAME` attribute stores the *original* (POSIX) name and parent directory MFT reference — critical for deleted file recovery even after rename.
  - **Journaling** — $LogFile records metadata transactions; recovery possible.
  - **Data Run Lists** — $DATA attribute stores disk-resident data in *data runs* (starting cluster + length pairs), enabling non-contiguous file storage.
  - **Compression & Encryption** — NTFS-level transparent features; the Encrypted File System (EFS) leaves forensic traces in the $SECURITY_DESCRIPTOR.

### C. ext2 / ext3 / ext4 (Linux File Systems)
- **ext2:** Non-journaled; **ext3:** added journaling; **ext4:** extent-based, larger filesystems, delayed allocation.
- **Structure:**
  1. **Superblock** (offset 1024 bytes) — magic `0xEF53`, block count, inode count, free counts, mount count.
  2. **Group Descriptors** — describe block groups.
  3. **Data Block Bitmap**, **Inode Bitmap** (per group).
  4. **Inode Table** — every file has an inode containing metadata (mode, owner, size, timestamps, direct + indirect block pointers).
  5. **Data Blocks** — file content.
  6. **Journal** (ext3/ext4, JBD2 format) — logs metadata changes for crash recovery.
- **Forensic Artifacts:**
  - **Inode timestamps:** `i_atime` (access), `i_mtime` (modification), `i_ctime` (inode change), `i_crtime` (creation, ext4 only), `i_dtime` (deletion time).
  - **Deleted inodes** are kept until reused; `istat` (Sleuth Kit) reads them.
  - **Directory entries** store inode number + filename + file type; deleted entries leave recoverable traces in the block (especially with ext4 `dir_index`).
  - **Journal recovery** — the `jbd2` journal can be replayed or parsed to reconstruct file operations.

### D. APFS (Apple File System)
- Default on macOS 10.13+ and iOS 10.3+.
- **Features:** clones, snapshots, crash protection, space sharing, native encryption.
- **Structure:** Container → Volumes → Objects (B-Tree).
- **Forensic Challenges:** Object IDs change across container operations, encryption is per-volume, clones complicate unique identification.

### E. exFAT
- Microsoft's file system for flash drives (>4 GB files).
- **Structure:** Boot sector, FAT region, cluster heap.
- **Forensic Use:** Common in USB drives, SD cards, digital cameras; no journaling (good for raw carving).

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — File System Comparisons

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** File system comparison questions (compare FAT vs. NTFS, or NTFS vs. ext4) appear almost every year. Memorize the table below.

| Property | FAT32 | NTFS | ext4 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Max File Size | 4 GB $- 1$ byte | $2^{64} - 1$ bytes (16 TiB practical) | 16 TiB (theoretical 1 EiB) |
| Max Volume Size | 8 TB (with 64 KB clusters) | 256 TB | 1 EiB |
| Journaling | ❌ No | ✅ Yes ($LogFile) | ✅ Yes (JBD2) |
| Access Control | ❌ No ACLs | ✅ ACLs (DACL/SACL) | ✅ POSIX + ACLs |
| Encryption | ❌ No | ✅ EFS, BitLocker | ❌ Native (eCryptfs/LUKS external) |
| ADS Support | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (named streams) | ❌ No (extended attrs) |
| File Recovery | Moderate (manual FAT analysis) | High (MFT + journal) | High (inode + journal) |
| Compression | ❌ No | ✅ Per-file (LZNT1) | ❌ No |
| MAC Times | Creation, Write, Access | MACE (4 timestamps) | atime, mtime, ctime, crtime, dtime |
| Default OS | DOS / Windows (legacy) | Windows NT family | Linux |
| Forensic Tool | `fls`, `icat` | `MFTParse`, `analyzeMFT` | Sleuth Kit (`istat`, `icat`) |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Incident Response:** Memory forensics catches in-memory malware that on-disk AV misses.
- **Litigation / e-Discovery:** File system forensics is the *backbone* of corporate legal holds.
- **Insider Threat Investigations:** USB device usage traces in `SYSTEM\MountedDevices` registry key.
- **Ransomware Recovery:** Identifying journal snapshots and shadow copies to restore pre-encrypted state.
- **Law Enforcement:** Tracing child exploitation material, financial fraud, intellectual property theft.
- **DFIR (Digital Forensics & Incident Response):** Cloud forensics is critical in modern enterprise breaches (SolarWinds, Capital One cases).

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following analytical derivations and worked-out models are the **mathematical / logical core** of the topic. KTU examiners frequently test the *flow of forensic operations* (process flow) and the *structural offsets / sizes* of file system artifacts. We will derive the key cluster-to-file mapping logic and present the evidence-acquisition flow as a fully explicit, step-by-step model.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Cluster-to-File Mapping (FAT32 Example)

> **Scenario:** A FAT32 volume has cluster size = **4 sectors × 512 bytes/sector = 2048 bytes**. A file occupies clusters **5, 6, 7, 9, 10** (cluster 8 is free). The FAT entries are read as:

| FAT Entry Index | Value (Hex) | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 5 | 0x00000006 | Next cluster = 6 |
| 6 | 0x00000007 | Next cluster = 7 |
| 7 | 0x00000009 | Next cluster = 9 (cluster 8 is skipped) |
| 8 | 0x00000000 | Free cluster |
| 9 | 0x0000000A | Next cluster = 10 |
| 10 | 0x0FFFFFFF | End-of-chain (EOF) |

**Goal:** Reconstruct the file's chain and compute its total size.

### Step 1: Identify the starting cluster from the directory entry.
The directory entry's first cluster field gives the **start cluster**.
Assume `START_CLUSTER = 5`.

### Step 2: Follow the FAT chain (chain traversal algorithm).

$$C_0 = 5$$
$$C_1 = \text{FAT}[C_0] = \text{FAT}[5] = 6$$
$$C_2 = \text{FAT}[C_1] = \text{FAT}[6] = 7$$
$$C_3 = \text{FAT}[C_2] = \text{FAT}[7] = 9$$
$$C_4 = \text{FAT}[C_3] = \text{FAT}[9] = 10$$
$$C_5 = \text{FAT}[C_4] = \text{FAT}[10] = 0x0FFFFFFF \quad \text{(EOF marker, stop)}$$

### Step 3: Total number of clusters in chain = 5 (clusters 5, 6, 7, 9, 10).

$$N_{\text{clusters}} = 5$$

### Step 4: Compute file size.

$$S_{\text{file}} = N_{\text{clusters}} \times S_{\text{cluster}}$$

$$S_{\text{file}} = 5 \times 2048 \text{ bytes}$$

$$S_{\text{file}} = 10240 \text{ bytes} = 10 \text{ KiB}$$

### Step 5: Forensic interpretation.
- **Cluster 8 is skipped** (intentionally free or previously a bad cluster).
- **A non-contiguous file** is normal in FAT (and NTFS) — this is *file fragmentation*.
- **Forensic note:** If the file were "deleted," the directory entry's first byte would be `0xE5`. The FAT entries, however, may still hold the chain — this is the foundation of *file carving*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: NTFS MFT File Size from Data Runs

> **Scenario:** An NTFS file has a `$DATA` attribute with the following data runs (cluster-based, from the MFT record):
> `0x22 0x30 0x50 0x10` → decoded as: start at cluster `0x30` (48), length `0x50` (80) clusters; then `0x11 0x20` → relative offset, length `0x20` (32) clusters.

The data run encoding is: `(length_field_size : offset_field_size : length : offset)` repeated.

### Step 1: First run
- Length field: 1 byte → `0x50` = **80 clusters**
- Offset field: 1 byte → `0x30` = **48 clusters** (absolute start = 48)

### Step 2: Second run
- Length field: 1 byte → `0x20` = **32 clusters**
- Offset field: 1 byte → `0x10` = **16 clusters** (relative offset from previous run end)

$$\text{Run 2 absolute start} = 48 + 80 + 16 = 144 \text{ clusters}$$

### Step 3: Total size.

$$S_{\text{file}} = (80 + 32) \times S_{\text{cluster}}$$

$$\text{If } S_{\text{cluster}} = 4096 \text{ bytes (typical NTFS)}$$

$$S_{\text{file}} = 112 \times 4096 = 458752 \text{ bytes} = 448 \text{ KiB}$$

### Step 4: Forensic implication.
- **Two data runs** = the file is **fragmented** (non-contiguous on disk).
- For **sparse files** or **resident files** (small files kept in MFT), the `$DATA` attribute may be marked *resident* — no cluster allocation needed, and the file content is stored inside the MFT record itself. This is a critical forensic distinction.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Image Hash Verification (Chain of Custody Integrity)

> **Scenario:** An investigator acquires a 500 GB disk image. The MD5 hash of the source device and image are:

$$H_{\text{source}} = \text{MD5}(\text{Source}) = \text{a1b2c3d4e5f6a7b8c9d0e1f2a3b4c5d6}$$
$$H_{\text{image}} = \text{MD5}(\text{Image}) = \text{a1b2c3d4e5f6a7b8c9d0e1f2a3b4c5d6}$$

### Step 1: Compare hashes.

$$H_{\text{source}} \stackrel{?}{=} H_{\text{image}}$$

Since the hashes are identical, the bit-stream image is **forensically sound** (1:1 copy).

### Step 2: SHA-256 cross-verification (best practice — two independent hash functions).

$$H_{\text{source, SHA256}} = \text{SHA256}(\text{Source})$$
$$H_{\text{image, SHA256}} = \text{SHA256}(\text{Image})$$

Both must match. The probability of a collision is:

$$P_{\text{collision, MD5}} \approx \frac{1}{2^{128}} \quad \text{(birthday bound, MD5 broken since 2004)}$$
$$P_{\text{collision, SHA256}} \approx \frac{1}{2^{128}} \quad \text{(practically negligible)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Standard:** Use **at least two independent hash algorithms** (e.g., MD5 + SHA-1, or SHA-1 + SHA-256) per NIST SP 800-86 guidelines. This dual-hash provides redundancy in case one algorithm is later broken.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Forensic Investigation Decision Tree

| Step | Question | Tool / Artifact | Forensic Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | What device? | Inventory sheet | Tag, label, photograph |
| 2 | Is it live or powered off? | Power state | If live: triage; if off: acquire |
| 3 | Live volatile data? | RAM, network conn, processes | `winpmem`, `netstat`, `pslist` |
| 4 | Disk or mobile? | Form factor | Choose acquisition method |
| 5 | Acquire image | Write-blocker + `dd`/`FTK Imager` | Bit-stream copy, hash both sides |
| 6 | Verify image | MD5 + SHA-1 | Hash match → integrity OK |
| 7 | Parse file system | `fls`, `MFT parser` | Recover files, slack, unallocated |
| 8 | Recover deleted | Carving (scalpel, photorec) | File signature search |
| 9 | Timeline | `log2timeline/plaso` | Build super-timeline |
| 10 | Report | Examiner's report | Document chain of custody |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Memory vs. Disk Forensics

| Property | Memory Forensics | Disk Forensics |
|---|---|---|
| Volatility | Highly volatile (lost on power-off) | Persistent |
| Data Captured | Process state, encryption keys, decrypted buffers, network sockets | Files, metadata, deleted artifacts, slack |
| Tools | Volatility, Rekall | Sleuth Kit, Autopsy, EnCase, FTK |
| Acquisition | `winpmem`, `LiME`, `AVML` | `dd`, `FTK Imager`, `dcfldd` |
| Use Case | Malware analysis, incident response | Post-mortem investigation, e-discovery |
| Court Acceptance | Higher (preserves volatile state) | Standard (well-established) |
| Challenge | RAM is constantly changing; need page-by-page atomicity | Deleted file recovery, encrypted volumes |

---

## 3.6 Exhaustive Windows CLI Workflow Example (PowerShell-based Live Triage)

> **Scenario:** A Windows 10 machine is suspected of compromise. The investigator boots to a USB-based WinPE environment and runs PowerShell.

### Step 1: Acquire memory.

$$\text{Command: `.\winpmem_mini_x64_rc2.exe E:\evidence\mem.raw`}$$

This dumps physical RAM to `E:\evidence\mem.raw`. Hash immediately:

$$\text{Get-FileHash E:\evidence\mem.raw -Algorithm SHA256}$$

### Step 2: Acquire the pagefile (a disk-resident memory artifact).

$$\text{copy C:\pagefile.sys E:\evidence\pagefile.sys}$$

### Step 3: Mount the system drive **read-only** in the forensic environment.

$$\text{Get-Volume -DriveLetter C \mid Set-Volume -IsReadOnly \$true}$$

### Step 4: Parse the registry hives.

$$\text{reg load HKLM\TempSys C:\Windows\System32\config\SYSTEM}$$
$$\text{reg query "HKLM\TempSys\Select" /v Current}$$

### Step 5: Enumerate recent USB device connections.

$$\text{reg query "HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USBSTOR" /s}$$

### Step 6: Export event logs.

$$\text{wevtutil epl Security E:\evidence\Security.evtx}$
$$\text{wevtutil epl System E:\evidence\System.evtx}$$
$$\text{wevtutil epl Application E:\evidence\Application.evtx}$$

### Step 7: Hash all artifacts.

$$\text{Get-ChildItem E:\evidence\ \mid Get-FileHash -Algorithm SHA256 \mid Export-Csv E:\hashes.csv}$$

### Step 8: Document and package.

The output `hashes.csv` becomes the **chain of custody receipt** and is admissible in court.

---

## 3.7 Forensic Hash Collision Probability (Worked Math)

> **Question:** A forensic image is hashed with both MD5 and SHA-1. What is the probability of an accidental collision in **both** algorithms simultaneously?

For MD5: $2^{128}$ possible outputs. For SHA-1: $2^{160}$ possible outputs.

The probability of an *accidental* collision in *both* (assuming independence, which is a reasonable worst-case forensic assumption) is:

$$P_{\text{both}} = P_{\text{MD5 collision}} \times P_{\text{SHA1 collision}}$$

$$P_{\text{both}} = \frac{1}{2^{128}} \times \frac{1}{2^{160}} = \frac{1}{2^{288}}$$

$$2^{288} \approx 4.97 \times 10^{86}$$

This is astronomically larger than the estimated number of atoms in the observable universe ($\sim 10^{80}$), making accidental dual collision **physically impossible**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Dual-hash verification is the **forensic gold standard** — it is computationally and physically infeasible to defeat by random collision.
