# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`
**Define the term "Slack Space" in the context of FAT file system forensics. Why is it significant?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Slack Space** is the area between the **end of a file's logical content** and the **end of the last cluster** allocated to that file. For example, if a file is 7000 bytes long on a volume with 4096-byte clusters, it occupies two clusters (8192 bytes), leaving **1192 bytes of slack space**.

- **File Slack** = difference between end of file and end of last cluster.
- **Drive/RAM Slack** = the space between the end of the original sector written and the next sector boundary within the last cluster.

**Significance:** Slack space often contains **residual data from prior file occupants**, deleted file fragments, and even **memory pages** (RAM slack) that may reveal information the user thought was deleted or never saved. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`
**What is an Alternate Data Stream (ADS) in NTFS? Give one forensic use case.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Alternate Data Stream (ADS)** is a feature of NTFS that allows a file to have multiple **independent data payloads** under the same filename. Each file has a default (unnamed) `$DATA` stream and may have one or more **named streams** (e.g., `resume.docx:metadata`).

**Forensic Use Case:** When users download files via Internet Explorer or Edge, NTFS automatically attaches a **Zone.Identifier** ADS (e.g., `:Zone.Identifier`) indicating the file's origin security zone (Internet, Local Intranet, Trusted). This **proves the provenance** of a file — critical in proving that a malicious file was downloaded from the web rather than created locally. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the structure of the **FAT32 file system** with a labelled diagram. List the four primary regions of a FAT32 volume.

**(b) [7 marks]** A FAT32 volume has Bytes-per-Sector = 512, Sectors-per-Cluster = 16, Reserved Sectors = 32, Number of FATs = 2, and FAT Size = 4096 sectors. Calculate the **byte offset of cluster 250** on the disk.

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — FAT32 Structure [7 marks]

A FAT32 volume is logically divided into **four regions** in the following order:

1. **Reserved Region** — Contains the Boot Sector (BPB) at sector 0, FSINFO at sector 1, and a backup of the boot sector (typically at sector 6). It spans `Reserved Sectors` count of sectors. (1 mark)

2. **FAT Region** — Contains **two copies** of the File Allocation Table (FAT1 and FAT2), each occupying `FAT Size` sectors. The FAT is the master cluster-chain map. (1 mark)

3. **Data Region (Root Directory Area)** — In FAT32, the **root directory is a cluster chain** (not a fixed table). The cluster number of the root directory is stored in the BPB field `Root Cluster` (offset `0x2C`). (1 mark)

4. **Data Area (Cluster Heap)** — All other files and directories exist here as cluster chains. **Cluster numbering starts at 2**; clusters 0 and 1 are reserved. (1 mark)

**Labelled Diagram:** *(3 marks)*

```
| Reserved Sectors | FAT1 | FAT2 | Cluster 2 (Root) | Cluster 3 | ... | Cluster N |
        |              |       |        |                |          |          |
    Boot Sector      Cluster-chain map     Files / Directories (variable length)
    (BPB, FSINFO)    (mirrored copies)    
```

**Key forensic points:**
- The boot sector's BPB reveals all geometry (sector size, cluster size, FAT size).
- Two FAT copies are used for redundancy — if FAT1 is corrupted, the OS falls back to FAT2.
- Deletion in FAT32: the directory entry's first byte becomes `0xE5` and the FAT chain is zeroed, but data persists.

---

#### Part (b) — Cluster Offset Calculation [7 marks]

**Given:**
- Bytes-per-Sector ($B$) = 512
- Sectors-per-Cluster ($S$) = 16
- Reserved Sectors ($R$) = 32
- Number of FATs ($F$) = 2
- FAT Size ($F_s$) = 4096 sectors
- Target Cluster ($C_n$) = 250

**Step 1 — Cluster Size** [1 mark]

$$C_{size} = B \cdot S = 512 \cdot 16 = 8192 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2 — Data Region Start (sectors)** [1 mark]

$$D_{region} = R + (F \cdot F_s) = 32 + (2 \cdot 4096) = 32 + 8192 = 8224 \text{ sectors}$$

**Step 3 — Data Region Start (bytes)** [1 mark]

$$D_{region_{bytes}} = 8224 \cdot 512 = 4{,}210{,}688 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Cluster 250 Offset** [3 marks]

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{offset} &= D_{region_{bytes}} + (C_n - 2) \cdot C_{size} \\
&= 4{,}210{,}688 + (250 - 2) \cdot 8192 \\
&= 4{,}210{,}688 + 248 \cdot 8192 \\
&= 4{,}210{,}688 + 2{,}031{,}616 \\
&= 6{,}242{,}304 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Cluster 250 begins at byte offset **6,242,304** on the disk.

**Verification:** Sector offset = $6{,}242{,}304 \div 512 = 12{,}192$ sectors. Cluster index = $(12{,}192 - 8224) \div 16 = 3968 \div 16 = 248$ → 248th usable cluster = cluster 250. ✓

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **NTFS Master File Table (MFT)** with its record structure. List any **six important system MFT entries** with their purpose.

**(b) [7 marks]** Compare FAT and NTFS file systems on the basis of: (i) metadata mechanism, (ii) journaling, (iii) access control, (iv) alternate data streams, (v) maximum file size, (vi) recovery of deleted files, (vii) forensic complexity.

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — NTFS MFT [7 marks]

The **Master File Table ($MFT)** is the cornerstone of NTFS. It is itself a file (`$MFT`, MFT entry 0) and is composed of **1024-byte records** (one per file/directory on the volume).

**MFT Record Structure** [3 marks]

- **Record Header (48 bytes)** — contains the signature `FILE`, the MFT entry number, flags (in-use / directory), and update sequence info.
- **Update Sequence Array** — for crash-consistency verification.
- **Attribute Sequence** — a flexible list of attribute records. Each attribute begins with a 16-byte header (type, length, non-resident flag) and contains attribute-specific data.
- **End Marker** — `0xFFFFFFFF` marks the end of the attribute list.
- **Unused Space** — padding to reach 1024 bytes.

**Six Critical System MFT Entries** [4 marks — list 6 × ~0.67 each]

| MFT Entry | Name | Purpose |
|-----------|------|---------|
| `0` | `$MFT` | The MFT itself |
| `1` | `$MFTMirr` | Backup of first 4 MFT records |
| `2` | `$LogFile` | NTFS journal for metadata transactions |
| `3` | `$Volume` | Volume metadata (label, version) |
| `4` | `$Defrag` | Used by defragmentation |
| `5` | `$Root` | Root directory index |
| `6` | `$Bitmap` | Cluster allocation map |
| `7` | `$Boot` | Boot sector backup |
| `8` | `$BadClus` | Bad-cluster list |
| `9` | `$Secure` | Security descriptors |
| `10` | `$UpCase` | Uppercase character mapping |

---

#### Part (b) — FAT vs NTFS Comparison [7 marks]

| Aspect | FAT | NTFS |
|--------|-----|------|
| **(i) Metadata mechanism** | 32-byte directory entries, fixed structure | 1024-byte MFT records with flexible attribute list |
| **(ii) Journaling** | None | Yes (`$LogFile` for metadata, `$UsnJrnl` for file-level changes) |
| **(iii) Access control** | No native ACLs (relies on share-level permissions) | Full Windows ACL model with per-file ACEs |
| **(iv) Alternate Data Streams** | Not supported | Supported (multiple `$DATA` streams per file) |
| **(v) Maximum file size** | 4 GB $-$ 1 byte (FAT32) | 16 TiB $-$ 1 KiB (practical); $2^{44} - 2^{6}$ bytes theoretical |
| **(vi) Recovery of deleted files** | Trivial (restore FAT chain + directory entry, or carve from data region) | Moderate (requires MFT record + intact cluster runs; MFT parser like `analyzeMFT`) |
| **(vii) Forensic complexity** | Low — simple linear structures; tools like `fatcat` suffice | High — B-tree indices, attribute parsing, journal analysis, ADS inspection |

**[Award 1 mark per comparison row × 7 rows = 7 marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Cluster numbering starts at 2** in FAT — forgetting the `-2` in the formula costs **2 marks** outright in numerical questions.
> 2. **Boundary confusion:** "Cluster count > 65,524" is FAT32, NOT "> 65,525" (which is just a number, not a rule).
> 3. **ADS misconception:** ADS data is **NOT counted in the file's reported size** by legacy tools. Always parse both unnamed and named `$DATA` streams.
> 4. **MFT entry zeroing:** MFT entries for deleted files are **marked unused, not wiped**. Recovery is possible until that entry is reused.
> 5. **Boot sector backup location** in FAT32 is **sector 6**, not sector 0 of the partition.
> 6. **Don't confuse `$LogFile` (metadata journal) with `$UsnJrnl` (file-level change journal)** — examiners love this trap.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2: Examining FAT and NTFS Disks**

- ✅ **FAT is a simple, table-based file system** with four regions: Reserved → FAT(s) → Root → Data.
- ✅ **FAT type thresholds:** `≤4084` clusters = FAT12; `4085–65524` = FAT16; `>65524` = FAT32.
- ✅ **Cluster numbering begins at 2** (clusters 0 and 1 are reserved).
- ✅ **Deletion in FAT** zeroes the FAT chain and overwrites the first byte of the directory entry with `0xE5` — **data persists**.
- ✅ **Slack space** is the residual area in the last cluster of a file — a major forensic artifact source.
- ✅ **NTFS uses a 1024-byte MFT record per file** with a flexible attribute list (`$SI`, `$FN`, `$DATA`, etc.).
- ✅ **Alternate Data Streams (ADS)** are NTFS-only and can hide arbitrary data after a colon in the filename (e.g., `file.txt:secret`).
- ✅ **The `$MFT` is MFT entry 0** — the first 16 entries are reserved for system metadata.
- ✅ **Timestamps in `$STANDARD_INFORMATION`** (MACE: Modified, Accessed, Created, Entry-modified) are key forensic timeline sources.
- ✅ **Journal files (`$LogFile`, `$UsnJrnl`)** record metadata operations and are valuable for reconstructing user activity.
- ✅ **Cluster offset formula:** $C_{offset} = (R + F \cdot F_s) \cdot B + (C_n - 2) \cdot (S \cdot B)$.
- ✅ **FAT32 max file size = 4 GB $-$ 1 byte**; **NTFS max ≈ 16 TiB** (practical).
- ✅ **Forensic acquisition must use a write-blocker** and produce a bit-stream image (`.E01`, `.DD`, or `.raw`) with hash verification.
- ✅ **The Sleuth Kit (TSK)** commands `mmls`, `fsstat`, `fls`, `icat`, `istat` are KTU-recommended tools for FAT/NTFS examination.
- ✅ **Autopsy** is the GUI front-end to TSK and supports both FAT and NTFS examination workflows.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Whole Disk Encryption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT602 - CYBER FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Whole Disk Encryption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Whole Disk Encryption (WDE)**, also known as **Full Disk Encryption (FDE)**, is a comprehensive data-protection mechanism in which **every sector** of a storage medium — including the operating system files, system registry, hibernation files, swap space, application binaries, and end-user data — is transformed into ciphertext using a symmetric block cipher, typically **AES-128** or **AES-256** operating in **XTS mode (IEEE 1619)**.

In a KTU 2024 Scheme context, WDE is treated as a **counter-forensic control** that an examiner must defeat (with proper legal authority) to access the *plaintext* contents of an encrypted volume during a forensic acquisition.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Locked House" Model

> [!NOTE]  
> **Analogy:** Imagine your house has **every single room, every cupboard, and even the foundation blueprint** locked behind a single master key.  
> - A normal password only locks the *front door* (file-level access).  
> - **Whole Disk Encryption** wraps the **entire house in a steel vault** so that without the key, even the *floor plan* and the *cement composition* of the walls are unreadable.  
> - When power is OFF, the disk reads as **random noise (ciphertext)** to anyone without the key — including forensic tools.

| Term | Plain-English Meaning | KTU Tag |
|------|-----------------------|---------|
| Plaintext | Original readable data | "Data at rest in clear" |
| Ciphertext | Scrambled, unreadable data | "Data at rest encrypted" |
| Encryption Key | The "master vault key" | Symmetric key, typically **128 / 256 bits** |
| Pre-Boot Authentication (PBA) | Entering the password **before** the OS loads | Mandatory in almost all WDE products |
| TPM | A hardware chip that **guards** the key | Trusted Platform Module, versions **1.2 / 2.0** |

### 1.3 Why Whole Disk Encryption Matters in Cyber Forensics

- **Default State**: Modern OSs ship with WDE ON by default (Windows BitLocker, macOS FileVault 2, Android FBE).
- **Anti-Forensics Threat**: A powered-off encrypted disk gives the examiner **no usable plaintext** — the **Cold-Boot Attack Window** is the only chance to grab keys from volatile RAM.
- **Scope of Investigation**: The forensic examiner must identify **which encryption product** is in use **before** deciding the acquisition strategy (live vs. dead, hardware vs. software).
- **Standard Metric**: Modern WDE solutions use a **256-bit AES key** — providing **2²⁵⁶ ≈ 1.16 × 10⁷⁷** possible combinations, which is computationally infeasible to brute-force with current technology.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Architecture of Whole Disk Encryption

WDE is not a single algorithm — it is a **stack of processes** that work together:

1. **Sector-Level Granularity** — Encryption is applied per **512-byte** (legacy) or **4096-byte (4Kn)** disk sector.
2. **Transparent Operation** — Once the user authenticates at boot, the OS sees a normal unencrypted volume. Decryption happens in real time inside the disk controller or driver.
3. **Independent Sector Encryption** — Each sector uses a **unique tweak (initialization vector)** derived from the sector's logical block address (LBA). This means two sectors with identical plaintext produce **different ciphertexts** — a property known as **semantic security**.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The tweak prevents **pattern leakage**. Without it, an attacker who knows one sector's plaintext could locate all duplicates across the disk.

### 2.2 The XTS Mode of Operation (IEEE 1619 Standard)

**XTS (XEX-based Tweaked codebook mode with ciphertext Stealing)** is the gold-standard block-cipher mode for disk encryption. It uses **two independent keys**:

- **Key1 (K₁)** → Used for the AES round operations.
- **Key2 (K₂)** → Used to compute the **tweak** α, where α = K₂ ⊗ Sector_Index.

This dual-key structure means an attacker must break **two keys simultaneously** to forge or substitute sectors.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Notation & Units | Engineering Use |
|---------|----------------------|------------------|-----------------|
| Total Keyspace | $K_{total} = 2^{n}$ where $n \in \{128, 256\}$ | $n$ = key length in **bits** | Measures brute-force resistance |
| Sector Tweak | $T_j = E_{K_2}(j) \otimes \alpha^{j}$ | $j$ = sector index, $\alpha$ = primitive element of $\text{GF}(2^{128})$ | Generates unique IV per sector |
| AES Round Count | $N_r = 10$ (AES-128), $12$ (AES-192), $14$ (AES-256) | dimensionless rounds | Determines encryption throughput |
| Block Size | $B_s = 128 \text{ bits} = 16 \text{ bytes}$ | fixed by AES standard | Input/output quantum |
| Disk Throughput Penalty | $\Delta T \approx 1\% \text{ to } 5\%$ | compared to plaintext I/O | Performance impact of WDE |
| Cold-Boot Time Window | $t_{cb} \le 60 \text{ seconds typical}$ | RAM decay time | Forensic acquisition window |
| Entropy of Passphrase | $H = L \cdot \log_2(N)$ | $L$ = length, $N$ = charset size | Password strength |
| BitLocker Key Lengths | $128$ or $256$ **bits** | symmetric AES key | Default Windows WDE key |
| TPM PCR Hashes | $\text{PCR} = \text{SHA-1 or SHA-256 of boot state}$ | 0–23 PCR registers | Sealed key protection |
| LUKS Header Size | $1\text{ MiB} \approx 1{,}048{,}576$ **bytes** | fixed metadata area | Where to find encryption parameters |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise Compliance**: HIPAA, PCI-DSS 4.0, and GDPR Article 32 mandate encryption of all data at rest — WDE satisfies this in **one stroke**.
- **Mobile Forensics**: Android File-Based Encryption (FBE) and iOS Data Protection are **direct descendants** of WDE.
- **Incident Response**: WDE complicates **dead-disk forensics** but enables **secure disposal** — destroying the key = destroying the data.
- **Self-Encrypting Drives (SEDs)**: Hardware WDE in SSDs/HDDs uses an **on-board AES engine**, leaving no performance penalty and providing instant cryptographic erase on decommission.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The XTS-AES Encryption Equation

We derive the **XTS encryption** of plaintext sector $P_j$ (where $j$ is the sector index) into ciphertext sector $C_j$.

**Step 1 — Compute the Tweak**

The tweak $T_j$ is generated by encrypting the sector number $j$ with the second key $K_2$ and then multiplying by $\alpha^j$ in the Galois Field $\text{GF}(2^{128})$:

$$
T_j = E_{K_2}(j) \otimes \alpha^{j}
$$

where $\alpha$ is the primitive polynomial element of $\text{GF}(2^{128})$, and $\otimes$ denotes multiplication in that field.

**Step 2 — XOR Plaintext with Tweak (First Pass)**

$$
PP_j = P_j \oplus T_j
$$

This step **removes the tweak** from the plaintext before encryption — it ensures that identical plaintext blocks at different sector addresses produce different ciphertexts.

**Step 3 — AES Block-Cipher Encryption with K₁**

$$
CC_j = E_{K_1}(PP_j)
$$

The standard AES algorithm is applied — either **10, 12, or 14 rounds** depending on the key size (128, 192, or 256 bits).

**Step 4 — XOR Ciphertext with Tweak (Second Pass)**

$$
C_j = CC_j \oplus T_j
$$

The tweak is XORed back into the result so the receiver can reverse the process symmetrically.

**Step 5 — Final XTS Encryption Equation**

$$
\boxed{\,C_j = E_{K_1}\!\bigl(P_j \oplus T_j\bigr) \oplus T_j\,}
$$

This single equation is the **heart of every WDE implementation** using XTS-AES.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Decryption

To recover plaintext, we apply the inverse operations:

**Step 1 — Recompute the Tweak (same as encryption)**

$$
T_j = E_{K_2}(j) \otimes \alpha^{j}
$$

**Step 2 — XOR Ciphertext with Tweak**

$$
CC_j = C_j \oplus T_j
$$

**Step 3 — AES Decryption with K₁**

$$
PP_j = D_{K_1}(CC_j)
$$

**Step 4 — XOR with Tweak to Recover Plaintext**

$$
\boxed{\,P_j = D_{K_1}\!\bigl(C_j \oplus T_j\bigr) \oplus T_j\,}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Brute-Force Keyspace Size

For an AES-256 key, the number of possible keys is:

$$
N_{keys} = 2^{256}
$$

Using the approximation $\log_{10}(2) \approx 0.30103$:

$$
\log_{10}\!\bigl(2^{256}\bigr) = 256 \times 0.30103 \approx 77.06
$$

Therefore:

$$
N_{keys} \approx 10^{77.06} \approx 1.16 \times 10^{77}
$$

Even at a hypothetical **10¹⁸ keys/second** (≈ one exaflop GPU cluster):

$$
t_{bf} = \frac{10^{77}}{10^{18} \times 3.15 \times 10^{7}} \approx 3.17 \times 10^{51} \text{ years}
$$

This is **10⁴¹ times the age of the universe** — proving AES-256 is computationally infeasible to brute-force.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Software vs. Hardware WDE

| Attribute | Software WDE | Hardware WDE (SED) |
|-----------|--------------|--------------------|
| Crypto Engine | CPU / OS driver | Disk controller ASIC |
| Performance Penalty | **1% – 5%** I/O slowdown | **0%** (negligible) |
| Key Storage | TPM chip or software | On-disk, locked by PSID |
| Cryptographic Erase | Reformats header | Single-key wipe, instant |
| Common Examples | BitLocker, FileVault 2, LUKS, VeraCrypt | Samsung SED, Seagate ISE, Opal 2.0 |
| Forensic Access Path | Memory dump + key extraction | PSID physical attack or escrow key |
| Cost | Free | Slightly higher drive cost |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 WDE Boot-Time Authentication Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Power On"] --> B["BIOS UEFI Initializes Hardware"]
    B --> C{"TPM Present and Active"}
    C -- "Yes" --> D["TPM Releases Sealed Key to Decrypt Volume Master Key"]
    C -- "No" --> E["User Enters Pre Boot Authentication PIN or Password"]
    E --> D
    D --> F["Volume Master Key Decrypts Each Sector On Demand"]
    F --> G["Operating System Bootloader Loads in Plaintext"]
    G --> H["Full Disk Now Presented to OS as Plaintext Volume"]
    H --> I["User Session Active"]
    F --> J["Forensic Image Acquisition Can Begin if Live System"]
    I --> K["Power Loss or Shutdown"]
    K --> L["All Sectors Revert to Ciphertext on Disk"]
```

### 4.2 Layered Architecture of WDE

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1["Application Layer - User Apps and OS"]
        A1["Word Processor Browser Email Client"]
        A2["File System NTFS ext4 APFS"]
    end

    subgraph L2["Encryption Filter Driver Layer"]
        B1["WDE Filter Driver BitLocker LUKS FileVault"]
        B2["AES XTS Engine in Software or Hardware"]
    end

    subgraph L3["Storage Hardware Layer"]
        C1["Disk Controller with Optional SED Crypto Engine"]
        C2["Physical Storage Medium HDD SSD NVMe"]
    end

    subgraph L4["Key Custody Layer"]
        D1["TPM Chip Seals the Master Key"]
        D2["User Password or Smart Card or Recovery Key"]
    end

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    D1 --> B2
    D2 --> B1
```

### 4.3 Forensic Attack Path Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Suspect Disk Seized in Powered Off State"] --> Q1{"Is Disk Encrypted"}
    Q1 -- "No" --> P1["Acquire with dd or FTK Imager Plaintext Image"]
    Q1 -- "Yes" --> Q2{"Is Disk a Self Encrypting Drive"}
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> P2["Attempt PSID Physical Attack or Vendor Escrow Key"]
    Q2 -- "No" --> Q3{"Was System Running at Seizure"}
    Q3 -- "Yes" --> P3["Dump RAM Immediately with WinPmem LiME or AVML"]
    P3 --> P4["Extract Volume Master Key from Memory"]
    Q3 -- "No" --> Q4{"Do You Possess Legal Authority and Suspect Password"}
    Q4 -- "Yes" --> P5["Suspect Provides Password at PBA"]
    Q4 -- "No" --> P6["Search for Key Material in Cloud Backups TPM Sniffing or Cold Boot"]
    P4 --> E["Decrypted Image Ready for Analysis"]
    P2 --> E
    P5 --> E
    P6 --> E
    P1 --> E
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Mermaid Legend:** All node identifiers follow the alphanumeric-prefix rule (e.g., `Q1`, `P3`). Reserved words like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph` are NEVER used as standalone node IDs. Labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text inside double quotes — no markdown bold, italics, or HTML tables.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`  
**Define Whole Disk Encryption. List any two advantages.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Whole Disk Encryption (WDE) is the process of encrypting **every sector** of a storage device — including the OS, applications, swap space, and user data — using a symmetric block cipher such as AES-128 or AES-256 operating in XTS mode, so that without the correct key, all data on the disk is rendered unreadable ciphertext.

**Advantages (any two, 1½ marks each):**

1. **Comprehensive Coverage** — Protects operating system files, swap space, hibernation files, and temporary data which are often overlooked by selective file-level encryption.
2. **Transparent to the User** — Once unlocked at boot, performance overhead is **1–5%** with software WDE and near-zero with hardware SEDs.
3. **Regulatory Compliance** — Single-step compliance with HIPAA, PCI-DSS, and GDPR Article 32.

---

**Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`  
**What is the role of a TPM chip in Whole Disk Encryption?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Trusted Platform Module (TPM)** is a hardware security chip soldered onto the motherboard that **stores and seals the Volume Master Key (VMK)** of a WDE implementation.

**Three key functions (1 mark each):**

1. **Sealing** — The TPM releases the VMK **only if specific Platform Configuration Registers (PCRs)** match the expected boot state, preventing disk transplantation attacks.
2. **Key Storage** — The master key never leaves the TPM in plaintext form, protecting it against purely software key-stealing malware.
3. **Anti-Tampering** — If an attacker boots from a USB or modifies the bootloader, the PCRs change and the TPM refuses to unseal the key, keeping the disk encrypted.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Areas:**  
> 1. Students **write "Whole Disk Encryption" but skip the operating mode (XTS)** — examiners deduct **2 marks** for incomplete algorithm specification.  
> 2. In diagrams, students **omit the tweak generation step** — the IEEE 1619 tweak is mandatory in the XTS-AES explanation and carries **3 marks** on its own.  
> 3. For forensic challenges, students list BitLocker only — full-credit answers mention **at least three products** (BitLocker, FileVault, LUKS, VeraCrypt, VeraCrypt rescue disk, eCryptfs).  
> 4. Failing to mention the **Cold-Boot Attack** as the primary forensic countermeasure loses **2 marks**.

---

#### **Question B-A (14 Marks) — OPTION 1**

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`**  
**(a) Explain the architecture and operational flow of Whole Disk Encryption with a neat block diagram. Discuss how AES-XTS mode provides semantic security at the sector level. (7 marks)**  
**(b) Discuss the forensic challenges posed by WDE and the standard methods used by investigators to defeat or bypass encryption during evidence acquisition. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer B-A (a) — 7 marks:**

**Architecture (3 marks):**  

WDE sits as a **filter driver layer** between the operating system's file system and the physical storage hardware. The architecture consists of four layers:

| Layer | Function |
|-------|----------|
| Application Layer | User apps read/write files normally |
| Filter Driver Layer | Intercepts all I/O requests, encrypts/decrypts on the fly |
| Storage Controller | Manages physical sectors (with optional SED AES engine) |
| Key Custody Layer | TPM + User Password/Smart Card + Recovery Key |

**Operational Flow (2 marks):**

1. At boot, the user enters a **Pre-Boot Authentication (PBA)** password.
2. The TPM validates boot integrity via **PCR values 0–7**.
3. On successful validation, the TPM **unseals the Volume Master Key (VMK)**.
4. The VMK is handed to the AES-XTS engine which begins **on-demand per-sector decryption**.
5. Each sector read from disk yields its **plaintext** to the OS; each sector written is **re-encrypted** before commit.

**AES-XTS Semantic Security (2 marks):**

- **Two independent keys** K₁ and K₂ are used (total = 256 or 512 bits).
- The **tweak** $T_j = E_{K_2}(j) \otimes \alpha^j$ ensures every sector has a **unique IV**.
- Identical plaintext blocks at different sector indices produce **completely different ciphertext blocks** — eliminating pattern leakage and providing **IND-CPA security** (indistinguishability under chosen-plaintext attack).

---

**Model Answer B-A (b) — 7 marks:**

**Forensic Challenges (3 marks):**

1. **Powered-Off Disk = Ciphertext Only** — No keys in memory means no decryption possible without the suspect's password.
2. **Anti-Forensic Design** — Tools like VeraCrypt offer **plausible deniability** via hidden volumes.
3. **Key Destruction Risk** — A suspect can trigger a **panic mode** that wipes headers and keys.
4. **TPM Sealing** — Moving the disk to another machine changes the PCRs and locks the key.

**Defeat Methods (4 marks):**

| Method | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| **Live Acquisition** | Capture RAM with WinPmem, LiME, AVML — extract VMK from kernel memory. |
| **Cold-Boot Attack** | Cool DRAM with compressed air, reboot into forensic Linux, dump residual key bits. |
| **Pre-Boot Interception** | Boot from forensic USB, capture PBA password at input level. |
| **TPM Sniffing** | Hardware tap on TPM SPI bus to intercept key release. |
| **Key Escrow / Recovery Key** | Subpoena Microsoft/Apple/Enterprise AD for escrow BitLocker recovery keys. |
| **PSID Physical Attack** | For SEDs, use the **Physical Secure ID** printed on the drive label to invoke crypto-erase or unlock. |

---

#### **Question B-B (14 Marks) — OPTION 2**

**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`**  
**(a) Differentiate between Software-based and Hardware-based Whole Disk Encryption. Compare their performance, security, and forensic implications in a tabular format. (7 marks)**  
**(b) Explain the IEEE 1619 XTS-AES mode of operation. Derive the mathematical expression used to encrypt a single disk sector and explain why the tweak value prevents pattern leakage. (7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer B-B (a) — 7 marks:**

**Definitions (2 marks):**

- **Software WDE** — Encryption engine runs as a CPU/OS driver. Examples: BitLocker, FileVault 2, LUKS, VeraCrypt.
- **Hardware WDE (SED)** — Encryption engine is built into the disk controller ASIC per the **Opal 2.0 / IEEE 1667** specification. Examples: Samsung SSD 870 EVO (SED variant), Seagate Barracuda ISE.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Software WDE | Hardware WDE (SED) |
|-----------|--------------|---------------------|
| Crypto Location | Host CPU | On-drive ASIC |
| Performance Overhead | **1–5%** | **0%** |
| Key Storage | TPM chip + password | Hidden in NAND, locked by PSID |
| Cryptographic Erase | Reformats LUKS header | Instant — destroys single media key |
| Anti-Evaporation | OS controls key release | Drive firmware gates key release |
| Forensic Difficulty | RAM dump can extract VMK | Requires PSID or hardware tap on drive firmware bus |
| Cost | Free | Marginal hardware premium |

**Conclusion (2 marks):**  
Hardware SEDs offer superior performance and instant sanitization, but are **forensically harder to defeat** because the key never leaves the drive's crypto-engine boundary. Software WDE is more common in consumer/enterprise laptops but leaves **memory-based attack vectors** open.

---

**Model Answer B-B (b) — 7 marks:**

**IEEE 1619 XTS-AES Explanation (3 marks):**

The **IEEE 1619-2018** standard specifies XTS-AES as the **mandatory block-cipher mode for sector-level storage encryption**. It uses:

- **Two AES keys**: K₁ (data encryption), K₂ (tweak generation).
- **One tweak** derived from the sector's logical block address (LBA).
- **No expansion of ciphertext length** — output is exactly the same size as input.

**Mathematical Derivation (3 marks):**

For sector $j$ with plaintext $P_j$:

$$
T_j = E_{K_2}(j) \otimes \alpha^{j}
$$

$$
C_j = E_{K_1}\!\bigl(P_j \oplus T_j\bigr) \oplus T_j
$$

Where:
- $T_j$ = tweak for sector $j$
- $\alpha$ = primitive element in $\text{GF}(2^{128})$
- $E_K$ = AES encryption with key $K$
- $\oplus$ = bitwise XOR

**Why Tweak Prevents Pattern Leakage (1 mark):**

Without the tweak, sectors $j$ and $k$ with identical plaintext $P_j = P_k$ would produce identical ciphertext, leaking structure (e.g., all-zero sectors in unused space would all hash the same). The tweak, which **changes with every sector index**, ensures $C_j \neq C_k$, providing full **semantic security** and preventing an attacker from fingerprinting known file signatures on the encrypted disk.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Whole Disk Encryption**

- ✅ **Definition:** Encryption of **every sector** of a disk, including OS, swap, and user data.
- ✅ **Default Algorithms:** **AES-128** or **AES-256**, in **XTS mode** (IEEE 1619).
- ✅ **Two-Key Structure of XTS:** K₁ for AES rounds, K₂ for tweak generation.
- ✅ **Tweak Formula:** $T_j = E_{K_2}(j) \otimes \alpha^j$ — unique per sector.
- ✅ **Final Encryption Equation:** $C_j = E_{K_1}(P_j \oplus T_j) \oplus T_j$.
- ✅ **TPM Role:** Seals the Volume Master Key, validates boot state via PCRs.
- ✅ **Pre-Boot Authentication:** Password entered **before** OS loads.
- ✅ **Software WDE Examples:** BitLocker, FileVault 2, LUKS, VeraCrypt, dm-crypt.
- ✅ **Hardware WDE Standard:** **Opal 2.0 / IEEE 1667** (Self-Encrypting Drives).
- ✅ **Performance Penalty:** **1–5%** (software), **0%** (hardware SED).
- ✅ **Keyspace of AES-256:** $\approx 1.16 \times 10^{77}$ — brute-force infeasible.
- ✅ **Cold-Boot Window:** $\le 60$ seconds — primary forensic opportunity for live key extraction.
- ✅ **Forensic Tools:** WinPmem, LiME, AVML (RAM dump), VeraCrypt rescue disk, FTK Imager.
- ✅ **Anti-Forensic Threat:** Plausible deniability via hidden volumes (VeraCrypt).
- ✅ **Compliance Drivers:** HIPAA, PCI-DSS 4.0, GDPR Article 32.
- ✅ **LUKS Header Size:** **1 MiB** (1,048,576 bytes) — must be preserved during acquisition.
- ✅ **Cryptographic Erase:** Single-key destruction = instant data sanitization on SEDs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Windows Registry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT602 - CYBER FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The Windows Registry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Windows Registry** is a **hierarchical, centralized database** used by the Microsoft Windows operating system to store low-level configuration settings, hardware information, software installation parameters, user preferences, and operational directives for the OS and installed applications. In the context of **Cyber Forensics**, the Registry is one of the most valuable sources of digital evidence, because it logs user activity, system state changes, recently accessed resources, and network connection artifacts that survive reboots, software updates, and partial system wipes.

From the standpoint of the **KTU 2024 PCCCT602 syllabus**, the Windows Registry is formally categorized as a **persistent volatile-data artifact repository** — meaning its contents survive power cycles (persistent) but can still be modified at runtime (volatile-access). It functions as the central nervous system of a Windows machine and is the **forensic equivalent of a digital fingerprint warehouse**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The Registry is to be studied in conjunction with File Systems, Data Carving, and Image Acquisition. Expect 14-mark ESE questions that tie Registry artifacts to broader forensic acquisition workflows.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Building's Control Room

Imagine a massive **high-rise office building**:

| Real-World Object | Windows Registry Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The building itself | The Windows Operating System |
| The **control room** in the basement | The **Registry Editor (regedit.exe)** |
| Floor directories | **Root Keys (Hives)** |
| Rooms on each floor | **Subkeys** |
| Documents inside each room | **Values (Data Entries)** |
| The building blueprint cabinet | **Registry Files (SAM, SYSTEM, SOFTWARE, etc.)** |
| The security guard at the door | **Access Control Lists (ACLs)** on keys |

Every time someone installs software, changes the desktop wallpaper, connects to a Wi-Fi network, or plugs in a USB drive, a "document" (a registry value) is filed in the appropriate "room" (subkey) of the correct "floor" (hive). When a forensic investigator enters the building, they walk straight to the control room and read every single document — recovering the complete history of activity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Forensic Insight:**
> Unlike a file system where data is intentionally created by users (documents, photos), Registry data is **automatically generated by the OS** in the background. This makes the Registry an **involuntary witness** — it records activity even when the user is actively trying to hide it.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants (Registry Vocabulary)

- **Maximum path length:** **255 characters** in a single key path
- **Maximum value name length:** **16,383 characters** (Windows 2000+)
- **Maximum value data size:** **~1 GB** per value (registry size limit)
- **Default hive size cap:** **~2 GB** for the entire system registry
- **Standard hive file format:** **Binary "regf"** (Registry Hive File)

These metrics matter in forensics because **oversized or truncated keys** often indicate tampering or malware injection.

---

## 1.4 Registry Core Components (Five Foundational Elements)

1. **Root Keys (Hives)** — The five top-level containers.
2. **Subkeys** — Folders within hives, organized hierarchically.
3. **Values (or Value Entries)** — The actual name/data pair entries.
4. **Data Types** — REG_SZ, REG_DWORD, REG_BINARY, etc.
5. **Registry Files (Hives on Disk)** — The persistent files that mirror the hives.

Each component has direct forensic implications that will be explored in the next section.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Root Keys (Hives) — The Forensic Big Five

The Windows Registry is organized into **five root keys**, each of which is a critical forensic target:

### 2.1.1 `HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT` (HKCR)
- **Purpose:** Stores file extension associations and COM object registrations.
- **Forensic Value:** Reveals which application is set to open a specific file type. Critical for identifying **mimic malware** that disguises `.exe` extensions as `.pdf`.
- **Persistence Mechanism:** Malware frequently adds entries here to hijack file associations.

### 2.1.2 `HKEY_CURRENT_USER` (HKCU)
- **Purpose:** Stores settings specific to the currently logged-in user.
- **Forensic Value:** Contains user activity, network drives, mapped printers, recent documents, typed URLs, and user-specific malware persistence.
- **Mapped From:** `HKEY_USERS\<User-SID>` of the active user.

### 2.1.3 `HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE` (HKLM)
- **Purpose:** Stores system-wide configuration: hardware, software, security policies, services.
- **Forensic Value:** Contains evidence of installed software, services, drivers, and **OS-level malware persistence** (e.g., Run keys, Image File Execution Options).
- **Persistence Mechanism:** Most commonly abused hive for malware autorun.

### 2.1.4 `HKEY_USERS` (HKU)
- **Purpose:** Contains all user profiles loaded on the system.
- **Forensic Value:** Each user's SID-keyed subkey shows the configuration of every account that has ever logged in.

### 2.1.5 `HKEY_CURRENT_CONFIG` (HKCC)
- **Purpose:** Pointer to the current hardware profile used at boot.
- **Forensic Value:** Useful for identifying hardware tampering or dual-boot configurations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When asked to identify "the most forensically valuable hive," the answer is **HKLM** for system-level malware and **HKCU** for user-level activity tracking. Always mention both.

## 2.2 Registry Hive Files on Disk

The in-memory registry is mirrored to **specific files on disk** inside `%SystemRoot%\System32\config\` and user profile folders. These are the **on-disk evidence** that forensic investigators directly acquire and analyze:

| Hive File | Live Location | Mounted Root Key | Forensic Importance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SAM** | `System32\config\SAM` | `HKLM\SAM` | User account names, password hashes, login counts, last login time |
| **SYSTEM** | `System32\config\SYSTEM` | `HKLM\SYSTEM` | Computer name, hardware config, services, drivers, USB history |
| **SOFTWARE** | `System32\config\SOFTWARE` | `HKLM\SOFTWARE` | Installed applications, OS version, network policies, autoruns |
| **SECURITY** | `System32\config\SECURITY` | `HKLM\SECURITY` | Security policies, audit configurations, account permissions |
| **DEFAULT** | `System32\config\DEFAULT` | `HKU\.DEFAULT` | Default user profile settings |
| **NTUSER.DAT** | `C:\Users\<username>\` | `HKCU` | Per-user preferences, typed URLs, recent files, MRU lists |
| **UsrClass.dat** | `C:\Users\<username>\AppData\Local\Microsoft\Windows\` | `HKCU\Software\Classes` | File associations, Jump Lists, recent items per user |

> [!NOTE]
> These hive files are **locked by the OS at runtime**. Live forensic acquisition requires either booting from a forensic USB, using Volume Shadow Copies, or leveraging tools like **FTK Imager**, **KAPE**, or **RegRipper**.

## 2.3 Registry Data Types (KTU High-Yield)

Every value stored in the Registry has a **data type**. Identifying the data type is essential for proper interpretation in forensic tools:

| Data Type | Encoding | Forensic Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **REG_SZ** | Fixed-length Unicode string | User names, file paths, descriptive labels |
| **REG_EXPAND_SZ** | Variable-length string with environment variables (`%SystemRoot%`) | Service parameters, dynamic paths |
| **REG_MULTI_SZ** | Multi-line string array | Service dependencies, network lists |
| **REG_DWORD** | 32-bit integer | Boolean flags, counters, timestamps (in some cases) |
| **REG_QWORD** | 64-bit integer | Large counters, NTFS timestamps (modern) |
| **REG_BINARY** | Raw binary | Raw data like user SID, hardware identifiers, hashes |
| **REG_NONE** | No defined type | Reserved, often seen in malware artifacts |
| **REG_RESOURCE_LIST** | Hardware resource descriptors | Plug-and-play device configurations |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Confusing `REG_SZ` with `REG_EXPAND_SZ`. The latter requires runtime expansion (e.g., `%SystemRoot%\System32`). A malware author may use this to **hide its true path** from naive string-search tools.

## 2.4 KTU Cheat Sheet — Forensically Critical Registry Keys

This is the **high-yield reference table** you must memorize for the KTU exam:

| Forensically Significant Key Path | What It Reveals |
|---|---|
| `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run` | Programs launching at system startup (autoruns) |
| `HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run` | User-level autoruns |
| `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services` | All installed services and their binaries |
| `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon` | Logon scripts, shell replacement (Shell_Hook) |
| `HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Internet Explorer\TypedURLs` | URLs typed in Internet Explorer (URL history) |
| `HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\TypedPaths` | Paths typed into Windows Explorer Run dialog |
| `HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Office\<version>\<app>\Place MRU` | Recent Office documents |
| `HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\RecentDocs` | Recently opened files |
| `HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\UserAssist` | Executable launch history (ROT-13 encoded GUIDs) |
| `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USBSTOR` | USB storage device history |
| `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USB` | All USB device history |
| `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion` | OS install date, registered owner, product ID |
| `HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\MountPoints2` | Mounted volume history (drives, network shares) |
| `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Installer\UserData` | Software installation records |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The UserAssist key** is ROT-13 encoded (a Caesar cipher) to obscure execution history. The forensic tool **RegRipper** automatically decodes it. KTU may ask you to explain the decoding process.

## 2.5 The "Why" Behind Registry Forensic Analysis

- **Why is the Registry forensically valuable?**
  Because it is **automatically written to** by the OS and applications, **without explicit user action**, creating an involuntary log of activity.

- **Why is it tamper-resistant?**
  Because hive files are **locked by the kernel** at runtime and protected by **ACLs**, requiring elevated privileges to modify.

- **Why is offline analysis preferred?**
  Because live registry acquisition can be **contaminated by running processes** that modify the registry in real-time (e.g., antivirus updates, system clock corrections).

- **How is it exploited by attackers?**
  Malware uses **Registry Run keys, Image File Execution Options (IFEO), Winlogon hijacks, and COM object hijacks** to achieve persistence across reboots.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Procedures & Forensic Workflow

> Since the Windows Registry is not a formula-based topic, the "derivation" here is replaced with **exhaustive, multi-step analytical procedures** that a forensic examiner follows — each step fully written out as KTU demands.

## 3.1 Procedure A: Identifying a Malware Persistence Mechanism via the Registry

### Problem Scenario (KTU 14-Mark Style)
*A Windows 10 machine is suspected of being infected with persistent malware. The system has been acquired using FTK Imager. Identify, with specific registry key paths, how you would determine the persistence mechanism of the malware.*

### Step 1: Acquire the Registry Hives Off-Line
**Logical reasoning:** The live registry may be modified by the running malware to erase its own footprints. The first step is to image the system and extract hive files.

$$
\text{Forensic Image} \rightarrow \text{Mount with FTK Imager} \rightarrow \text{Navigate to } \%SystemRoot\%\backslash System32\backslash config
$$

Extract the following hive files:
$$
\text{SAM, SYSTEM, SOFTWARE, SECURITY, DEFAULT}
$$

For each user, extract:
$$
\text{NTUSER.DAT from } C:\backslash Users\backslash \langle username\rangle \backslash
$$
$$
\text{UsrClass.dat from } C:\backslash Users\backslash \langle username\rangle \backslash AppData\backslash Local\backslash Microsoft\backslash Windows\backslash
$$

### Step 2: Open Hives in a Forensic Tool
**Logical reasoning:** Use a tool that reads raw hive files without needing a live OS — this prevents any contamination.

Recommended toolset:
- **Registry Explorer (Eric Zimmerman)** — Modern, view-based
- **RegRipper (Harlan Carvey)** — Automated plugin-based parser
- **FTK Registry Viewer** — Integrated with FTK suite

### Step 3: Examine HKLM\..\..\Run Keys
**Logical reasoning:** The `Run` and `RunOnce` keys are the most common malware persistence vectors. Any suspicious entry must be flagged.

$$
\text{Path: } HKLM\backslash SOFTWARE\backslash Microsoft\backslash Windows\backslash CurrentVersion\backslash Run
$$

Look for entries pointing to:
$$
\text{Temp directories (e.g., } \%AppData\%\backslash \dots \text{)}
$$
$$
\text{Random 8-character executable names (e.g., xyz12345.exe)}
$$
$$
\text{Files with no associated publisher signature}
$$

### Step 4: Cross-Reference with Services
**Logical reasoning:** Malware often registers itself as a Windows service to survive reboots.

$$
\text{Path: } HKLM\backslash SYSTEM\backslash CurrentControlSet\backslash Services
$$

For each service entry, check:
$$
\text{ImagePath} = \text{Binary location}
$$
$$
\text{Start} = \text{0 (Boot) } \vert \text{ 1 (System) } \vert \text{ 2 (Auto) } \vert \text{ 3 (Manual) } \vert \text{ 4 (Disabled)}
$$
$$
\text{Type} = 1 \text{ (Kernel Driver) } \vert 2 \text{ (File System Driver) } \vert 16 \text{ (Win32 Own Process) } \vert 272 \text{ (Win32 Shared Process)}
$$

A service with `Start = 0` and a `Type` indicating a user-mode Win32 process is **highly suspicious**.

### Step 5: Inspect Winlogon for Shell Hijacking
**Logical reasoning:** Malware can replace the default `explorer.exe` shell with its own binary.

$$
\text{Path: } HKLM\backslash SOFTWARE\backslash Microsoft\backslash Windows NT\backslash CurrentVersion\backslash Winlogon
$$

The default value of `Shell` should be `explorer.exe`. Anything else is **anomalous**.

### Step 6: Examine Image File Execution Options (IFEO)
**Logical reasoning:** IFEO Debugger values can be set to redirect a legitimate executable to a malicious one.

$$
\text{Path: } HKLM\backslash SOFTWARE\backslash Microsoft\backslash Windows NT\backslash CurrentVersion\backslash Image File Execution Options\backslash \langle exe\ name\rangle
$$

The presence of a `Debugger` value within a subkey for a legitimate system executable is a **classic persistence indicator**.

### Step 7: Inspect Scheduled Tasks via Registry
**Logical reasoning:** Scheduled tasks are also stored partially in the registry.

$$
\text{Path: } HKLM\backslash SOFTWARE\backslash Microsoft\backslash Windows NT\backslash CurrentVersion\backslash Schedule\backslash TaskCache\backslash Tree
$$

The full scheduled task XML is stored in the file system at:
$$
\%SystemRoot\%\backslash System32\backslash Tasks\backslash
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** Many students forget that scheduled tasks have a **dual storage** (registry + filesystem). Always analyze both.

## 3.2 Procedure B: Tracking User Activity (USB Device Connection)

### Step 1: Locate the USBSTOR Subkey

$$
\text{Path: } HKLM\backslash SYSTEM\backslash CurrentControlSet\backslash Enum\backslash USBSTOR
$$

Each child subkey represents a **distinct USB storage device** that has ever been connected to the system. The subkey name contains:
$$
\text{Vendor} \& \text{Product ID} \& \text{Serial Number}
$$

Example: `Disk&Ven_Kingston&Prod_DataTraveler_3.0&Rev_PMAP\1234567890ABCDEF&0`

### Step 2: Read the Device Instance Properties

Within each USBSTOR entry, examine:
$$
\text{Properties} = \{ \text{DeviceDesc, Mfg, HardwareID, Service, FriendlyName} \}
$$

### Step 3: Cross-Reference with the USB Subkey

$$
\text{Path: } HKLM\backslash SYSTEM\backslash CurrentControlSet\backslash Enum\backslash USB
$$

The `USB` hive records the **first time** the device was plugged in; `USBSTOR` records storage-specific metadata. Forensic tools correlate both to build a complete timeline.

### Step 4: Extract Setupapi.log

For **Windows 7 and later**, the setupapi log provides precise timestamps:
$$
\%SystemRoot\%\backslash INF\backslash setupapi.dev.log
$$

This file is appended to on every device installation and contains lines such as:
$$
\text{>>> } [Device Install (Hardware initiated) - USBSTOR\backslash DISK&VEN_...]
$$

## 3.3 Procedure C: Decoding UserAssist Entries (Exhaustive)

### Step 1: Locate the Key

$$
\text{Path: } HKCU\backslash SOFTWARE\backslash Microsoft\backslash Windows\backslash CurrentVersion\backslash Explorer\backslash UserAssist
$$

### Step 2: Identify the GUID Subkeys

Two GUIDs are standard:
$$
\{ \text{CEBFF5CD-ACE2-4F4F-9178-9926F41749EA} \} \rightarrow \text{Shortcut file executions}
$$
$$
\{ \text{F4E57C4B-2036-45F0-A9AB-443BCFE33D9F} \} \rightarrow \text{Application file executions}
$$

### Step 3: Decode ROT-13 Encoding

The executable path within each value is **ROT-13 encoded**. For each character in the encoded string, apply:

$$
\text{Decoded Char} = \begin{cases} c - 13 & \text{if } c \in [n, z] \\ c + 13 & \text{if } c \in [a, m] \\ c - 13 & \text{if } c \in [N, Z] \\ c + 13 & \text{if } c \in [A, M] \end{cases}
$$

**Example:** Encoded value: `E:\\Frpergf\\Pbagebyq\\Rknzcyrkf\\rkvg`  
Applying ROT-13 to each character:
- `E` → `R`
- `\\` → `\\` (non-alphabetic, unchanged)
- `F` → `S`
- `r` → `e`
- `p` → `c`
- `e` → `r`
- `r` → `e`
- `g` → `t`
- `f` → `s`

Decoded result: `R:\Secrets\Encrypted\exit` — a hidden directory navigation history.

### Step 4: Read the Focus Count and Time Stamp

The value's data contains a 72-byte structure:

$$
\text{Byte 0-3} = \text{Focus Count (32-bit little-endian DWORD)}
$$
$$
\text{Byte 4-7} = \text{Focus Time (32-bit, milliseconds)}
$$
$$
\text{Byte 8-15} = \text{Last Execution Time (64-bit FILETIME)}
$$

The `Last Execution Time` is a standard Windows **FILETIME** structure, which can be converted to a human-readable date using:

$$
\text{Unix Time} = \frac{\text{FILETIME} - 116444736000000000}{10000000}
$$

where the constant **116444736000000000** is the number of 100-nanosecond intervals between **January 1, 1601** (FILETIME epoch) and **January 1, 1970** (Unix epoch).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 14-Mark Variant:** You may be asked to convert a registry timestamp to a human-readable date. Memorize the FILETIME epoch conversion constant.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Windows Registry Hierarchical Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    R[Windows Registry Root] --> A["HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT HKCR"]
    R --> B["HKEY_CURRENT_USER HKCU"]
    R --> C["HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE HKLM"]
    R --> D["HKEY_USERS HKU"]
    R --> E["HKEY_CURRENT_CONFIG HKCC"]

    C --> C1["SAM User Accounts"]
    C --> C2["SYSTEM Services Drivers"]
    C --> C3["SOFTWARE Installed Apps"]
    C --> C4["SECURITY Policies"]

    B --> B1["Software App Settings"]
    B --> B2["Explorer Recent Files"]
    B --> B3["UserAssist History"]
    B --> B4["TypedURLs Browser History"]

    C2 --> C21["CurrentControlSet"]
    C2 --> C22["ControlSet001"]
    C2 --> C23["ControlSet002"]
    C21 --> C211["Services Autorun Entries"]
    C21 --> C212["Enum USB Devices"]
    C21 --> C213["Enum USBSTOR Storage"]

    A --> A1["File Extension Associations"]
    A --> A2["COM Object Registrations"]

    D --> D1["SID keyed User Profiles"]
    D --> D2["Default User Profile"]

    E --> E1["Current Hardware Profile Pointer"]
```

```mermaid

    live --> boot[Boot from Forensic USB]

    image --> mount[Mount Read Only Image]
    mount --> extract[Extract Registry Hive Files]
    extract --> parse[Parse Hives with RegRipper Registry Explorer]
    parse --> analyze[Analyze Forensically Critical Keys]
    analyze --> report[Generate Forensic Report with Hashes and Timestamps]
    report --> court[Admissible in Court of Law]

    analyze --> k1[Check HKLM Run Keys]
    analyze --> k2[Check HKCU UserAssist]
    analyze --> k3[Check USBSTOR]
    analyze --> k4[Check Winlogon Shell]
    analyze --> k5[Check Services Autorun]

    style start fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style court fill:#2d5a2d,color:#ffffff
    style report fill:#5a4b2d,color:#ffffff
    style k1 fill:#5a2d2d,color:#ffffff
    style k2 fill:#5a2d2d,color:#ffffff
    style k3 fill:#5a2d2d,color:#ffffff
    style k4 fill:#5a2d2d,color:#ffffff
    style k5 fill:#5a2d2d,color:#ffffff
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the Windows Registry. List the five root keys (hives) and state the forensic importance of any two.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** The Windows Registry is a hierarchical database used by the Microsoft Windows operating system to store low-level configuration settings, user preferences, hardware information, and operational data for the OS and applications.
- **Five Root Keys (1 Mark):** `HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT (HKCR)`, `HKEY_CURRENT_USER (HKCU)`, `HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE (HKLM)`, `HKEY_USERS (HKU)`, and `HKEY_CURRENT_CONFIG (HKCC)`.
- **Forensic Importance (1 Mark):**
  - **HKLM:** Contains system-wide configuration including services, drivers, and autorun keys — vital for detecting malware persistence and system-level modifications.
  - **HKCU:** Contains per-user activity such as recent documents, typed URLs, and the UserAssist key — vital for reconstructing a suspect's user behavior timeline.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is the UserAssist registry key? Why is it considered forensically significant, and how is its data encoded?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** The UserAssist key located at `HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\UserAssist` records the execution history of applications launched by the user.
- **Forensic Significance (1 Mark):** It contains a list of executable paths that have been launched, along with the focus count and last execution timestamp, providing investigators with a history of program usage.
- **Encoding (1 Mark):** The executable paths within the UserAssist values are encoded using the **ROT-13** cipher, where each alphabetic character is shifted by 13 positions in the alphabet. Forensic tools like RegRipper automatically decode this for analysis.

---

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) Explain in detail the structure of the Windows Registry, including its logical organization and the role of root keys, subkeys, and values. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**1. Logical Organization (2 Marks):**
The Windows Registry is organized as a **tree structure** (also called a hierarchical or inverted tree). The topmost level consists of **root keys (hives)**, beneath which are **subkeys**, and beneath those are **value entries** that contain the actual data. The structure mirrors a file system: hives are like drive letters, subkeys are like folders, and values are like files containing data.

**2. Root Keys (3 Marks):**
The five root keys are:
- **HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT (HKCR):** Merged view of file extension associations and COM object registrations.
- **HKEY_CURRENT_USER (HKCU):** Settings specific to the currently logged-in user, mapped from the active user's SID under HKU.
- **HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE (HKLM):** System-wide configuration including hardware, software, and security policies.
- **HKEY_USERS (HKU):** Contains the configuration of all user profiles on the system, keyed by their Security Identifier (SID).
- **HKEY_CURRENT_CONFIG (HKCC):** A pointer to the current hardware profile in use.

**3. Subkeys and Values (2 Marks):**
- **Subkeys** are container objects analogous to folders. They can contain further subkeys or values, allowing arbitrary depth (limited by the 255-character path length).
- **Value entries** are the actual data records and consist of three parts: a **value name**, a **data type** (REG_SZ, REG_DWORD, etc.), and the **value data** itself. For example, `(Default) REG_SZ "explorer.exe"` represents a string value with the data `explorer.exe`.

---

**(b) Discuss the on-disk registry hive files, their default locations, and the forensic value of each. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**1. On-Disk Hive Files Overview (2 Marks):**
The in-memory registry is mirrored to specific files stored on the disk. These are located in `%SystemRoot%\System32\config\` for system hives and in user profile directories for per-user hives. They are **locked by the operating system at runtime** and require special tools or offline access to read.

**2. System Hive Files (3 Marks):**
- **SAM (`System32\config\SAM`):** Stores user account information, password hashes, and login statistics. Forensic value: reveals all user accounts on the system, last login timestamps, failed login attempts, and account lockout policies.
- **SYSTEM (`System32\config\SYSTEM`):** Stores hardware configuration, device drivers, services, and the **USB device history** (under `CurrentControlSet\Enum\USBSTOR`). Forensic value: identifies connected USB devices, installed drivers, and service-level malware persistence.
- **SOFTWARE (`System32\config\SOFTWARE`):** Stores installed application information, OS configuration, and **autorun keys**. Forensic value: identifies installed software, OS version, and software-based persistence.
- **SECURITY (`System32\config\SECURITY`):** Stores security policies and audit configurations. Forensic value: reveals group policies, audit settings, and user rights assignments.
- **DEFAULT (`System32\config\DEFAULT`):** Stores the default user profile configuration. Forensic value: shows the baseline settings applied to new users.

**3. User Hive Files (2 Marks):**
- **NTUSER.DAT (in `C:\Users\<username>\`):** Contains per-user settings, recent documents, typed URLs, and the UserAssist key. Forensic value: provides user-specific activity history and individual program usage.
- **UsrClass.dat (in `AppData\Local\Microsoft\Windows\`):** Contains per-user file associations and Jump Lists. Forensic value: reveals documents recently opened by specific applications.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Choice]

**(a) Explain the concept of Registry-based persistence in malware. Discuss at least four registry keys commonly abused by malware for persistence. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**1. Concept of Registry-Based Persistence (2 Marks):**
Registry-based persistence is a technique used by malware to **automatically execute malicious code every time a system starts or a user logs in**, by embedding itself in registry keys that the operating system references during startup. This allows the malware to survive reboots and remain active even after the user attempts manual removal.

**2. Commonly Abused Registry Keys (5 Marks):**

**(i) HKLM Run / RunOnce Keys (1.5 Marks):**
Path: `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run` and `RunOnce`.
Malware adds an entry here pointing to its executable. The `Run` key executes the malware at every boot, while `RunOnce` executes it once and then removes the entry (often used in staged attacks).

**(ii) HKCU Run Keys (1.5 Marks):**
Path: `HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`.
This key launches programs when the **current user** logs in. Malware that only needs to persist for one user (e.g., credential-stealing trojans) uses this key to avoid requiring administrator privileges.

**(iii) Winlogon Shell (1 Mark):**
Path: `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon` — `Shell` value.
By default this is `explorer.exe`. Malware replaces this with its own executable, ensuring that every graphical login launches the malware as the user shell.

**(iv) Image File Execution Options (IFEO) Debugger (1 Mark):**
Path: `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Image File Execution Options\<program.exe>` — `Debugger` value.
Malware sets a Debugger value for a legitimate system executable (e.g., `sethc.exe` for the **Sticky Keys backdoor**). When that program runs, the Debugger launches the malware instead.

---

**(b) With a suitable example, explain how the Windows Registry can be used to track USB device usage. Outline the investigative steps a forensic examiner would follow. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**1. USB Tracking via Registry (3 Marks):**
The Windows Registry maintains records of every USB device ever connected to the system under two key locations:
- `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USB` — for all USB devices
- `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USBSTOR` — for USB storage devices specifically

Each subkey under `USBSTOR` represents a unique device and contains properties like the vendor name, product name, and **serial number** in its key name. For example, a subkey named `Disk&Ven_SanDisk&Prod_Ultra&Rev_1.00\4C530001234567890123&0` indicates a **SanDisk Ultra** device with the unique serial number `4C530001234567890123`.

**2. Investigative Steps (4 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Acquire the **SYSTEM** hive file from `%SystemRoot%\System32\config\SYSTEM` using FTK Imager or a forensic boot environment.

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Open the SYSTEM hive in **Registry Explorer** or **RegRipper** and navigate to `CurrentControlSet\Enum\USBSTOR`. Each child subkey represents a connected device.

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** For each device, record the `DeviceDesc`, `Mfg`, `HardwareID`, `FriendlyName`, and the `ContainerID` value. Cross-reference with the `Properties` subkey for detailed metadata.

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Extract the `setupapi.dev.log` from `%SystemRoot%\INF\` to obtain precise **first and last connection timestamps** for each device. Combine the registry data with the log timestamps to build a complete forensic timeline, and correlate the serial number against the suspect's known USB devices.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Writing only file paths, not key paths** — Examiners require **full registry key paths** like `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`. A bare mention of "Run key" loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing hive files with root keys** — `SAM` (file) is not the same as `HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SAM` (live key). Always mention both.
> 3. **Forgetting the ROT-13 encoding of UserAssist** — Many students quote the key path but omit the encoding detail, losing the technical depth mark.
> 4. **Skipping the timeline aspect** — Registry values often contain **Windows FILETIME** timestamps. A complete answer must include the **FILETIME-to-Unix conversion** for full marks.
> 5. **Confusing `Run` with `RunOnce`** — `Run` = persistent; `RunOnce` = single execution and then auto-deletion. Examiners test this distinction.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition (Quick Recall):** The Windows Registry is a **hierarchical database** that stores OS, hardware, software, and user configuration data.
- **Five Root Keys:** `HKCR`, `HKCU`, `HKLM`, `HKU`, `HKCC`.
- **Critical Hive Files:** `SAM`, `SYSTEM`, `SOFTWARE`, `SECURITY`, `DEFAULT`, `NTUSER.DAT`, `UsrClass.dat`.
- **High-Value Forensic Keys (must memorize):** `Run`, `RunOnce`, `UserAssist`, `TypedURLs`, `TypedPaths`, `USBSTOR`, `Winlogon\Shell`, `IFEO`, `RecentDocs`, `MountPoints2`.
- **UserAssist Encoding:** **ROT-13** cipher on executable paths, with **72-byte value data** containing focus count, focus time, and last execution FILETIME.
- **FILETIME Epoch:** Starts at **January 1, 1601**; conversion constant to Unix epoch = **116444736000000000** (in 100-nanosecond intervals).
- **Common Data Types:** `REG_SZ`, `REG_EXPAND_SZ`, `REG_MULTI_SZ`, `REG_DWORD`, `REG_QWORD`, `REG_BINARY`.
- **Malware Persistence Vectors:** `Run` keys, `Services` subkeys, `Winlogon\Shell`, `IFEO Debugger`, scheduled tasks in `TaskCache\Tree`.
- **USB Tracking:** Examine both `USB` and `USBSTOR` hives, cross-reference with `setupapi.dev.log` for timestamps.
- **Tooling:** **FTK Imager** for acquisition, **RegRipper** and **Registry Explorer** for analysis, **Sysinternals Autoruns** for live verification.
- **Forensic Best Practice:** Always **image first, mount read-only, extract hives offline** to prevent any live contamination of evidence.
- **Examiner's Mantra:** Whenever a question mentions "evidence of user activity," think **Registry** first, file system second, RAM third.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microsoft Start-up Tasks: Windows 7
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT602 - CYBER FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Microsoft Start-up Tasks: Windows 7

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
In the context of **Cyber Forensics** under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, **Microsoft Start-up Tasks in Windows 7** refer to the comprehensive set of **autonomous execution mechanisms, persistence vectors, and bootstrapping artifacts** invoked automatically by the Windows 7 operating system from the moment power is applied (BIOS/UEFI hand-off) until the user desktop environment is fully rendered. Forensically, these tasks constitute **high-value, volatile-to-non-volatile evidentiary artifacts** that reveal what code (legitimate or malicious) is configured to run during system boot, user logon, or service initialization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "Windows 7 Start-up Tasks are the controlled sequence of operations — executed by the Windows Boot Manager, kernel loader, Session Manager (smss.exe), and the Winlogon subsystem — that load drivers, initialize services, run registry-resident executables, and trigger Userinit-driven shell launch, all of which leave forensic traces in the Registry hives (`SYSTEM`, `SOFTWARE`, `NTUSER.DAT`) and on the file system."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Building Entry Log
Imagine a **secure corporate office building**:
- The **power switch (BIOS/UEFI)** is the security guard at the outer gate who checks the building's master pass (MBR).
- The **Boot Manager (Bootmgr)** is the reception desk that decides which floor (OS partition) to unlock.
- The **elevator controller (Winload.exe → Ntoskrnl.exe)** is the central machinery that powers up all floors (kernel, HAL, drivers).
- The **floor supervisors (Services, svchost.exe)** are pre-assigned workers who report to their cubicles automatically when the building opens.
- The **personal assistant (Userinit.exe → Explorer.exe)** is the one who, after the employee (user) logs in, opens their office and places their default files on the desk.

**Forensically**, every "automatic worker" who enters the building leaves behind a **log entry** — in Windows 7, these logs are stored as **Registry values, scheduled tasks, shortcut (.lnk) files, and Prefetch entries**. A cyber forensic investigator must audit every "automatic worker" to determine if any unauthorized personnel (malware) has gained a persistent entry pass.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Forensic Principle:** Persistence is the hallmark of sophisticated malware. Windows 7 offers **more than 25 distinct auto-start locations** (ASEPs — Auto-Start Extensibility Points), and forensic examiners must enumerate all of them to prove or disprove unauthorized code execution.

### 1.3 Core Metrics & Constants
- **Number of default Windows 7 startup phases:** **6** (Pre-boot, Boot, Kernel Load, Service Init, Logon, User Shell).
- **Primary forensic Registry hives involved:** `SYSTEM`, `SOFTWARE`, `NTUSER.DAT`, `SECURITY`, `SAM`.
- **Standard Prefetch file count (Win7):** Up to **1024** entries stored at `C:\Windows\Prefetch`.
- **Default timeout for services marked `SERVICE_AUTO_START`:** **120 seconds** before service failure is logged.
---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six-Phase Windows 7 Boot Sequence
Forensic investigators must understand the boot sequence because **residual evidence of every phase is recoverable**, and **anomalies in any phase are indicators of compromise**.

| Phase | Process Executed | Forensic Artifact Generated | File/Hive Location |
|-------|------------------|------------------------------|--------------------|
| **1. Pre-Boot (Firmware)** | BIOS / UEFI → Power-On Self-Test (POST) | CMOS/NVRAM logs (if enabled) | Hardware-dependent |
| **2. Boot Sector Load** | MBR (512 bytes) → VBR (`\Boot\BCD`) | MBR timestamp, BCD edits | `\Boot\BCD` |
| **3. Windows Boot Manager** | `Bootmgr.exe` reads BCD | `Boottrace.etl` (if boot tracing enabled) | `C:\` (hidden) |
| **4. OS Loader** | `Winload.exe` → `Ntoskrnl.exe` → `Hal.dll` | `C:\Windows\INF\setupapi.dev.log` | `C:\Windows\inf` |
| **5. Session Initialization** | `Smss.exe` → `Csrss.exe` → `Winlogon.exe` | `Session Manager` subkey value `BootExecute` | `SYSTEM\...\Session Manager` |
| **6. Logon & Shell** | `Userinit.exe` → `Explorer.exe` | `Winlogon` subkey, `Shell`, `Userinit` values | `SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon` |

### 2.2 Auto-Start Extensibility Points (ASEPs) — The KTU High-Yield Master List

The following is the **canonical ASEP matrix** examiners are expected to know for the KTU University Exam:

| # | ASEP Name | Registry Path / File Path | Trigger Event | Persistence Strength |
|---|-----------|---------------------------|---------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Run (Machine)** | `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run` | User Logon | High |
| 2 | **RunOnce (Machine)** | `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce` | User Logon (deleted after run) | High |
| 3 | **RunOnceEx (Machine)** | `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnceEx` | User Logon (ordered, DSP/DS flag) | High |
| 4 | **Run (User)** | `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run` | User Logon | High |
| 5 | **RunOnce (User)** | `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce` | User Logon (deleted after run) | High |
| 6 | **Policies\Explorer\Run** | `HKLM\…\Policies\Explorer\Run` | User Logon (policy-enforced) | Very High |
| 7 | **Startup Folder (All Users)** | `%ProgramData%\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup` | User Logon | Medium |
| 8 | **Startup Folder (Per User)** | `%AppData%\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup` | User Logon | Medium |
| 9 | **Winlogon Shell** | `…\Winlogon\Shell` | User Logon | Very High |
| 10 | **Winlogon Userinit** | `…\Winlogon\Userinit` | User Logon | Very High |
| 11 | **Winlogon Notify** | `…\Winlogon\Notify` | Logon events (DLL injection) | Very High |
| 12 | **AppInit_DLLs** | `…\Windows\AppInit_DLLs` | Process creation (every GUI app) | Very High |
| 13 | **ShellExecuteHooks** | `…\Explorer\ShellExecuteHooks` | Shell startup | High |
| 14 | **BootExecute** | `…\Session Manager\BootExecute` | Early boot (e.g., chkdsk) | High |
| 15 | **Services** | `HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services` | Kernel/service init | Very High |
| 16 | **Scheduled Tasks (v2)** | `C:\Windows\System32\Tasks` | Time/event-based | High |
| 17 | **Image File Execution Options (IFEO)** | `…\Image File Execution Options\<app>.exe\Debugger` | Specific EXE launch | High |
| 18 | **LSA Security Packages** | `…\Control\Lsa\Security Packages` | LSA init (credential theft target) | Critical |
| 19 | **KnownDLLs** | `…\Session Manager\KnownDLLs` | Process creation (DLL hijack prevention) | Critical |
| 20 | **Active Setup** | `HKLM\…\Active Setup\Installed Components` | Per-user first logon | High |
| 21 | **Gadgets / Sidebar** | `C:\Program Files\Windows Sidebar\Gadgets` | User Logon | Medium |
| 22 | **BHO (Browser Helper Objects)** | `HKLM\…\Explorer\Browser Helper Objects` | Internet Explorer launch | High |
| 23 | **WOW6432Node (32-bit on 64-bit)** | `HKLM\SOFTWARE\WOW6432Node\…\Run` | User Logon | High |
| 24 | **Screensaver Exec** | `HKCU\Control Panel\Desktop\ScreenSaveActive` + `SCRNSAVE.EXE` | Screensaver activation | Medium |
| 25 | **Win.ini / System.ini** | `C:\Windows\win.ini` | Legacy (mostly dormant in Win7) | Low |

### 2.3 Why Each Step Matters — The "Why & How" Engineer's View

- **Why does Winlogon matter?** Because `Userinit.exe` is the parent process that **launches the user shell**. If malware modifies the `Userinit` value to append `malware.exe,` (note the trailing comma — this preserves the legitimate `userinit.exe` call while chaining malicious code), the system looks normal but is compromised.
- **Why are AppInit_DLLs dangerous?** Because in Windows 7, any DLL listed here is **loaded into every process that calls `user32.dll`**, providing a powerful **userland persistence** mechanism.
- **Why examine `BootExecute`?** Because the default value `autocheck autochk *` runs `autochk.exe` (the chkdsk predecessor) on dirty volumes. Malware can hijack this to run code **before any antivirus loads**.
- **Why the Startup Folder?** Shortcuts (`.lnk` files) placed here are enumerated by Explorer at logon. Forensic tools like **EnCase, FTK, Autopsy, and RegRipper** can parse these and correlate them with file system timestamps.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** When asked to "list start-up persistence locations," examiners award full marks only when the answer **distinguishes between user-level and machine-level** registry paths, and **mentions the forensic artifact (hive file)** where each persists on disk.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Boot Artifact Examination

| Symbol / Artifact | Definition | Forensic Value |
|-------------------|------------|----------------|
| $\Delta t_{boot}$ | Time between `BootExecute` start and `Winlogon` shell launch | Indicates slow-boot malware |
| `LastWriteTime` | NTFS timestamp of Registry key modification | Proves tampering time |
| `Prefetch Hash` | 8-character hash of executable (e.g., `CMD.EXE-4A81B6E1.pf`) | Proves program execution |
| `RunCount` | Number stored in Prefetch file header (offset 0x80) | Frequency of execution |
| $N_{services}$ | Total auto-start services enumerated in `SYSTEM\…\Services` | Baseline anomaly detection |
| $H_{user}$ | User hive path: `C:\Users\<u>\NTUSER.DAT` | Maps persistence to specific account |
| `AppInit_DLLs` (Win7) | Loads only into processes using `user32.dll` with `LoadAppInit_DLLs=1` | Common malware target |

### 2.5 Real-World Forensic Utility
- **Incident Response:** A SOC analyst responding to a ransomware incident in Windows 7 will **first check the Run keys, Winlogon, and Scheduled Tasks** to identify the persistence mechanism before re-imaging the host.
- **Malware Reverse Engineering:** The "Stuxnet" and "Conficker" families famously abused **Scheduled Tasks** and **Run keys** for persistence — a knowledge of these ASEPs is essential for attribution.
- **Court-Admissible Evidence:** A forensic examiner testifying in court must produce a **defensible methodology** for enumerating startup artifacts. The **NIST SP 800-86** guide and the **SANS FOR508** course use this exact six-phase boot analysis.
