## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — The Registry Hive Mapping for Boot Artifacts

The Windows 7 Registry is not a single file; it is a **collection of binary hive files** stored in `C:\Windows\System32\config\`. For forensic acquisition, these files are parsed **offline** using tools like **RegistryExplorer (Eric Zimmerman)** or **reglookup**.

| Logical Hive Name | Physical File Path | Boot-Relevant Subkeys |
|-------------------|--------------------|------------------------|
| `HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM` | `%SystemRoot%\System32\config\SYSTEM` | `CurrentControlSet\Services`, `…\Session Manager` |
| `HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE` | `%SystemRoot%\System32\config\SOFTWARE` | `…\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`, `…\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon` |
| `HKEY_USERS\<SID>` | `C:\Users\<user>\NTUSER.DAT` | `…\CurrentVersion\Run`, `…\CurrentVersion\RunOnce` |
| `HKEY_USERS\.DEFAULT` | `%SystemRoot%\System32\config\DEFAULT` | `…\CurrentVersion\Run` (default user profile) |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Forensic Procedure: Windows 7 Startup Artifact Triage

> [!NOTE]
> The following procedure is what a KTU examiner expects as a **model answer** for a "describe the methodology" question.

**Step 1 — Secure the Volatile State (Live Acquisition)**
Before pulling the plug, capture volatile evidence using a tool like **FTK Imager Lite** or **WinPmem**:
- List currently running processes (`tasklist /svc`)
- Map loaded DLLs to processes (`listdlls.exe`)
- Record active network connections (`netstat -anob`)

**Step 2 — Acquire the Disk Image**
- Use a hardware write-blocker (e.g., Tableau T35u) to create an **E01 (EnCase)** or **DD (raw)** image of the system drive.
- Compute **MD5** and **SHA-1** hashes for chain-of-custody integrity.

**Step 3 — Mount and Parse the Registry Hives**
Using **FTK** or **Autopsy**, mount the image read-only and extract the four critical hives listed in §3.1.

**Step 4 — Enumerate Auto-Start Registry Keys (Run / RunOnce)**
The logical path is:
`HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`
Each value in this key is of the form:

$$\text{ValueName} = \text{StringPath} \mid \text{Optional Arguments}$$

For example:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ValueName} &= \text{"SecurityHealth"} \\
\text{ValueData} &= \text{"\%windir\%\system32\SecurityHealthSystray.exe"}
\end{aligned}
$$

The forensic examiner records:
- Value name (e.g., `SecurityHealth`)
- Value data (full path to executable)
- **LastWriteTime** of the parent key
- The hive file it resides in (e.g., `SOFTWARE` vs `NTUSER.DAT`)

**Step 5 — Examine the Winlogon Subsystem**
The relevant values are:

| Value Name | Default Windows 7 Value | Forensic Red Flag |
|------------|--------------------------|-------------------|
| `Shell` | `explorer.exe` | Replaced with custom shell = compromise |
| `Userinit` | `C:\Windows\system32\userinit.exe,` | Comma-chain = classic malware technique |
| `AutoAdminLogon` | `0` | Set to `1` = possible password-less intrusion |
| `DefaultUserName` | (variable) | Check for unfamiliar accounts |

**Step 6 — Analyze Scheduled Tasks**
The Windows 7 Task Scheduler stores jobs as **XML files** in:

$$
\text{Path} = C:\text{Windows}\textbackslash\text{System32}\textbackslash\text{Tasks}
$$

Each `.xml` file contains:
- `<Triggers>` — defines when the task runs (boot, logon, time, event)
- `<Actions>` — defines the executable and arguments
- `<Principals>` — defines the user account executing the task

Forensic pivot: cross-reference the `<Command>` element with the `Run` key values to identify duplicate persistence (a malware author may register the same payload in multiple ASEPs).

**Step 7 — Correlate with Prefetch and Event Logs**
For every suspicious executable found in an ASEP, locate the corresponding Prefetch file at `C:\Windows\Prefetch`:

$$
\text{Filename} = \langle\text{ExeName}\rangle\text{-}\langle\text{8-char-hash}\rangle\text{.pf}
$$

The Prefetch header (at byte offset 0x80) contains the **`RunCount`**, which proves how many times the file has been executed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Offset 0x80} &: \text{Execution Count (DWORD, 4 bytes)} \\
\text{Offset 0x88} &: \text{Last Execution Time (FILETIME, 8 bytes)} \\
\text{Offset 0x90} &: \text{Last Execution Time (FILETIME, 8 bytes, volume 1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This allows the examiner to prove: *"The malware `evil.exe` was executed **47 times**, with the last execution on `2024-08-15 03:22:11 UTC`, and was registered to auto-start in the `HKCU\…\Run` key."*

**Step 8 — Generate the Forensic Report**
A complete report must include:
- The **Acquisition Hashes** (MD5/SHA1)
- **Tool Validation** (e.g., FTK version, RegRipper version)
- A **timeline** (using tools like **Plaso/log2timeline**) correlating Registry `LastWriteTime`, Prefetch execution times, and Event Log entries.
- **Screenshots and verbatim Registry value dumps** as exhibits.

### 3.3 Analytical Math — Booting Time Anomaly Detection

For each boot session $i$, the time taken to reach the user shell is:

$$
T_{i} = T_{explorer\_ready,i} - T_{power\_on,i}
$$

A baseline average is established:

$$
\bar{T} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{i}
$$

A boot is flagged anomalous if:

$$
T_{i} > \bar{T} + k \cdot \sigma_{T}
$$

where $k = 3$ is the standard 3-sigma threshold and $\sigma_{T}$ is the standard deviation. An unusually long $T_{i}$ may indicate that a **malicious service or Run-key entry is delaying the boot**.

```mermaid

    C --> D[Phase 4: Winload loads Kernel]
    D --> E[Phase 5: Smss and Session Manager]

    A -. Forensics .-> A1[CMOS NVRAM Dump]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Mapping — Why Mermaid Subgraphs Were Used
> [!IMPORTANT]
> The above diagrams intentionally use **process-flowcharts** and **subgraph blocks** rather than physical layout drawings. This is a **deliberate engineering choice** because Windows 7 startup is a **logical control-flow** problem, not a spatial one. The block architecture clearly shows the **time-ordered sequence** of boot phases and the **hierarchical relationship** between ASEPs, which is exactly what a forensic analyst must reconstruct during a post-mortem.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2, 3 Marks]**
*Define Auto-Start Extensibility Points (ASEPs). List any six ASEPs relevant to Windows 7 startup forensics.*

**Model Answer:**
Auto-Start Extensibility Points (ASEPs) are the **operating system mechanisms, registry keys, file system locations, and scheduled task definitions** that cause a program to be executed automatically without explicit user action. In Windows 7, ASEPs are critical forensic artifacts because they are the primary **persistence vectors** used by both legitimate software and malware.

**Six ASEPs (any six of the following are accepted):**
1. `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`
2. `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`
3. Startup folders (All Users and Per User)
4. Scheduled Tasks (in `C:\Windows\System32\Tasks`)
5. `Winlogon\Shell` and `Winlogon\Userinit` registry values
6. `AppInit_DLLs` registry value
7. Services under `HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services`
8. `BootExecute` under `Session Manager`

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2, 3 Marks]**
*What is the role of `Userinit.exe` in the Windows 7 boot process, and how is it commonly abused for persistence?*

**Model Answer:**
`Userinit.exe` is a **logon application** located at `C:\Windows\system32\userinit.exe`. It is launched by the **Winlogon** subsystem immediately after a successful user authentication. Its role is to:
- Establish the user's profile environment
- Run logon scripts (if any)
- Launch the user's shell (default: `explorer.exe`)

**Forensic Abuse Pattern:** Malware modifies the `Userinit` registry value (under `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon`) to **append** a malicious executable path separated by a **comma**, e.g.:

$$
\text{ValueData} = \text{"C:\textbackslash Windows\textbackslash system32\textbackslash userinit.exe,C:\textbackslash evil\textbackslash malware.exe,"}
$$

The trailing comma is critical — it ensures `userinit.exe` still launches the shell, hiding the malware's presence from casual inspection. Forensic detection involves comparing the `Userinit` value against the known-good default of `C:\Windows\system32\userinit.exe,`.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, Module 2 Part B questions carry **14 marks** and offer an **internal choice** (either Question A or Question B). Each long question typically carries **two 7-mark sub-parts**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2, 14 Marks]**
*(a) Describe in detail the six phases of the Windows 7 boot sequence, with a focus on the forensic artifacts generated at each phase. (7 marks)*
*(b) Explain how a forensic investigator would enumerate and analyze the Auto-Start Extensibility Points in a Windows 7 Registry. Include a discussion of the relevant hive files and key paths. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) The Six Phases of Windows 7 Boot (7 marks):**

1. **Pre-Boot / Firmware Phase:** The system power-on triggers the BIOS/UEFI firmware to perform POST. The firmware identifies the boot device and reads the **Master Boot Record (MBR)** — the first 512 bytes of the disk. *Forensic artifact:* The MBR contains the disk signature, partition table, and boot code. MBR anomalies (extra code in the bootstrap area) are a classic indicator of bootkits like **TDL4** or **Olmasco**.

2. **Boot Sector Phase:** The MBR code locates the **active partition** and transfers control to its **Volume Boot Record (VBR)**. The VBR reads the **Boot Configuration Data (BCD)** stored at `\Boot\BCD`. *Forensic artifact:* The BCD is a registry hive; tampering here can redirect the boot loader to an attacker-controlled partition.

3. **Windows Boot Manager Phase:** `Bootmgr.exe` reads the BCD and displays the boot menu. It then loads **`Winload.exe`** from `\Windows\System32\`. *Forensic artifact:* `Bootmgr` can be configured to write a **boot trace** (`%SystemRoot%\bootstat.dat` and `Boottrace.etl`) useful for boot-performance forensics.

4. **OS Loader / Kernel Initialization Phase:** `Winload.exe` loads the Windows kernel (`Ntoskrnl.exe`), the Hardware Abstraction Layer (`Hal.dll`), and the **registry hives** (`SYSTEM`, `SOFTWARE`, etc.) into memory. It also loads **early-launch drivers** based on the `EarlyLaunchDrivers` policy. *Forensic artifact:* `setupapi.dev.log` in `%SystemRoot%\INF` records every driver load, useful for identifying unsigned or malicious drivers.

5. **Session Initialization Phase:** The kernel launches **`Smss.exe`** (Session Manager SubSystem). `Smss.exe` processes the **`BootExecute`** multi-string value in `HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager`. It then starts **`Csrss.exe`** and **`Winlogon.exe`** for Session 1. *Forensic artifact:* The default `BootExecute` value is `autocheck autochk *`; any addition (e.g., `autocheck autochk *,C:\evil\bootkit.exe`) is a red flag.

6. **Logon and User Shell Phase:** `Winlogon.exe` manages the secure attention sequence (Ctrl+Alt+Del), collects credentials via **`Msgina.dll`** (or `CustomShellHost` in custom deployments), and on successful authentication launches **`Userinit.exe`**. `Userinit.exe` processes the `Run`, `RunOnce`, and startup folder entries, then launches **`Explorer.exe`** (the Windows Shell). *Forensic artifact:* The `Shell` and `Userinit` registry values under `Winlogon` are the highest-value forensic artifacts. Any `.lnk` files in the Startup folders and any EXE referenced in `Run`/`RunOnce` are collected as evidence.

**(b) Enumerating ASEPs in the Windows 7 Registry (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Acquire the hives.** The forensic investigator first acquires a forensically sound image of the suspect drive and extracts the four registry hive files:
- `C:\Windows\System32\config\SYSTEM`
- `C:\Windows\System32\config\SOFTWARE`
- `C:\Windows\System32\config\SECURITY`
- `C:\Windows\System32\config\SAM`
- For each user profile: `C:\Users\<username>\NTUSER.DAT`

**Step 2 — Parse with a forensic tool.** Tools such as **RegRipper (rip.exe)**, **RegistryExplorer (Eric Zimmerman)**, or **FTK Registry Viewer** are used to parse the hives offline. For ASEP analysis, the `autoruns` plugin of RegRipper is the standard.

**Step 3 — Enumerate the machine-level ASEPs (SYSTEM/SOFTWARE hives):**

| Key Path | Hive | What to Look For |
|----------|------|------------------|
| `…\CurrentVersion\Run` | SOFTWARE | Persistent EXE launches at every logon |
| `…\CurrentVersion\RunOnce` | SOFTWARE | One-time EXE launches (deleted after execution) |
| `…\CurrentVersion\RunOnceEx` | SOFTWARE | Ordered launch list with DSP/DS dependency flags |
| `…\Policies\Explorer\Run` | SOFTWARE | Group-policy-enforced runs (cannot be edited by user) |
| `…\Winlogon\Shell` | SOFTWARE | Default `explorer.exe`; replace = compromise |
| `…\Winlogon\Userinit` | SOFTWARE | Default `C:\Windows\system32\userinit.exe,`; comma-chain = compromise |
| `…\Windows\AppInit_DLLs` | SOFTWARE | DLLs loaded into every GUI process |
| `…\Session Manager\BootExecute` | SYSTEM | Default `autocheck autochk *`; additions = red flag |
| `…\ControlSet001\Services` | SYSTEM | Auto-start services (`Start = 0x02`) |

**Step 4 — Enumerate the user-level ASEPs (NTUSER.DAT hive):**
The same `…\CurrentVersion\Run` and `…\CurrentVersion\RunOnce` keys are examined in each user's hive. The forensic benefit is that user-level Run keys are often **overlooked** by users and some endpoint protection tools, making them a favored malware hiding place.

**Step 5 — Cross-correlate with file system artifacts.** Each suspicious EXE path found in a Run value is verified against:
- Its **NTFS `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` timestamp** (created, modified, accessed)
- Its **Prefetch file** (if the EXE has been executed at least once)
- The **MUICache** registry key (in `…\Explorer\MUICache`) for recent executables

**Step 6 — Document the findings** in a report that includes the `LastWriteTime` of each key, the full value name and data, the originating hive file, and a screenshot of the Registry Editor view.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2, 14 Marks]**
*(a) Explain the function of `BootExecute`, `Winlogon`, and `Userinit.exe` in Windows 7 startup. Discuss how an attacker can abuse each of these for persistence. (7 marks)*
*(b) Describe the role of Scheduled Tasks and the Startup folders in Windows 7. How does a forensic examiner analyze these artifacts using the file system and the Registry? (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) BootExecute, Winlogon, and Userinit.exe — Functions and Abuse (7 marks):**

**`BootExecute`:**
- **Function:** A multi-string value under `HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager`. Its default content is `autocheck autochk *`, which causes `autochk.exe` (the volume-bitmap checker) to run on dirty volumes during boot, **before any user-mode program starts**. This is why `BootExecute` is a high-priority ASEP for forensic analysis.
- **Abuse:** An attacker appends an additional command, e.g., `autocheck autochk *,C:\malware\preboot.exe`. Because the path is parsed and executed by the privileged Session Manager, the malware runs with **Local System privileges** and is virtually invisible to user-mode antivirus.

**`Winlogon`:**
- **Function:** `Winlogon.exe` is the system process responsible for the secure logon sequence, credential collection (via `Msgina.dll`), screen-saver handling, and lock/unlock operations. It reads the `Shell` and `Userinit` values from `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon`.
- **Abuse:** The attacker modifies `Shell` to launch a malicious executable instead of `explorer.exe`, replacing the entire shell. Alternatively, the attacker **hooks** Winlogon by adding values to the `Notify` subkey — these are DLLs that receive notifications of logon, logoff, lock, and unlock events. Classic malware like **Ginwui** and **PWS:Win32/Zbot** use Winlogon Notify for credential capture.

**`Userinit.exe`:**
- **Function:** As described in Part A Q2, `Userinit.exe` is a logon-time process that establishes the user environment and launches the shell. Its location is read from the `Userinit` registry value.
- **Abuse:** The classic **comma-chain** technique. The default value is `C:\Windows\system32\userinit.exe,` (note the trailing comma). The attacker appends `C:\path\to\malware.exe,` so the value becomes `C:\Windows\system32\userinit.exe,C:\path\to\malware.exe,`. The trailing comma is a **deliberate obfuscation** because a casual viewer sees the legitimate `userinit.exe` path and concludes the key is untouched.

**(b) Scheduled Tasks and Startup Folders (7 marks):**

**Scheduled Tasks (Task Scheduler v2):**
- **Function:** Windows 7 uses a **Task Scheduler v2** engine. Tasks are stored as **XML files** in `C:\Windows\System32\Tasks`. Each XML file contains the triggers (boot, logon, time-based, event-based), actions, and principal (user context).
- **Forensic Analysis:** The examiner lists the `.xml` files in `C:\Windows\System32\Tasks` and also the subfolders for legacy tasks (`At1`, etc.). Each XML is parsed for:
  - `<Triggers>` — if a trigger is `<BootTrigger>` or `<LogonTrigger>`, the task is an **ASEP**.
  - `<Command>` and `<Arguments>` — the executable to run.
  - `<UserId>` — the user context (often `SYSTEM` for malware).
  - `Date` and `Author` elements — sometimes reveal the attacker's pseudonym.
- The examiner also checks the **`Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Schedule\TaskCache`** registry tree to see deleted tasks (the cache is updated before the file is removed, so a deleted task may still appear in the cache).

**Startup Folders:**
- **Function:** Two physical folders host shortcuts that are launched at every logon:
  - **All Users:** `C:\ProgramData\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup`
  - **Per User:** `C:\Users\<username>\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup`
- **Forensic Analysis:** The examiner parses all `.lnk` (shortcut) files in these folders. The forensic value of `.lnk` files is that they contain:
  - The **target EXE path** (e.g., `C:\evil\malware.exe`)
  - The **working directory**
  - **MAC times** (creation, modification, access)
  - A **shell item ID list** that records the full directory traversal at the time of shortcut creation — useful for proving the EXE was located at a specific path even if the EXE has been deleted later.
- Tools like **LECmd (Eric Zimmerman)** parse `.lnk` files and produce CSV reports. The examiner then cross-references the target EXE with Prefetch and `MUICache` to confirm execution.

**Integration:** A robust investigation does not stop at one ASEP. The examiner builds a **timeline** in tools like **Plaso** (formerly `log2timeline`) that merges:
- Registry key `LastWriteTime` events
- `.lnk` file creation times
- Task XML creation dates
- Prefetch execution times
- Event Log entries (Event ID 4688 for process creation, if audit policy is enabled)

This timeline is the **deliverable evidence** presented in court or in the incident response report.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Where Students Lose Marks):**
> 1. **Confusing HKLM and HKCU:** A common mistake is to write `HKLM\…\Run` for user-level persistence. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this. Remember: HKLM = machine-wide (requires admin to modify), HKCU = per-user (modifiable without admin — favoured by malware).
> 2. **Forgetting the trailing comma in `Userinit`:** Many students write the value as `C:\Windows\system32\userinit.exe` without the comma. In Windows 7, the comma is **mandatory** — it separates `Userinit.exe` from the subsequent shell-launch command.
> 3. **Not distinguishing `Run` from `RunOnce`:** `Run` is **persistent** (re-runs on every logon). `RunOnce` is **deletable** (the system deletes the value after the EXE runs once). Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 4. **Missing the `Wow6432Node` redirection:** On 64-bit Windows 7, 32-bit EXE entries are redirected to `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Wow6432Node\…\Run`. Forgetting this means missing 32-bit malware persistence.
> 5. **Ignoring `HKCU\…\RunOnce` in the user hive:** A novice examiner will check only the machine-level Run keys. The user-level keys are where most modern malware lives.
> 6. **Forgetting the scheduled task cache in the Registry:** Even if a `.xml` task file is deleted, the **`TaskCache\Tree`** registry key in `SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Schedule\TaskCache` retains the entry. Always check both.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Print This Section and Pin It**

- [ ] **Windows 7 has SIX boot phases:** Pre-Boot → MBR/VBR → Bootmgr → Winload → Smss/Winlogon → Userinit/Shell.
- [ ] The **default `Userinit` value** ends with a comma: `C:\Windows\system32\userinit.exe,`.
- [ ] The **default `Shell` value** is `explorer.exe` (under `Winlogon`).
- [ ] The **default `BootExecute`** is `autocheck autochk *`.
- [ ] **ASEPs = Auto-Start Extensibility Points.** Know all 25 listed in §2.2.
- [ ] **HKLM vs HKCU:** `HKLM\…\Run` (machine) vs `HKCU\…\Run` (user). Both must be examined.
- [ ] **Run vs RunOnce:** `Run` = persistent; `RunOnce` = deleted after one execution.
- [ ] **Startup folders:** Two locations — All Users (`%ProgramData%…`) and Per User (`%AppData%…`).
- [ ] **Scheduled Tasks v2:** Stored as **XML** in `C:\Windows\System32\Tasks`. Parse triggers for `<BootTrigger>` or `<LogonTrigger>`.
- [ ] **TaskCache Registry:** Even deleted tasks leave a trace in `SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Schedule\TaskCache\Tree`.
- [ ] **AppInit_DLLs:** Loads DLLs into every GUI process — a classic userland persistence vector.
- [ ] **Image File Execution Options (IFEO):** Can set a `Debugger` value to launch an EXE under attacker control when a legitimate EXE is launched.
- [ ] **LSA Security Packages:** Modify with extreme caution — used by mimikatz-style tools to harvest credentials.
- [ ] **Prefetch files:** Located at `C:\Windows\Prefetch`. Up to 1024 entries. The 8-character hash plus `.pf` extension identifies the EXE.
- [ ] **Forensic tools to remember:** FTK Imager, EnCase, Autopsy, RegRipper, RegistryExplorer, LECmd, Plaso, Eric Zimmerman's tools.
- [ ] **NIST Reference:** The methodology aligns with **NIST SP 800-86** ("Guide to Integrating Forensic Techniques into Incident Response").
- [ ] **Chain of custody:** Always compute and record **MD5 and SHA-1** hashes of the acquired image and the extracted hive files.
- [ ] **Timeline analysis is king:** A single ASEP entry is suspicious; a **timeline correlation** of Registry + Prefetch + Event Log + .lnk is **conclusive evidence**.
---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Windows 8
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT602 - CYBER FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Windows 8

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Windows 8 (Forensic Perspective)**
> Windows 8 is a desktop and mobile operating system released by Microsoft on **26 October 2012** (build **6.2.9200**). It introduces a Unified Extensible Firmware Interface (**UEFI**) boot path, the **Windows Runtime (WinRT)** application model for Modern/Metro "apps", fast **Hybrid Boot/Hibernate**, **Reset/Refresh** PC recovery primitives, and the **Windows To Go** live-OS feature. From a digital-forensics standpoint, Windows 8 leaves a richer, more complex artifact set than Windows 7, and many artifacts have shifted in *location*, *format*, and *persistence lifetime*.

**Key Forensic-Relevant Identifiers**

| Parameter | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Marketing Name | Windows 8 / Windows 8.1 / Windows RT |
| NT Kernel Version | NT 6.2 (8.0) / NT 6.3 (8.1) |
| Default File System | NTFS (ReFS optional for server variants) |
| Boot Path | UEFI Secure Boot + GPT **OR** Legacy BIOS + MBR |
| Default Page File | `C:\pagefile.sys` (size auto) |
| Default Hibernation File | `C:\hiberfil.sys` (≈75 % of RAM) |
| Hybrid Sleep Image | `C:\hiberfil.sys` (compressed) |
| Windows Side-by-Side | `C:\Windows\WinSxS` |
| Modern App Repository | `C:\Program Files\WindowsApps` (encrypted container folders) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Office Building"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Think of a Windows 8 computer as a **24×7 smart office building**:
> - The **UEFI firmware** is the *security gate* at the entrance — it checks every visitor's ID *before* the OS even starts.
> - The **Registry** is the *HR database* — every employee (user, service, app) has a record there, and any change is logged.
> - The **$MFT** is the *library catalogue* — it lists every file, when it was created, last accessed, and which shelf (cluster) it sits on.
> - **Modern Apps** are the *new interns* who came in 2012 — they live in a different wing (AppX container) and use a *different ID card* (WinRT contracts).
> - **Shadow Copies** are the *building's night-shift janitors* — they silently make snapshots of entire floors at scheduled times, often unknown to the original occupant.

When a forensic investigator arrives, they don't photograph the building only once; they interrogate the gate logs, audit the HR database, and interview the night-shift janitors. **That is exactly what Windows 8 forensics entails.**

## 1.3 Why Windows 8 is a "Pivotal" Subject in KTU Module 2

> [!TIP]
> Examiners love (and fear) Windows 8 because it is the **first mainstream consumer Windows that genuinely bridges desktop and mobile-paradigm code**. This duality creates *two parallel evidence universes*: the classical Win32 world and the WinRT/Modern-App world. KTU frequently tests whether the student can map a forensic question to the **correct universe**.

**The Three Forensic Novelties Introduced in Windows 8**

1. **Secure Boot + UEFI** — boot-sector rootkits are no longer trivially possible; a forensic investigator must image the EFI System Partition (ESP) separately.
2. **Modern/Metro Apps + AppX Packaging** — application data is stored inside encrypted, access-controlled `C:\Program Files\WindowsApps` folders; the user-scoped data lives in `%LOCALAPPDATA%\Packages\<PackageFamilyName>`.
3. **Reset / Refresh / Push-Button Reset (PBR)** — a user can wipe the OS partition in **two clicks**, making **pre-acquisition triage** (volatility ordering) more critical than ever.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Windows 8 Architecture (Forensic Lens)

The operating system is logically partitioned into the following layers, **each of which leaves a distinct forensic footprint**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Layer 1 — Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL) + UEFI Firmware**
> The HAL is hardware-specific code living in `C:\Windows\System32\hal.dll`. UEFI firmware stores boot entries in NVRAM (Non-Volatile RAM). Forensically, the **EFI System Partition (ESP)** is a small FAT32 partition (typically **100 MB – 500 MB**, GUID `C12A7328-F81F-11D2-BA4B-00A0C93EC93B`) that holds the **Windows Boot Manager** (`\EFI\Microsoft\Boot\bootmgfw.efi`) and the **BCD store** (`\EFI\Microsoft\Boot\BCD`).

> [!NOTE]
> **Layer 2 — Kernel (NTOSKRNL.EXE)**
> Lives at `C:\Windows\System32\ntoskrnl.exe`. Manages processes, threads, virtual memory, the I/O manager, and the **Windows Subsystem**. Critical for memory forensics — the kernel's `_EPROCESS` linked list is the canonical way to enumerate running processes from a memory dump.

> [!NOTE]
> **Layer 3 — Executive Services**
> Includes the *Object Manager*, *Configuration Manager* (which loads registry hives), *I/O Manager*, *Security Reference Monitor*, *Plug-and-Play Manager*, and *Power Manager*. Each is auditable: the *Power Manager* leaves `C:\Windows\System32\winevt\Logs\Microsoft-Windows-Kernel-Power.evtx`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Layer 4 — Win32 + WinRT Subsystems**
> Win32 = classical desktop (`kernel32.dll`, `user32.dll`, `gdi32.dll`).
> WinRT = Modern apps (`Windows.UI.Xaml.dll`, `Windows.Storage.dll`).
> Forensically, the two worlds **do not share storage** — Modern app data lives in `%LOCALAPPDATA%\Packages\<AppName>\AC` and `%LOCALAPPDATA%\Packages\<AppName>\LocalCache\Local\` whereas Win32 app data lives in `C:\Users\<User>\AppData\Roaming` or `Local`.

## 2.2 Boot & Shutdown Forensics

**Hybrid Boot (Fast Startup)** — Introduced as default in Windows 8. The shutdown sequence is actually a **log-off + hibernate**: the kernel session is written to `hiberfil.sys` and reloaded on next power-on. Forensically:

- `hiberfil.sys` is **not deleted on shutdown** (unlike Windows 7's default behaviour).
- It can be parsed with **Volatility** `imageinfo` + `hibinfo` plugins (e.g., `vol.py -f hiberfil.sys --profile=Win8SP0x86 hibinfo`).
- It frequently contains **BitLocker volume master keys**, **LSASS credential material**, and **mounted TrueCrypt/VeraCrypt headers** in memory at the moment of shutdown.

**UEFI Secure Boot Chain** — The measured-boot log (`C:\Windows\Boot\EFI\bootmgfw.efi.mbr` for the loader, plus TPM PCR banks 0–7) can attest to *which* binaries were loaded.

## 2.3 Registry Forensics — Hive Map

The Registry in Windows 8 is composed of **five root hives**, each backed by a physical file under `C:\Windows\System32\config\`:

| Hive | Mounted Under | Forensic Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `SAM` | `HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SAM` | User account **RID**, **LM/NT hash**, login counts, last login timestamp |
| `SECURITY` | `HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SECURITY` | LSA secrets, domain cached credentials, **DPAPI master key** |
| `SOFTWARE` | `HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE` | Installed software, USB device **first-install** serial, MRU typed paths, services config |
| `SYSTEM` | `HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM` | Hardware config, services, **USB device last-insert time** (`USBSTOR`), timezone |
| `DEFAULT` | `HKEY_USERS\.DEFAULT` | Default user profile (logon screen background, etc.) |

> [!TIP]
> Per-user hives — **`NTUSER.DAT`** and **`UsrClass.dat`** — live under each profile (`C:\Users\<User>\` and `C:\Users\<User>\AppData\Local\Microsoft\Windows\`). The *first* time a user logs in, `NTUSER.DAT` is **created from a template** and **stamped with that user's SID** in the registry header.

**Key Forensic Locations in `NTUSER.DAT`**

- `…\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\TypedPaths` — paths manually typed into Explorer address bar.
- `…\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\RunMRU` — last commands typed in the *Run* dialog (`Win+R`).
- `…\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\ComDlg32\OpenSavePidlMRU` — files opened/saved via standard dialog boxes.
- `…\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Applets\Paint\Recent Pictures` — recent MSPaint images.
- `…\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Search\RecentApps` — Modern app launches.
- `…\Software\Microsoft\Office\<ver>\<App>\Place MRU` — Office file MRU list.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Artifact Locations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise the following table — it is the most testable content in this module. The artifact list is sorted by forensic **evidentiary value** (HIGH → LOW).

| Artifact | Path (Windows 8) | Format | Evidentiary Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Registry SAM** | `C:\Windows\System32\config\SAM` | Binary hive | **HIGH** — account hashes |
| **$MFT** | `C:\$MFT` | Resident in NTFS metadata | **HIGH** — every file MAC times |
| **$LogFile** | `C:\$LogFile` | NTFS journal | **HIGH** — file create/delete events |
| **$UsnJrnl` ($J)** | `C:\$Extend\$UsnJrnl` | NTFS change journal | **HIGH** — file modifications |
| **Event Logs** | `C:\Windows\System32\winevt\Logs\*.evtx` | XML-based binary | **HIGH** — System, Security, App |
| **Prefetch** | `C:\Windows\Prefetch\*.pf` | Compressed binary | **MEDIUM** — program executions |
| **Superfetch** | `C:\Windows\Prefetch\Ag*.db` | ESE database | **MEDIUM** — popularity-based loads |
| **Recycle Bin** | `C:\`$Recycle.Bin\‹SID›\ | `$I` + `$R` pairs | **MEDIUM** — original path + deletion time |
| **LNK Files** | `C:\Users\‹U›\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Windows\Recent\*.lnk` | LNK v2 binary | **MEDIUM** — target path, MAC, volume serial |
| **Jump Lists** | `C:\Users\‹U›\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Windows\Recent\AutomaticDestinations\*.automaticDestinations-ms` | LNK container | **MEDIUM** — recent taskbar items |
| **Shadow Copies** | `C:\System Volume Information\‹GUID›\{…\}\` | VSS differentials | **MEDIUM-HIGH** — historical file versions |
| **Volume Shadow Copy Catalog** | `C:\System Volume Information\spp.db` | ESE database | **HIGH** — list of all restore points |
| **Thumbcache** | `C:\Users\‹U›\AppData\Local\Microsoft\Windows\Explorer\thumbcache_*.db` | ESE database | **MEDIUM** — preview of viewed images |
| **Modern App Data** | `C:\Users\‹U›\AppData\Local\Packages\‹PackageName›\AC\` | App-specific | **MEDIUM** — app usage |
| **Windows Notifications** | `C:\Users\‹U›\AppData\Local\Microsoft\Windows\Notifications\appdb.dat` | ESE database | **LOW-MEDIUM** — toast notifications |
| **Hiberfil** | `C:\hiberfil.sys` | Raw memory | **HIGH** — memory at shutdown |
| **Pagefile** | `C:\pagefile.sys` | Raw memory | **HIGH** — process memory pages |
| **BitLocker Recovery** | `C:\Windows\System32\Recovery\*.BEK`, `C:\Windows\System32\reagent.xml` | Encrypted blob | **HIGH** — needed to decrypt volume |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU students often confuse `$LogFile` and `$UsnJrnl`. Remember:
> - **`$LogFile`** records low-level NTFS metadata changes (file create, delete, rename, attribute update) → used to **recover deleted files**.
> - **`$UsnJrnl` ($J)** records user-data changes (file content modifications) → used to **build a timeline of edits**.

## 2.5 Logical & Physical Disk Layout

A default Windows 8 installation on a UEFI/GPT disk has **at least 4 partitions**:

| # | Type | Filesystem | Typical Size | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Recovery | NTFS (hidden) | **350–500 MB** | WinRE (Windows Recovery Environment) |
| 2 | EFI System Partition | **FAT32** | **100 MB** | `\EFI\Microsoft\Boot\bootmgfw.efi` + BCD |
| 3 | MSR (Microsoft Reserved) | RAW | **128 MB** | Alignment; otherwise invisible |
| 4 | Windows (OS) | NTFS | rest of disk | `C:\` — actual OS volume |

A Legacy/BIOS + MBR installation has only 2 partitions (System Reserved ≈ **350 MB**, then Windows).

> [!TIP]
> Real-world forensic rule: **always image the EFI System Partition separately**, especially when investigating a UEFI-level bootkit or compromised firmware.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of Windows 8 Forensics

- **Incident Response (IR):** triage of compromised endpoints with fast PowerShell + KAPE-style acquisition.
- **Litigation / e-Discovery:** produces defensible artifacts admissible in court (chain of custody over `$MFT` timestamps).
- **Insider Threat Hunting:** identifies use of `Reset` (a *wiping anti-forensic* primitive) by cross-correlating `System Volume Information\spp.db` and the latest install date in `SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion`.
- **Child Exploitation / CSAM investigations:** the **Thumbnail Cache** and **Search index** are critical because they may still contain previews even if the original file is moved to an external drive.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Timeline Math & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Decoding the Windows FILETIME Timestamp (KTU High-Yield)

Every NTFS timestamp, every registry `RegQueryInfoKey` last-write, every `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` entry, every event-log record — **all** use the **Windows FILETIME** format: a **64-bit unsigned integer** representing the number of **100-nanosecond intervals** since **12:00 AM, 1 January 1601 (UTC)**.

The reason 1601 (and not 1970) was chosen is that the Gregorian calendar has a **400-year leap cycle**; 1601 is the first year of the cycle, which made mod-400 arithmetic convenient in the early 16-bit Word processors that became the ancestor of NT.

**The conversion identity:**

$$
\text{UnixEpoch} = \frac{\text{FILETIME} - \text{EpochOffset}}{\text{TicksPerSecond}}
$$

where the constants are:

$$
\text{EpochOffset} = 116\,444\,736\,000\,000\,000 \quad \text{(100 ns ticks from 1601-01-01 to 1970-01-01)}
$$

$$
\text{TicksPerSecond} = 10\,000\,000
$$

**Step-by-Step Worked Example**

Suppose the `$MFT` standard-information attribute of `C:\Users\Alice\Desktop\evidence.docx` contains the **last-write** timestamp in raw little-endian bytes: `0x01 D5 3C 8E 6F 0F D9 01`.

We reconstruct the **64-bit value**:

$$
\text{FILETIME} = 0x01\text{D9}0\text{F6F}8E3\text{CD5}01
$$

Converting to decimal (showing the 4 step hops):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FILETIME}_{\text{dec}} &= (0x01\,\text{D9}) \cdot 2^{48} + (0x0\text{F}\,\text{6F}) \cdot 2^{32} + (0x8E\,3C) \cdot 2^{16} + 0xD5\,01 \\[4pt]
&= 0x01\text{D9}0\text{F6F}8E3\text{CD5}01_{\text{hex}} \\[4pt]
&= 132\,587\,286\,412\,345\,601 \;\text{ 100-ns ticks}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now subtract the **1601 → 1970 epoch offset**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{\text{ticks}} &= 132\,587\,286\,412\,345\,601 - 116\,444\,736\,000\,000\,000 \\[4pt]
&= 16\,142\,550\,412\,345\,601 \;\text{ ticks}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert to seconds (divide by $10^{7}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{seconds}} &= \frac{16\,142\,550\,412\,345\,601}{10^{000\,0000}} \\[4pt]
&= 1\,614\,255\,041.2345601 \;\text{ seconds since 1970-01-01 UTC}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert seconds to a calendar date:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Years} &\approx 51.16 \;\text{y} \Rightarrow 2021 \\[4pt]
\text{Remaining days} &\approx 58 \\[4pt]
\text{Result} &\approx \textbf{2021-02-27 11:30:41 UTC}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> For an instant mental check on exam day, the **hand-rule** is: drop the last 7 digits, treat the rest as a *Unix epoch*, then add 51 years. Anything after `0x01D9…` is in the 2010s+.

## 3.2 The NTFS $MFT Entry Anatomy (How an Investigator Reads a File)

Every file and directory on an NTFS volume is represented by a single **$MFT entry** (typically **1024 bytes**). A Windows 8 forensic examiner parses each entry in this fixed order:

| Offset (hex) | Field | Length | Forensic Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `0x00` | Signature `"FILE"` | 4 bytes | Entry validity check |
| `0x04` | Update Sequence offset | 2 bytes | USN fix-up location |
| `0x06` | Update Sequence size | 2 bytes | USN length |
| `0x08` | $LogFile Sequence Number ($LSN) | 8 bytes | Journal position |
| `0x10` | Sequence number | 2 bytes | Increments per re-use |
| `0x12` | Hard-link count | 2 bytes | Number of directory links |
| `0x14` | First attribute offset | 2 bytes | Start of attribute list |
| `0x16` | Flags (`0x01` = in-use, `0x02` = directory) | 2 bytes | State |
| `0x18` | Used size of entry | 4 bytes | Real payload bytes |
| `0x1C` | Allocated size | 4 bytes | Slot size (=1024) |
| `0x20` | File reference to base record | 8 bytes | For extended attributes |
| `0x28` | Next attribute ID | 2 bytes | Allocator |
| `0x2A` | Padding | 2 bytes | Reserved |
| `0x2C` | **MFT Record Number** | 4 bytes | Unique file ID |
| `+offsets…` | Attribute sequence | variable | `$STANDARD_INFORMATION`, `$FILE_NAME`, `$DATA`, etc. |

**Derivation of the "Date Stamp" Triple in `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` (Attribute 0x10)**

NTFS stores **four** MAC-like timestamps, but the first three are **user-modifiable** (and therefore defeatable) and the last one is **system-maintained** (and authoritative):

$$
\text{SI Attribute} = \{\,M_{c},\;M_{m},\;M_{a},\;M_{e}\,\}
$$

where

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{c} &= \text{File Creation Time} \quad \text{(modifiable)} \\
M_{m} &= \text{File Modification Time} \quad \text{(modifiable)} \\
M_{a} &= \text{MFT Entry Last-Access Time} \ \text{(default: NOT updated for performance on Win 8)} \\
M_{e} &= \text{MFT Entry Modification Time} \quad \text{(system-managed — ANTI-FORENSIC resistant)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU favourite question: *"Why is `LastAccessTime` not updated in Windows 8 by default?"* — Answer: the NTFS driver treats the per-file access-time update as an *expensive* metadata write (forces a log-file flush). To speed up file searches on mechanical HDDs, Microsoft set `NtfsDisableLastAccessUpdate = 1` as the **default in Windows 8 and later**. Investigators therefore have to use `$M_e` (MFT modification) and `$FILE_NAME` (Attribute 0x30) timestamps for chronology.

## 3.3 Timeline Super-Timeline Construction (Step-by-Step)

A *super-timeline* merges **MFT + $LogFile + $UsnJrnl + Event Logs + Registry + Prefetch + LNK + Jump Lists** into a single CSV. The body formula:

$$
T_{\text{super}} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n}\, \text{sort}_{\text{FILETIME}}\bigl(\,A_{i}\,\bigr)
$$

where each $A_{i}$ is the timestamped record set from source $i$ (e.g., $A_{1}=$$MFT$, $A_{2}=$$$LogFile$, $A_{3}=$$EventLog$, …).

**Step-by-step forensic workflow:**

1. **Acquire** a forensically sound image of the Windows 8 volume (e.g., `FTK Imager` → `.E01`, hashed with SHA-256).
2. **Extract** `$MFT`, `$LogFile`, `$UsnJrnl:$J`, registry hives, event logs, and the Recycle Bin into a working directory.
3. **Parse** each artifact with a dedicated tool:

$$
\begin{aligned}
MFT &\xrightarrow{\text{MFTECmd.py}} \text{CSV} \\
LogFile &\xrightarrow{\text{LogFileParser}} \text{CSV} \\
UsnJrnl &\xrightarrow{\text{MFTECmd -f ... --usn}} \text{CSV} \\
Evtx &\xrightarrow{\text{evtx_dump / EvtxECmd}} \text{CSV} \\
Registry &\xrightarrow{\text{RegistryExplorer/RegRipper}} \text{CSV} \\
Prefetch &\xrightarrow{\text{PECmd}} \text{CSV} \\
LNK &\xrightarrow{\text{LECmd}} \text{CSV} \\
JumpLists &\xrightarrow{\text{JLECmd}} \text{CSV}
\end{aligned}
$$

4. **Normalize** every record to a single tuple `(timestamp_utc, source, message, artifact_path)`.
5. **Sort** the merged CSV by `timestamp_utc` (the timestamp column).
6. **Filter** on the incident window (e.g., `2024-08-15 12:00 → 2024-08-16 12:00`).
7. **Hypothesize** events (e.g., "USB inserted → file copy → file modification → LNK click → process execution via Prefetch").

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping a Forensic Question to the Correct Artifact

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This 5×5 matrix is a *high-yield KTU pattern*. Examiners frequently ask: *"To prove X, which artifact would you examine?"* Use this matrix to construct a clean answer.

| User Activity (Question) | Primary Artifact | Secondary Artifact | Registry Key | Event Log ID |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **USB device was inserted** | `SYSTEM\MountedDevices` + `USBSTOR` | `$MFT` of `setupapi.dev.log` | `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USBSTOR` | 20001 / 20003 |
| **File was downloaded from web** | Browser DB (Chrome/Edge) | `$MFT` MAC times of download folder | `NTUSER.DAT\…\TypedURLs` | — |
| **External drive was used** | `SYSTEM\MountedDevices` | `$LogFile` | `HKLM\SYSTEM\MountedDevices` | 20001 |
| **Program was executed** | `Prefetch\*.pf` (last run) | `RecentApps` registry / LNK | `…\Explorer\RunMRU` | 4688 (with cmd line) |
| **File was deleted** | `Recycle Bin $I` file | `$LogFile` journal entry | — | 4660 |

## 3.5 Why the `$MFT` Can Be Used to *Count* Files (Derivation)

A classic KTU numerical: given a suspect's NTFS volume of $S$ bytes, cluster size $c$ bytes, and an $MFT$ of $N$ records of $r$ bytes each, derive the *minimum* time to linearly walk the $MFT$:

$$
T_{\text{walk}} = \frac{N \cdot r}{R_{\text{disk}}}
$$

with $R_{\text{disk}}$ being the sequential read throughput (≈ **150 MB/s** for a 7,200-RPM HDD). For $N = 1\,200\,000$ records and $r = 1024$ bytes:

$$
T_{\text{walk}} = \frac{1\,200\,000 \times 1024}{150 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{1.2288 \times 10^{9}}{1.5 \times 10^{8}} \approx 8.19 \;\text{seconds}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, MFT reads are *not* purely sequential (the $MFT$ itself can be fragmented), so realistic walk-times are 2–5× the linear estimate. This is the **engineering justification** for using `MFTECmd` to stream the parsed output rather than repeatedly opening files in a hex editor.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Windows 8 Forensics — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HW["HARDWARE LAYER"]
        CPU[CPU]
        RAM[RAM DIMM]
        DISK[GPT Partitioned Disk]
        NIC[NIC]
    end

    subgraph FW["FIRMWARE LAYER"]
        UEFI[UEFI Secure Boot]
        ESP[EFI System Partition - FAT32]
    end

    subgraph KERN["KERNEL LAYER"]
        NTOS[ntoskrnl.exe]
        HAL[hal.dll]
        DRV[Drivers .sys]
    end

    subgraph SUBSYS["SUBSYSTEM LAYER"]
        WIN32[Win32 Subsystem]
        WINRT[WinRT Subsystem]
        POWMGR[Power Manager]
    end

    subgraph FS["FILE SYSTEM LAYER"]
        NTFS[NTFS Volume C:]
        MFT[$MFT]
        LOG[$LogFile]
        USN[$UsnJrnl]
        VSS[Volume Shadow Copy]
    end

    subgraph REG["REGISTRY LAYER"]
        SAM[HKLM\\SAM]
        SEC[HKLM\\SECURITY]
        SW[HKLM\\SOFTWARE]
        SYS[HKLM\\SYSTEM]
        NTU[NTUSER.DAT]
    end

    subgraph ART["USER ARTIFACT LAYER"]
        EVT[Event Logs .evtx]
        PREF[Prefetch .pf]
        LNK[LNK Files]
        JL[Jump Lists]
        RBIN[Recycle Bin]
        APPX[Modern App Data]
        THUMB[Thumbcache]
    end

    CPU --> NTOS
    RAM --> NTOS
    DISK --> ESP
    DISK --> NTFS
    NIC --> DRV

    UEFI --> NTOS
    ESP --> UEFI
    HAL --> NTOS
    NTOS --> DRV
    NTOS --> WIN32
    NTOS --> WINRT
    NTOS --> POWMGR

    WIN32 --> NTFS
    WINRT --> APPX
    NTFS --> MFT
    NTFS --> LOG
    NTFS --> USN
    NTFS --> VSS

    NTOS --> REG
    NTU --> REG

    WIN32 --> EVT

    style HW fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0

    style FS fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#ad1457
    style REG fill:#fffde7,stroke:#f9a825
    style ART fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#00838f
```

## 4.2 Windows 8 Boot & Acquisition Flow (Forensic Triage)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power On] --> B[UEFI Firmware Verifies Boot Chain]
    B --> C{Secure Boot Enforced}
    C -- yes --> D[Load bootmgfw.efi from ESP]
    C -- no --> E[Fallback Legacy MBR Boot]
    D --> F[BCD Parsed - winload.efi started]
    F --> G[NTOSKRNL Loaded - HAL Initialised]
    G --> H[Registry Hives Mounted]
    H --> I[SMSS.exe - Session Manager]
    I --> J[Win32 + WinRT Subsystems Started]
    J --> K{Forensic Action Required}
    K -- live triage --> L[Volatile: netstat, pslist, kdb, hiberfil memory]
    K -- post mortem --> M[Power Off and Image C: with FTK]
    L --> N[Acquire Memory - winpmem / FTK Imager Lite]
    M --> O[SHA-256 Hash the Image]
    N --> P[Parse with Volatility]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Timeline + Artifact Analysis]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — Artifact Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q0[Investigator Query: Prove X] --> Q1{Type of Activity}
    Q1 -- file system --> Q2{NTFS Activity}
    Q2 -- creation --> A1[$MFT M_c timestamp]
    Q2 -- deletion --> A2[$LogFile + Recycle Bin $I]
    Q2 -- modification --> A3[$UsnJrnl + $M_e]
    Q2 -- access time --> A4[NOT reliable on Win8 default]

    Q1 -- user activity --> Q3{What did the user do}
    Q3 -- typed URL --> B1[TypedURLs in NTUSER.DAT]
    Q3 -- ran program --> B2[Prefetch .pf]
    Q3 -- opened file --> B3[LNK in Recent folder]
    Q3 -- plugged USB --> B4[SYSTEM MountedDevices + USBSTOR]

    Q1 -- system event --> Q4{Kernel or Service}
    Q4 -- service start --> C1[Event 7036]
    Q4 -- logon logoff --> C2[Security 4624 4625 4634]
    Q4 -- scheduled task --> C3[TaskCache registry tree]

    style A1 fill:#bbdefb
    style A2 fill:#bbdefb
    style A3 fill:#bbdefb
    style A4 fill:#ffcdd2
    style B1 fill:#c8e6c9
    style B2 fill:#c8e6c9
    style B3 fill:#c8e6c9
    style B4 fill:#c8e6c9
    style C1 fill:#fff9c4
    style C2 fill:#fff9c4
    style C3 fill:#fff9c4
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above are designed for **maximum printability** — every node label is plain uppercase, no formatting tags, no reserved keywords. The layered architecture visually maps the **layer-to-evidence** relationship: firmware → ESP image, kernel → memory image, file system → `$MFT`/`$LogFile`/`$UsnJrnl`, registry → hive files, user artifacts → profile folder.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 2]]**
> **Differentiate between the `SYSTEM` and `SOFTWARE` registry hives in Windows 8. What is the primary forensic value of each when investigating a suspected USB exfiltration case? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- The `SYSTEM` hive (`C:\Windows\System32\config\SYSTEM`) stores **hardware configuration** and device installation records, including the `Enum\USBSTOR` sub-tree and `MountedDevices`, which record the **serial number, vendor/product IDs, and last-insert time** of every USB mass-storage device. **(1 Mark)**
- The `SOFTWARE` hive stores the **logical** counterpart: installed software metadata, MRU lists of typed paths in Explorer, and the `Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\USBSTOR` first-insert timestamps. **(1 Mark)**
- In a USB exfiltration case, `SYSTEM` proves the *device* was attached; `SOFTWARE` proves the *software* that ran during the exfiltration. Cross-correlating both is the **forensically correct method.** **(1 Mark)**

---

> **[Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]]**
> **Explain the Windows 8 "Hybrid Boot" (Fast Startup) feature. Why is it forensically significant? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- Hybrid Boot **replaces the traditional shutdown with a hibernate of the kernel session**: the contents of the kernel and loaded drivers are written into `C:\hiberfil.sys` and the system is **powered off**, not fully shut down. On next power-on, the kernel state is reloaded instead of re-initialised. **(1 Mark)**
- Forensically, this means `hiberfil.sys` **always contains a memory snapshot** of the OS at the moment the user clicked "Shut Down" — including the **BitLocker FVEK**, **LSASS authentication tokens**, **open file handles**, and **encryption key material** in RAM. **(1 Mark)**
- Investigators should **always image `hiberfil.sys`** and parse it with the `hibinfo` and `hashdump` plugins of **Volatility**, even when the user claims the machine was "off" at the time of seizure. **(1 Mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice)

> ### ⬛ Question A — 14 Marks  [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Series I]

**A (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **layered architecture of Windows 8** from a forensic-investigator's perspective. For each layer, list the **primary artifact** that an investigator should image. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**
1. **Hardware Layer** — CPU, RAM, Disk, NIC. Artifact: raw RAM (via `winpmem`), NIC capture (`tcpdump` on a SPAN port). **(1 Mark)**
2. **Firmware / UEFI Layer** — `bootmgfw.efi` in the EFI System Partition (FAT32, GUID `C12A7328-F81F-11D2-BA4B-00A0C93EC93B`), BCD store, NVRAM boot entries. Artifact: full image of the ESP. **(1 Mark)**
3. **Kernel Layer** — `ntoskrnl.exe`, `hal.dll`, drivers. Artifact: memory dump for the `_EPROCESS` linked list. **(1 Mark)**
4. **Subsystem Layer** — Win32 + WinRT. Win32 produces Event Logs, Prefetch, LNK, Jump Lists. WinRT produces Modern App Data (`%LOCALAPPDATA%\Packages`). **(2 Marks)**
5. **File-System Layer (NTFS)** — `$MFT`, `$LogFile`, `$UsnJrnl`, `pagefile.sys`, `hiberfil.sys`, Volume Shadow Copies. **(1 Mark)**
6. **Registry Layer** — `SAM`, `SECURITY`, `SOFTWARE`, `SYSTEM`, `DEFAULT`, plus per-user `NTUSER.DAT` and `UsrClass.dat`. **(1 Mark)**

---

**A (b)** An investigator suspects that an employee named *"Rahul"* used an unauthorised **USB pen-drive** to copy a confidential `clientlist.xlsx` from a Windows 8 workstation on **17 August 2024** between **14:00 and 16:00 hours**. List, in correct **forensic order**, every artifact you would examine, the **specific registry key or file path**, and explain **why** each artifact is required. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**
1. **Device Presence Proof:** `SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USBSTOR\Ven_Prod_‹Serial›` — shows the pen-drive's VID/PID and **device last-insert timestamp**. **(1 Mark)**
2. **Drive Letter Mounting:** `SYSTEM\MountedDevices` — shows the **volume serial number** of the USB mapped to `E:` or `F:`. **(1 Mark)**
3. **File Copy Evidence:** the `setupapi.dev.log` at `C:\Windows\INF\setupapi.dev.log` records the **install time of the USB driver**. **(1 Mark)**
4. **File Modification MAC Times:** the `$MFT` record of `C:\Users\Rahul\Desktop\clientlist.xlsx` will have `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` MAC times with `$M_a$` and `$M_e$` updated within the suspect window. **(1 Mark)**
5. **NTFS Journaling:** `$LogFile` and `$UsnJrnl` will both contain entries for the file's create/modify during 14:00–16:00. **(1 Mark)**
6. **Recycle Bin / LNK:** if Rahul then deleted the file from the USB, the corresponding `$R` and `$I` files in the USB's `$Recycle.Bin` will yield original-path and deletion time. A desktop `clientlist.xlsx.lnk` will appear in Rahul's *Recent* folder. **(1 Mark)**
7. **Event Log:** Security Event ID **4663** (file system access attempted) and `Microsoft-Windows-Kernel-Pnp/Configuration` Event ID **20001/20003** will corroborate the timeline. **(1 Mark)**

---

> ### ⬛ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**B (a)** Explain the **NTFS Master File Table ($MFT)** structure of Windows 8. With suitable diagrams, describe the **first 48 bytes of an MFT record** and the **$STANDARD_INFORMATION attribute** (Attribute Type `0x10`). Why is the **`$M_e` (MFT modification) timestamp** forensically more reliable than `$M_c$`, `$M_m$`, or `$M_a`? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**
- An NTFS volume logically contains a hidden table called the `$MFT` located at the *MFT Data Attribute* of the **$MFT metafile** (MFT record **#0**). Each user file/directory occupies a record of typically **1024 bytes**. **(1 Mark)**
- The first **48 bytes** (header) contain: `FILE` signature (4 B), USN offset (2 B), USN size (2 B), LSN (8 B), sequence number (2 B), hard-link count (2 B), first-attribute offset (2 B), flags (2 B), used size (4 B), allocated size (4 B), base-record reference (8 B), next attribute ID (2 B), MFT record number (4 B). **(2 Marks)**
- The `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` attribute (Type `0x10`) stores **four 64-bit FILETIME** values: `$M_c$` (creation), `$M_m$` (modification), `$M_a$` (last access), and `$M_e$` (MFT entry modification), plus four more for NTFS quota and security descriptor changes. **(1 Mark)**
- `$M_c$`, `$M_m$`, `$M_a$` are **user-modifiable** through APIs like `SetFileTime()` and are routinely changed by installers, archivers, and anti-forensic tools. **`$M_e$` is updated only by the NTFS driver** in response to a *real* change to the file's metadata, and cannot be set independently by user-mode code. **(2 Marks)**
- Therefore `$M_e$` is the **authoritative timestamp** for forensic chronology. Furthermore, in Windows 8, the `$M_a$` value is *not* updated by default (`NtfsDisableLastAccessUpdate = 1`), making the last-access field **unreliable by design** and forcing investigators to rely on `$M_e$` and `$UsnJrnl`. **(1 Mark)**

---

**B (b)** Discuss the forensic value of the **Event Log (`*.evtx`)** subsystem in Windows 8. List **at least 8 critical Event IDs** and the forensic question each one answers. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**
- Windows 8 stores events in **binary XML** files (`.evtx`) under `C:\Windows\System32\winevt\Logs\`. The three canonical logs are **System**, **Security**, and **Application**, plus hundreds of *Operational* and *Analytic* logs in `Microsoft-Windows-*` subfolders. **(1 Mark)**
- **Critical Event IDs and their forensic value:** **(6 Marks) — list any 8 for full marks**

| Event ID | Log | Forensic Question Answered |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **4624** | Security | Successful logon (with logon type, source IP, user) |
| **4625** | Security | Failed logon (often the first sign of brute-force) |
| **4634/4647** | Security | Logoff / user-initiated logoff |
| **4663** | Security | File accessed (with object name + access mask) |
| **4688** | Security | Process created (parent → child lineage; **command-line auditing**) |
| **4698/4702** | Security | Scheduled task created / updated |
| **4720/4722/4723/4724/4725/4726/4738** | Security | Account create/enable/pwd-change/disable/delete/modify |
| **1102** | Security | **Audit log was cleared** — strong anti-forensic indicator |
| **20001/20003** | `Microsoft-Windows-Kernel-Pnp/Device Configuration` | USB device install / removal |
| **7034/7035/7036** | System | Service unexpected exit / state change |
| **104** | `Microsoft-Windows-Eventlog` | Event log was cleared |
| **6005/6006/6008/6009** | System | Boot / clean shutdown / unexpected shutdown / OS info |
| **1001** | Application | Application crash (BSOD → bug-check code) |

- **Examiner's note:** Event **1102** ("The audit log was cleared") and Event **104** are *forensic gold* — they prove the user or attacker attempted to **destroy evidence**, which is itself admissible as consciousness of guilt. **(Bonus, +1 line)**

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing `$LogFile` with `$UsnJrnl`** — they record *different* things. `$LogFile` = metadata journal (create/delete/rename); `$UsnJrnl` = user-data changes (file content). Half-mark penalty per confusion in any 14-mark answer.
> 2. **Forgetting the EFI System Partition (ESP)** — many students image only `C:\` and miss the UEFI evidence entirely. Examiners will deduct **1 mark** per 14-mark answer if ESP is not mentioned in a UEFI-related question.
> 3. **Assuming `LastAccessTime` is updated** — Windows 8 disables this by default. Stating otherwise is a **factual error** worth 1 mark.
> 4. **Using Python code in a non-CS theory question** — even though `MFTECmd` is a Python tool, *do not paste code* in a KTU theory exam. Name the tool and the inputs only.
> 5. **Ignoring the 1601-vs-1970 epoch** when computing a timestamp — examiners award 1 mark for the **correct epoch offset** mention.
> 6. **Mixing up `%APPDATA%` vs `%LOCALAPPDATA%`** — `Roaming` lives in `…\AppData\Roaming`, `Local` in `…\AppData\Local`. Forensically, `Roaming` is what syncs to a domain server; `Local` is machine-only.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Architecture (5 layers):** Firmware (UEFI/ESP) → Kernel (NTOSKRNL) → Subsystem (Win32+WinRT) → File System (NTFS) → User Artifacts. Each layer ⇒ a corresponding acquisition step.
- **Key new feature in Win 8:** **Hybrid Boot** ⇒ `hiberfil.sys` always present, contains RAM at shutdown.
- **5 Registry hives (machine scope):** `SAM`, `SECURITY`, `SOFTWARE`, `SYSTEM`, `DEFAULT` — all live in `C:\Windows\System32\config\`.
- **2 per-user hives:** `NTUSER.DAT` and `UsrClass.dat` — live under each profile.
- **NTFS core metadata files:** `$MFT` (MFT), `$LogFile` (metadata journal), `$MFTMirr` (MFT backup copy #1), `$Bitmap` (cluster allocation), `$Boot` (boot sector), `$BadClus` (bad clusters), `$Secure` (security descriptors), `$UpCase` (Unicode uppercase table), `$Extend` (extension namespace containing `$UsnJrnl`, `$Quota`, `$ObjId`, `$Reparse`).
- **Timestamp triad:** `$M_c$`, `$M_m$`, `$M_a$` (user-mode mutable) vs `$M_e$` (kernel-managed — trustworthy). Last access time is **disabled by default** in Windows 8.
- **FILETIME conversion identity:** $\text{Unix} = (\text{FILETIME} - 116\,444\,736\,000\,000\,000) \div 10\,000\,000$.
- **Modern Apps:** data under `%LOCALAPPDATA%\Packages\‹PackageFamilyName›\`; main package under `C:\Program Files\WindowsApps\` (encrypted ACL).
- **Volume Shadow Copy:** `C:\System Volume Information\‹GUID›\{…}\` — restores prior file versions; catalog in `spp.db` (ESE).
- **Recycle Bin:** `$I` (info) and `$R` (renamed original) pairs in `C:\$Recycle.Bin\‹SID›\`. `$I` stores the **original full path** and **deletion timestamp**.
- **Event Log:** `.evtx` files; 4624 (logon), 4625 (failed logon), 4663 (file access), 4688 (process create), 4698 (scheduled task), 1102 (log cleared).
- **Prefetch:** `C:\Windows\Prefetch\*.pf` — first 30 + last 8 run times of every program (max 1024 entries).
- **Super-timeline:** sort the union of all parsed artifact CSVs by FILETIME to construct the master chronology.
- **Toolchain mantra:** image with **FTK Imager**, parse with **MFTECmd / EvtxECmd / RegRipper / PECmd / LECmd / JLECmd**, analyse with **TimelineExplorer**, recover memory with **Volatility**.
- **Anti-forensic detection flags:** Event 1102 (log clear), use of `cipher /w:`, presence of `sdelete`, **Reset** push-button reset, `fsutil usn deletejournal`, and absence of `$LogFile` updates.
- **Always image:** the entire disk (or the *system* partition AND the ESP) — never just the `C:\` folder. SHA-256 hash the source and the image; chain of custody.
- **KTU answer-writing tip:** for any 7-mark part, use a **diagram + bullet list + example Event ID / registry path** pattern. Examiners reward specificity (a real path) over generality.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Windows 10, Examining UNIX and Linux Disk Structures and Boot Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT602 - CYBER FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Windows 10, Examining UNIX and Linux Disk Structures and Boot Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Windows 10 Disk Structure — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Windows 10 Disk Architecture):**
> The Windows 10 disk structure is the logical and physical organization of data on a storage medium, governed by a **partitioning scheme** (MBR or GPT), a **volume management layer**, and a **file system** (primarily **NTFS — New Technology File System**, with legacy support for **FAT32** and **exFAT**). The structure defines how files, directories, metadata, security descriptors, and the Master File Table (MFT) are stored, addressed, and retrieved.

**Key Components of a Windows 10 Disk:**

| Layer | Description |
|---|---|
| **Physical Disk** | The raw hardware (HDD, SSD, NVMe). |
| **Partition Table** | **MBR** (Master Boot Record, 512-byte legacy) or **GPT** (GUID Partition Table, modern UEFI-based). |
| **Volume** | A logical container formatted with a filesystem. |
| **Filesystem** | NTFS, FAT32, exFAT, or ReFS (Resilient File System). |
| **Cluster** | The smallest unit of disk allocation (default **4 KB** on NTFS). |
| **Sector** | The smallest physical unit (**512 bytes** legacy, **4096 bytes** on Advanced Format drives). |
| **MFT (Master File Table)** | NTFS metadata file that records every file and directory. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — Windows Disk as a Library:**
> Think of a Windows 10 disk as a vast, multi-floor library. The **partition table** is the building's blueprint (telling you which floor is which). The **filesystem (NTFS)** is the cataloging system used by the librarians. The **Master File Table (MFT)** is the master index card catalog — every single book (file) has a card listing its title, author, location (sector/cluster), and access permissions. The **clusters** are the actual bookshelf compartments, and **sectors** are the individual pages within each compartment.

### 1.2 Windows 10 Boot Process — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Windows 10 Boot Process):**
> The Windows 10 boot process is the sequence of firmware-level and operating-system-level operations that initializes hardware, locates the OS kernel (`ntoskrnl.exe`), loads the **System Registry Hive**, starts core services, and finally presents the **Logon Screen (winlogon.exe)**. It transitions from **POST → Firmware (BIOS/UEFI) → Boot Manager → Windows Boot Loader → Kernel → Session Manager → Logon**.

**Key Boot Phases (Windows 10):**
1. **Power-On Self-Test (POST)** — Hardware diagnostic by firmware.
2. **Firmware Initialization** — **BIOS** (Legacy) or **UEFI** (Unified Extensible Firmware Interface).
3. **Boot Manager** (`bootmgr`) — Reads the **BCD (Boot Configuration Data)** store.
4. **Windows Boot Loader** (`winload.exe`) — Loads the OS kernel.
5. **NT Kernel** (`ntoskrnl.exe` + `hal.dll`) — Initializes drivers, memory manager, scheduler.
6. **Session Manager** (`smss.exe`) — Starts the Windows subsystem.
7. **Logon Process** (`winlogon.exe`) — Presents the secure attention sequence (**Ctrl+Alt+Del**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — Windows 10 Boot as Waking a Smart Building:**
> Imagine a smart office building powering up in the morning. **POST** is the safety inspection (checking elevators, fire alarms). **BIOS/UEFI** is the central control room that decides which tenant's space to open. **Boot Manager (bootmgr)** reads the tenant directory (BCD). **Windows Boot Loader (winload.exe)** is the building supervisor who unlocks the main office (loads the kernel). **NT Kernel** turns on the lights, HVAC, and security cameras (drivers + services). Finally, **winlogon.exe** opens the front desk (Logon Screen) for employees to authenticate.

### 1.3 UNIX/Linux Disk Structure — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (UNIX/Linux Disk Architecture):**
> The UNIX/Linux disk structure refers to the organization of a block storage device under the Linux **Virtual Filesystem Switch (VFS)** layer, partitioned typically via **MBR** or **GPT**, formatted with a native filesystem such as **ext2**, **ext3**, **ext4**, **XFS**, **Btrfs**, or **ZFS**. Every file is represented by an **inode (index node)** containing its metadata, with the file's actual data blocks referenced via direct, indirect, double-indirect, and triple-indirect pointers. The **superblock** at a fixed offset stores critical filesystem metadata.

**Key Components of a Linux Disk:**

| Layer | Description |
|---|---|
| **Device File** | `/dev/sda`, `/dev/nvme0n1` — abstraction of the physical disk. |
| **Partition Table** | MBR or GPT. |
| **Filesystem** | ext2/ext3/ext4, XFS, Btrfs, ZFS. |
| **Block Group** | Logical grouping of inodes and data blocks (ext-family). |
| **Superblock** | Filesystem metadata header (size, inodes count, free blocks). |
| **Inode** | Metadata record for a file/directory (**128 bytes** or **256 bytes**). |
| **Data Block** | Smallest allocation unit (typically **1 KB**, **2 KB**, or **4 KB**). |
| **Directory Entry** | Maps human-readable names to inode numbers. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — Linux Disk as a Museum Archive:**
> Picture a museum's underground archive. The **superblock** is the master ledger on the wall, listing the total number of artifacts, the archive rooms, and the curator's name. The **inodes** are individual artifact tags — each tag has a unique ID and describes the artifact (size, color, era) but does NOT contain the artifact itself. The **data blocks** are the actual climate-controlled storage rooms. The **directory entries** are the signboards above each doorway, telling visitors "Room 42" (filename) maps to "Artifact Tag #1847" (inode). The **VFS layer** is the museum's front desk — no matter which archive (ext4, XFS, Btrfs) is used, the front desk gives you a uniform ticket.

### 1.4 UNIX/Linux Boot Process — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Linux/UNIX Boot Process):**
> The Linux boot process is the controlled sequence initiated by firmware that loads and executes a **bootloader** (GRUB, LILO, systemd-boot), which loads the **Linux kernel** (`vmlinuz`) and an **initial RAM disk (initramfs)**, transfers control to the kernel, mounts the root filesystem, and finally executes the first user-space process — traditionally `/sbin/init`, now typically **`/sbin/systemd`** (PID 1) — which brings the system to the desired **runlevel/target** (multi-user, graphical, etc.).

**Key Boot Phases (Linux):**
1. **Power-On Self-Test (POST)** — Firmware-level hardware check.
2. **Firmware (BIOS or UEFI)** — Identifies boot device.
3. **Bootloader** — **GRUB2** (GRand Unified Bootloader v2) is default on most distros.
4. **Kernel Loading** — `vmlinuz-<version>` is decompressed and executed.
5. **initramfs / initrd** — Temporary rootfs containing essential drivers.
6. **Root Filesystem Mount** — Real `/` is mounted from the actual partition.
7. **PID 1 / systemd** — First user-space process; brings up services and targets.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — Linux Boot as Launching a Rocket:**
> A rocket launch is a perfect metaphor. **POST** is the engineering checklist. **Firmware (BIOS/UEFI)** is mission control authorizing ignition. **GRUB2** is the launch director selecting the mission profile. The **kernel** is the rocket itself (the core engine). **initramfs** is the detachable booster pack that gets the rocket through the atmosphere (providng temporary storage and drivers). **Mounting the real root filesystem** is the staging that releases the booster. **systemd (PID 1)** is the mission commander who deploys payloads (services) according to the flight plan (target unit).

### 1.5 Forensics Significance — Why This Matters in Cyber Forensics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Forensic Relevance:**
> - **Windows 10** dominates enterprise/consumer endpoints → NTFS artifacts (MFT, USN Journal, $LogFile, $MFTMirr, Alternate Data Streams) are primary evidence sources.
> - **Linux/UNIX** dominates servers, cloud, IoT, and mobile (Android) → inode analysis, log files under `/var/log`, bash history (`~/.bash_history`), and `/proc` are critical evidence sources.
> - The **boot process** leaves artifacts (Prefetch, Shimcache, Amcache on Windows; `dmesg`, `journalctl`, GRUB logs on Linux) that establish **timeline of execution** and prove/anti-alibi.
