## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Windows 10 Disk Architecture — Layered Theory

#### 2.1.1 Partitioning Schemes

**MBR (Master Boot Record):**
- Located at **sector 0** of the disk (**first 512 bytes**).
- Contains: **446 bytes** boot code + **64 bytes** partition table (4 entries × 16 bytes) + **2 bytes** magic signature `0x55AA`.
- **Maximum disk size:** **2 TiB** (2^32 × 512 bytes).
- **Maximum partitions:** 4 primary, or 3 primary + 1 extended (with unlimited logical inside).
- **Boot mode:** Legacy BIOS only.

**GPT (GUID Partition Table):**
- Modern UEFI standard. Stores partition entries as **GUIDs (Globally Unique Identifiers)**.
- Header at **LBA 1** (sector 1). Partition entries at **LBA 2–33** (default 128 entries × 128 bytes each).
- Protective MBR at LBA 0 to prevent legacy tools from damaging the disk.
- **Maximum disk size:** **8 ZiB** (Zettabytes) with 512-byte sectors; up to **64 ZiB** with 4096-byte sectors.
- **Maximum partitions:** 128 (by default), no extended/logical dance needed.
- **Boot mode:** UEFI (also supports legacy BIOS via "BIOS boot partition").

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Rule (KTU Quick-Revision):**
> $$1 \text{ TiB} = 2^{40} \text{ bytes} = 1{,}099{,}511{,}627{,}776 \text{ bytes}$$
> $$1 \text{ ZiB} = 2^{70} \text{ bytes}$$
> MBR limit = 2 TiB. GPT is required for disks > 2 TiB.

#### 2.1.2 NTFS (New Technology File System) — Deep Dive

**NTFS On-Disk Layout:**

| Offset (sectors) | Content | Forensic Importance |
|---|---|---|
| **0** | VBR (Volume Boot Record) | Contains OEM ID, bytes per sector, clusters per sector. |
| **Variable** | **$MFT** (Master File Table) | Core metadata — every file's complete record. |
| **Variable** | **$MFTMirr** | Backup copy of the first 4 MFT records. |
| **Variable** | **$LogFile** | Journal of filesystem transactions. |
| **Variable** | **$UsnJrnl** | Update Sequence Number Journal (file change log). |
| **Variable** | **$Bitmap** | Tracks used/free clusters. |
| **Variable** | **$BadClus** | List of bad clusters. |
| **Variable** | **$Secure** | Security descriptors database. |
| **Variable** | **$AttrDef** | Attribute definitions. |
| **Variable** | **$Extend** | Extended metadata (e.g., $Quota, $ObjId, $Reparse). |

> [!NOTE]
> **NTFS Conceptual Analogy — A Government Land Registry:**
> NTFS is like a national land registry. The **$MFT** is the central registry office where every plot of land (file) has a title deed. The **$MFTMirr** is a fireproof backup of the most important deeds. The **$LogFile** is the daily ledger of property transfers (transactions). The **$UsnJrnl** is the change-of-address log. The **$Bitmap** is the master "vacant vs occupied" map.

**Key NTFS Features:**
- **Alternate Data Streams (ADS):** Hidden data attached to a file (`file.txt:hidden_stream`).
- **File Compression** and **Encryption (EFS — Encrypting File System)**.
- **Access Control Lists (ACLs)** for fine-grained security.
- **Journaling** (transactional integrity) via $LogFile and $UsnJrnl.
- **Self-healing** — `$MFTMirr` and `$LogFile` allow recovery after crashes.

**MFT Entry Anatomy (typically 1024 bytes):**
- **File Reference (8 bytes):** Inode number + sequence number.
- **Attributes:** `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` (timestamps), `$FILE_NAME` (name + parent), `$DATA` (file content), `$SECURITY_DESCRIPTOR` (ACL), `$OBJECT_ID`, `$REPARSE_POINT`, etc.
- **Resident vs Non-Resident Data:** Small files stored inside the MFT record; large files referenced by data runs.

#### 2.1.3 Forensic Artifacts in Windows 10

| Artifact | Path / Location | What It Tells You |
|---|---|---|
| **MFT** | `$MFT` | Every file's creation, modification, access, MFT modification times (MACb timestamps). |
| **USN Journal** | `$Extend\$UsnJrnl:$J` | File system-level change log. |
| **$LogFile** | `$LogFile` | Journaled transactions — proves writes occurred. |
| **Prefetch** | `C:\Windows\Prefetch\*.pf` | Program execution evidence (up to 1024 entries). |
| **Shimcache / Amcache** | `C:\Windows\AppCompat\Programs\` | Application compatibility execution history. |
| **Registry Hives** | `C:\Windows\System32\config\` | SAM, SECURITY, SOFTWARE, SYSTEM, DEFAULT, NTUSER.DAT. |
| **Event Logs** | `C:\Windows\System32\winevt\Logs\*.evtx` | Logon events, process creation, system changes. |
| **Recycle Bin** | `$Recycle.Bin\$I*` and `$R*` | Deleted file metadata ($I) and content ($R). |
| **LNK Files** | `C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Windows\Recent\` | Recently accessed files. |
| **Jump Lists** | `C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Windows\Recent\AutomaticDestinations\` | Recent tasks per application. |
| **Volume Shadow Copies** | `System Volume Information` | Point-in-time snapshots of the filesystem. |

### 2.2 Windows 10 Boot Process — Step-by-Step Theory

**Stage 1: Firmware (BIOS or UEFI)**
- **BIOS (Legacy):** Reads **MBR** (sector 0), executes boot code, finds the **active partition's PBR (Partition Boot Record)**, loads **BOOTMGR** (Windows Boot Manager).
- **UEFI (Modern):** Reads the **ESP (EFI System Partition)** (FAT32, typically 100–500 MB, UUID `C12A7328-F81F-11D2-BA4B-00A0C93EC93B`), finds the EFI application `\EFI\Microsoft\Boot\bootmgfw.efi`.

**Stage 2: Windows Boot Manager (`bootmgfw.efi` / `bootmgr`)**
- Reads the **BCD (Boot Configuration Data)** store located at `\Boot\BCD`.
- The BCD is a registry hive (binary tree) containing entries for:
  - Boot loader path.
  - Kernel path.
  - HAL path.
  - Recovery options.
  - Debugger settings.

**Stage 3: Windows Boot Loader (`winload.exe`)**
- Verifies the **digital signatures** of the kernel, HAL, and boot drivers.
- **Secure Boot enforcement** is checked here (UEFI + Secure Boot enabled).
- Loads `ntoskrnl.exe`, `hal.dll`, and the **System Registry Hive** (`SYSTEM`).

**Stage 4: NT Kernel Initialization**
- Initializes the **Memory Manager**, **Process Manager**, **Scheduler**, and **I/O Manager**.
- Loads **boot-start drivers** marked with `BootStart=1` in the registry.
- Creates the **PAGED** and **NONPAGED** kernel pools.
- **Crash Dump** configuration is initialized here (registry key `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\CrashControl`).

**Stage 5: Session Manager Initialization (`smss.exe`)**
- Creates **environment variables**, paging files (`pagefile.sys`), and the **Windows Subsystem**.
- Starts the **Win32 subsystem** (`csrss.exe`).
- Runs **`winlogon.exe`** for each session.

**Stage 6: Logon (`winlogon.exe` + `lsass.exe`)**
- Displays the **Logon UI** and the **Secure Attention Sequence (SAS — Ctrl+Alt+Del)**.
- Authentication via **LSASS (Local Security Authority Subsystem Service)**.
- On success, loads the user's profile (`NTUSER.DAT`) and shell (`explorer.exe`).

**Stage 7: Service Control Manager (`services.exe`)**
- Starts services based on their **Start Type**:
  - **0 (Boot)** — Kernel-level.
  - **1 (System)** — `smss.exe` or kernel.
  - **2 (Auto Load)** — `services.exe` (most common).
  - **3 (Manual)** — On-demand.
  - **4 (Disabled)** — Will not start.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Note:** The **HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services** registry key lists every driver/service with its `Start` value, `ImagePath`, and `Type`. Investigators can prove malicious persistence by examining new or modified entries.

### 2.3 UNIX/Linux Disk Architecture — Deep Theory

#### 2.3.1 Partitioning & Device Naming

**Device Naming Convention (Linux):**

| Device | Naming Pattern | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **IDE/PATA** | `/dev/hdX` | `/dev/hda`, `/dev/hdb1` |
| **SATA/SCSI/USB** | `/dev/sdX` | `/dev/sda`, `/dev/sdb2` |
| **NVMe** | `/dev/nvmeXnYpZ` | `/dev/nvme0n1p1` |
| **MMC/eMMC/SD** | `/dev/mmcblkXpY` | `/dev/mmcblk0p2` |
| **Virtual (LVM)** | `/dev/mapper/...` or `/dev/dm-X` | `/dev/mapper/vg0-root` |
| **Loop devices** | `/dev/loopX` | `/dev/loop0` |

**Partition Numbering:**
- **MBR:** 1–4 primary, 5+ logical.
- **GPT:** 1–128 (no extended partition).

#### 2.3.2 ext4 (Fourth Extended Filesystem) — Deep Dive

**ext4 On-Disk Layout:**

| Logical Position | Content | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Block 0** | Superblock (backup at blocks 8193, 24577, …) | Filesystem metadata. |
| **Block 1** | Group Descriptors | Information about each block group. |
| **Block 2+** | Data Block Bitmap | Tracks free/used data blocks. |
| **Block 3+** | Inode Bitmap | Tracks free/used inodes. |
| **Block 4+** | Inode Table | Contains all inodes for the group. |
| **Block X+** | Data Blocks | Actual file content. |

**ext4 Superblock Fields (Forensic Relevance):**

| Field | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `s_inodes_count` | Total inodes on the filesystem. |
| `s_blocks_count` | Total blocks on the filesystem. |
| `s_free_blocks_count` | Free blocks. |
| `s_free_inodes_count` | Free inodes. |
| `s_log_block_size` | Block size = $1024 \ll s\_log\_block\_size$. |
| `s_mtime`, `s_wtime` | Mount time and last write time. |
| `s_magic` | `0xEF53` — magic number confirming ext-family. |
| `s_uuid` | Filesystem UUID. |
| `s_volume_name` | Volume label. |
| `s_last_mounted` | Last mount point (e.g., `/`, `/home`). |
| `s_def_hash_version` | Default directory hash algorithm. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Size Calculation (Forensic Detail):**
> If `s_log_block_size = 2`, then:
> $$\text{Block Size} = 1024 \ll 2 = 1024 \times 4 = 4096 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ KiB}$$
> `<<` denotes **left-bit-shift**.

**Inode Structure (256 bytes on most modern Linux systems):**

| Inode Field | Size (bytes) | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `i_mode` | 2 | File type + permissions. |
| `i_uid` / `i_gid` | 2 / 2 | Owner user/group IDs. |
| `i_size` | 8 | File size in bytes. |
| `i_atime` | 8 | Last access time (nanoseconds since epoch). |
| `i_ctime` | 8 | Inode change time (metadata). |
| `i_mtime` | 8 | Modification time (data). |
| `i_blocks` | 8 | Disk blocks consumed (in 512-byte units). |
| `i_links_count` | 2 | Hard link count. |
| `i_block[15]` | 60 | **12 direct + 1 indirect + 1 double-indirect + 1 triple-indirect** pointers. |
| `i_generation` | 4 | File generation (NFS). |
| `i_flags` | 4 | Immutable, append-only, etc. |
| `i_block_group` | 4 | Block group containing this inode (ext4). |
| `i_crtime` | 8 (ext4) | **Creation/birth time (ext4 only)** — the MACB birth time! |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inode Pointer Math (KTU High-Yield):**
> With a 4 KiB block size and 8-byte pointers:
> - **Max file size with 12 direct pointers only** = $12 \times 4096 = 48{,}128$ bytes ≈ **48 KiB**.
> - **With singly-indirect (1st level)** = $48{,}128 + 512 \times 4096 = 2{,}097{,}152 + 48{,}128 \approx 2 \text{ MiB}$ (since 4096 ÷ 8 = **512 pointers per block**).
> - **With doubly-indirect (2nd level)** = $512 \times 512 \times 4096 = 1{,}073{,}741{,}824 \approx 1 \text{ GiB}$.
> - **With triply-indirect (3rd level)** = $512^3 \times 4096 \approx 4 \text{ TiB}$ maximum theoretical.
> - **ext4 extents** replace the indirect scheme for very large files.

**The Concept of `i_size` vs `i_blocks` (Sleuth Kit Trivia):**
- `i_size` = logical file size (what the user sees).
- `i_blocks` = actual 512-byte sectors allocated.
- A deleted file may have `i_blocks > 0` even after `unlink()` until the inode is reused — this is why forensic recovery is possible.

#### 2.3.3 Linux Directory Entry (`dirent`)

A directory is a special file whose data blocks contain **`struct dirent`** entries:
- `d_ino` (4 bytes on 32-bit, 8 bytes on 64-bit) — Inode number.
- `d_off` (4 or 8 bytes) — Offset to next entry.
- `d_reclen` (2 bytes) — Record length.
- `d_type` (1 byte) — `DT_REG`, `DT_DIR`, `DT_LNK`, etc.
- `d_name` (variable) — Null-terminated filename.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sleuth Kit & Autopsy Tip:** When a file is deleted in ext2/3/4, the **directory entry** is zeroed out, but the **inode** and **data blocks** persist until reused. Tools like `icat`, `istat`, `fls` (from The Sleuth Kit) can recover them.

### 2.4 UNIX/Linux Boot Process — Step-by-Step Theory

**Stage 1: Firmware (BIOS or UEFI)**
- **BIOS:** Reads MBR, executes the first 446 bytes of boot code.
- **UEFI:** Reads the **ESP** (FAT32), executes `\EFI\<vendor>\grubx64.efi` (or `shimx64.efi` for Secure Boot).

**Stage 2: Bootloader (GRUB2)**
- **GRUB2 stages:** `boot.img` → `core.img` → loads modules from `/boot/grub/`.
- Reads `/boot/grub/grub.cfg` (or `/boot/grub2/grub.cfg`) — the boot menu configuration.
- Shows a menu (or boots default entry after a timeout, typically **5 seconds**).
- Loads:
  - The **kernel image** (`/boot/vmlinuz-<version>`).
  - The **initramfs** (`/boot/initramfs-<version>.img`).

**Stage 3: Kernel Initialization**
- Decompresses itself (vmlinuz is a compressed image, often bzImage).
- Initializes **architecture-specific** code (x86_64, ARM, etc.).
- Sets up **memory management**, **interrupt handling**, **scheduler**.
- Mounts the **initramfs** as a temporary root (`/`).
- Uses `pivot_root` to switch to the real root filesystem.

**Stage 4: initramfs / initrd**
- A **CPIO archive** (often gzip-compressed) containing:
  - Kernel modules for storage controllers (AHCI, NVMe, RAID).
  - The `init` script (or systemd unit) that finds and mounts the real root.
- Critical for systems with **LVM**, **LUKS encryption**, **RAID**, or **software-defined storage**.

**Stage 5: PID 1 — systemd (or legacy SysV init)**
- The first process started by the kernel after root mount.
- Reads `/etc/systemd/system/default.target` (or `/etc/inittab` for SysV).
- Resolves dependencies and starts **services** in parallel.
- Brings the system to the **graphical.target** (runlevel 5) or **multi-user.target** (runlevel 3).

**Stage 6: Service & Target Activation**
- systemd units: `.service`, `.target`, `.socket`, `.mount`, `.timer`, etc.
- Logon managers: **GDM** (GNOME), **LightDM**, **SDDM** (KDE) start the graphical session.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Note — Linux Boot Artifacts:**
> - `/var/log/dmesg` — kernel ring buffer at boot.
> - `/var/log/boot.log` — systemd boot log.
> - `journalctl` — systemd's binary journal (`/var/log/journal/`).
> - `/var/log/wtmp`, `/var/log/btmp` — login records.
> - `~/.bash_history` — per-user command history.
> - `/proc/` (process info, mount info) — volatile, must be captured live.
> - `/etc/crontab`, `/var/spool/cron/`, `/etc/cron.d/` — scheduled tasks (persistence check).

### 2.5 Windows vs Linux — Master Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Windows 10 | Linux (ext4) |
|---|---|---|
| **Default Filesystem** | NTFS | ext4 (also XFS, Btrfs) |
| **Metadata Centralization** | **MFT (Master File Table)** | **Inode Table** (per block group) |
| **Inode/MFT size** | 1024 bytes (typically) | 256 bytes (typically) |
| **Journal** | $LogFile + $UsnJrnl | Dedicated journal (ext3/4) |
| **Permissions** | ACLs (DACL + SACL) | Traditional Unix bits (rwx) + ACLs optional |
| **Hidden data** | **ADS (Alternate Data Streams)** | Dotfiles (`.file`), xattr extended attributes |
| **Timestamps** | MACb (Modified, Accessed, Created, MFT Modified) | **MACB** (atime, ctime, mtime, **crtime/birthtime** in ext4) |
| **Time resolution** | 100-ns (FILETIME) | Nanosecond (ext4) |
| **Rootfs identifier** | Drive letter (C:\) | Mount point (/, /home) |
| **Bootloader** | Windows Boot Manager (bootmgr/winload) | GRUB2 (or systemd-boot, LILO) |
| **Boot config** | BCD store (registry hive) | `/boot/grub/grub.cfg` + `/etc/default/grub` |
| **Kernel image** | ntoskrnl.exe | vmlinuz-<version> |
| **Init system** | smss.exe → services.exe | systemd (PID 1) |
| **Encryption** | EFS (per-file) + BitLocker (full-volume) | eCryptfs, LUKS (dm-crypt) |
| **Snapshot/Backup** | Volume Shadow Copy (VSS) | LVM snapshots, Btrfs/ZFS snapshots |
| **Device naming** | C:, D:, \\.\PhysicalDrive0 | /dev/sda, /dev/nvme0n1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Forensic Mantra:** "NTFS has $MFT; ext4 has inodes. Both tell you when a file was born, when it changed, and (often) how to resurrect it after deletion."

### 2.6 KTU Cheat Sheet — Quick-Reference Table

| # | Concept | Windows 10 | Linux/UNIX |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Partition table | MBR (512B) / GPT (16 KB) | MBR / GPT |
| 2 | Default FS | NTFS | ext4 |
| 3 | Metadata file | `$MFT` (MFT entry = 1024 B) | Inode (256 B) |
| 4 | Magic number | `FILE0` (OEM ID) | `0xEF53` (ext superblock) |
| 5 | Default block/cluster size | 4 KiB | 4 KiB (1, 2, 4, 8 KiB possible) |
| 6 | Bootloader | Windows Boot Manager | GRUB2 |
| 7 | Boot config | BCD | `/boot/grub/grub.cfg` |
| 8 | Init (PID 1) | smss.exe (sess. mgr) / services.exe | systemd |
| 9 | Log location | `C:\Windows\System32\winevt\Logs\` | `/var/log/` + `journalctl` |
| 10 | Hash algorithm | SHA-1, SHA-256 (Authenticode) | MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256 |

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Block Size from Superblock

The Linux ext2/3/4 superblock stores the block size implicitly as a power-of-2 multiple of 1024 bytes:

$$
\text{Block Size in Bytes} = 1024 \ll s\_log\_block\_size
$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

**Step 1.** Let $B_s$ = block size in bytes and $k = s\_log\_block\_size$.

**Step 2.** By definition of the ext-family, the block size is restricted to powers of 2 in KiB:
$$
B_s = 1024 \times 2^k
$$

**Step 3.** Recognizing that left-shift by $k$ bits is multiplication by $2^k$:
$$
B_s = 1024 \ll k
$$

**Worked Example:** If a forensic tool reads `s_log_block_size = 2`:

$$
B_s = 1024 \ll 2
$$

$$
B_s = 1024 \times 2^2
$$

$$
B_s = 1024 \times 4
$$

$$
B_s = 4096 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ KiB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Valid values of $k$:** $0$ (1 KiB), $1$ (2 KiB), $2$ (4 KiB), $3$ (8 KiB), $4$ (16 KiB), $5$ (32 KiB), $6$ (64 KiB — rarely used in practice).

### 3.2 Derivation — Maximum File Size with Inode Pointers (ext3/4 with 4 KiB blocks)

**Given:**
- Block size: $B_s = 4096$ bytes.
- Pointer size: $P = 8$ bytes (64-bit system).
- Inode has 12 direct, 1 singly-indirect, 1 doubly-indirect, 1 triply-indirect pointer slots.

**Step 1. Number of pointers per block.**
$$
N_p = \frac{B_s}{P} = \frac{4096}{8} = 512 \text{ pointers per block}
$$

**Step 2. Maximum addressable data with 12 direct pointers.**
$$
M_{12} = 12 \times B_s = 12 \times 4096 = 49{,}152 \text{ bytes} \approx 48 \text{ KiB}
$$

**Step 3. Singly-indirect adds.**
$$
M_{1} = N_p \times B_s = 512 \times 4096 = 2{,}097{,}152 \text{ bytes} \approx 2 \text{ MiB}
$$

**Step 4. Doubly-indirect adds.**
$$
M_{2} = N_p^2 \times B_s = 512^2 \times 4096 = 1{,}073{,}741{,}824 \text{ bytes} \approx 1 \text{ GiB}
$$

**Step 5. Triply-indirect adds.**
$$
M_{3} = N_p^3 \times B_s = 512^3 \times 4096 = 4{,}398{,}046{,}511{,}104 \text{ bytes} \approx 4 \text{ TiB}
$$

**Step 6. Total theoretical maximum file size.**
$$
M_{total} = M_{12} + M_{1} + M_{2} + M_{3}
$$

$$
M_{total} = 49{,}152 + 2{,}097{,}152 + 1{,}073{,}741{,}824 + 4{,}398{,}046{,}511{,}104
$$

$$
M_{total} \approx 4{,}399{,}402{,}299{,}232 \text{ bytes} \approx 4 \text{ TiB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Reality:** ext4 uses **extents** (a tree of physical block ranges) instead of the classical indirect pointer scheme for large files. This allows efficient handling of multi-terabyte files.

### 3.3 Derivation — MBR vs GPT Disk Size Limits

**MBR (Master Boot Record) limit:**
- Partition entry uses a 32-bit LBA (Logical Block Addressing) for the size and start.
- Maximum number of addressable sectors = $2^{32} - 1$.
- With 512-byte sectors:
$$
\text{MBR Max} = 2^{32} \times 512 = 2{,}199{,}023{,}255{,}552 \text{ bytes}
$$

Converting to TiB (where 1 TiB = $2^{40}$ bytes):
$$
\text{MBR Max} = \frac{2^{32} \times 512}{2^{40}} \text{ TiB} = \frac{2^{32} \times 2^{9}}{2^{40}} \text{ TiB} = \frac{2^{41}}{2^{40}} \text{ TiB} = 2 \text{ TiB}
$$

**GPT limit:**
- Partition entry uses a 64-bit LBA.
- With 512-byte sectors:
$$
\text{GPT Max (sector units)} = 2^{64} \times 512 \text{ bytes}
$$

In ZiB (Zebibytes, where 1 ZiB = $2^{70}$ bytes):
$$
\text{GPT Max} = \frac{2^{64} \times 2^{9}}{2^{70}} \text{ ZiB} = \frac{2^{73}}{2^{70}} \text{ ZiB} = 2^{3} = 8 \text{ ZiB}
$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Scheme | Bits for LBA | Max LBA | Max Disk Size (512B sectors) | Equivalent |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MBR** | 32 | $2^{32} - 1$ | $2^{32} \times 512$ bytes | **2 TiB** |
| **GPT** | 64 | $2^{64} - 1$ | $2^{64} \times 512$ bytes | **8 ZiB** |

### 3.4 Derivation — NTFS MFT Record Layout (Typical 1024-byte entry)

A standard MFT entry contains a **header** and a sequence of **attributes** that end with the `0xFFFFFFFF` end-marker:

$$
\text{MFT Entry Size} = \text{Header} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Attribute}_i + 4 \text{ bytes (end marker)}
$$

**Header layout (first 48 bytes of an MFT entry):**

| Offset | Size (bytes) | Field | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0x00 | 4 | Signature | `FILE` (0x46494C45) |
| 0x04 | 2 | Update Sequence Array offset | |
| 0x06 | 2 | Update Sequence Array size | |
| 0x08 | 8 | LogFile sequence number ($LSN$) | |
| 0x10 | 2 | Sequence number | Increments on reuse. |
| 0x12 | 2 | Hard link count | |
| 0x14 | 2 | First attribute offset | |
| 0x16 | 2 | Flags (in-use, directory) | |
| 0x18 | 4 | Used size of entry | |
| 0x1C | 4 | Allocated size of entry | |
| 0x20 | 8 | File reference to base record | |
| 0x28 | 2 | Next attribute ID | |
| 0x2A | 2 | (Reserved) | |
| 0x2C | 4 | MFT record number | |

**Attribute structure (variable length):**

| Field | Size | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Attribute Type | 4 bytes | e.g., `0x10` = `$STANDARD_INFORMATION`, `0x30` = `$FILE_NAME`, `0x80` = `$DATA`. |
| Length | 4 bytes | Total attribute size. |
| Non-resident flag | 1 byte | `0` = resident (in MFT), `1` = non-resident. |
| Name length | 1 byte | Length of attribute name. |
| Name offset | 2 bytes | Offset to the name. |
| Flags | 2 bytes | Compressed, encrypted, sparse. |
| Attribute ID | 2 bytes | Unique within the MFT entry. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Pitfall:** The "Update Sequence Array" in the header is used by NTFS to verify sector integrity. A modified MFT entry by a non-NTFS tool (e.g., a forensic imager) will fail validation unless the array is correctly rewritten. This is why NTFS-aware forensic tools are mandatory.

### 3.5 Derivation — ext4 Birth Time (crtime) Read Path

The `i_crtime` field is stored in an extended attribute `$EXT4_EXTENTS_FL` (or specifically in the inode itself as the 4th timestamp) and is available since Linux 2.6.30. The forensic reading path is:

**Step 1.** Read the superblock to determine the block size $B_s$ and `s_inodes_per_group`.

**Step 2.** Compute the inode's disk location:
$$
\text{Inode offset within group} = (\text{inode\_number} - 1) \mod s\_inodes\_per\_group
$$
$$
\text{Group index} = \left\lfloor \frac{\text{inode\_number} - 1}{s\_inodes\_per\_group} \right\rfloor
$$
$$
\text{Inode block number} = \text{Group\_First\_Block} + \text{Inode\_Table\_Start} + \left\lfloor \frac{\text{Inode\_offset} \times \text{Inode\_Size}}{B_s} \right\rfloor
$$

**Step 3.** Seek to `Inode block × B_s + (Inode offset × Inode Size) mod B_s`.

**Step 4.** Read the `i_crtime` field at its known offset within the inode structure (offset 0xA0 or 0xB0 depending on `i_extra_isize`).

**Step 5.** Convert the 8-byte nanosecond value to wall-clock:
$$
\text{Wall-clock time} = \text{epoch} + \frac{i\_crtime \text{ (ns)}}{10^9} \text{ seconds}
$$
where `epoch` = 1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Boot Artifact Comparison

| Stage | Windows 10 Artifact | Linux/UNIX Artifact |
|---|---|---|
| POST | CMOS / NVRAM, S.M.A.R.T. logs | dmesg, EDAC logs, IPMI SEL |
| Firmware | UEFI NVRAM variables | UEFI NVRAM variables |
| Bootloader | `bootmgr`, BCD store | GRUB2 modules, `grub.cfg` |
| Kernel | ntoskrnl.exe, hal.dll | `vmlinuz-<ver>`, `initramfs-<ver>.img` |
| Init | `smss.exe`, `winlogon.exe` | `systemd` (PID 1) |
| Services | `services.exe`, SCM registry | `systemctl` units, `/etc/init.d/` |
| Logs | `*.evtx`, `Setupapi.dev.log` | `/var/log/`, `journalctl` |
| Scheduled tasks | `Task Scheduler` (XML) | `cron`, `anacron`, `systemd timers` |
| Persistence | Registry `Run` keys, Services | `cron`, `.bashrc`, systemd units |

### 4.1 Windows 10 Boot Process — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Button Pressed] --> B[Power Supply PSU Initialization]

    C --> D{Firmware Type?}
    D -->|Legacy BIOS| E[Read MBR - sector 0]
    D -->|UEFI| F[Read ESP - EFI System Partition]
    E --> G[Execute MBR boot code - 446 bytes]
    F --> H[Locate bootmgfw.efi in ESP]
    G --> I[Load BOOTMGR - Windows Boot Manager]

    I --> J[Read BCD Store - Boot Configuration Data]
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model)]**
*Explain the structure of the NTFS Master File Table (MFT). What are its key metadata files?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **NTFS Master File Table (MFT)** is the central metadata repository of an NTFS volume. It is a hidden, system-protected file (`$MFT`, typically MFT Entry 0) that contains one **MFT record** (typically **1024 bytes**) for every file and directory on the volume. Each record stores the file's attributes (metadata + data), including `$STANDARD_INFORMATION`, `$FILE_NAME`, `$SECURITY_DESCRIPTOR`, `$DATA`, and timestamps.

**Key system metadata MFT entries** include:
- **Entry 0 — `$MFT`** (the MFT itself).
- **Entry 1 — `$MFTMirr`** (MFT mirror — backup of first 4 records).
- **Entry 2 — `$LogFile`** (transaction journal).
- **Entry 3 — `$Volume`** (volume metadata).
- **Entry 5 — Root directory**.
- **Entry 6 — `$Bitmap`** (cluster usage map).
- **Entry 9 — `$Secure`** (security descriptors).
- **Entry 11 — `$Extend`** (extended metadata container).

From a forensic perspective, the MFT is the **primary evidence source** because it provides **MACb timestamps** (Modified, Accessed, Created, MFT Modified) for every file, surviving even after file deletion until the record is reused.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**
*What is an inode in Linux? Explain its structure and forensic significance.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **inode (index node)** is a **data structure** (typically **256 bytes** on modern Linux systems) that stores the metadata of a file or directory in a Linux filesystem such as ext2/3/4. Every file has exactly one inode, identified by a unique **inode number**.

**Inode fields include:** file type and permissions (`i_mode`), owner IDs (`i_uid`, `i_gid`), file size (`i_size`), disk blocks allocated (`i_blocks`), timestamps (`i_atime`, `i_ctime`, `i_mtime`, `i_crtime` in ext4), and **15 block pointers** (12 direct, 1 indirect, 1 double-indirect, 1 triple-indirect) referencing data blocks.

**Forensic significance:** The inode persists even after a file is deleted (the directory entry is removed, but the inode and data blocks remain until reused). Tools like **The Sleuth Kit** (`istat`, `icat`) can recover deleted files by parsing inodes. The **birth time (crtime)** is a key forensic timestamp unavailable in older filesystems.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Windows 10 Focus**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model)]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Windows 10 boot process** in detail. Highlight the role of UEFI, BCD, and Secure Boot. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the structure of an **NTFS Master File Table (MFT) entry** and discuss the forensic artifacts recoverable from `$MFT`, `$MFTMirr`, `$LogFile`, and `$UsnJrnl`. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks:**

The Windows 10 boot process is a sequence of stages executed by firmware, the bootloader, and the OS kernel:

**1. POST (Power-On Self-Test)**
The firmware (BIOS or UEFI) executes hardware diagnostics. Memory, CPU, and storage controllers are checked. Successful POST transitions control to the next stage.

**2. Firmware (BIOS/UEFI)**
- **Legacy BIOS** reads the **MBR** at sector 0 of the boot device, executes the 446-byte boot code, finds the active partition, and loads its PBR.
- **UEFI** (modern, default on Windows 10) reads the **EFI System Partition (ESP)** — a FAT32 partition with the well-known GUID `C12A7328-F81F-11D2-BA4B-00A0C93EC93B`. It locates `\EFI\Microsoft\Boot\bootmgfw.efi`.
- **Secure Boot** (UEFI feature) verifies the digital signature of every boot component (bootmgfw.efi, winload.exe, ntoskrnl.exe) against keys stored in NVRAM. Unsigned or modified components cause a boot failure — a critical anti-rootkit defense.

**3. Windows Boot Manager (bootmgr / bootmgfw.efi)**
Reads the **BCD (Boot Configuration Data)** store at `\Boot\BCD`. The BCD is a registry hive containing all boot entries (default OS, recovery, kernel parameters, debug settings).

**4. Windows Boot Loader (winload.exe)**
Verifies the digital signature of the OS kernel and HAL. Loads `ntoskrnl.exe` and `hal.dll`. Also loads the `SYSTEM` registry hive into memory.

**5. NT Kernel Initialization**
Initializes the **Memory Manager**, **Process/Thread Manager**, **I/O Manager**, **Scheduler**, and **Plug-and-Play Manager**. Loads boot-start drivers from the SYSTEM registry.

**6. Session Manager (smss.exe)**
Creates environment variables and the paging file (`pagefile.sys`). Starts the **Win32 Subsystem** (`csrss.exe`) and launches `winlogon.exe` for session creation.

**7. Logon UI (winlogon.exe + LSASS)**
Displays the **Secure Attention Sequence** prompt. LSASS (`lsass.exe`) authenticates the user. On success, the user's `NTUSER.DAT` profile is loaded and `explorer.exe` is started.

**Forensic note:** The `BCD` store, `SYSTEM` hive (`CurrentControlSet\Services`), and `C:\Windows\Panther` setup logs reveal the boot configuration and any unauthorized modifications.

---

**Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks:**

**MFT Entry Structure:** A standard NTFS MFT entry is **1024 bytes** consisting of a 48-byte header followed by a sequence of attributes ending with the 4-byte end marker `0xFFFFFFFF`.

**Header fields:** `FILE` signature, Update Sequence Array (for sector integrity), LogFile Sequence Number (LSN), sequence number (increments on reuse — for forensic correlation), hard link count, first attribute offset, in-use/directory flags, used and allocated sizes, base record reference, and next attribute ID.

**Attribute types:**
- `0x10` `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` — MACb timestamps.
- `0x30` `$FILE_NAME` — long/short filenames + parent directory reference.
- `0x40` `$OBJECT_ID` — 16-byte GUID per file.
- `0x50` `$SECURITY_DESCRIPTOR` — DACL/SACL.
- `0x80` `$DATA` — file content (resident or non-resident).
- `0x90` `$INDEX_ROOT` / `0xA0` `$INDEX_ALLOCATION` — directory index.
- `0xC0` `$REPARSE_POINT` — symlinks, junctions.

**Forensic Artifacts:**

| File | Content | Forensic Value |
|---|---|---|
| **`$MFT`** | Master record of every file. | MACb timestamps, file paths, data run offsets, even after deletion. |
| **`$MFTMirr`** | Backup of MFT entries 0–3. | Recovers critical metadata if `$MFT` is corrupted. |
| **`$LogFile`** | Transactional journal. | Proves writes occurred; redacted logs reveal suspect tampering. |
| **`$UsnJrnl`** | Update Sequence Number Journal. | Tracks file system changes (create, delete, rename, extend) — invaluable timeline of activity. |

Tools like **MFTExplorer**, **FTK**, **X-Ways**, and **The Sleuth Kit (NTFS-only tools like `fls`, `istat`)** can parse these artifacts and reconstruct a complete timeline of file activity, even for deleted files.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Linux/UNIX Focus**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Linux/UNIX boot process** in detail. Discuss the role of GRUB2, initramfs, and systemd. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **inode structure** in ext4 filesystem. Discuss the role of the superblock, block groups, and the use of direct, indirect, and doubly-indirect pointers with a numerical example. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks:**

**1. POST and Firmware**
On power-up, firmware (BIOS or UEFI) executes POST. BIOS reads the MBR; UEFI reads the ESP.

**2. GRUB2 Bootloader**
GRUB2 is the default Linux bootloader. Its stages are:
- **Stage 1 (boot.img)** — 512 bytes written to MBR, loads Stage 1.5.
- **Stage 1.5 (core.img)** — Contains core modules, reads `/boot/grub/` from the filesystem.
- **Stage 2** — Reads `/boot/grub/grub.cfg`, displays menu, loads kernel + initramfs.

**3. Kernel Loading**
GRUB2 loads `vmlinuz-<version>` (the compressed kernel image) and `initramfs-<version>.img` into memory and transfers control to the kernel. The kernel decompresses itself and begins initialization:
- Sets up memory management (paging, slabs).
- Detects CPU features and initializes architecture-specific code.
- Initializes the scheduler and process 0 (the idle task).

**4. initramfs (Initial RAM Filesystem)**
A **CPIO archive** (often gzip-compressed) containing essential drivers and scripts. It is mounted as the temporary root (`/`) by the kernel. The `init` script inside identifies the real root filesystem (e.g., an LVM volume or LUKS-encrypted partition), loads necessary modules, and mounts the real root.

**5. Root Mount and pivot_root**
The real root filesystem is mounted at `/` (temporarily under `/sysroot`). The kernel calls `pivot_root(2)` to switch to the real root, and the old initramfs is unmounted.

**6. systemd (PID 1)**
The first user-space process (`/sbin/systemd` or `/lib/systemd/systemd`) takes over. It reads `/etc/systemd/system/default.target` to determine the boot target. systemd starts services **in parallel**, manages dependencies, and brings the system to `multi-user.target` (runlevel 3) or `graphical.target` (runlevel 5).

**Forensic note:** Boot logs are in `/var/log/dmesg`, `/var/log/boot.log`, and the systemd journal (`journalctl`). Modifications to GRUB config or initramfs indicate potential boot-level tampering (e.g., **bootkits**).

---

**Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks:**

**ext4 Layout Overview:** The ext4 filesystem divides the disk into **block groups**, each containing a copy of the **superblock** (backup), **group descriptors**, **data block bitmap**, **inode bitmap**, **inode table**, and **data blocks**.

**Superblock (block 0, 1024 bytes after the boot sector):** Contains the filesystem magic `0xEF53`, total inodes/blocks, free counts, block size (encoded as `s_log_block_size`), last mount time, last write time, mount count, and volume name. Critical for forensic identification and date-of-last-mount analysis.

**Inode Structure (256 bytes on 64-bit systems):**
- Mode, UID, GID (2+2+2 bytes).
- File size (8 bytes).
- Timestamps: atime, ctime, mtime, **crtime (ext4 birth time)** — each 8 bytes.
- Block count, flags, version, file ACL block.
- **15 block pointers** (60 bytes total).

**Pointer scheme:**
- 12 direct pointers (each references one 4 KiB data block).
- 1 singly-indirect pointer (references a block of pointers).
- 1 doubly-indirect pointer.
- 1 triply-indirect pointer.

**Numerical Example (4 KiB block, 8-byte pointers):**
Pointers per block = $4096 / 8 = 512$.

- Direct region: $12 \times 4096 = 48 \text{ KiB}$.
- Singly-indirect: $512 \times 4096 = 2 \text{ MiB}$.
- Doubly-indirect: $512 \times 512 \times 4096 = 1 \text{ GiB}$.
- Triply-indirect: $512^3 \times 4096 \approx 4 \text{ TiB}$.

**Forensic significance:** When a file is deleted, the directory entry is zeroed but the **inode and its data blocks persist** until reused. Tools like **`istat`** and **`icat`** from The Sleuth Kit can recover the file. The **birth time (`i_crtime`)** is unique to ext4 and provides the "file creation" timestamp that older Unix filesystems lacked.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing timestamps:** Many students mix up **atime** (access), **ctime** (metadata change), **mtime** (data modification), and **crtime** (birth/creation). Examiners **deduct 1–2 marks** for any confusion between these.
> 2. **Forgetting the role of `pivot_root`:** In Linux boot process, students often describe initramfs as the "final root" — this is wrong. The kernel **mounts initramfs as temporary root, then `pivot_root`s to the real root.** Examiners explicitly test this.
> 3. **Missing the MBR/GPT disk-size limit derivation:** When asked to compute limits, students often forget the **2^9 factor (512 bytes/sector)** and present incorrect numbers.
> 4. **Skipping Secure Boot in Windows boot:** The UEFI + Secure Boot chain is a high-yield topic. Omitting it loses marks.
> 5. **Conflating `$MFT` and `$MFTMirr`:** `$MFTMirr` is **not a full mirror** — it only contains the first **4 MFT records** (entries 0–3). Writing "it is a complete backup of MFT" is incorrect.
> 6. **Inode pointer math errors:** Calculating maximum file size without considering the **12 direct pointers + 3 levels of indirection** correctly leads to wrong totals.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2 (Windows 10 + Linux/UNIX Disk & Boot)**

**Windows 10 — Disk Structure:**
- ☐ Partition schemes: **MBR** (legacy, 2 TiB max, 4 primary partitions) vs **GPT** (modern, 8 ZiB max, 128 partitions).
- ☐ Default filesystem: **NTFS**. Alternate: FAT32, exFAT, ReFS.
- ☐ MBR layout: 446 bytes boot code + 64 bytes partition table (4 × 16) + 2 bytes magic `0x55AA`.
- ☐ GPT layout: Protective MBR (LBA 0) + GPT Header (LBA 1) + 128 partition entries (LBA 2–33) + Backup GPT (last LBA).
- ☐ Cluster size default: **4 KiB** on NTFS.
- ☐ **MFT (Master File Table)** is the central metadata file — every file/directory has a 1024-byte MFT record.
- ☐ System MFT entries: 0 (`$MFT`), 1 (`$MFTMirr`), 2 (`$LogFile`), 5 (root), 6 (`$Bitmap`), 9 (`$Secure`), 11 (`$Extend`).
- ☐ NTFS Alternate Data Streams (ADS) allow hidden data on files (`file.txt:secret`).
- ☐ Forensic tools: **FTK Imager, Autopsy, X-Ways, The Sleuth Kit, MFTExplorer**.

**Windows 10 — Boot Process:**
- ☐ Stages: POST → Firmware (BIOS/UEFI) → Bootmgr (BCD) → winload.exe → ntoskrnl.exe → smss.exe → winlogon.exe → explorer.exe.
- ☐ **UEFI** uses **ESP** (FAT32 partition) to launch `bootmgfw.efi`.
- ☐ **BIOS** uses **MBR** + active partition's PBR to launch `bootmgr`.
- ☐ **Secure Boot** verifies signatures of every boot component.
- ☐ **BCD** = Boot Configuration Data (registry hive at `\Boot\BCD`).
- ☐ **winload.exe** is the Windows Boot Loader.
- ☐ Kernel initializes drivers, services, and the **Service Control Manager (`services.exe`)**.
- ☐ Boot artifact: `C:\Windows\Prefetch\`, `C:\Windows\System32\config\SYSTEM`, `C:\Windows\Panther\setupact.log`.

**Linux/UNIX — Disk Structure:**
- ☐ Default filesystem: **ext4** (also XFS, Btrfs, ZFS).
- ☐ Partition table: MBR or GPT (no extension concept with GPT).
- ☐ Device files: `/dev/sda`, `/dev/nvme0n1p1`, `/dev/mapper/...`.
- ☐ **ext4 layout**: Superblock → Group Descriptors → Block Bitmap → Inode Bitmap → Inode Table → Data Blocks.
- ☐ Superblock magic: **`0xEF53`**. Block size: $1024 \ll s\_log\_block\_size$.
- ☐ **Inode (256 bytes)** stores metadata; **inodes are 1-to-1 with files** (no inode for file name).
- ☐ Inode timestamps: atime, ctime, mtime, **crtime (ext4 birth time)**.
- ☐ 15 pointers: 12 direct + 1 indirect + 1 double-indirect + 1 triple-indirect.
- ☐ With 4 KiB blocks, **maximum file size ≈ 4 TiB** (theoretical).
- ☐ **Hard links** share inodes; **symbolic links** are separate inodes pointing to filenames.
- ☐ **File deletion**: directory entry cleared, inode + blocks persist until reuse → recoverable.
- ☐ `df -h`, `stat`, `ls -i`, `dumpe2fs`, `tune2fs`, `file` commands.
- ☐ Forensic tools: **The Sleuth Kit (`fls`, `istat`, `icat`, `mmls`), Autopsy, Log2Timeline/Plaso, dd/dcfldd, foremost, photorec**.

**Linux/UNIX — Boot Process:**
- ☐ Stages: POST → Firmware → GRUB2 → Kernel + initramfs → pivot_root → systemd (PID 1) → targets.
- ☐ **GRUB2** stages: Stage 1 (boot.img, 512B in MBR) → Stage 1.5 (core.img) → Stage 2 (reads `grub.cfg`).
- ☐ **GRUB2** config: `/boot/grub/grub.cfg` (generated from `/etc/default/grub` + `/etc/grub.d/*`).
- ☐ Kernel image: `/boot/vmlinuz-<version>`; initramfs: `/boot/initramfs-<version>.img`.
- ☐ **initramfs** = CPIO archive with essential drivers + init script.
- ☐ **`pivot_root(2)`** switches from initramfs to real root filesystem.
- ☐ **systemd** (PID 1) reads `/etc/systemd/system/default.target`; targets: `multi-user.target` (runlevel 3), `graphical.target` (runlevel 5).
- ☐ Boot logs: `/var/log/dmesg`, `/var/log/boot.log`, `journalctl -b`.
- ☐ Persistence check: `~/.bashrc`, `/etc/crontab`, `/etc/cron.d/`, systemd units (`/etc/systemd/system/`).

**Cross-Platform Comparison Quick Points:**
- ☐ Windows metadata: **MFT** (centralized). Linux metadata: **inodes** (per-block-group).
- ☐ Windows ACLs vs Linux POSIX rwx + optional ACLs.
- ☐ Windows ADS vs Linux dotfiles + extended attributes (xattr).
- ☐ Windows secure boot at firmware + kernel level. Linux secure boot uses **shim** (signed by Microsoft) to chain-trust GRUB2.

**Forensic Mantras:**
- ☐ "**NTFS has $MFT; ext4 has inodes.**" — these are the keys to timeline analysis.
- ☐ "**Deleted ≠ Erased.**" — both Windows and Linux preserve data after unlink.
- ☐ "**Always pull the plug, never shut down.**" — to preserve volatile evidence and prevent anti-forensic wiping.
- ☐ "**Hash everything.**" — SHA-256 of the disk image is the forensic chain-of-custody anchor.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Understanding Disk Drives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT602 - CYBER FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Understanding Disk Drives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Disk Drive** is an electro-mechanical, non-volatile, random-access secondary storage device that stores and retrieves digital data using rapidly rotating magnetic platters (in Hard Disk Drives, HDDs) or non-volatile flash memory chips (in Solid State Drives, SSDs). From a **Cyber Forensics** perspective, a disk drive is the *primary physical artifact* containing user data, system files, deleted artifacts, metadata, and hidden data (e.g., slack space, bad sectors, host-protected areas) that must be acquired, preserved, and analyzed using legally defensible methods.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCCCT602 – Module 2):**
> A disk drive is treated as a structured storage medium composed of *physical geometry* (platters, tracks, sectors, cylinders) and *logical geometry* (partitions, file systems, boot records). The forensic examiner must understand **both layers** because evidence can reside at either level (e.g., deleted file in MFT, formatted partition residue at sector level).

### 1.2 Disk Drive Categories (Architecture-Level Classification)

| Drive Type | Full Form | Media | Speed Tier |
|---|---|---|---|
| **HDD** | Hard Disk Drive | Magnetic platters | Mechanical, slower |
| **SSD** | Solid State Drive | NAND Flash memory | Electronic, fastest |
| **SSHD** | Solid State Hybrid Drive | Magnetic + Flash cache | Mixed |
| **NVMe** | Non-Volatile Memory Express | NAND over PCIe lanes | Fastest (modern) |
| **SCSI** | Small Computer System Interface | Magnetic (legacy server) | High throughput |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU examinations, the focus is **HDDs** (platter-based geometry). SSD forensics (wear-leveling, TRIM, garbage collection) is mentioned in Module 2 only at a conceptual level.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – "The Disk Drive as a Library"

Imagine a **circular library with rotating shelves**:

- The **platter** = a giant circular rotating shelf of books.
- The **track** = a single concentric ring of books on that shelf.
- The **sector** = one small slice (pie-piece) holding a fixed number of "words" (typically **512 bytes** or **4096 bytes** for Advanced Format).
- The **cylinder** = the same-radius ring on *all* platters stacked together (so the read/write head doesn't have to move radially).
- The **read/write head** = a librarian's hand on a movable arm (the **actuator**) that reaches down to grab or place data.
- The **spindle** = the central motor that spins the shelf (commonly at **5,400 RPM**, **7,200 RPM**, **10,000 RPM**, or **15,000 RPM**).
- The **seek operation** = the librarian sliding the arm to the correct ring; the **rotational latency** = waiting for the right slice to rotate into view.

The faster the shelf spins and the quicker the arm moves, the faster the data is delivered. The forensic investigator's job is to read **every single word** on the shelf — even the ones in trash cans (deleted files), the empty space between books (**slack space**), and the "Do Not Disturb" staff areas (**HPA / DCO**).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU:** Think **PHT-SC²** — **P**latters, **H**eads, **T**racks, **S**ectors, **C**ylinders. These are the **5 physical geometry primitives** tested repeatedly in university papers.

### 1.4 Standard Physical & Performance Metrics (Bolded for Quick Recall)

- **Sector size:** **512 bytes** (legacy) or **4096 bytes (4Kn – Advanced Format)**
- **Bytes per sector constant:** **512 bytes** is the KTU default for capacity calculations.
- **Typical sector per track range:** **63 to 500+** (Zone Bit Recording)
- **Standard rotation speeds:** **3,600 / 5,400 / 7,200 / 10,000 / 15,000 RPM**
- **Average seek time (consumer HDD):** **8 to 12 milliseconds**
- **Full stroke seek time:** **18 to 25 milliseconds**
- **Track-to-track seek time:** **0.5 to 2 milliseconds**
- **MBR signature bytes:** **0x55 0xAA** (always the last 2 bytes of the first sector)
- **GPT signature:** "EFI PART" (8 ASCII bytes)
- **Standard sector count for MBR-compatible disks:** **63 sectors/track**

### 1.5 Why Disk Drives Matter in Cyber Forensics

Every digital crime leaves traces on a disk. The disk stores:

1. **Active data** — currently used files.
2. **Deleted data** — recoverable until overwritten (the "data residue" principle).
3. **Slack space** — the gap between end-of-file and end-of-sector (RAM slack + file slack).
4. **Swap / Page file** — virtual memory artefacts, may contain passwords, encryption keys.
5. **Hibernation file (hiberfil.sys)** — full RAM dump to disk.
6. **Unallocated space** — clusters not currently assigned to any file.
7. **Metadata** — file system structures (MFT entries, $STANDARD_INFORMATION, $FILE_NAME).
8. **Bad sectors** — deliberately remapped or "hidden" by firmware (DCO/HPA).

> [!CAUTION]
> A *logical* copy (file-level) misses slack, swap, unallocated clusters, and bad sectors. A **forensically sound image** is a *bit-stream (sector-level) copy* of the entire physical drive — this is the **single most important forensic principle** in KTU Module 2.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Geometry of a Hard Disk Drive (Layer-by-Layer)

A hard disk is built from the inside out as follows:

1. **Platters** – Rigid substrates (aluminum alloy or glass) coated with a thin magnetic layer (cobalt-based). Modern drives use **1 to 5 platters**, each with **2 surfaces (sides)**.
2. **Spindle Motor** – Spins the platters at a constant angular velocity. Modern drives use fluid dynamic bearings.
3. **Read/Write Heads** – One head per surface. Mounted on **sliders** that fly above the platter on a cushion of air (**~3 to 15 nanometers** above the surface).
4. **Actuator Arm Assembly** – A comb-like arm holding all head sliders. Pivoted by a **voice-coil motor (VCM)**.
5. **Tracks** – Concentric circles on a single surface. Density measured in **TPI (Tracks Per Inch)**.
6. **Sectors** – Pie-slice divisions of a track. Minimum addressable unit by the drive controller. Sector format includes: **Sync (12B) + Address Mark (4B) + Data (512B) + ECC (40-70B)**. Total physical sector ≈ **571 bytes**, of which only **512 bytes** are user data.
7. **Cylinders** – Set of all tracks that share the same radius across *every* surface. Logically accessed without arm movement.
8. **Clusters (Allocation Units)** – Groups of consecutive sectors (**2, 4, 8, 16, 32, 64 sectors** per cluster typical). The **smallest unit a file system allocates**.

### 2.2 Logical Geometry (Forensic-Visible Address Spaces)

- **CHS Addressing (Cylinder-Head-Sector):** Legacy 3D coordinate system. Limited to **8.4 GB** due to 24-bit addressing constraints (1,024 cylinders × 16 heads × 63 sectors × 512 bytes ≈ 8.4 GB). The **"8.4 GB Barrier"** is a KTU favorite.
- **LBA (Logical Block Addressing):** Modern linear 48-bit addressing. Maximum addressable size = **2^48 × 512 bytes = 128 PiB**.
- **HPA (Host Protected Area):** Hidden area set via ATA commands, not visible to BIOS. Common on manufacturer recovery partitions.
- **DCO (Device Configuration Overlay):** Even more hidden area, configured at the factory.
- **MBR (Master Boot Record):** Sector 0, LBA 0. Contains bootstrap code, partition table, and signature.
- **GPT (GUID Partition Table):** Modern replacement; uses 34 sectors at start, backup at end.

### 2.3 Zone Bit Recording (ZBR) vs Constant Angular Velocity (CAV)

| Parameter | CAV (Legacy) | ZBR (Modern) |
|---|---|---|
| Sectors per track | **Constant** (e.g., 63) | **Variable** – more on outer tracks |
| Linear bit density | **Uneven** (denser on inner) | **Approximately constant** |
| Total capacity | Smaller | Larger (≈ **+20% to +30%**) |
| Spindle speed | **Constant** | **Constant** |
| Used in | Old MFM/RLL drives | All modern HDDs since 1990s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because ZBR uses variable sectors/track, the **disk capacity formula must be summed across all zones**, not multiplied by a single sector count. The standard KTU CHS formula is therefore an *approximation* valid only for CAV-era disks.

### 2.4 Access Time Decomposition (The "ST" Stack)

When the OS requests a sector, the drive performs four steps in sequence:

$$
T_{access} = T_{seek} + T_{rot\_lat} + T_{transfer} + T_{controller}
$$

Where:
- **$T_{seek}$** – Time to move the actuator arm to the correct cylinder. Average ≈ 1/3 of full stroke.
- **$T_{rot\_lat}$** – Time to wait for the correct sector to rotate under the head. Average = ½ of a full rotation.
- **$T_{transfer}$** – Time to read/write the data once positioned.
- **$T_{controller}$** – Firmware processing overhead (~0.1 to 0.3 ms).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas are universally required for PCCCT602 Module 2 problems.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Disk Capacity (CHS) | $C = N_{cyl} \times N_{head} \times N_{sec/track} \times B$ | bytes | $B = 512$ typically |
| 2 | Disk Capacity (ZBR) | $C = \sum_{i=1}^{Z} (N_{sec,i} \times B)$ | bytes | Sum over all zones |
| 3 | Full Rotation Time | $T_{rot} = \dfrac{60}{RPM}$ | seconds | |
| 4 | Average Rotational Latency | $T_{lat} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times T_{rot} = \dfrac{30}{RPM}$ | seconds | |
| 5 | Average Seek Time | $T_{seek} = \dfrac{1}{3} \times T_{FSST}$ | ms | Full stroke seek time basis |
| 6 | Data Transfer Rate (Sequential) | $R = \dfrac{N_{sec/track} \times B \times RPM}{60}$ | bytes/sec | Per track |
| 7 | Transfer Time for $N$ bytes | $T_{trans} = \dfrac{N}{R}$ | seconds | |
| 8 | Total Access Time | $T_{acc} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{trans} + T_{ctrl}$ | seconds | |
| 9 | 8.4 GB CHS Limit | $1024 \times 16 \times 63 \times 512$ | bytes | 8,455,200,768 B |
| 10 | MBR Partition Entry Size | $4 \times 16$ | bytes | 4 entries, 16 B each |
| 11 | Max LBA Address | $2^{48} - 1$ | logical blocks | 48-bit LBA |
| 12 | Max Addressable LBA Size | $2^{48} \times 512$ | bytes | $\approx 128$ PiB |
| 13 | Cluster Count | $N_{cluster} = \dfrac{Partition\,Size}{Cluster\,Size}$ | integer | File system metric |
| 14 | Slack Space | $E - (F \bmod C) \cdot B$ | bytes | $E$=end of cluster, $F$=file size, $C$=cluster size, $B$=bytes per sector |

> [!WARNING]
> In the KTU formula table above, **vertical bars were replaced with $\mid$ or $\vert$** to prevent markdown table-breaking. KTU papers may use absolute-value notation; render it as $\vert x \vert$ in your answer sheets.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Data Recovery Industry:** Knowledge of ZBR zone boundaries is critical because recovery tools must re-create the zone translation table.
- **Forensic Imaging:** The 8.4 GB barrier caused early Windows 98 systems to "lose" disk space, prompting FAT32/LBA adoption. Examiners working on legacy 1990s evidence must understand this.
- **Performance Tuning:** Database administrators use these formulas to predict RAID rebuild times.
- **Anti-Forensics Detection:** HPA/DCO usage is a key red flag in corporate espionage investigations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 – Disk Capacity Using CHS Geometry

**Problem:** A legacy HDD reports 1,024 cylinders, 16 read/write heads (i.e., 8 platters), and 63 sectors per track. Each sector is 512 bytes. Compute the total user-addressable capacity.

**Step 1 – Identify the formula.**
From the formula sheet: $C = N_{cyl} \times N_{head} \times N_{sec/track} \times B$.

**Step 2 – Substitute known values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 1024 \times 16 \times 63 \times 512
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 – Multiply the first two terms.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1024 \times 16 &= 16384
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 – Multiply by sectors per track.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
16384 \times 63 &= 16384 \times 60 + 16384 \times 3 \\
&= 983040 + 49152 \\
&= 1032192
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 – Multiply by bytes per sector.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1032192 \times 512 &= 1032192 \times 500 + 1032192 \times 12 \\
&= 516096000 + 12386304 \\
&= 528482304
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 – Convert to GB using $1 \text{ GB} = 1024^3 \text{ bytes} = 1{,}073{,}741{,}824 \text{ bytes}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{GB} &= \frac{528{,}482{,}304}{1{,}073{,}741{,}824} \\
&\approx 0.492 \text{ GB} \\
&\approx 504 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This calculation under the **maximum** CHS allowed by the original ATA spec (1,024 × 16 × 63) yields exactly **528,482,304 bytes ≈ 504 MB**, which is the *real* legacy CHS ceiling. The 8.4 GB figure is the *theoretical addressable* limit when 16-bit cylinders are extended.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 – The "8.4 GB Barrier" CHS Limit (Theoretical)

**Problem:** Show that the maximum CHS addressable space using 24-bit ATA addressing is 8,455,200,768 bytes.

**Step 1 – Recognize 24 bits split as 10:8:6 bits.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{cyl} &= 2^{10} = 1024 \\
N_{head} &= 2^{8} = 256 \quad \text{(but spec limits to 16)} \\
N_{sec/track} &= 2^{6} - 1 = 63 \quad \text{(1-based addressing)} \\
B &= 512 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 – Plug into the capacity formula using the 10:8:6 full theoretical limit (1024 × 256 × 63).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 1024 \times 256 \times 63 \times 512
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 – Combine $1024 \times 256 = 262{,}144$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 262{,}144 \times 63 \times 512
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 – Compute $262{,}144 \times 63$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
262{,}144 \times 63 &= 262{,}144 \times 60 + 262{,}144 \times 3 \\
&= 15{,}728{,}640 + 786{,}432 \\
&= 16{,}515{,}072
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 – Multiply by 512.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 16{,}515{,}072 \times 512 \\
&= 16{,}515{,}072 \times 500 + 16{,}515{,}072 \times 12 \\
&= 8{,}257{,}536{,}000 + 198{,}180{,}864 \\
&= 8{,}455{,}716{,}864 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> The widely cited "8.4 GB" arises because most ATA implementations cap $N_{head}$ at **16** (not 256), giving $1024 \times 16 \times 63 \times 512 = 8{,}455{,}200{,}768$ bytes ≈ 8.4 GB. KTU accepts either interpretation; **always state your assumption explicitly**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 – Average Rotational Latency for 7,200 RPM Disk

**Problem:** A forensic imaging workstation uses 7,200 RPM enterprise drives. Compute the average rotational latency.

**Step 1 – Convert RPM to revolutions per second.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \frac{7200}{60} = 120 \text{ revolutions per second}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 – Time for one full revolution.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{rot} &= \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{120} \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 – Convert to milliseconds.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{rot} &= \frac{1000}{120} = 8.333 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 – Average latency is half a revolution.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{lat} &= \frac{T_{rot}}{2} = \frac{8.333}{2} = 4.1667 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Alternative compact formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{lat} &= \frac{30}{RPM} \text{ seconds} = \frac{30000}{7200} \text{ ms} = 4.167 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 – Total Access Time Decomposition

**Problem:** A 7,200 RPM HDD has a full-stroke seek time of 21 ms, average seek = 1/3 of full stroke, and transfers 1,024,000 bytes at 60 MB/s. Find total access time, ignoring controller overhead.

**Step 1 – Average seek time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{seek} &= \frac{1}{3} \times 21 = 7 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 – Average rotational latency (from 3.3).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{lat} &= 4.167 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 – Transfer time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{trans} &= \frac{1{,}024{,}000}{60 \times 10^6} = 0.01707 \text{ s} = 17.067 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 – Sum all components.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{access} &= 7 + 4.167 + 17.067 = 28.234 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the **transfer time dominates** for large reads. This is why **forensic imaging is throughput-limited**, not seek-limited. Tools like **dd, FTK Imager, EnCase** use sequential reads to maximize $T_{trans}$ efficiency.

### 3.5 Derivation 5 – ZBR Total Capacity (Multi-Zone Sum)

**Problem:** A 3-zone disk has 200 / 250 / 320 sectors/track in zones 1/2/3, with each zone containing 1,000 tracks. Sector size = 512 bytes. Compute total capacity.

**Step 1 – Apply the ZBR sum formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \sum_{i=1}^{3} (N_{sec,i} \times N_{track,i} \times B)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 – Substitute values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= (200 \times 1000 \times 512) + (250 \times 1000 \times 512) + (320 \times 1000 \times 512)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 – Factor out the constant $1000 \times 512 = 512{,}000$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 512{,}000 \times (200 + 250 + 320) \\
&= 512{,}000 \times 770
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 – Final multiplication.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 512{,}000 \times 770 \\
&= 512{,}000 \times 700 + 512{,}000 \times 70 \\
&= 358{,}400{,}000 + 35{,}840{,}000 \\
&= 394{,}240{,}000 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 – Convert to GiB.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{GiB} &= \frac{394{,}240{,}000}{1{,}073{,}741{,}824} \approx 0.367 \text{ GiB} \approx 376 \text { MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.6 Derivation 6 – Slack Space Calculation

**Problem:** A file is 1,700 bytes long, cluster size = 4 KiB, sector size = 512 bytes. Compute **file slack** (between EOF and end of cluster) and **RAM slack** (between EOF and end of last sector touched).

**Step 1 – Compute the sector used by the file.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{sec} &= \left\lceil \frac{1700}{512} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 3.320 \right\rceil = 4 \text{ sectors}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 – Compute RAM slack (within the last partially-used sector).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{ram} &= 4 \times 512 - 1700 = 2048 - 1700 = 348 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 – Compute file slack (between end of last sector and end of cluster).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{file} &= 4096 - 2048 = 2048 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 – Total slack (RAM + file).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{total} &= 348 + 2048 = 2396 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!CAUTION]
> This slack space often contains **fragments of previously deleted files, memory dumps, and passwords**. Forensic image tools must capture slack. **FTK** and **EnCase** capture by default; raw `dd` does **not** include NTFS file slack unless used with `slack=` flags.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Hard Disk Drive

The diagram below maps the *interaction* of components during a forensic read operation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Host Computer via ATA / SATA / NVMe Interface] --> B[Drive Controller Firmware]
    B --> C{Cache Buffer DRAM 8 to 256 MB}
    C --> D[Command Queue NCQ or Tagged Command Queue]
    D --> E[Actuator Arm Voice Coil Motor VCM]
    E --> F[Read Write Head on Slider]
    F --> G[Platter Surface Magnetic Media]
    G --> H[Preamp and Channel Electronics]
    H --> C
    C --> I[ECC Error Correction Code Engine]
    I --> J[Sector Buffer 512 or 4096 bytes]
    J --> K[SATA / PCIe Transport to Host]
    K --> L[Forensic Imaging Tool FTK Imager or dd]
    L --> M[Bit Stream E01 or DD Image File]

    subgraph GeometryLayer["Physical Geometry Mapping"]
        P1[Platters 1 to 5 surfaces] --> P2[Tracks thousands per surface]
        P2 --> P3[Sectors 512 B or 4 Kn]
        P3 --> P4[Cylinders aligned across surfaces]
    end

    subgraph ForensicLayer["Forensic Data Sources"]
        Q1[Active Files in Clusters] --> R[Logical Layer]
        Q2[Deleted Entries in MFT or FAT] --> R
        Q3[Slack Space File and RAM] --> R
        Q4[Unallocated Clusters] --> R
        Q5[HPA DCO Hidden Areas] --> R
    end
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow — CHS-to-LBA Address Translation (Forensic Address Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CHS Triple Cylinder Head Sector] --> B[Validate CHS Range]
    B --> C{Is CHS Within BIOS Int 13h Limit 8 4 GB}
    C -- Yes --> D[Direct CHS Mapping to Physical Sector]
    C -- No --> E[Apply LBA Translation Formula]
    E --> F[LBA Number 0 to 2 to the 48 minus 1]
    D --> G[Physical Sector on Platter]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Read 512 byte Block]
    H --> I[Return to Forensic Tool]

    L[LBA Formula] -.->|LBA equals C times HeadsPerCyl plus H times SectorsPerTrack plus S minus 1| E
```

### 4.3 Disk Forensic Acquisition Workflow (Mermaid State Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Suspect Drive Seized] --> S1[Document Make Model Serial Sectors RPM Firmware]
    S1 --> S2[Connect via Hardware Write Blocker Tableau or WiebeTech]
    S2 --> S3{Verify Blocker Status Write Blocked LED}
    S3 -- OK --> S4[Hash Original Drive MD5 or SHA1]
    S4 --> S5[Create Bit Stream Image with dd or FTK]
    S5 --> S6[Hash Resulting Image]
    S6 --> S7[Compare Hashes Match]
    S7 -- Match --> S8[Image Admissible in Court]
    S7 -- Mismatch --> S9[Re image and Document Anomaly]
    S9 --> S5
    S8 --> S10[Analyze with Autopsy or EnCase]
    S10 --> S11[Recover Deleted Files Slack Unallocated]
    S11 --> S12[Generate Forensic Report with Chain of Custody]
```

### 4.4 Visual Layout of a Single Track (Pictorial Reference via Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inner Track Smaller Radius Few Sectors] --- B[Middle Track Medium Radius More Sectors]
    B --- C[Outer Track Largest Radius Most Sectors ZBR]
    A --> A1[Sector 1 to 200]
    B --> B1[Sector 201 to 450]
    C --> C1[Sector 451 to 700]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**
*Define the following terms with respect to disk drive geometry: (a) Track, (b) Sector, (c) Cylinder.*

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Track:** A track is a concentric circular path on the surface of a magnetic platter on which data is magnetically recorded. Modern drives contain **tens of thousands of tracks per surface**, with density measured in **TPI (Tracks Per Inch)**. Tracks are numbered from the outermost (track 0) inward.

(b) **Sector:** A sector is the smallest individually addressable unit on a track. It typically holds **512 bytes** of user data in legacy drives and **4,096 bytes (4 Kn)** in modern Advanced Format drives. Each sector includes synchronization bytes, address marks, ECC, and user data. The OS does not read partial sectors.

(c) **Cylinder:** A cylinder is the set of all tracks that share the same radius on *every* surface of *every* platter in the drive. Reading data from a cylinder requires **no radial movement of the actuator arm**, which is why cylinder-based addressing was historically used to optimize sequential access.

> Each sub-answer ≈ 1 mark. Examiners expect a *one-line definition + a numerical example or unit*. **Missing the byte count or TPI mention costs 0.5 marks.**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Sample Question Bank, Cyber Forensics]**
*Differentiate between CHS addressing and LBA addressing in hard disks. Why did the industry migrate to LBA?*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | CHS (Cylinder-Head-Sector) | LBA (Logical Block Addressing) |
|---|---|---|
| Coordinate system | 3D (C, H, S) | 1D linear index |
| Max address space | 24-bit ≈ 8.4 GB (10:8:6) | 48-bit ≈ 128 PiB |
| Address translation | BIOS Int 13h required | Direct linear mapping |
| Used in | Pre-1996 systems | All modern drives |

**Migration reasons:**
1. The CHS scheme imposed a hard **8.4 GB limit** that conflicted with rapidly growing drive capacities.
2. ZBR disks have *variable* sectors/track, making CHS physically meaningless; LBA bypasses geometry.
3. Simplified OS-level addressing, eliminating BIOS translation bugs.
4. Required for drives larger than **137 GB** (the 28-bit LBA limit pre-2002).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **physical structure of a Hard Disk Drive** and describe the **role of each component** in data storage and retrieval. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hard disk has **6,144 cylinders, 8 read/write heads, and 63 sectors per track**. Each sector is **512 bytes**. Calculate the **total user-addressable capacity** in MB and GB. Also determine the **average rotational latency** if the disk spins at **10,000 RPM**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

1. **Platters (2 marks):** Rigid disks made of aluminum or glass, coated with magnetic material. Typical 1–5 platters. Each has 2 surfaces.
2. **Spindle Motor (1 mark):** Rotates platters at constant angular velocity (3,600–15,000 RPM).
3. **Read/Write Heads (1 mark):** One per surface; mounted on sliders flying **3–15 nm** above the surface on air cushion.
4. **Actuator Arm + VCM (1 mark):** Voice-coil motor positions the heads radially across the platters.
5. **Tracks, Sectors, Cylinders (2 marks):** Logical geometry — tracks are concentric rings, sectors are pie slices of 512 B, cylinders are vertically aligned tracks.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Capacity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 6144 \times 8 \times 63 \times 512 \\
&= 6144 \times 8 \times 32256 \\
&= 49152 \times 32256
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $49152 \times 32256$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
49152 \times 32000 &= 1{,}572{,}864{,}000 \\
49152 \times 256 &= 12{,}582{,}912 \\
\text{Sum} &= 1{,}585{,}446{,}912 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

In MB (1 MB = 1,024 × 1,024 = 1,048,576 bytes):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{MB} &= \frac{1{,}585{,}446{,}912}{1{,}048{,}576} = 1512 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

In GB (1 GB = 1,073,741,824 bytes):

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{GB} &= \frac{1{,}585{,}446{,}912}{1{,}073{,}741{,}824} \approx 1.476 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Average Rotational Latency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{lat} &= \frac{30}{RPM} = \frac{30}{10000} = 0.003 \text{ s} = 3 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **forensic significance of HPA, DCO, slack space, and unallocated clusters**. Why is a bit-stream image superior to a logical copy in evidence acquisition? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **total access time** for a hard disk with the following specifications: full stroke seek = 18 ms, RPM = 5,400, sectors per track = 100, bytes per sector = 512, and the file to read is **512,000 bytes**. Assume average seek = 1/3 of full stroke and ignore controller overhead. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

- **HPA (Host Protected Area) (2 marks):** Hidden region set via ATA command `SET MAX ADDRESS`. Often contains manufacturer recovery partitions. Forensic tools like `hdparm` or `EnCase` can detect and image it.
- **DCO (Device Configuration Overlay) (1 mark):** Set at factory, can reduce visible capacity further. Forensic acquisition *must* use ATA commands to expose the real disk size.
- **Slack Space (2 marks):** The space between the end of file data and the end of the cluster. Contains RAM residues and fragments of previously deleted files.
- **Unallocated Clusters (1 mark):** Clusters not assigned to any active file but may still contain data of deleted files.
- **Bit-stream vs. Logical (1 mark):** A bit-stream (sector-level) image captures **everything** — deleted files, slack, swap, HPA, DCO, bad sectors. A logical copy only captures active files visible to the OS. **Bit-stream is the gold standard** for forensic admissibility.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 – Average Seek Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{seek} &= \frac{1}{3} \times 18 = 6 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 – Average Rotational Latency at 5,400 RPM:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{rot} &= \frac{60}{5400} = 0.01111 \text{ s} = 11.111 \text{ ms} \\
T_{lat} &= \frac{T_{rot}}{2} = 5.555 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 – Transfer Rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{N_{sec/track} \times B \times RPM}{60} \\
&= \frac{100 \times 512 \times 5400}{60} \\
&= \frac{27{,}648{,}000}{60} \\
&= 460{,}800 \text{ bytes/second}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 – Transfer Time for 512,000 bytes:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{trans} &= \frac{512{,}000}{460{,}800} = 1.111 \text{ seconds} = 1111 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 – Total Access Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{access} &= T_{seek} + T_{lat} + T_{trans} \\
&= 6 + 5.555 + 1111 \\
&= 1122.555 \text{ ms} \approx 1.123 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall – Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in latency** — Students often write $T_{lat} = 0.0055$ s but forget to convert to ms when summing with seek time. KTU deducts **1 mark** per unit error.
> 2. **Assuming CAV sector counts** — Modern ZBR drives have variable sectors/track. If the problem says "63 sectors/track" *explicitly*, use that. Otherwise state the assumption.
> 3. **Forgetting to subtract 1 in CHS** — In CHS addressing, sectors are 1-indexed, so the formula uses $(S - 1)$ in LBA translation. KTU deducts 0.5 marks for this.
> 4. **Using the 8.4 GB limit incorrectly** — Many students compute $1024 \times 256 \times 63 \times 512$ instead of $1024 \times 16 \times 63 \times 512$. Always state the head count assumption.
> 5. **Confusing file slack with RAM slack** — File slack = end of last sector to end of cluster; RAM slack = end of file to end of last sector. Examiners frequently test this distinction for 1 mark.
> 6. **Skipping the HPA/DCO discussion** — A "forensic image" answer without mentioning HPA/DCO is incomplete and loses 2 marks in 7-mark questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Understanding Disk Drives**

- [ ] **Physical Geometry Primitives:** Platter → Surface → Track → Sector → Cylinder. Remember **PHT-SC²**.
- [ ] **Standard sector size = 512 bytes** (unless Advanced Format, 4 Kn = 4096 bytes).
- [ ] **CHS Capacity Formula:** $C = N_{cyl} \times N_{head} \times N_{sec/track} \times 512$.
- [ ] **CHS Limits:** 10:8:6 bit split → 1024 × 16 × 63 → 8,455,200,768 bytes ≈ 8.4 GB.
- [ ] **LBA uses 48 bits** → max addressable ≈ 128 PiB.
- [ ] **ZBR** = variable sectors/track (more on outer tracks), CAV = constant. Modern drives use ZBR.
- [ ] **Rotational Latency** (avg) = $30 / RPM$ seconds = $30000 / RPM$ ms.
- [ ] **Seek Time** (avg) = 1/3 × Full Stroke Seek Time. Typical FSST = 18–25 ms.
- [ ] **Total Access Time** = Seek + Rotational Latency + Transfer + Controller Overhead.
- [ ] **Transfer Rate** = (Sectors/track × 512 × RPM) / 60 bytes/sec.
- [ ] **MBR Layout:** 446 B bootstrap + 64 B partition table (4 × 16 B) + 2 B signature (0x55AA).
- [ ] **GPT Signature:** "EFI PART" string at LBA 1.
- [ ] **HPA = Host Protected Area** (set via ATA command); **DCO = Device Configuration Overlay** (factory-set).
- [ ] **Slack Space = RAM slack + File slack.** RAM slack ≤ 511 bytes; File slack = cluster remainder.
- [ ] **Bit-stream image >> Logical copy** for forensic admissibility. Always use a **write-blocker**.
- [ ] **Chain of Custody** must accompany every forensic image. Hash (MD5/SHA-1) before and after imaging.
- [ ] **Forensic Tools:** FTK Imager, EnCase, Autopsy, dd, dcfldd, Guymager.
- [ ] **SSD vs HDD:** SSD has no moving parts, no seek/rotational latency, but introduces TRIM, wear-leveling, and garbage collection challenges for forensics.
- [ ] **Interfaces:** ATA/IDE (legacy), SATA (modern consumer), SCSI (enterprise), NVMe (PCIe, modern high-speed).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for the Eight Most Tested Disk Concepts in KTU:**
> **"GHOST-CAP"** — **G**eometry, **H**PA/DCO, **O**rdering (CHS/LBA), **S**lack space, **T**ransfer rate, **C**apacity formula, **A**ccess time, **P**artition structures (MBR/GPT). Cover all eight for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solid State Storage Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT602 - CYBER FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Solid State Storage Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solid State Storage Devices (SSSDs)** are non-volatile data storage media built entirely from solid-state electronic components — primarily **NAND or NOR flash memory** chips — with **no moving mechanical parts**, **no spinning platters**, and **no read/write heads**. They store data as **trapped electrical charges** in floating-gate transistors arranged in a grid of **blocks** and **pages**.

In the context of **Cyber Forensics (PCCCT602)**, Solid State Storage Devices are critical evidence carriers. Unlike Hard Disk Drives (HDDs), SSDs actively perform **garbage collection**, **wear leveling**, and **TRIM commands**, which **destroy residual data** automatically — making traditional forensic assumptions (e.g., slack space always contains deleted data) **invalid**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Magic Notebook vs. The Typewriter**
> 
> - An **HDD** is like an **old typewriter with ink ribbons** — to find a word, the mechanical arm must physically move to the exact line and column where that word was typed. It has gears, motors, and rotation (mechanical latency).
> 
> - An **SSD** is like a **magic notebook** — every page is instantly accessible from anywhere (random access), the notebook automatically **erases pencil marks** you make on unused pages to keep itself tidy (**TRIM**), and it shuffles your writing around so that no single page wears out from overuse (**wear leveling**). For a forensic examiner, this is both a **blessing** (fast acquisition) and a **curse** (data is silently destroyed).

### 1.3 Why SSDs Matter in Cyber Forensics

| Forensic Challenge | Impact on Traditional Methods |
|---|---|
| **TRIM Command** | OS tells SSD to mark deleted blocks as empty → data physically wiped |
| **Wear Leveling** | Data is moved to a different physical location than the OS sees |
| **Garbage Collection** | Background process invalidates stale data pages |
| **No Platters** | Concepts like *CHS addressing*, *bad sectors*, *head skew* disappear |
| **Encryption (SED)** | Self-Encrypting Drives may render raw data unreadable without keys |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** The PCCCT602 syllabus explicitly lists *Solid State Storage Devices* under Module 2, emphasizing the need to understand how their **non-deterministic data-removal behavior** challenges classical forensic procedures such as recovery of deleted files, slack space analysis, and data-carving.

### 1.4 Key Metrics (Bold Constants & Standards)

- **NAND Flash Cell Size:** typically **3 nm – 19 nm** process node (as of 2024–2025).
- **SSD Interface Speeds:** SATA III = **6 Gbps**, NVMe PCIe 4.0 = **64 Gbps**, NVMe PCIe 5.0 = **128 Gbps**.
- **Standard P/E (Program/Erase) Endurance:** SLC = **100,000 cycles**, MLC = **10,000 cycles**, TLC = **1,000–3,000 cycles**, QLC = **1,000 cycles**.
- **TRIM Command:** Defined in **ATA Command Set (ACS)** and the **NVM Express (NVMe) 1.4+** specification.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SSD Architecture — Step-by-Step Logic

An SSD is a **hierarchical, layered device**. To master the KTU-level question, trace data from the host system down to the silicon cell.

### Step 1: Host Interface Layer
- The host (computer) talks to the SSD via a standard bus.
- Two dominant interfaces in 2024–2025:
  - **SATA (Serial ATA)** — legacy, AHCI protocol, capped at ~**600 MB/s**.
  - **NVMe (Non-Volatile Memory Express)** — modern, designed for parallelism over **PCIe lanes**, up to **~14,000 MB/s** (PCIe 5.0).

### Step 2: SSD Controller (The "Brain")
- A **microcontroller** (often ARM-based) that runs **firmware**.
- Performs the **Flash Translation Layer (FTL)** mapping — the **single most forensic-critical component** — because it remaps Logical Block Addresses (LBAs) to arbitrary physical NAND locations.

### Step 3: DRAM Cache
- High-speed volatile memory used as a **write buffer** and to cache the **FTL mapping table**.
- Forensic note: data in the DRAM cache is **lost on power-off** — a key reason SSDs sometimes lose "in-flight" writes.

### Step 4: NAND Flash Array
- The actual storage medium, organized as:
  - **Channel** → **Chip** → **Die** → **Plane** → **Block** → **Page**
- A typical 2.5" SSD contains **8 to 32+ NAND packages** wired in parallel for bandwidth.

### Step 5: Power-Loss Protection
- Optional capacitors store enough energy to flush DRAM cache to NAND on sudden power loss.

## 2.2 NAND Flash Cell Types — Why It Matters for Forensics

| Cell Type | Bits per Cell | P/E Endurance | Forensics Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SLC** (Single-Level Cell) | 1 | 100,000 | Rare; military/enterprise; very durable, slower decay |
| **MLC** (Multi-Level Cell) | 2 | 10,000 | Older consumer SSDs |
| **TLC** (Triple-Level Cell) | 3 | 1,000–3,000 | Mainstream consumer 2024 |
| **QLC** (Quad-Level Cell) | 4 | 1,000 | High-density, lowest endurance |
| **PLC** (Penta-Level Cell) | 5 | <1,000 | Emerging 2025+ |

## 2.3 The Three Forensically-Critical SSD Operations

### A. Wear Leveling
**Why?** NAND cells wear out after a finite number of Program/Erase (P/E) cycles. To extend drive life, the FTL spreads writes evenly.

**Two types:**
- **Dynamic Wear Leveling:** Maps new writes to the least-used free block.
- **Static Wear Leveling:** Periodically moves *cold* (rarely-changed) data so its block can be reused.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Impact:** A file's *logical* location (what the OS sees) and *physical* location (where bits actually sit) are **decoupled**. The FTL table is the only authoritative mapping.

### B. Garbage Collection (GC)
- NAND can only write to **empty pages**, but can only erase at the **block** level.
