| File Carving Header | $\text{Offset} = f(\text{HeaderSignature})$ | Use magic bytes to recover deleted files |
| Hash Comparison | $H = \text{SHA-256}(F)$ | Verify known-bad file hash against NSRL whitelist |
| Recycle Bin SID | $\text{SID}_{R} = \text{SID}_{U} - 100$ | Recycler's SID is user SID minus 100 |
| Volume Serial Number | $\text{VSN} = \text{UTC time of format} \mod 2^{32}$ | Derived from formatting time |

### 2.4 Real-World Forensic Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Court-Admissible Reconstruction:** In legal proceedings (criminal cases, corporate internal investigations, e-discovery), the investigator presents a chronological narrative backed by timestamps extracted from at least three independent artifact sources — the **triangulation principle**. A single artifact is *suggestive*; three corroborating artifacts are *conclusive*.

**Industry applications:**
- **Law enforcement:** Homicide, fraud, child exploitation investigations
- **Corporate:** Insider threat detection, data exfiltration, IP theft
- **Incident response:** Lateral movement tracking, malware execution timeline
- **Civil litigation:** E-discovery, email forensics, contract dispute
- **Insurance:** Claims fraud validation

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: NTFS Timestamp Resolution

NTFS stores all timestamps as **64-bit Windows FILETIME** values. The conversion to human-readable form is a two-stage transformation.

**Stage 1 — FILETIME to Epoch:**

$$ \text{EpochSeconds} = \frac{\text{FILETIME} - 116444736000000000}{10000000} $$

**Stage 2 — Epoch to UTC:**

$$ \text{UTC} = \text{EpochSeconds} - \text{TimezoneOffset} - \text{DST} $$

**Worked Example — Decoding a Prefetch Last-Run Timestamp:**

Given a Prefetch file reports a last-run time as raw FILETIME value:
$$ F = 132456789012345678 $$

**Step 1 — Subtract the Windows epoch offset:**

$$ \Delta = 132456789012345678 - 116444736000000000 = 16012053012345678 $$

**Step 2 — Convert 100-nanosecond intervals to seconds:**

$$ S = \frac{16012053012345678}{10000000} = 1601205301.2345678 $$

**Step 3 — Convert Epoch to UTC date:**

Converting Unix epoch **1601205301** seconds yields:

$$ \text{Date} = 2020\text{-}09\text{-}28 \quad 12\text{:}35\text{:}01 \text{ UTC} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** A KTU question may present a raw FILETIME value and ask for the date. Memorize the constant $\mathbf{116{,}444{,}736{,}000{,}000{,}000}$ — it represents the number of 100-nanosecond intervals between **1601-01-01** (NTFS epoch) and **1970-01-01** (Unix epoch).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Windows Artifact-to-Timestamp Mapping

| Artifact | Timestamp Type | Accuracy | Persistence After Deletion |
|---|---|---|---|
| Event Log (4624) | Logon time | Second-level | Until log rotation |
| Prefetch (`.pf`) | Last 8 run times | Second-level | Survives until OS reset |
| UserAssist | Last run + count | Second-level | Survives until profile wipe |
| LNK file | Target modification time | Second-level | Survives until manual deletion |
| ShellBags | Folder access time | Day-level | **Persists even after folder deletion** |
| Jump Lists | App open + file open | Second-level | Persists in `AutomaticDestinations` |
| Shimcache | File modification time | Second-level | Persists in `AppCompat` registry |
| BAM | Last execution time | Second-level | Persists in `SYSTEM` hive |
| Recycle Bin `$I` file | Deletion time | Second-level | Persists until bin emptied |
| USB MountedDevices | First connection time | Second-level | Persists in registry |
| Browser History | Visit time | Microsecond-level | Until DB vacuumed |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step: Building a Super-Timeline (Plaso / log2timeline)

**Step 1 — Acquire the forensic image** (using FTK Imager, `dd`, or Guymager).
**Step 2 — Hash-verify** the image: $\text{SHA-256}(\text{image}) = \text{expected}$.
**Step 3 — Run Plaso:**
```bash
log2timeline.py --storage-file timeline.plaso evidence.E01
```
**Step 4 — Run psort to extract CSV:**
```bash
psort.py -o l2tcsv -w timeline.csv timeline.plaso
```
**Step 5 — Filter by user SID:**
```bash
grep "S-1-5-21-*" timeline.csv > user_timeline.csv
```
**Step 6 — Pivot by artifact source** (Prefetch + UserAssist + BAM) to confirm every program execution has at least **two corroborating sources** (triangulation).
**Step 7 — Annotate** malicious entries, mark gaps as suspicious, and export.

### 3.4 Analytical Table: Linux User Activity Sources

| Artifact | Path / Command | Information Revealed |
|---|---|---|
| `.bash_history` | `~/.bash_history` | All typed commands (often without timestamps unless `HISTTIMEFORMAT` is set) |
| `wtmp` | `last` command | Logins, logouts, reboots, duration |
| `btmp` | `lastb` command | Failed login attempts with source IP |
| `utmp` | `who`, `w` commands | Currently logged-in users |
| `/var/log/auth.log` | `auth.log` | SSH, sudo, PAM authentications |
| `lastlog` | `lastlog` command | Last login time per user |
| `faillog` | `faillog` command | Failed login count per user |
| `.viminfo` | `~/.viminfo` | Recently opened files in vim |
| `.lesshst` | `~/.lesshst` | Recently viewed files in `less` |
| `.wget-hsts` | `~/.wget-hsts` | HTTPS hosts downloaded via wget |
| `.ssh/` | `~/.ssh/known_hosts` | SSH servers the user connected to |
| `.recently-used.xbel` | GTK file picker | Recently opened files in GUI apps |
| `~/.local/share/` | XDG user dirs | Application-specific recent files |

### 3.5 Correlation Heuristic — The "Three-Confirmation Rule"

For an action to be forensically admitted, the investigator should obtain at least three independent confirmations:

$$
\text{Confidence}(A) = \begin{cases} \text{High} & \text{if } \vert C_A \vert \geq 3 \\ \text{Medium} & \text{if } \vert C_A \vert = 2 \\ \text{Low (suggestive only)} & \text{if } \vert C_A \vert = 1 \end{cases}
$$

where $C_A$ is the set of independent artifact sources confirming action $A$.

**Example:** "User executed `cmd.exe` at 14:32:15"
- ✅ **Prefetch** shows `CMD.EXE-...pf` with last-run at 14:32:15
- ✅ **UserAssist** `{CEBFF5CD-ACE2-4F4F-9178-9926F41749EA}` count incremented at 14:32:15
- ✅ **BAM** registry value shows execution at 14:32:15

Confidence = **High** (3 sources).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 User Activity Reconstruction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Evidence Source] --> B[Acquisition]
    B --> C[Verification Hash]
    C --> D[Artifact Extraction]
    D --> E1[Authentication]
    D --> E2[File System]
    D --> E3[Program Execution]
    D --> E4[Network]
    D --> E5[Peripheral]
    E1 --> F[Timeline Normalization]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    F --> G[Correlation Triangulation]
    G --> H[Super Timeline Output]
    H --> I[Investigative Narrative]
    I --> J[Court Admissible Report]
```

### 4.2 Windows User Activity Subsystem Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph OS[Operating System]
        subgraph RegistryLayer[Registry Hives]
            R1[SAM]
            R2[SYSTEM]
            R3[NTUSER DAT]
            R4[UsrClass DAT]
            R5[SECURITY]
        end
        subgraph FileSystemLayer[File System]
            F1[Prefetch pf]
            F2[Jump Lists]
            F3[LNK Files]
            F4[ShellBags]
            F5[Recycle Bin IR]
            F6[Thumbcache]
        end
        subgraph LogLayer[Log Sources]
            L1[Security Event Log]
            L2[Powercfg]
            L3[setupapi dev log]
            L4[BAM]
        end
        subgraph MemoryLayer[Volatile Memory]
            M1[RAM Artifacts]
            M2[Network Connections]
            M3[Process List]
        end
    end
    RegistryLayer --> Investigator[Forensic Investigator]
    FileSystemLayer --> Investigator
    LogLayer --> Investigator
    MemoryLayer --> Investigator
    Investigator --> Timeline[Super Timeline]
```

### 4.3 Super-Timeline Reconstruction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start Investigation] --> Acquire[Acquire Forensic Image]
    Acquire --> Verify[SHA 256 Verification]
    Verify --> Extract[Extract All Artifact Types]
    Extract --> ParsePrefetch[Parse Prefetch]
    Extract --> ParseUserAssist[Parse UserAssist ROT13]
    Extract --> ParseEventLog[Parse Security Event Log]
    Extract --> ParseLNK[Parse LNK Files]
    Extract --> ParseBrowser[Parse Browser SQLite]
    Extract --> ParseRegistry[Parse Registry Hives]
    ParsePrefetch --> Merge[Merge by Timestamp]
    ParseUserAssist --> Merge
    ParseEventLog --> Merge
    ParseLNK --> Merge
    ParseBrowser --> Merge
    ParseRegistry --> Merge
    Merge --> Correlate[Triangulate by User SID]
    Correlate --> Filter[Filter Suspicious Events]
    Filter --> Output[Generate Court Report]
```

### 4.4 Browser Forensics Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Browser[Web Browser Engine] --> B1[History DB]
    Browser --> B2[Cookies DB]
    Browser --> B3[Login Data DB]
    Browser --> B4[Cache Directory]
    Browser --> B5[Local Storage]
    Browser --> B6[Bookmarks]
    Browser --> B7[Downloads]
    Browser --> B8[Top Sites]
    B1 --> F[Forensic Analyzer]
    B2 --> F
    B3 --> F
    B4 --> F
    B5 --> F
    B6 --> F
    B7 --> F
    B8 --> F
    F --> Reconstruct[Reconstruct Web Session]
    Reconstruct --> Report[Investigation Report]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]** Define **User Activity** in the context of digital forensics. List any **four** categories of user activity artifacts.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
User Activity in digital forensics refers to the reconstruction of actions performed by a user on a computing system through the analysis of residual digital artifacts left behind by the operating system, applications, and services.
The four categories are:
1. **Authentication artifacts** — logon events, SID, Kerberos tickets
2. **File system artifacts** — MAC times, LNK files, Recycle Bin
3. **Program execution artifacts** — Prefetch, UserAssist, BAM, Amcache
4. **Network artifacts** — browser history, cookies, email logs

*(Award 0.5 for definition, 2.5 for categories — examiner's discretion per item.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model QP]** What is the significance of **NTFS MAC times** in user activity reconstruction? Mention **two limitations**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
NTFS MAC times (**Modified, Accessed, Changed/Metadata changed, Born**) record when file content was modified, when the file was last read, when the MFT entry was last altered, and when the file was created respectively. They provide a **chronological fingerprint** of file interactions and are pivotal for timeline reconstruction.

Two limitations:
1. **$STANDARD_INFORMATION attribute** can be modified by `timestomp` tools; only **$FILE_NAME** attribute is harder to forge.
2. **Last Access Time** updates may be disabled by `NtfsDisableLastAccessUpdate` registry policy (default in Windows 10+), reducing its forensic value.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **Q3(a). [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** With suitable examples, explain the role of **Windows Registry** in user activity forensics. List and explain **any five** registry keys relevant to user activity. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Windows Registry is a hierarchical database (`HKLM`, `HKCU`, `HKU`, `HKCR`, `HKCC`) that stores low-level settings and user-specific configuration. It is a *goldmine* of user activity evidence because most GUI actions persist settings here.

**Five Registry Keys Relevant to User Activity:**

| # | Key Path | Hive | Information |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `NTUSER.DAT\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\RunMRU` | NTUSER.DAT | Most Recently Used commands from `Run` dialog (Win+R) |
| 2 | `NTUSER.DAT\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\TypedPaths` | NTUSER.DAT | URLs typed into Explorer address bar |
| 3 | `NTUSER.DAT\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\UserAssist\{GUID}\Count` | NTUSER.DAT | Programs launched via GUI (ROT13 encoded) |
| 4 | `NTUSER.DAT\Software\Microsoft\Office\<version>\<app>\Place MRU` | NTUSER.DAT | Recent Office file paths |
| 5 | `SYSTEM\MountedDevices` | SYSTEM | USB drives and volumes mounted, with serial numbers |
| 6 | `NTUSER.DAT\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\RecentDocs` | NTUSER.DAT | Recently opened documents by extension |
| 7 | `SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Enum\USBSTOR` | SYSTEM | History of USB storage devices by vendor/product/serial |

**Example:** A user opens `cmd.exe` 47 times. The UserAssist key value (after ROT13 decryption) records the count as 47 and the last execution timestamp. This confirms program execution even if the Prefetch file is wiped.

*(Award 2 marks for explaining the registry's role, 5 marks for explaining the keys.)*

---

> **Q3(b). [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** Explain **Prefetch files** as a forensic artifact. How do they help in reconstructing program execution? Mention the forensic tool used for parsing them. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Prefetch Files — Definition:**
Prefetch files are created by the Windows **Superfetch / SysMain** service to optimize application loading. Located at `C:\Windows\Prefetch\*.pf`, they are stored in a proprietary binary format (MAM-compressed).

**Information Contained:**
- Executable name (up to 29 characters in filename)
- Run count (number of times executed)
- Last **8 run timestamps** (second-level resolution)
- Files and directories referenced during execution (resource usage tracking)

**Forensic Significance:**
1. **Confirms program execution** — even if the executable is deleted, the Prefetch survives until OS reset or manual cleanup.
2. **Timestamp reconstruction** — provides historical execution times.
3. **Resource path leakage** — reveals DLLs, config files, and user documents opened.
4. **Persistence post-uninstall** — some apps leave Prefetch behind even after removal.

**Parsing Tool:**
- **PECmd** (Eric Zimmerman Tools) — primary tool.
- **Windows Prefetch File Format** parser by Mark Russinovich (originally for `prefetch.exe`).
- **MFTECmd** and **Plaso** also parse Prefetch.

**Example Output:**
```
CMD.EXE-2A4B7F9C.pf
Run Count: 47
Last Run Times: 2024-09-15 14:32:15, ..., 2024-09-01 09:15:42
```

*(Award 2 marks for definition, 3 marks for forensic value, 2 marks for tool.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **Q4(a). [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Discuss **browser forensics** as part of user activity reconstruction. Explain any **five artifacts** that can be recovered from web browsers. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Browser Forensics Definition:**
Browser forensics is the extraction and analysis of artifacts left by user interactions with web browsers — searches, page visits, downloads, logins, and cached content.

**Five Artifacts:**

1. **History Database** (`History` SQLite file in profile): Contains URLs visited, visit counts, typed URLs, last visit time, and transition type (typed, clicked, redirect). Table: `urls`, `visits`.

2. **Cookies Database** (`Cookies` SQLite): Session cookies, persistent cookies, creation/last-access timestamps, host, name. Reveals authentication to web apps.

3. **Login Data** / **Logins** (`Login Data` SQLite): Saved usernames and *encrypted* passwords. Decryptable on a live system using DPAPI keys.

4. **Cache** (`Cache\Cache_Data\` directory or `Code Cache\`, `Data Cache\`): HTTP response objects, images, scripts. Timestamps reveal viewing time.

5. **Local Storage** (HTML5): Per-origin key-value pairs in `Local Storage\leveldb\`. Reveals application state, JWT tokens, user preferences.

6. **Bookmarks** (`Bookmarks` JSON file in modern Chromium): Bookmarked URLs with last-modified timestamps.

7. **Downloads** (`History` SQLite `downloads` table): Filename, source URL, target path, download time.

**Cross-Browser Locations:**
- Chrome: `%LOCALAPPDATA%\Google\Chrome\User Data\Default\`
- Firefox: `%APPDATA%\Mozilla\Firefox\Profiles\<profile>\`
- Edge: `%LOCALAPPDATA%\Microsoft\Edge\User Data\Default\`

---

> **Q4(b). [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** Explain the role of **Linux log files** in reconstructing user activity. Discuss any **five log files** with their forensic significance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Five Linux Log Files for User Activity:**

| # | Log File | Path | Forensic Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `auth.log` | `/var/log/auth.log` | SSH logins, sudo usage, PAM authentications, user switching (`su`) |
| 2 | `wtmp` | `/var/log/wtmp` (binary) | Successful logins/logouts/reboots — read via `last` command |
| 3 | `btmp` | `/var/log/btmp` (binary) | Failed login attempts with source IP — read via `lastb` |
| 4 | `syslog` | `/var/log/syslog` | General system activity, service starts/stops, kernel messages |
| 5 | `bash_history` | `~/.bash_history` | User-typed commands; **may be modified** by attacker |
| 6 | `lastlog` | `/var/log/lastlog` (binary) | Last login time per user account |
| 7 | `secure` | `/var/log/secure` (RHEL) | Authentication and authorization events |
| 8 | `cron` | `/var/log/cron` | Scheduled task executions |

**Worked Example:**
```bash
last -f /var/log/wtmp
# Output: user1  pts/2  192.168.1.10  Mon Sep 15 14:00 - 16:32 (02:32)

grep "sudo" /var/log/auth.log
# Sep 15 14:05:12 server sudo: user1 : TTY=pts/2 ; PWD=/home/user1 ; COMMAND=/bin/cat /etc/shadow
```
This proves `user1` from `192.168.1.10` ran `sudo cat /etc/shadow` — a privileged file disclosure event.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing M-time and C-time** in NTFS — Modified = content changed; Changed/Metadata = MFT entry changed. Many students swap these.
> 2. **Forgetting the ROT13 encoding** of UserAssist keys — examiners specifically look for this awareness; mentioning it scores the extra mark.
> 3. **Stating "Prefetch shows all run times"** — WRONG. Windows stores only the **last 8** run times in Prefetch.
> 4. **Writing only the path of a log file without explaining its forensic value** — KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes the **"why"** behind every artifact (as per NEP 2020 outcome-based education).
> 5. **Not mentioning SID-based user attribution** — Every artifact must be tied to a specific SID or UID for court admissibility.
> 6. **Confusing `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` (modifiable) with `$FILE_NAME` (harder to forge)** timestomping defenses.
> 7. **Ignoring anti-forensics awareness** — Examiners award marks for recognizing that `sdelete`, `timestomp`, log clearing, and Prefetch disabling are deliberate anti-forensic techniques.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

✅ **Rapid Revision Checklist — User Activity**

- 🔑 **MAC times** = Modified, Accessed, Changed (MFT), Born (NTFS only)
- 🔑 **Prefetch** stores **last 8 runs** at `C:\Windows\Prefetch\`
- 🔑 **UserAssist** is **ROT13 encoded** in `NTUSER.DAT`
- 🔑 **BAM** key location: `SYSTEM\...\BAM\State\UserSettings\<SID>`
- 🔑 **Shimcache/Amcache** = `C:\Windows\appcompat\Programs\`
- 🔑 **ShellBags persist even after folder deletion**
- 🔑 **LNK files** in `Recent\` reveal original target path + MAC times
- 🔑 **Recycle Bin** = `$I` (metadata) + `$R` (file content)
- 🔑 **Browser History** = SQLite `urls` + `visits` tables
- 🔑 **Triangulation Rule** = ≥ 3 independent sources for high confidence
- 🔑 **Linux**: `wtmp` (success), `btmp` (failures), `~/.bash_history`
- 🔑 **FILETIME conversion**: subtract **116444736000000000**, divide by **10000000**
- 🔑 **SID -100 = Recycler's SID** pattern
- 🔑 **Always correlate by SID/UID** for user attribution
- 🔑 **Anti-forensics awareness** = `sdelete`, `timestomp`, log clearing, Prefetch disabling — examiner-favored discussion point

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Connections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT602 - CYBER FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Network Connections

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Network Connections** in the context of Cyber Forensics refers to the logical and physical pathways established between computing devices, servers, and network endpoints that enable bidirectional data exchange governed by standardized communication protocols. In digital forensic investigations, a *network connection* is a **forensically significant artifact** consisting of the 5-tuple identity: **Source IP Address, Source Port, Destination IP Address, Destination Port, and Transport Protocol**, often supplemented with timestamps, packet payloads, and socket states (ESTABLISHED, LISTENING, TIME_WAIT, CLOSED).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** A *network connection* is a virtual circuit identified by the 5-tuple `⟨SrcIP, SrcPort, DstIP, DstPort, Proto⟩`. Any forensic analysis of a compromised host begins with enumerating active and historical connections via the socket table.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **postal system** between two cities:
- The **IP address** is the *house number + street name* (where the mail goes).
- The **Port number** is the *apartment number inside the house* (which resident receives it).
- The **Protocol (TCP/UDP)** is the *postal service type* (registered post vs. ordinary mail).
- The **Packet** is a *single envelope* with headers and a letter inside.
- The **Socket** is the *mailbox* where letters accumulate.

A forensic investigator is essentially a **postal inspector** checking every mailbox, every envelope's postmark (timestamp), and tracking which mail was sent where, when, and by whom. Active connections on a machine are *open mailboxes*, while closed connections leave *historical stamps* in log files.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology in Bold

| Term | Standard Value/Range |
|---|---|
| IPv4 Address Length | **32 bits (4 bytes)** |
| IPv6 Address Length | **128 bits (16 bytes)** |
| TCP Port Range | **0 – 65535** |
| Well-Known Ports | **0 – 1023** |
| Registered Ports | **1024 – 49151** |
| Dynamic/Ephemeral Ports | **49152 – 65535** |
| Default MTU (Ethernet) | **1500 bytes** |
| OSI Model Layers | **7 layers** |
| TCP/IP Model Layers | **4 layers** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 of PCCCT602 emphasizes identifying network connection artifacts in volatile memory (RAM), log files (firewall, IDS, proxy), and packet captures (PCAP files) for forensic reconstruction.

### 1.4 Categories of Network Connections

Network connections can be classified along multiple axes:

1. **By Physical Medium:**
   - **Wired (Twisted Pair, Coaxial, Fiber Optic)**
   - **Wireless (Wi-Fi 802.11 a/b/g/n/ac/ax, Cellular 4G/5G, Satellite, Bluetooth)**
   - **Hybrid (PLC, Microwave Backhaul)**

2. **By Geographic Scope:**
   - **PAN (Personal Area Network)** – range < **10 m**
   - **LAN (Local Area Network)** – range < **1 km**, e.g., office, campus
   - **MAN (Metropolitan Area Network)** – range < **100 km**, e.g., city
   - **WAN (Wide Area Network)** – global, e.g., Internet
   - **GAN (Global Area Network)** – intercontinental

3. **By Connection State (Forensic View):**
   - **ESTABLISHED** – data transfer active
   - **LISTENING** – server awaiting connection (potential backdoor)
   - **TIME_WAIT** – recently closed (post-termination timer)
   - **CLOSE_WAIT** – remote side closed, local pending close
   - **SYN_SENT** – half-open connection (SYN flood indicator)
   - **NONE** – historical/logged connection

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The TCP/IP Four-Layer Model (Operational Breakdown)

The **TCP/IP model** is the foundational framework governing all modern network connections. Unlike the 7-layer OSI model, it consolidates functionality into 4 practical layers:

1. **Network Access / Link Layer (Layer 1-2 equivalent)**
   - **Function:** Defines physical medium, framing, MAC addressing.
   - **Key Protocols:** Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), ARP, PPP.
   - **Forensic Artifact:** MAC address tables, ARP cache, switch CAM tables.
   - **Key Device:** Hub, Switch, Bridge, NIC (Network Interface Card).

2. **Internet Layer (Layer 3 equivalent)**
   - **Function:** Logical addressing, routing, packet forwarding.
   - **Key Protocols:** IPv4, IPv6, ICMP, IGMP, IPSec.
   - **Forensic Artifact:** Routing tables, IP packet headers, TTL values, IP-ID sequences.
   - **Key Device:** Router, Layer-3 Switch.

3. **Transport Layer (Layer 4 equivalent)**
   - **Function:** End-to-end reliability, flow control, multiplexing via ports.
   - **Key Protocols:** TCP (connection-oriented, reliable), UDP (connectionless, best-effort).
   - **Forensic Artifact:** Socket table (`netstat`, `ss`, `lsof`), sequence numbers, window sizes.
   - **Key Concept:** The 5-tuple uniquely identifies a logical connection.

4. **Application Layer (Layer 5-7 equivalent)**
   - **Function:** User-facing services and data exchange semantics.
   - **Key Protocols:** HTTP/HTTPS, FTP, SSH, DNS, SMTP, SMB, Telnet, SNMP.
   - **Forensic Artifact:** Application logs, transaction logs, packet payloads (if unencrypted).
   - **Key Device:** Application server, proxy, firewall.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Layer

- **Why Layered?** → Modularity, vendor interoperability, independent protocol evolution.
- **How is a connection established?** → Through the **TCP 3-Way Handshake** (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK), a sequence of control flags that creates a stateful, reliable virtual circuit.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are examinable. Memorize the variables, units, and boundary conditions.**

| # | Formula / Law | Meaning / Application | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **End-to-End Delay** $D_{e2e} = D_{prop} + D_{trans} + D_{queue} + D_{proc}$ | Total time for a packet to travel source → destination | $D_{prop} = d/s$ (distance/speed), $D_{trans} = L/R$ (bits/rate) |
| 2 | **Propagation Delay** $D_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | Time for signal to traverse the medium | $d$ = distance (m), $v$ = signal speed (m/s) |
| 3 | **Transmission Delay** $D_{trans} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | Time to push all bits of packet onto the wire | $L$ = packet size (bits), $R$ = link rate (bps) |
| 4 | **Queuing Delay** $D_{queue} = \dfrac{\rho}{1-\rho} \cdot T_{service}$ | Waiting time at router buffer | $\rho$ = traffic intensity ($La/R$), $\rho < 1$ for stability |
| 5 | **Throughput** $\text{Throughput} = \min(R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n)$ | Effective end-to-end rate of transfer | Bottleneck-limited; bps |
| 6 | **Bandwidth-Delay Product** $BDP = R \times RTT$ | Max unacknowledged data in flight | bits; determines TCP window |
| 7 | **Packet Loss Probability** $P_{loss} \approx 1 - e^{-\lambda T}$ | Poisson arrival model | $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $T$ = time window |
| 8 | **IPv4 Subnet Hosts** $N_{hosts} = 2^{h} - 2$ | Usable hosts per subnet | $h$ = host bits; subtract network + broadcast |
| 9 | **Number of Subnets** $N_{subnets} = 2^{s}$ | Total subnetworks from borrowed bits | $s$ = subnet bits borrowed |
| 10 | **Subnet Mask from Prefix** $\text{Mask} = 2^{32-p} - 1$ (in CIDR) | Convert `/p` to dotted-decimal mask | $p$ = prefix length; bits |
| 11 | **Cyclic Check Validity (CRC)** $E = 2^{n} - k + 1$ | Detectable error burst length | $n$ = CRC bits, $k$ = data bits |
| 12 | **UDP Checksum** $\text{Chk} = \sim \sum_{16\text{-bit words}} \pmod{2^{16}}$ | Internet checksum (1's complement sum) | Ones' complement of 16-bit word sum |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The two `-2` subtractions in $N_{hosts} = 2^{h} - 2$ account for the **network address** (all host bits = 0) and the **broadcast address** (all host bits = 1). Many students lose marks by writing $2^h$ instead.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Throughput & Delay calculations** underpin **QoS (Quality of Service)** engineering in telecom (VoIP, video streaming).
- **Subnetting formulas** are foundational in **enterprise network design, VLAN planning, and IP exhaustion analysis** (critical during IPv4 depletion era).
- **CRC & Checksum** are used in **forensic integrity verification** of packet captures and digital evidence.
- **Bandwidth-Delay Product** dictates **TCP window sizing** for high-latency long-haul links (e.g., intercontinental undersea cables).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: TCP 3-Way Handshake

The TCP 3-Way Handshake is a **synchronous state-establishment protocol** used to negotiate initial sequence numbers (ISNs) and synchronize the connection state between client and server.

**Step 1: Client sends SYN**

The client initiates the connection by transmitting a TCP segment with the **SYN flag set to 1** and an initial sequence number $ISN_C$ chosen randomly (per RFC 6528 to prevent TCP sequence prediction attacks).

$$
\text{Client} \rightarrow \text{Server}: \quad \text{SYN}, \, Seq = ISN_C, \, Ack = 0
$$

**Step 2: Server responds with SYN-ACK**

The server, upon receiving the SYN, allocates resources (buffer, socket entry in LISTEN → SYN_RCVD transition) and replies with both **SYN and ACK flags set**, acknowledging the client's ISN and announcing its own $ISN_S$.

$$
\text{Server} \rightarrow \text{Client}: \quad \text{SYN}=1, \, ACK=1, \, Seq=ISN_S, \, Ack=ISN_C+1
$$

**Step 3: Client sends final ACK**

The client confirms the server's ISN. The connection transitions to **ESTABLISHED** on both sides. The Acknowledgment Number is the server's $ISN_S + 1$ because the SYN flag consumes one sequence number.

$$
\text{Client} \rightarrow \text{Server}: \quad \text{ACK}=1, \, Seq=ISN_C+1, \, Ack=ISN_S+1
$$

**Generalized Verification Equation:**

$$
\forall \text{ established connection:} \quad \text{State} = f(\text{5-tuple}, \, ISN_C, \, ISN_S, \, \text{flags}, \, \text{window})
$$

**Forensic Significance:** Each SYN packet increments the IP-ID counter (in older OS), creating a **fingerprintable side-channel** that investigators use to correlate pre-attack reconnaissance with post-attack payload delivery.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Subnetting and Host Counting

**Problem:** Given network `192.168.10.0/26`, determine: (a) subnet mask, (b) number of subnets created from default class C, (c) number of usable hosts per subnet, (d) network address, (e) broadcast address, (f) first and last usable host.

**Step 1: Determine the default class and prefix**

The first octet is `192`, which falls in the range **192–223** ⇒ **Class C** network. Default Class C prefix = `/24`.

**Step 2: Calculate the new prefix and subnet mask**

Given prefix `/26`, the number of bits borrowed for subnetting is:

$$
s = p_{\text{new}} - p_{\text{default}} = 26 - 24 = 2 \text{ bits}
$$

The subnet mask in binary (32 bits total) becomes:

$$
\underbrace{11111111.11111111.11111111}_{24 \text{ network bits}}.\underbrace{11}_{2 \text{ subnet bits}}\underbrace{000000}_{6 \text{ host bits}}
$$

Converting `11111111.11111111.11111111.11000000` to decimal:

$$
255.255.255.\left(128 + 64 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0\right) = 255.255.255.192
$$

**Step 3: Number of subnets**

$$
N_{subnets} = 2^{s} = 2^{2} = 4 \text{ subnets}
$$

**Step 4: Number of usable hosts per subnet**

Host bits $h = 32 - p_{\text{new}} = 32 - 26 = 6$ bits. Therefore:

$$
N_{hosts} = 2^{h} - 2 = 2^{6} - 2 = 64 - 2 = 62 \text{ usable hosts}
$$

**Step 5: Enumerate the four subnets**

| Subnet # | Network Address | First Host | Last Host | Broadcast |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 192.168.10.0 | 192.168.10.1 | 192.168.10.62 | 192.168.10.63 |
| 1 | 192.168.10.64 | 192.168.10.65 | 192.168.10.126 | 192.168.10.127 |
| 2 | 192.168.10.128 | 192.168.10.129 | 192.168.10.190 | 192.168.10.191 |
| 3 | 192.168.10.192 | 192.168.10.193 | 192.168.10.254 | 192.168.10.255 |

**Verification using increment step:**

$$
\text{Block size} = 2^{h} = 2^{6} = 64
$$

Each successive subnet starts at the previous network address + 64, confirming the table.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Forensic Volatility Order of Network Artifacts

| Artifact | Volatility | Persistence | Forensic Tool | Recovery Difficulty |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Active Socket Table | **Seconds** | Volatile (RAM) | `netstat`, `ss`, `lsof`, `CurrPorts` | High (live system only) |
| ARP Cache | **Seconds-Minutes** | Volatile | `arp -a`, Wireshark | High |
| Routing Table | Minutes | Volatile | `route print`, `ip route` | High |
| DNS Cache | Minutes-Hours | Volatile | `ipconfig /displaydns` | Medium |
| Firewall Logs | Hours-Days | Persistent | Windows Firewall, iptables logs | Low |
| PCAP Captures | Days-Months | Persistent | Wireshark, tcpdump, NetworkMiner | Low |
| Web Proxy Logs | Months | Persistent | Squid, Bluecoat logs | Low |
| NetFlow Records | Months | Persistent | NetFlow collector, SiLK | Low |
| ISP Records | Years | Persistent (subpoena) | Court order required | Variable (jurisdictional) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order of Volatility (RFC 3227):** When triaging a live system, capture network artifacts from most volatile (active sockets) to least volatile (logs) — this is a KTU-favorite question worth 3–5 marks.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Bandwidth-Delay Product for Forensic Window Reconstruction

The **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)** is critical in forensic reconstruction of bulk data exfiltration timelines.

**Step 1: Define the variables.**

Let $R$ = link rate in bits per second, and $RTT$ = round-trip time in seconds.

$$
BDP = R \times RTT \quad \text{(units: bits)}
$$

**Step 2: Convert to bytes for readability.**

$$
BDP_{bytes} = \dfrac{R \times RTT}{8}
$$

**Step 3: Apply to a realistic scenario.**

Consider an attacker exfiltrating data over a transcontinental link with $R = 100$ Mbps and $RTT = 200$ ms.

$$
BDP = 100 \times 10^{6} \times 0.2 = 2 \times 10^{7} \text{ bits} = 2.5 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 4: Forensic interpretation.**

At any instant, up to **2.5 MB** of attacker data can be **in-flight (unacknowledged)** across the network. Investigators reconstructing exfiltration volume must account for this in-flight data to avoid under-counting by up to one BDP per session.

**Generalized Exfiltration Estimation:**

$$
V_{exfil} \approx (N_{packets} \times L_{avg}) + (N_{sessions} \times BDP)
$$

where $L_{avg}$ is the average captured packet size and $N_{sessions}$ is the number of distinct TCP sessions observed.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 TCP/IP Layered Architecture with Forensic Artifact Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    app["APPLICATION LAYER"]
    http["HTTP HTTPS FTP SSH DNS SMTP SMB"]
    arf["Forensic Artifact: Application Logs Proxy Logs Payload Data"]

    trans["TRANSPORT LAYER"]
    tcp["TCP UDP"]
    trf["Forensic Artifact: Socket Table Sequence Numbers Window Sizes Flags"]

    net["INTERNET LAYER"]
    ip["IPv4 IPv6 ICMP IPSec"]
    nrf["Forensic Artifact: Routing Table IP Headers TTL IP ID"]

    link["NETWORK ACCESS LAYER"]
    eth["Ethernet 802.3 WiFi 802.11 ARP"]
    lrf["Forensic Artifact: MAC Table ARP Cache Frame Headers"]

    app --> http --> arf
    trans --> tcp --> trf
    net --> ip --> nrf
    link --> eth --> lrf

    app --> trans
    trans --> net
    net --> link
```

### 4.2 TCP 3-Way Handshake State Machine

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as CLIENT
    participant S as SERVER

    Note over C: State CLOSED
    Note over S: State LISTEN

    C->>S: SYN Seq ISN_C
    Note over C: State SYN_SENT
    Note over S: State SYN_RCVD

    S->>C: SYN Seq ISN_S Ack ISN_C_plus_1
    Note over S: Resources allocated

    C->>S: ACK Seq ISN_C_plus_1 Ack ISN_S_plus_1
    Note over C: State ESTABLISHED
    Note over S: State ESTABLISHED

    Note over C,S: Connection Open - Data Transfer Begins
```

### 4.3 Network Forensics Investigation Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INCIDENT DETECTION] --> B[NETWORK ISOLATION]
    B --> C[LIVE PACKET CAPTURE]
    C --> D[STORAGE OF PCAP EVIDENCE]
    D --> E[CHAIN OF CUSTODY DOCUMENTATION]
    E --> F[TRAFFIC RECONSTRUCTION]
    F --> G[PROTOCOL PARSING AND DECODING]
    G --> H[ARTIFACT EXTRACTION]
    H --> I[5-TUPLE CORRELATION]
    I --> J[TIMELINE RECONSTRUCTION]
    J --> K[ANOMALY IDENTIFICATION]
    K --> L[EVIDENCE REPORTING]
    L --> M[COURT ADMISSIBILITY REVIEW]

    subgraph A1["Live System Artifacts"]
        S1[Active Sockets]
        S2[ARP Cache]
        S3[DNS Cache]
        S4[Routing Table]
    end

    subgraph A2["Persistent Artifacts"]
        P1[Firewall Logs]
        P2[IDS IPS Alerts]
        P3[Proxy Logs]
        P4[NetFlow Records]
    end

    C -.-> A1
    D -.-> A2
```

### 4.4 Network Connection Classification Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    NC[NETWORK CONNECTIONS]

    NC --> PHY[Physical Medium]
    NC --> GEO[Geographic Scope]
    NC --> STATE[Connection State]
    NC --> PROTO[Protocol Type]

    PHY --> WIRED[Wired]
    PHY --> WIRELESS[Wireless]
    PHY --> HYBRID[Hybrid]

    WIRED --> TWIST[Twisted Pair Cat5e Cat6]
    WIRED --> COAX[Coaxial]
    WIRED --> FIBER[Fiber Optic SMF MMF]

    WIRELESS --> WIFI[WiFi 802.11]
    WIRELESS --> CELL[Cellular 4G 5G]
    WIRELESS --> SAT[Satellite]
    WIRELESS --> BT[Bluetooth]

    GEO --> PAN[PAN less than 10m]
    GEO --> LAN[LAN less than 1km]
    GEO --> MAN[MAN less than 100km]
    GEO --> WAN[WAN Global]

    STATE --> EST[ESTABLISHED]
    STATE --> LIS[LISTENING]
    STATE --> SYN[SYN_SENT]
    STATE --> TW[TIME_WAIT]
    STATE --> CW[CLOSE_WAIT]

    PROTO --> TCP[TCP Connection Oriented]
    PROTO --> UDP[UDP Connectionless]
    PROTO --> ICMP[ICMP Control]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define a network connection in the context of cyber forensics. What is the 5-tuple used to uniquely identify a TCP connection?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **network connection** is a logical communication channel established between two endpoints for the exchange of data, governed by standardized protocols (TCP/IP suite). In cyber forensics, it is treated as a forensic artifact that reveals communication patterns, attacker C2 (Command-and-Control) channels, and data exfiltration routes.

The **5-tuple** that uniquely identifies a TCP connection consists of:
1. **Source IP Address** – logical address of the sender
2. **Source Port Number** – sender's application port (0–65535)
3. **Destination IP Address** – logical address of the receiver
4. **Destination Port Number** – receiver's service port (e.g., 80, 443)
5. **Transport Protocol** – TCP or UDP

**Marking Scheme:** Definition (1 mark), 5-tuple enumeration (2 marks).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List and briefly explain the four layers of the TCP/IP model with one protocol example per layer.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The TCP/IP model has four layers:

1. **Network Access Layer** – Handles physical transmission and data link framing. *Example: Ethernet (IEEE 802.3).*
2. **Internet Layer** – Provides logical addressing and routing. *Example: IPv4 / IPv6.*
3. **Transport Layer** – Ensures end-to-end communication, multiplexing via ports. *Example: TCP, UDP.*
4. **Application Layer** – Provides user-facing services. *Example: HTTP, DNS, FTP.*

**Marking Scheme:** Layer name + function (0.5 each = 2 marks), one protocol example per layer (0.25 each = 1 mark). Total = 3 marks.

---

### PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Set 1**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **TCP 3-Way Handshake** used to establish a connection. Clearly state the flags, sequence numbers, and state transitions at each step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Class C network `200.10.50.0` is subnetted using a `/27` mask. Calculate:
- (i) Subnet mask in dotted decimal
- (ii) Number of subnets
- (iii) Number of usable hosts per subnet
- (iv) The network, first host, last host, and broadcast addresses of the **2nd subnet**.
**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) TCP 3-Way Handshake (7 marks):**

The TCP 3-Way Handshake synchronizes sequence numbers and allocates resources on both endpoints before data transfer.

**Step 1 — SYN (Client → Server) [1 mark]**
- The client sends a TCP segment with the **SYN flag = 1**.
- An **Initial Sequence Number (ISN_C)** is randomly chosen (per RFC 6528).
- Client state transitions: **CLOSED → SYN_SENT**.

**Step 2 — SYN-ACK (Server → Client) [2 marks]**
- Server replies with **SYN = 1, ACK = 1**.
- Server's own ISN_S is sent; ACK number = ISN_C + 1.
- Server state: **LISTEN → SYN_RCVD**.

**Step 3 — ACK (Client → Server) [1 mark]**
- Client acknowledges with **ACK = 1**, Ack = ISN_S + 1.
- Both states transition to **ESTABLISHED**.
- Data transfer can now begin.

**Diagram (3 marks):** Sequence diagram showing the three segments, flags, and state transitions. (See SECTION 4.2 for reference.)

---

**(b) Subnetting Calculation (7 marks):**

Given: `200.10.50.0/27` (Class C default = /24)

**(i) Subnet Mask [1 mark]:**
Bits borrowed $s = 27 - 24 = 3$. Subnet mask = `255.255.255.224`.

**(ii) Number of Subnets [1 mark]:**
$$N_{subnets} = 2^{s} = 2^{3} = 8 \text{ subnets}$$

**(iii) Usable Hosts per Subnet [1 mark]:**
Host bits $h = 32 - 27 = 5$.
$$N_{hosts} = 2^{5} - 2 = 32 - 2 = 30 \text{ hosts}$$

**(iv) 2nd Subnet Details [4 marks — 1 mark each]:**
Block size $= 2^{5} = 32$. The 2nd subnet (index 1) starts at offset $1 \times 32 = 32$.

| Field | Address |
|---|---|
| Network Address | `200.10.50.32` |
| First Usable Host | `200.10.50.33` |
| Last Usable Host | `200.10.50.62` |
| Broadcast Address | `200.10.50.63` |

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Resit Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **OSI 7-layer model** and compare it with the **TCP/IP 4-layer model**. Tabulate at least three differences. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Order of Volatility (RFC 3227)** as applied to network forensic evidence collection. Construct a forensic acquisition procedure for a live Linux server suspected of being compromised, listing the commands used at each step. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) OSI vs TCP/IP Model (7 marks):**

**OSI 7-Layer Model (Reference Model) [2 marks]:**
1. Physical — bits over medium
2. Data Link — MAC, framing, error detection
3. Network — IP, routing
4. Transport — TCP, UDP, ports
5. Session — dialogues, synchronization
6. Presentation — encryption, compression, encoding
7. Application — user services (HTTP, SMTP)

**TCP/IP 4-Layer Model (Practical Implementation) [2 marks]:**
1. Network Access (Link + Physical)
2. Internet (Network)
3. Transport (Transport)
4. Application (Session + Presentation + Application)

**Comparison Table [3 marks]:**

| Parameter | OSI Model | TCP/IP Model |
|---|---|---|
| Layers | 7 | 4 |
| Nature | Conceptual/Reference | Practical/Implementation |
| Developed by | ISO | DARPA (US DoD) |
| Rigidity | Strict layer boundaries | Loose boundaries |
| Protocol dependency | Protocol-independent | Protocol-centric (TCP, IP) |
| Usage | Teaching/Design | Internet/Global networks |

---

**(b) Order of Volatility & Live Acquisition (7 marks):**

**Order of Volatility — Definition [2 marks]:**
RFC 3227 defines that evidence must be collected in order from **most volatile** (lost first) to **least volatile** (most persistent). For network artifacts, this means capturing live connections before logs.

**Linux Forensic Acquisition Procedure [5 marks]:**

| Order | Artifact | Command |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Date/Time (Time Skew) | `date -u` |
| 2 | Network Interfaces (Live) | `ip addr`, `ifconfig` |
| 3 | Active Connections | `netstat -tunap`, `ss -tunap` |
| 4 | Listening Services | `netstat -tulnp` |
| 5 | ARP Cache | `arp -an` |
| 6 | Routing Table | `netstat -rn`, `ip route` |
| 7 | DNS Cache | `cat /etc/resolv.conf`, `nscd -g` |
| 8 | Open Files (Sockets) | `lsof -i` |
| 9 | Live Packet Capture | `tcpdump -w evidence.pcap` |
| 10 | Process List | `ps auxf` |
| 11 | Firewall Rules (Persistent) | `iptables -L -n -v` |
| 12 | System Logs (Persistent) | `/var/log/auth.log`, `/var/log/syslog` |

Each step preserves evidence in decreasing order of volatility. Hashing with `sha256sum` should accompany each artifact for **chain of custody**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Subnetting arithmetic:** Students often forget to subtract 2 for network and broadcast addresses. Writing $2^5 = 32$ instead of **30 usable hosts** costs full marks.
> 2. **TCP 3-Way Handshake:** Many omit the **state transition names** (SYN_SENT, SYN_RCVD, ESTABLISHED). Examiners award 1 mark specifically for state diagram.
> 3. **5-tuple:** Writing only 3 fields (IP+IP+Port) instead of 5 is a frequent error; the protocol field is mandatory.
> 4. **OSI vs TCP/IP mapping:** Do not claim "OSI is used on the Internet" — the **Internet uses TCP/IP**; OSI is a reference model.
> 5. **Order of Volatility:** Always capture **clock skew** (`date -u`) FIRST, else all subsequent timestamps lose admissibility in court.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Network Connection Definition:** A logical channel identified by the **5-tuple** `⟨SrcIP, SrcPort, DstIP, DstPort, Proto⟩`.
- **TCP/IP Layers:** Network Access, Internet, Transport, Application — each with distinct forensic artifacts.
- **OSI Layers:** 7 layers (Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application).
- **TCP 3-Way Handshake:** **SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK** with state transitions **CLOSED → SYN_SENT → SYN_RCVD → ESTABLISHED**.
- **Port Ranges:** Well-known (0–1023), Registered (1024–49151), Dynamic (49152–65535).
- **Subnetting Formula:** $N_{hosts} = 2^{h} - 2$, $N_{subnets} = 2^{s}$, $h = 32 - p$.
- **Order of Volatility (RFC 3227):** Capture most volatile (RAM, sockets) → least volatile (disk logs).
- **Forensic Tools:** `netstat`, `ss`, `lsof`, `tcpdump`, Wireshark, NetworkMiner, Volatility, FTK.
- **Key Network Artifacts:** Socket table, ARP cache, DNS cache, routing table, firewall logs, PCAP files, NetFlow records.
- **Bandwidth-Delay Product:** $BDP = R \times RTT$ — critical for exfiltration volume estimation.
- **Always Hash Evidence:** `sha256sum` or `md5sum` for chain-of-custody integrity.
- **IPv4 = 32 bits, IPv6 = 128 bits** — common exam trap.
- **MTU = 1500 bytes** for standard Ethernet — affects packet fragmentation forensics.
- **Connection States to recognize:** ESTABLISHED, LISTENING, TIME_WAIT, CLOSE_WAIT, SYN_SENT (SYN flood indicator).
- **Common Forensic Subnet Trick:** `/30` → 2 usable hosts (point-to-point links); `/31` → 2 hosts (RFC 3021); `/32` → 1 host (loopback).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Running Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT602 - CYBER FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Running Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In Cyber Forensics, a **Running Process** is an instance of a computer program (executable image) that is currently loaded into **Random Access Memory (RAM)** and is being executed by one or more threads under the supervision of the operating system kernel. From a forensic standpoint, running processes constitute the most **volatile form of digital evidence**, as they exist only as long as the system is powered on and the process is not terminated by the user, the OS, or a crash.

Each running process is uniquely identified by a numeric identifier called the **Process Identifier (PID)** and maintains a hierarchical relationship with its initiator through the **Parent Process Identifier (PPID)**. The collection, analysis, and documentation of running processes form a foundational pillar of **Live Forensics** and **Memory Forensics** investigations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Running processes fall under the **Live Forensics / Volatile Data Acquisition** segment of the Cyber Forensics syllabus. Examiners frequently test students on the *Order of Volatility (RFC 3227)* and the *system commands* used to capture process information during a live response.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large, busy hospital emergency room. Each doctor walking around, treating patients, and making decisions is like a **running process** — actively performing work, holding temporary information (clipboards, charts, stethoscope readings), and consuming resources. The hospital administrator is the **Operating System (OS) kernel**, keeping a master register of who is doing what, when they arrived (start time), and who called them in (parent process).

Now imagine a crime has occurred in the hospital. A forensic investigator arrives and needs to know:
- **Who is currently in the building?** (List of running processes)
- **What are they doing right now?** (Active threads, open files, network connections)
- **Who sent them?** (Parent process / origin / spawn chain)
- **Are any of them acting suspiciously?** (Unknown PIDs, unsigned binaries, processes running from `C:\Temp\`)

If the investigator waits too long or pulls the power plug, the doctors go home, the charts are destroyed, and the evidence vanishes forever. This is precisely why **running processes are the most fragile, time-sensitive evidence** in digital forensics.

### 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Process** | An executing program with its own memory space, registers, and system resources. |
| **PID (Process Identifier)** | A unique numeric ID assigned by the OS to identify a process. Typical range: **4 to 65,536** on 32-bit Windows; up to **4,194,303** on 64-bit. |
| **PPID (Parent PID)** | The PID of the process that spawned (created) the current process. |
| **Thread** | A lightweight execution unit *inside* a process. One process can host multiple threads. |
| **Handle** | An abstract reference (integer token) to an open OS resource (file, socket, registry key). |
| **Daemon / Service** | A long-running background process, often started at boot by the OS or another service. |
| **Zombie / Orphan** | A terminated process awaiting cleanup by its parent (zombie) or re-parented to PID 1 (orphan). |
| **Image Path** | The full filesystem location of the executable that backs the process. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Forensic Significance:** On Windows, every running process has a corresponding kernel object called an **EPROCESS** structure. On Linux, this is the **`task_struct`** in kernel memory. Forensic tools like **Volatility** and **Rekall** parse these structures directly from a memory dump to enumerate processes — even rootkits that hide from the OS API.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Running Process

A running process is not merely code; it is a complex collection of resources managed by the kernel. For forensic purposes, we break a process into the following **logical layers**:

1. **Executable Image Layer** — The binary file on disk that was loaded (e.g., `C:\Windows\System32\svchost.exe`).
2. **Memory Space Layer** — Virtual address space containing code, data, stack, and heap segments.
3. **Kernel Object Layer** — OS metadata structures (EPROCESS on Windows, `task_struct` on Linux).
4. **Handle Table Layer** — References to open files, sockets, registry keys, and other kernel objects.
5. **Thread Layer** — One or more execution contexts sharing the process's resources.
6. **Environment Layer** — Environment variables, current working directory, command-line arguments.

### 2.2 The Five-State Process Model

Every running process cycles through a finite set of states. This is the **classic five-state model** taught in OS fundamentals and tested in KTU examinations.

| State | Description |
|---|---|
| **New** | Process is being created. OS allocates a PID and prepares structures. |
| **Ready** | Process is loaded into RAM and waiting for CPU time on the ready queue. |
| **Running** | Process is currently being executed by the CPU. Only **one** process per core can be in this state. |
| **Blocked / Waiting** | Process cannot proceed until an I/O event, signal, or resource becomes available. |
| **Terminated / Exit** | Process has finished execution. OS reclaims its resources (PID is freed). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Implication:** When a forensic investigator dumps memory, processes in the **Ready** and **Running** states leave the richest artifacts. Processes that have just transitioned to **Terminated** may still have residual structures in the kernel pool that can be recovered for a short window.

### 2.3 Parent-Child Process Tree

Processes rarely exist in isolation. The OS uses a **hierarchical, tree-shaped spawn model**:

- **Init / smss.exe (Windows) / systemd (Linux)** is the root (typically PID 1 or PID 4 on Windows).
- Each new process is spawned by an existing process using system calls like `fork()`/`exec()` (Unix) or `CreateProcess()` (Windows).
- This creates a **process tree** that maps the chain of execution.

Forensically, the parent-child tree is a **goldmine** because:
- Malware often spawns child processes (e.g., `winword.exe` → `cmd.exe` → `powershell.exe`).
- Process injection attacks (e.g., Process Hollowing) cause **PID inconsistencies** that can be detected by comparing the on-disk binary hash with the in-memory image.

### 2.4 Order of Volatility (RFC 3227)

The **Internet Engineering Task Force (IETF) RFC 3227** establishes the canonical order in which digital evidence should be collected, from most to least volatile:

$$
\text{CPU Registers / Cache} \;\rightarrow\; \text{RAM (Running Processes)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Network State} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Disk Artifacts} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Logs} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Physical Configuration} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Archival Media}
$$

**Running processes sit at rank #2** — they are the second-most volatile evidence and must be captured *before* the investigator pulls the plug.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Cyber Forensics is a procedural science, not a heavy-mathematics subject. The "formulas" below are operational rules, key numeric thresholds, and command-syntax templates that recur in KTU theory questions.

| # | Concept | Notation / Rule | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **PID Range (32-bit Windows)** | $\text{PID} \in [4,\ 65536]$ | Integer |
| 2 | **PID Range (64-bit Windows)** | $\text{PID} \in [4,\ 4194303]$ | Integer (4 $\times$ $2^{20} - 1$) |
| 3 | **PPID Definition** | $\text{PPID} = f(\text{spawning process PID})$ | Integer |
| 4 | **PID Reuse Cycle** | $\text{PID} = \text{Next free slot in table}$ | OS-dependent, $\le 65536$ |
| 5 | **Memory-to-Disk Hash Mismatch** | $H_{\text{memory}} \neq H_{\text{disk}} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Tampering suspected}$ | SHA-1 / MD5 / SHA-256 |
| 6 | **Order of Volatility Rank** | $V_{\text{process}} = 2$ (per RFC 3227) | Ordinal |
| 7 | **Threads per Process** | $T \geq 1$, $T \in \mathbb{N}$ | Integer |
| 8 | **Forensic Time Window** | $t_{\text{evidence}} \propto \frac{1}{V_{\text{volatility}}}$ | Seconds (volatile) to Years (archival) |

**Where:**
- $H_{\text{memory}}$ = cryptographic hash of the in-memory executable image.
- $H_{\text{disk}}$ = cryptographic hash of the file on disk.
- $V_{\text{process}}$ = volatility rank of running processes in RFC 3227.
- $T$ = number of threads in the process.
- $t_{\text{evidence}}$ = the duration for which the evidence remains available.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

In professional incident response and SOC (Security Operations Center) environments, running-process analysis is used to:

- **Detect malware** that hides its presence (rootkits, fileless malware).
- **Trace lateral movement** by mapping the process spawn tree across endpoints.
- **Correlate network connections** with the PIDs that own them.
- **Investigate insider threats** by analyzing process activity during off-hours.
- **Support legal proceedings** by producing a defensible chain of custody for volatile evidence.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> While Cyber Forensics is not a calculus-heavy subject, it relies on **logical derivations** of investigative steps, hash-comparison proofs, and process-tree constructions. Below are the canonical analytical workflows a KTU student must master.

### 3.1 Analytical Workflow: Live Process Acquisition

When a forensic investigator first encounters a live system, the following ordered procedure is used to capture running-process evidence. Each step is derived from the **principle of least intrusion** and the **order of volatility**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & \text{Arrive at scene, photograph the screen.} \\
\text{Step 2: } & \text{Note system time and document investigator actions.} \\
\text{Step 3: } & \text{Enumerate running processes (volatile data).} \\
\text{Step 4: } & \text{Capture network connections per PID.} \\
\text{Step 5: } & \text{Dump physical memory (RAM image).} \\
\text{Step 6: } & \text{Collect login sessions and scheduled tasks.} \\
\text{Step 7: } & \text{Hash every collected artifact for chain of custody.} \\
\text{Step 8: } & \text{Repeat steps 3-7 on each subsequent host in scope.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why this order?** Steps 3-4 must precede Step 5 because the act of dumping RAM itself loads kernel utilities (e.g., `winpmem`, `Magnet RAM Capture`) as new processes, polluting the volatile dataset. By enumerating processes *first*, the investigator freezes a clean snapshot.

### 3.2 Analytical Proof: Detecting Process Hollowing via Hash Mismatch

**Process Hollowing** is a defense-evasion technique where malware:
1. Launches a legitimate process (e.g., `svchost.exe`) in a suspended state.
2. Unmaps the legitimate code from memory.
3. Injects malicious code into the freed address space.
4. Resumes the thread.

The on-disk binary remains the *legitimate* file, but the in-memory image is *malicious*. We can detect this with a hash-comparison derivation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } H_{\text{disk}} & = \text{SHA-256}(\text{file at } \texttt{C:\textbackslash Windows\textbackslash System32\textbackslash svchost.exe}) \\
\text{Let } H_{\text{memory}} & = \text{SHA-256}(\text{in-memory executable section of PID } 1234) \\
\text{Let } \text{ImagePath}_{\text{PID 1234}} & = \texttt{C:\textbackslash Windows\textbackslash System32\textbackslash svchost.exe} \\
\text{Decision Rule: } & \begin{cases} \text{Clean}, & \text{if } H_{\text{disk}} = H_{\text{memory}} \\ \text{Suspicious}, & \text{if } H_{\text{disk}} \neq H_{\text{memory}} \end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A naïve student may conclude that any hash mismatch is malware. In reality, **self-extracting archives, JIT-compiled runtimes, and digitally signed modules** may legitimately exhibit in-memory differences. Always cross-correlate with digital signatures and known-good baselines.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Process Enumeration Commands Across OS

| OS | Command | Output Fields | Forensic Caveat |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Windows (CMD)** | `tasklist /v` | PID, Image Name, Session, Mem Usage, Window Title | Live only; misses hidden processes. |
| **Windows (PowerShell)** | `Get-Process` | PID, Path, Company, Handles, Threads | Pipeline-friendly for filtering. |
| **Windows (Sysinternals)** | `pslist -t` | PID, PPID, threads, priority, tree view | Gold standard for tree mapping. |
| **Linux** | `ps -ef` | UID, PID, PPID, C, STIME, TTY, CMD | Cross-checks `/proc` filesystem. |
| **Linux** | `ps auxf` | Forest view of parent-child tree | ASCII tree visualization. |
| **macOS** | `ps -axo pid,ppid,comm` | PID, PPID, command name | BSD-style flags. |
| **Memory Forensics** | `volatility -f mem.dmp --profile=Win10x64 pslist` | Offset, Name, PID, PPID, Threads, Started, Exit | Bypasses rootkits via EPROCESS scanning. |

### 3.4 Construction of a Process Spawn Tree (Worked Example)

Consider the following events observed on a compromised Windows host during live response:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Event 1: } & \text{User double-clicks} \texttt{ invoice.docm}. \\
\text{Event 2: } & \texttt{winword.exe} \text{(PID 4012) is launched.} \\
\text{Event 3: } & \text{Macro fires, spawning} \texttt{ cmd.exe} \text{(PID 4280, PPID 4012).} \\
\text{Event 4: } & \texttt{cmd.exe} \text{executes PowerShell:} \texttt{ powershell.exe} \text{(PID 4310, PPID 4280).} \\
\text{Event 5: } & \text{PowerShell downloads and runs} \texttt{ updater.exe} \text{(PID 4400, PPID 4310).} \\
\text{Event 6: } & \texttt{updater.exe} \text{spawns} \texttt{ svchost.exe} \text{(PID 4500, PPID 4400).}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Constructed Tree:**

$$
\texttt{winword.exe (4012)} \;\rightarrow\; \texttt{cmd.exe (4280)} \;\rightarrow\; \texttt{powershell.exe (4310)} \;\rightarrow\; \texttt{updater.exe (4400)} \;\rightarrow\; \texttt{svchost.exe (4500)}
$$

**Forensic Conclusion:** A legitimate user opening a Word document should *never* result in `cmd.exe` and `powershell.exe` being spawned. The chain from `winword.exe → cmd.exe → powershell.exe` is a textbook signature of a **macro-based malware delivery**.

### 3.5 Volatility Process Enumeration Algorithm (Logical Form)

Volatility uses **linked-list traversal** through kernel structures. The logical derivation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Input: } & \text{Raw memory dump } M, \text{ OS profile } P \\
\text{Output: } & \text{List of processes } \{p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n\} \\
\text{Step 1: } & \text{Locate} \texttt{PsActiveProcessHead} \text{ in kernel memory using profile } P. \\
\text{Step 2: } & \text{Follow} \texttt{ActiveProcessLinks} \text{(doubly-linked list) to enumerate EPROCESS blocks.} \\
\text{Step 3: } & \text{For each EPROCESS, extract Name, PID, PPID, CreateTime, ExitTime.} \\
\text{Step 4: } & \text{Cross-reference with PEB (Process Environment Block) to recover image path.} \\
\text{Step 5: } & \text{Output enriched process list to investigator.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This algorithm is what makes Volatility able to **detect hidden processes** — it does not trust the OS API. It walks the kernel's own bookkeeping, which rootkits often fail to fully sanitize.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Figure 1: The Five-State Process Model

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New
    New --> Ready : Admitted
    Ready --> Running : Scheduler Dispatch
    Running --> Ready : Preempted
    Running --> Blocked : I_O or Event Wait
    Blocked --> Ready : I_O or Event Complete
    Running --> Terminated : Exit
    New --> Terminated : Abort
    Terminated --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The arrows represent state transitions triggered by the OS scheduler, I/O completion, or explicit process actions. Forensically, the **Running → Terminated** edge is where evidence begins to decay in volatile memory.

### 4.2 Figure 2: Live Forensics Acquisition Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Investigator Arrives at Scene] --> B[Photograph Screen and Note Time]
    B --> C[Document Investigator Actions in Log]
    C --> D[Enumerate Running Processes]
    D --> E[Map Network Connections to PIDs]
    E --> F[Dump Physical Memory to External Drive]
    F --> G[Collect Open Files and Registry Hives]
    G --> H[Compute Cryptographic Hashes of Artifacts]
    H --> I[Verify Hashes and Seal Chain of Custody]
    I --> J[Transport Artifacts to Forensic Lab]
    J --> K[Lab Analysis with Tools like Volatility]

    subgraph VolatileLayer["VOLATILE EVIDENCE ZONE  Capture First"]
        D
        E
        F
    end

    subgraph StableLayer["LESS VOLATILE EVIDENCE ZONE  Capture Second"]
        G
    end

    subgraph CustodyLayer["CHAIN OF CUSTODY ZONE  Required for All Artifacts"]
        H
        I
        J
        K
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The VolatileLayer (running processes, network connections, RAM) MUST be acquired *before* the StableLayer (files, registry) because pulling power or rebooting destroys the volatile dataset irreversibly.

### 4.3 Figure 3: Process Spawn Tree of a Macro-Based Attack

```mermaid
graph TD
    winword["winword.exe PID 4012"]
    cmd["cmd.exe PID 4280 PPID 4012"]
    ps["powershell.exe PID 4310 PPID 4280"]
    upd["updater.exe PID 4400 PPID 4310"]
    svch["svchost.exe PID 4500 PPID 4400"]

    winword --> cmd
    cmd --> ps
    ps --> upd
    upd --> svch

    style winword fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style cmd fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    style ps fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    style upd fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    style svch fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Color Legend:** Blue = legitimate user process. Orange = suspicious shell child. Red = confirmed malicious payload. The transition from blue to orange is the **Indicator of Compromise (IoC)** the investigator should flag.

### 4.4 Figure 4: Volatility Memory Forensics Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Disk["ON-DISK MEMORY DUMP"]
        M[mem.dmp raw bytes]
    end

    subgraph VolEngine["VOLATILITY ENGINE"]
        P[Profile Selector e.g. Win10x64]
        K[Kernel Structure Locator PsActiveProcessHead]
        L[Linked List Walker ActiveProcessLinks]
        R[EPROCESS Parser Name PID PPID Time]
        X[PEB Cross Reference Image Path Command Line]
    end

    subgraph Output["FORENSIC OUTPUT"]
        T1[pslist Table]
        T2[pstree Tree]
        T3[psscan Hidden Process Scan]
        T4[cmdline Command Line Args]
    end

    M --> K
    P --> K
    K --> L
    L --> R
    R --> X
    X --> T1
    X --> T2
    X --> T3
    X --> T4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why four outputs from one input?** Different plugins answer different investigative questions: `pslist` gives the live registry, `pstree` reveals parent-child anomalies, `psscan` finds terminated or hidden processes via pool-tag scanning, and `cmdline` recovers the exact command that launched each process.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
> **Q1.** Define a *running process* in the context of cyber forensics. Why is it classified as highly volatile evidence?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A running process is an active instance of a program loaded into RAM and being executed by the OS, uniquely identified by a **PID** and traceable to a **parent process** via its **PPID**. It is classified as highly volatile because it resides in **Random Access Memory (RAM)**, which loses all its contents the moment the system is powered off, rebooted, or crashes. Per **RFC 3227**, running processes occupy the second rank in the order of volatility, just below CPU registers, and must therefore be the *first* type of evidence captured during a live forensic response.
>
> *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for PID/PPID, 1 mark for volatility reasoning and RFC 3227.)*

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
> **Q2.** List any **three** commands used to enumerate running processes on a Windows system during a live forensic investigation.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. `tasklist` — built-in Windows command-line utility that displays PID, image name, session number, and memory usage of all running processes.
> 2. `Get-Process` — PowerShell cmdlet that returns rich process objects including path, company, handle count, and supports filtering.
> 3. `pslist -t` — Sysinternals utility that displays processes in a **tree view**, revealing parent-child relationships, which is critical for spotting abnormal spawn chains.
>
> *(1 mark per command with a one-line justification.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **five-state process model** used by operating systems. Draw a state-transition diagram and describe each state in the context of forensic evidence preservation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Order of Volatility** as defined in **RFC 3227**. Explain why running processes occupy rank 2 and outline the consequences if a forensic investigator fails to capture them before shutting down the system. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Seven Marks Breakdown:**

The **Five-State Process Model** represents the lifecycle of a process from creation to termination. It is essential for forensic investigators because each state determines *what* evidence exists and *how long* it will remain available.

The states are:
1. **New (1 mark):** The process is being created. The OS allocates a PID, sets up the PCB/EPROCESS structure, and loads the executable image. Forensic relevance: creation time and parent process are recorded and persist until the process terminates.
2. **Ready (1 mark):** The process is fully loaded and waiting in the scheduler's ready queue for CPU time. Forensic relevance: the process image, loaded DLLs, and handle table are all resident in memory and can be enumerated.
3. **Running (1 mark):** The process is currently executing on a CPU core. Forensic relevance: registers and cache lines hold the most ephemeral data, often requiring live memory acquisition to recover.
4. **Blocked / Waiting (1 mark):** The process has been suspended because it issued an I/O request or is waiting for an event/signal. Forensic relevance: the process still occupies memory, and its kernel object remains intact, allowing full enumeration.
5. **Terminated (1 mark):** The process has finished or been killed. The OS reclaims its PID and most resources. Forensic relevance: residual structures may linger in kernel pools (process artifacts) for a brief window, recoverable by tools like Volatility's `psscan`.

**State diagram (1 mark):**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New
    New --> Ready : Admitted
    Ready --> Running : Scheduler Dispatch
    Running --> Ready : Preempted
    Blocked --> Ready : I_O Complete
    Running --> Blocked : I_O Request
    Running --> Terminated : Exit
    New --> Terminated : Abort
    Terminated --> [*]
```

**Concluding remark (1 mark):** Investigators prefer to capture memory while processes are in the Ready, Running, or Blocked states because the structures are most complete; the Terminated state is a last-chance recovery window.

---

**(b) — Seven Marks Breakdown:**

**RFC 3227 Order of Volatility (3 marks):**

The IETF's *Guidelines for Evidence Collection and Archiving* (RFC 3227) prescribes the following descending order in which evidence should be collected during a live response:

$$
\text{CPU / Cache} \;\rightarrow\; \boxed{\text{RAM / Running Processes}} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Network State} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Process / System Logs} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Disk Files} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Remote Logs} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Physical Configuration} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Archival Media}
$$

**Why running processes sit at rank 2 (2 marks):**

- **They live in RAM** — a power loss or hard reboot destroys them within seconds.
- **They contain rich, time-sensitive intelligence** — currently logged-in users, active network sockets, loaded malicious modules, and the parent-child spawn chain.
- **They cannot be reconstructed from disk** — once a process exits, its memory contents (encryption keys, decrypted buffers, command history) are gone forever.

**Consequences of failing to capture processes before shutdown (2 marks):**

1. **Loss of volatile malware artifacts** — in-memory-only payloads (fileless malware, reflective DLLs) vanish without a trace.
2. **Loss of encryption keys and decrypted data** — keys held in process memory are destroyed, making later disk decryption impossible.
3. **Loss of network session state** — active TCP/UDP connections and their owning PIDs are no longer recoverable.
4. **Loss of evidentiary value** — the investigator may be unable to prove what was happening *at the moment of incident*, weakening the legal case.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **parent-child process tree** model. How does this model help forensic investigators detect malware infections? Provide a real-world example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Process Hollowing** technique used by advanced malware. Derive a forensic detection method based on **hash comparison** between the on-disk binary and the in-memory image. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Seven Marks Breakdown:**

**Concept (2 marks):**
A process tree is a hierarchical representation of all running processes on a system, rooted at the OS initializer (PID 1 on Linux / `smss.exe` on Windows). Each node is a process; each edge points from a parent to the child it spawned. Children inherit security tokens, environment variables, and a portion of the parent's address space in some operating systems.

**Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    smss["smss.exe PID 4 Root"]
    winlogon["winlogon.exe PID 432 PPID 4"]
    explorer["explorer.exe PID 1820 PPID 432"]
    userapp["legit_app.exe PID 2100 PPID 1820"]
    malware["malware.exe PID 2150 PPID 1820"]
    payload["payload.dll Injected into PID 2100"]

    smss --> winlogon
    winlogon --> explorer
    explorer --> userapp
    explorer --> malware
    userapp -.-> payload
```

**Forensic value (2 marks):**
Investigators use the tree to spot **anomalous spawn patterns**. A user opening a document should not result in `cmd.exe` or `powershell.exe` being a child of `winword.exe`. Such a tree edge is an **Indicator of Compromise (IoC)**.

**Real-world example (1 mark):**
In the 2017 *NotPetya* outbreak, the malware arrived via the legitimate `svchost.exe` or `wmic.exe` parents and spawned `cmd.exe` to execute `psexec` commands. Investigators reconstructed the entire kill chain purely from process tree analysis on compromised endpoints.

---

**(b) — Seven Marks Breakdown:**

**Process Hollowing – Mechanism (3 marks):**

Process Hollowing is a defense-evasion technique where malware performs the following steps:

1. Creates a new legitimate process (e.g., `notepad.exe`) in a **suspended** state using `CreateProcess()` with the `CREATE_SUSPENDED` flag.
2. Uses `NtUnmapViewOfSection()` to **unmap** the legitimate executable from the process's memory.
3. Allocates new memory in the hollowed process via `VirtualAllocEx()` and writes malicious code into it with `WriteProcessMemory()`.
4. Resumes the thread using `ResumeThread()`. The process now appears as `notepad.exe` in the task list, but is actually running attacker code.

**Why it defeats naïve forensics (1 mark):**
The on-disk file at `C:\Windows\System32\notepad.exe` remains untouched, and antivirus tools that scan only the filesystem will see only the legitimate binary. The malicious code lives *only* in memory.

**Forensic Detection via Hash Comparison (3 marks):**

Let:
- $F$ = the on-disk executable file at the image path reported by the process.
- $M$ = the in-memory executable section extracted from the process's address space (using Volatility's `malfind` or `dlllist`).
- $H(x)$ = a cryptographic hash function (SHA-256).

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{disk}} & = \text{SHA-256}(F) \\
H_{\text{memory}} & = \text{SHA-256}(M) \\
\text{If } H_{\text{disk}} & \neq H_{\text{memory}} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Process has been tampered with (hollowed).} \\
\text{If } H_{\text{disk}} & = H_{\text{memory}} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Process is consistent (no obvious hollowing).}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Practical tooling:**
- **Volatility's `malfind` plugin** identifies injected memory regions with RWX (read-write-execute) permissions — a hallmark of hollowed code.
- **Process Hacker / Sysinternals Process Explorer** can show the on-disk path and in-memory MD5, allowing quick visual comparison.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall / Valuation Warning:**
> - **Do not** confuse Process Hollowing with **Process Doppelgänging** (which abuses the TxF transaction mechanism) or simple **DLL Injection** (which loads a malicious DLL into a legitimate process without unmapping the original). Examiners deduct marks if the student uses these terms interchangeably.
> - **Do not** state that hash mismatch *definitively* proves malware — it is a *strong indicator* that requires corroborating evidence (digital signature failure, unsigned parent, anomalous spawn tree, unusual network activity).
> - **Always** specify the hash algorithm (SHA-256 preferred over MD5 in modern KTU papers).
> - Forgetting to mention **chain of custody** when presenting the hash mismatch in a court-of-law scenario will cost 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 3 — Running Processes**

- **Definition:** A running process is an active program in RAM, identified by a unique **PID** and traceable via its **PPID**.
- **Volatility Rank:** Running processes sit at **rank 2** in the RFC 3227 order of volatility, immediately below CPU registers/cache.
- **Five States:** New → Ready → Running → Blocked → Terminated. Each state has different forensic recoverability.
- **Parent-Child Tree:** The process spawn tree (rooted at `smss.exe` / PID 1) is the most powerful visual tool for spotting abnormal execution chains.
- **Live vs Post-Mortem:** Live forensics captures running processes before shutdown; post-mortem analysis reconstructs them from disk artifacts (prefetch, registry UserAssist, shimcache, amcache).
- **Key Windows Commands:** `tasklist`, `tasklist /v`, `Get-Process` (PowerShell), `pslist -t`, `pslist -x` (Sysinternals).
- **Key Linux Commands:** `ps -ef`, `ps auxf`, `top`, `htop`, `/proc/<pid>/` filesystem.
- **Memory Forensics Tools:** **Volatility** (pslist, pstree, psscan, malfind, cmdline, dlllist), **Rekall**, **Magnet RAM Capture**, **WinPmem**, **FTK Imager**.
- **Hash Comparison Test:** Always compare $H_{\text{disk}}$ vs. $H_{\text{memory}}$ to detect Process Hollowing, DLL Injection, and reflective loading.
- **Suspicious Indicators:** Unsigned parent → signed child, `cmd.exe` / `powershell.exe` as children of Office applications, processes running from `%TEMP%` or `C:\Users\Public\`, PID reuse anomalies.
- **Chain of Custody:** Every volatile artifact must be hashed, timestamped, and logged before the system is shut down.
- **Rootkit Countermeasure:** Trust the *kernel structures* (EPROCESS linked list, pool tags) over the *OS API* — this is why memory forensics beats live enumeration for hidden processes.
