- **Common Exam Traps:** Mixing up PID and PPID, claiming hashes always prove malware, forgetting to mention RFC 3227, calling `cmd.exe` itself malicious without context.
- **Remember:** *A process is not a program.* A program on disk is a passive file; a process in memory is a living, executing entity with its own resources and lifespan.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Open File Handlers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT602 - CYBER FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Open File Handlers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In digital and memory forensics, an **Open File Handler** (commonly called a **File Handle**) is a transient, OS-allocated abstract reference value (typically a 32-bit or 64-bit unsigned integer on Windows, or a small integer file descriptor on Unix/Linux systems) that uniquely identifies an *open* instance of a file, directory, socket, pipe, registry key, or any other kernel-managed object currently being accessed by a running process. The handle itself is not the file — it is a token, index, or ticket that allows the owning process to perform I/O operations on the underlying object through the kernel.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 – Highlight Definition**
> *"An Open File Handler is a per-process reference value returned by the operating system kernel when a process successfully opens a file (or any other object). The forensic significance lies in the fact that the handler remains valid only while the file is open — making it a live, volatile indicator of process activity, file usage, and potential evidence of malicious behaviour."*

The internal data structure that stores all open file handlers for a process is called the **Handle Table** (`_HANDLE_TABLE` in the Windows kernel), and the per-entry structure is `_HANDLE_TABLE_ENTRY`. Forensic tools such as **Sysinternals Handle.exe**, **Process Explorer**, **Process Monitor**, and the **Volatility `handles` plugin** enumerate this table to reconstruct what a suspect process was doing at the moment of acquisition.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hotel Room Key" Model

Imagine you walk into a luxury hotel:

- The **hotel building** = the entire filesystem (rooms = files).
- The **reception desk** = the operating system kernel.
- Your **room key card** = the file handle. It does not contain the room; it just *unlocks* it.
- The **key card database at reception** = the kernel's handle table (`_HANDLE_TABLE`).
- The **guest's name on the booking** = the owning process (`_EPROCESS`).
- When you check out, the key is **invalidated** (handle closed); when the power goes out (system crash), the cards stop working (volatile evidence).

If a forensic investigator arrives at the hotel and reads the reception's *currently active key card database*, they can tell **who** (which process) currently has **which room** (which file) open — even if the guest is asleep. This is exactly what enumerating open file handlers reveals: live, in-progress file access by processes at memory-acquisition time.

## 1.3 Why Open File Handlers Matter in Cyber Forensics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Forensic Truths About File Handlers**
> 1. **Live Evidence of Intent** – A file that is *currently open* by a process is direct proof the process was actively interacting with it. This is far stronger than mere filesystem timestamps.
> 2. **Volatility** – Handles exist only in RAM. They vanish on reboot, crash, or process termination. A memory image (`.raw`, `.mem`, `.dmp`) is the *only* place to recover them.
> 3. **Process-to-Object Mapping** – Handles tie a *process identity* to a *resource identity* (file path, registry key, network socket), allowing reconstruction of behavioural timelines.

Typical investigative uses include:

- Detecting a process that has a **locked log file, encryption key file, or ransom note target** open.
- Discovering **memory-mapped executable files** (`.exe` / `.dll` handles pointing to disk) — a sign of process injection or fileless malware.
- Identifying **deleted-but-still-open files** — files unlinked from the directory but still resident because a process holds a handle (the classic anti-forensics technique).
- Mapping **network sockets** (a socket is just a special handle in Windows).

## 1.4 Platform-Specific Terminology

| Platform | Term | Format | Stored In |
|---|---|---|---|
| Windows (NT Kernel) | **Handle** (`HANDLE`) | 32-bit unsigned integer (index) | `_HANDLE_TABLE` → `_HANDLE_TABLE_ENTRY` per process |
| Linux / Unix | **File Descriptor (fd)** | Small non-negative integer | `struct files_struct → fdtable → struct file *` per process |
| macOS (XNU) | **File Descriptor** | Integer, BSD-style | Per-process `fd_table` |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU Module 3, the Windows kernel internals are the *primary* focus because Windows is the dominant target platform in most cyber-forensics casework and is the platform for which the deepest forensic tooling (Volatility) exists.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Windows Handle Architecture (Conceptual Walk-Through)

When a user-mode process calls `CreateFile("C:\\Users\\Victim\\secret.docx", ...)`, the following chain of events occurs inside the Windows NT kernel:

1. The **I/O Manager** receives the system call via the `NtCreateFile` syscall stub in `ntdll.dll`.
2. The **Object Manager** is invoked to create or open a kernel object of type `File`.
3. A new entry is inserted into the calling process's handle table.
4. A **handle value** (e.g., `0x000000C4`) is returned to user mode.
5. The process now uses this handle for subsequent `ReadFile` / `WriteFile` calls.

For the forensic analyst, the critical structures are:

### 2.1.1 `_EPROCESS` (Executive Process Object)

Every process in Windows is described by a kernel-resident structure called `_EPROCESS`. For handle forensics, the relevant fields are:

| Offset (Symbolic) | Field | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `+0x0f4` (Win 7) | `UniqueProcessId` | PID |
| `+0x140` (Win 7) | `ActiveProcessLinks` | Doubly-linked list of all processes |
| `+0x0e0` / `+0x418` (varies) | `Pcb` / `Process` | Sub-structures |
| **+0x0c8** (x86, Win 7) | **`ObjectTable`** | **Pointer to the `_HANDLE_TABLE` for this process** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The `ObjectTable` field is the **single most important pointer** for handle forensics. If you can walk it, you can enumerate every object the process has open.

### 2.1.2 `_HANDLE_TABLE`

The handle table is the central index structure. Its key fields:

| Field | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `TableCode` | Encodes the table level (lowest 2 bits) **and** a pointer to the actual table data |
| `QuotaProcess` | Pointer to the `_EPROCESS` that owns the table |
| `UniqueProcessId` | PID of the owning process |
| `HandleTableList` | Links the table into the kernel's global list |
| `HandleCount` | Total number of valid handles |
| `HandleTableLock` | Guard for SMP safety |

### 2.1.3 `_HANDLE_TABLE_ENTRY`

Each individual handle occupies one entry. Modern forensic analysis must interpret the **tag bits** in the `ObjectPointerBits` field:

| Field | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `ObjectPointerBits` | Pointer to the kernel **OBJECT_HEADER** (low 3 bits stripped/used as attributes) |
| `GrantedAccess` | 32-bit access mask (e.g., `0x0012019F` = `FILE_READ_DATA | FILE_WRITE_DATA | ...`) |
| `UsualFlags` | Low 3 bits encode `OBJ_INHERIT`, `OBJ_AUDIT`, `OBJ_PROTECT` etc. |
| `ObjectTypeIndex` | Index into the global `ObTypeIndexTable` (e.g., index `0x03` = *File*) |
| `HandleAttributes` | Bit `0x02` = `HANDLE_PROTECT_FROM_CLOSE` (anti-forensics flag!) |

## 2.2 The Three-Level Handle Table Layout

The handle value is **not** a flat array index. The Windows kernel supports a *three-level* table for scalability:

| Table Level (bits 0–1 of TableCode) | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `0` | **Low-level** table only (single page, 512 entries on x86, 1024 on x64) |
| `1` | Mid-level table (up to 512 mid-level tables × 512 entries) |
| `2` | High-level table (up to 512 high-level tables × 512^3 entries — effectively unlimited) |

A handle value is decomposed as:

$$
H = \underbrace{h_{top}}_{\text{High-level index}} \,\|\, \underbrace{h_{mid}}_{\text{Mid-level index}} \,\|\, \underbrace{h_{low}}_{\text{Low-level index}} \,\|\, \underbrace{t}_{\text{2-bit table-level tag}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, most KTU exam questions will use the **single-level (level 0)** case because it has at most **512 entries** per process (sufficient for the question stem). Always extract the table level from `TableCode & 0x3` first.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> The following markdown table contains the complete, exam-ready formulas and decoding rules for Open File Handlers. *Pipes are written using `\vert` and `\mid` to preserve the markdown table.*

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Decode table level from `TableCode` | $\text{level} = \text{TableCode} \;\&\; \text{0x3}$ | `0`=L0, `1`=L1, `2`=L2 |
| 2 | Strip table-level tag from `TableCode` | $\text{table\_ptr} = \text{TableCode} \; \&\; \text{~0x3}$ | Pointer is page-aligned |
| 3 | Low-level table entry count (x86) | $N_{L0} = 512$ | One page / 8-byte entry |
| 4 | Low-level table entry count (x64) | $N_{L0} = 1024$ | One page / 8-byte entry |
| 5 | Multi-level max handles (L0) | $H_{\max} = 512$ (x86), $1024$ (x64) | Most user processes |
| 6 | Multi-level max handles (L1) | $H_{\max} = 512^2$ (x86), $1024^2$ (x64) | Heavy-use processes |
| 7 | Entry size | $S_{entry} = 8$ bytes (x86) or $16$ bytes (x64) | Per `_HANDLE_TABLE_ENTRY` |
| 8 | Strip tag bits from object pointer | $\text{object} = \text{ObjectPointerBits} \; \&\; \text{~0x7}$ | Low 3 bits = `Audit`/`Inherit`/`Protect` |
| 9 | Decode `ObjectTypeIndex` (Win 7 typical) | $\text{file} = 0x03$ | Compare with `ObTypeIndexTable` |
| 10 | Anti-forensics flag detection | $\text{HandleAttributes} \;\&\; \text{0x02} \neq 0$ | `HANDLE_PROTECT_FROM_CLOSE` |
| 11 | Linux fd → file pointer | $\text{file} = \text{fdt} \rightarrow \text{fd}[\text{fd\_num}]$ | `struct file *` lookup |
| 12 | Volatility plugin | `vol.py --profile=Win7SP1x64 -f mem.raw handles -p <PID>` | Lists all handles of PID |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Forensics Utility

Open-file-handler analysis is a foundational primitive that feeds into:

- **Live incident response** — triaging a compromised host by listing which processes have sensitive files open.
- **Memory forensics (post-mortem)** — reconstructing malware behaviour from a `.raw` memory dump when the disk image is unavailable or encrypted.
- **Ransomware investigation** — locating the process that holds the encryption-key file or ransom-note target open.
- **Anti-forensics detection** — flagging handles with `HANDLE_PROTECT_FROM_CLOSE` (rare in legitimate code, common in malware trying to prevent forensic shutdown).
- **Fileless malware analysis** — every injected DLL or hollowed executable still has a `File` handle pointing to the on-disk binary, providing a critical IOCs (Indicators of Compromise).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Resolving a Handle Value to a Kernel Object

> **Problem Stem (KTU-style):** A forensic analyst dumps the `_EPROCESS` of `notepad.exe` (PID `0x042C`) from a Windows 7 x86 memory image. The `ObjectTable` field is `0x8A123000`. The `TableCode` field read from that handle table is `0x9F456000`. The analyst selects handle value `0x00000040`. Determine the table level, the entry index, and the byte offset of the corresponding `_HANDLE_TABLE_ENTRY`.

### Step 1 — Extract the table level from `TableCode`

$$
\text{level} = \text{TableCode} \;\&\; \text{0x3}
$$

$$
\text{level} = \text{0x9F456000} \;\&\; \text{0x3} = \text{0x0}
$$

**Logic:** The lowest two bits of `0x9F456000` are both `0`, so the value is in a *low-level (Level 0)* table. This means the table is a single page holding up to 512 handle entries on x86.

### Step 2 — Strip the table-level tag bits to obtain the base pointer

$$
\text{table\_ptr} = \text{TableCode} \;\&\; \text{~0x3}
$$

$$
\text{table\_ptr} = \text{0x9F456000} \;\&\; \text{0xFFFFFFFC} = \text{0x9F456000}
$$

**Logic:** The base pointer is page-aligned (multiple of 4). Because level is 0, the low 2 bits were already zero, so the pointer is unchanged.

### Step 3 — Compute the entry index from the handle value

For a Level-0 table on x86, the handle value is simply a flat index into the 512-entry array, but the lowest 2 bits encode the *expected* level (used for validation). Strip them to obtain the true index:

$$
\text{index} = \text{handle} \;\gg\; 2
$$

$$
\text{index} = \text{0x00000040} \;\gg\; 2 = \text{0x10} \;(\text{decimal } 16)
$$

**Logic:** Dividing the 32-bit handle by 4 yields the slot number. The two low bits of the handle are reserved for level validation by the kernel.

### Step 4 — Compute the byte offset of the entry

Each `_HANDLE_TABLE_ENTRY` is **8 bytes** on x86:

$$
\text{offset} = \text{index} \times \text{sizeof}(\text{\_HANDLE\_TABLE\_ENTRY})
$$

$$
\text{offset} = 16 \times 8 = 128 \text{ bytes} = \text{0x80}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the absolute address of the entry

$$
\text{entry\_addr} = \text{table\_ptr} + \text{offset}
$$

$$
\text{entry\_addr} = \text{0x9F456000} + \text{0x80} = \text{0x9F456080}
$$

**Logic:** The forensic analyst can now read 8 bytes at virtual address `0x9F456080` from the memory image to obtain the `_HANDLE_TABLE_ENTRY`. The `ObjectPointerBits` (lower 32 bits on x86) and `GrantedAccess` will be inside these 8 bytes.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Detecting `HANDLE_PROTECT_FROM_CLOSE`

> **Problem Stem (KTU-style):** An analyst reads the `_HANDLE_TABLE_ENTRY` at the address found in Derivation 1. The 8 bytes (little-endian) at `0x9F456080` are: `C4 1E 92 82 9F 01 00 00`. The lower 32 bits (`ObjectPointerBits + UsualFlags`) are `0x82921EC4`, and the upper 32 bits (`ObjectTypeIndex` and `GrantedAccess`) are `0x0000019F`. Determine (a) the actual `OBJECT_HEADER` address, and (b) whether this handle has anti-forensics protection enabled.

### Step 1 — Extract the lower 32 bits and separate the object pointer

The lower 32 bits combine the object pointer (top 29 bits) with the **3 low-order tag bits** (UsualFlags). The object pointer is obtained by clearing the low 3 bits:

$$
\text{object\_header} = \text{ObjectPointerBits} \;\&\; \text{~0x7}
$$

$$
\text{object\_header} = \text{0x82921EC4} \;\&\; \text{0xFFFFFFF8} = \text{0x82921EC0}
$$

**Logic:** The three stripped low bits `0x4` (`100₂`) tell us which UsualFlags are set. `0x4` is **OBJ_AUDIT** (the second of the 3 tag bits). This is a normal audit-flag, *not* a close-protection flag.

### Step 2 — Interpret the upper 32 bits

The upper 32 bits on x86 split as:

- **Lower 16 bits** = `ObjectTypeIndex` (0x019F low half) — actually on x86 the layout is `ObjectTypeIndex (8 bits) | HandleAttributes (8 bits) | GrantedAccess (16 bits)`.
- For this example, the 16-bit `GrantedAccess` value is the low 16 bits of `0x0000019F`, which is `0x019F` = `0000 0001 1001 1111₂` in binary.

> [!NOTE]
> The exact layout of the upper 32 bits depends on the Windows build. KTU questions usually simplify by stating the `GrantedAccess` directly. The standard value `0x001F` corresponds to `FILE_READ_DATA | FILE_WRITE_DATA | ...` — a *read/write* file handle.

### Step 3 — Detect `HANDLE_PROTECT_FROM_CLOSE`

$$
\text{protect} = \text{HandleAttributes} \;\&\; \text{0x02}
$$

**Logic:** The `HandleAttributes` byte (bits 8–15 of the upper 32) is `0x00` in this example, so `protect = 0`. **No anti-forensics flag is set.** The handle *can* be closed normally, so no malware trickery is indicated *for this particular entry*.

### Step 4 — Convert the object header pointer to a `File` object and finally to a path

The investigator walks the `OBJECT_HEADER` at `0x82921EC0`, then follows `TypeIndex == 0x03` (a `File` object), then the `ObjectName` field (a `UNICODE_STRING`). The Volatility plugin automates this entire chain, but the manual chain is:

$$
\text{Entry} \rightarrow \text{OBJECT\_HEADER} \rightarrow \text{ObjectType} \rightarrow \text{ObjectName} \rightarrow \text{DevicePath}
$$

$$
\text{DevicePath} \rightarrow \text{DriveLetter} \rightarrow \text{User-visible path (e.g., C:\textbackslash Users\textbackslash ...)}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of File-Handle Forensic Sources

| Forensic Source | Data Recovered | Persistence | Tooling |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Live `Handle.exe` / Process Explorer** | Real-time handles of running processes | Volatile — dies with process | Sysinternals, built-in to Windows |
| **Memory dump (`handles` plugin)** | Snapshot of all handles of all processes at acquisition | Captured volatile state | Volatility, Rekall |
| **Windows Registry (`Amcache`, `RecentFileCache`)** | Historical file-execution evidence | Persistent (on disk) | RegistryExplorer, AmcacheParser |
| **MFT / `$MFT`** | File creation / modification metadata | Persistent | Sleuth Kit, Autopsy |
| **LNK files / Jump lists** | Recently opened files by user | Persistent | LECmd, JLECmd |
| **Prefetch (`%WINDIR%\Prefetch`)** | Recently executed programs and their files | Persistent (last 1024) | PECmd |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Open file handlers are the *only* evidence source that proves a file was open *right now* in memory.** Every other source above is either a *historical* or *on-disk* record. This uniqueness makes handle forensics irreplaceable in live-incident response.

---

## 3.4 Linux / Unix Side-Derivation (Brief)

On Linux, the equivalent of a Windows handle is the **file descriptor (fd)**. The kernel structure is `struct files_struct`, which contains `struct fdtable fdtab`, an array of `struct file *` pointers. A user process has three standard fds always open:

| fd | Symbolic Constant | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `0` | `STDIN_FILENO` | Standard input |
| `1` | `STDOUT_FILENO` | Standard output |
| `2` | `STDERR_FILENO` | Standard error |

The `lsof` command (List Open Files) and the Volatility plugin `linux_lsof` perform handle/FD enumeration. The conceptual formula is:

$$
\text{file} = \text{task} \rightarrow \text{files} \rightarrow \text{fdt} \rightarrow \text{fd}[\text{fd\_num}]
$$

**Logic:** Starting from the process descriptor (`task_struct`), follow the `files` pointer to the per-process file table, then index into the `fd` array by the small integer fd number to obtain the `struct file *`. This `struct file` contains the `f_path` (dentry + mount) which resolves to the on-disk path.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Architecture — From Process to File

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[EPROCESS for PID 0x042C] --> B[ObjectTable pointer]
    B --> C[HANDLE_TABLE struct]
    C --> D[TableCode field]
    D --> E{Level 0/1/2}
    E -->|0| F0[Low-level Table 512 entries x86]
    E -->|1| F1[Mid-level Table 512 sub-tables]
    E -->|2| F2[High-level Table 512 mid-tables]
    F0 --> G[HANDLE_TABLE_ENTRY slot N]
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[ObjectPointerBits strip low 3 bits]
    H --> I[OBJECT_HEADER]
    I --> J[TypeIndex 0x03 equals File]
    I --> K[ObjectName UNICODE_STRING]
    K --> L[Device path Device HarddiskVolume2 ...]
    L --> M[User path C colon backslash Users backslash ...]
    G --> N[GrantedAccess access mask]
    G --> O[HandleAttributes anti-forensics check]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram follows the **Alpha-Rule** for node IDs: every ID is purely alphanumeric (`A`, `B`, … `O`) and never uses reserved keywords. Labels are kept in plain uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting to ensure clean Mermaid rendering.

## 4.2 Handle Table Memory Layout (Level 0, x86)

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 12
    block:table["HANDLE_TABLE Page (4 KB)"]:12
        columns 8
        block:ent0["Entry 0"]:1
        block:ent1["Entry 1"]:1
        block:ent2["Entry 2"]:1
        block:ent3["Entry 3"]:1
        block:entN["..."]:1
        block:ent16["Entry 16"]:1
        block:ent17["Entry 17"]:1
        block:ent511["Entry 511"]:1
    space:2
    block:fields["Each 8-byte HANDLE_TABLE_ENTRY contains:"]:12
        columns 4
        block:f1["ObjectPointerBits 4 bytes"]:3
        block:f2["ObjectTypeIndex 1 byte"]:1
        block:f3["HandleAttributes 1 byte"]:1
        block:f4["GrantedAccess 2 bytes"]:1
        block:f5["UsualFlags in low 3 bits of ObjectPointer"]:3
```

## 4.3 Forensic Workflow — Open File Handlers in an Investigation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Acquire memory image .raw .lime .dmp] --> P1[Identify profile Win7SP1x64]
    P1 --> P2[Run Volatility pslist to find PIDs]
    P2 --> P3[Run handles plugin for target PID]
    P3 --> P4{Object type is File?}
    P4 -->|Yes| P5[Record file path and GrantedAccess]
    P4 -->|No| P6[Skip Key Event Mutant etc]
    P5 --> P7{HandleAttributes has 0x02?}
    P7 -->|Yes| P8[Flag as anti-forensics suspicious]
    P7 -->|No| P9[Standard open file evidence]
    P8 --> P10[Cross-correlate with MFT and Prefetch]
    P9 --> P10
    P10 --> P11[Build timeline of file access]
    P11 --> P12[Report IOC indicators]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram — Windows Handles vs. Linux File Descriptors

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Windows[Windows NT Architecture]
        W1[EPROCESS] --> W2[ObjectTable]
        W2 --> W3[HANDLE_TABLE]
        W3 --> W4[HANDLE_TABLE_ENTRY x N]
        W4 --> W5[OBJECT_HEADER]
        W5 --> W6[File / Key / Section ...]
    end
    subgraph Linux[Linux Kernel Architecture]
        L1[task_struct] --> L2[files_struct]
        L2 --> L3[fdtable]
        L3 --> L4[struct file pointer array]
        L4 --> L5[struct file]
        L5 --> L6[dentry + vfsmount]
        L6 --> L7[path on filesystem]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — Conceptual Definition `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]`

> **Q1.** Define an *Open File Handler* in the context of Windows memory forensics. Why is the analysis of open file handlers considered a *volatile* forensic activity, and what is the practical consequence for an investigator?

**Model Answer (≈3 marks):**

An **Open File Handler** is a 32-bit (or 64-bit) reference value returned by the Windows kernel to a user-mode process when it successfully opens a file (or any other object such as a registry key, socket, or section). Internally, the handle is an *index* into a per-process structure called the `_HANDLE_TABLE`, with each slot containing an `_HANDLE_TABLE_ENTRY` that points to a kernel object (e.g., a `File` object of type index `0x03`).

The handle exists *only* in volatile memory (RAM). It is created by the kernel when the file is opened and destroyed when the file is closed or the process exits. As soon as the system is powered off, the process terminates, or the file is closed, the handle vanishes with no on-disk trace. Consequently, the *only* way to recover open file handlers for forensic purposes is to acquire a **memory image** (e.g., using `winpmem`, `FTK Imager`, or `LiME`) *before* the system is shut down. Failing to capture memory in time permanently destroys this evidence.

---

### Question 2 — Tool & Identification `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]`

> **Q2.** List any **three** forensic tools (live or post-mortem) that can be used to enumerate open file handlers on a Windows system. For each tool, state whether it works on a *live* system or on a *memory image*.

**Model Answer (≈3 marks):**

| # | Tool | Mode | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sysinternals Handle.exe** (by Mark Russinovich) | Live | Command-line; lists handles of all processes. Equivalent GUI: **Process Explorer**. |
| 2 | **Process Monitor (Procmon)** | Live | Real-time filter on `CreateFile` / `ReadFile` / `WriteFile`; logs file-handle operations. |
| 3 | **Volatility `handles` plugin** | Post-mortem (memory image) | Works on a `.raw` / `.mem` / `.lime` dump; e.g., `vol.py -f mem.raw --profile=Win7SP1x64 handles -p 1060`. |
| 4 (alt.) | **Rekall `handles` plugin** | Post-mortem | Open-source alternative to Volatility. |

**Examiner's Note (1 mark for accurate categorization):** Award full credit only if the student clearly distinguishes *live* tools (work on running OS) from *post-mortem* tools (work on captured memory image). Marks are commonly lost for vague answers like "Volatility runs on Windows" without specifying the image-based mode.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Windows Handle Internals `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 3]`

> **Q A.** (a) With a neat block diagram, describe the **Windows internal handle architecture** for an open file. Your answer must include the role of `_EPROCESS`, `_HANDLE_TABLE`, `_HANDLE_TABLE_ENTRY`, and `OBJECT_HEADER`. Clearly explain how the `TableCode` field encodes the table level, and how the `ObjectPointerBits` field encodes the object address along with `UsualFlags`. **\[7 Marks\]**
>
> (b) An investigator extracts a memory image and finds that the `TableCode` field of process `svchost.exe` (PID `0x01A4`) on a Windows 7 x86 system is `0x8C12E004`. The investigator is interested in handle value `0x0000002C`. Compute:
>   (i) the table level,
>   (ii) the actual base pointer to the handle table data,
>   (iii) the index of the `_HANDLE_TABLE_ENTRY` for the given handle,
>   (iv) the byte offset of the entry from the start of the low-level table, and
>   (v) the absolute virtual address of the entry. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) Architectural Description — 7 Marks

**1. `_EPROCESS` (1 mark):** The kernel-resident executive process object. It contains a field `ObjectTable` (offset varies by build, e.g., `+0x0c8` on Win 7 x86) that points to the process's `_HANDLE_TABLE`. This is the entry point for handle forensics.

**2. `_HANDLE_TABLE` (2 marks):** A per-process table that stores all open handles. The crucial field is `TableCode`. The lowest **2 bits** of `TableCode` encode the **table level** (`0`=single-level, `1`=mid-level, `2`=high-level). The remaining upper bits encode the **page-aligned base pointer** to the table data. Other fields: `QuotaProcess`, `HandleTableList`, `UniqueProcessId`, `HandleCount`, `HandleTableLock`.

**3. `_HANDLE_TABLE_ENTRY` (2 marks):** Each 8-byte (x86) entry represents one handle. It contains:
- `ObjectPointerBits` (lower 32 bits on x86) — pointer to the `OBJECT_HEADER` with the **low 3 bits** repurposed as `UsualFlags` (`OBJ_INHERIT`, `OBJ_AUDIT`, `OBJ_PROTECT`).
- `GrantedAccess` — 32-bit access mask.
- `ObjectTypeIndex` (8 bits) — index into `ObTypeIndexTable`. For files, this is `0x03` on Windows 7.
- `HandleAttributes` (8 bits) — bit `0x02` = `HANDLE_PROTECT_FROM_CLOSE` (anti-forensics indicator).

**4. `OBJECT_HEADER` (1 mark):** A structure placed *before* every kernel object. It contains metadata such as the `Type` pointer, the `Name` (as `UNICODE_STRING`), and the `HandleInfoList`. Following the chain `_HANDLE_TABLE_ENTRY → OBJECT_HEADER → Type/Name` resolves the open handle to a *user-visible path* (e.g., `C:\Users\Victim\secret.docx`).

**5. Diagram (1 mark):** Include the chain `EPROCESS → ObjectTable → HANDLE_TABLE → HANDLE_TABLE_ENTRY → OBJECT_HEADER → ObjectName`. The Mermaid figure in Section 4.1 of these notes is acceptable as reference.

#### (b) Numerical Computation — 7 Marks

**Given:** `TableCode = 0x8C12E004`, handle `= 0x0000002C`, architecture = Windows 7 x86.

**(i) Table Level (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{level} = \text{TableCode} \;\&\; \text{0x3} = \text{0x8C12E004} \;\&\; \text{0x3} = \text{0x0}
$$

**Level = 0** (single-level, low-level table only — up to 512 entries on x86).

**(ii) Base Pointer (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{table\_ptr} = \text{TableCode} \;\&\; \text{~0x3} = \text{0x8C12E004} \;\&\; \text{0xFFFFFFFC} = \text{0x8C12E004}
$$

(Stripping the low 2 bits leaves the pointer unchanged because they were already 0.)

**(iii) Entry Index (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{index} = \text{handle} \;\gg\; 2 = \text{0x0000002C} \;\gg\; 2 = \text{0x0000000B} \;(\text{decimal } 11)
$$

**(iv) Byte Offset (1.5 marks):**

$$
\text{offset} = \text{index} \times 8 = 11 \times 8 = 88 \text{ bytes} = \text{0x58}
$$

**(v) Absolute Address (1 mark):**

$$
\text{entry\_addr} = \text{table\_ptr} + \text{offset} = \text{0x8C12E004} + \text{0x58} = \text{0x8C12E05C}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part B — Question A)**
> 1. **Forgetting to mask `TableCode` with `0x3`** — students often use the *full* `TableCode` value as the pointer, producing a misaligned (non-page-aligned) read and a kernel crash on the next dereference. Always mask first.
> 2. **Using `& 0xF` instead of `& 0x3`** — the table-level tag is only 2 bits, not 4. A 4-bit mask would wrongly classify every Level-0 table as Level-0 and corrupt the level determination.
> 3. **Forgetting to right-shift the handle by 2 bits** — the handle value itself is *not* the array index; the index is `handle >> 2`.
> 4. **Wrong entry size** — must be 8 bytes on x86, not 4 or 16. A 4-byte entry would give an offset of 44 instead of 88, reading a different (wrong) entry.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Anti-Forensics & Live Triage `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 3]`

> **Q B.** (a) Explain the concept of `HANDLE_PROTECT_FROM_CLOSE` and why it is considered a *forensic anti-analysis* technique. Describe how an investigator can detect this flag using the `_HANDLE_TABLE_ENTRY` structure. Mention the typical malware family in which this technique is observed. **\[7 Marks\]**
>
> (b) During live triage of a suspected compromised Windows 10 host, the analyst uses **Process Explorer** to list the open handles of `lsass.exe`. The output shows 38 file handles, 4 section handles, 12 key handles, and 7 mutant handles. Among the file handles, three paths are noteworthy:
>   (i) `C:\Windows\Temp\lsass.dmp`,
>   (ii) `C:\Users\Public\keylog.txt`,
>   (iii) `C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts`.
> For each path, briefly explain (1) the **forensic significance** and (2) the **likely attack technique** the presence of such an open handle suggests. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

#### (a) `HANDLE_PROTECT_FROM_CLOSE` — 7 Marks

**Definition (2 marks):** `HANDLE_PROTECT_FROM_CLOSE` is a flag (bit `0x02`) inside the `HandleAttributes` byte of an `_HANDLE_TABLE_ENTRY`. When set, the kernel **refuses to close the handle** even if user-mode code (or another tool) calls `CloseHandle` on it. The only way to close such a handle is from kernel mode with the necessary privileges, or by terminating the owning process.

**Why Anti-Forensic (2 marks):** Malware sets this flag to prevent:
- Antivirus or EDR products from closing the file handle and quarantining the file.
- Forensic tools from releasing a lock on a file before copying it (e.g., Active Directory `ntds.dit`, browser cookie databases, locked log files).
- Investigators from interfering with a malicious file the malware is actively using (e.g., the encryption key file during ransomware execution).

**Detection (2 marks):** Read the `_HANDLE_TABLE_ENTRY` from the memory image. Extract the `HandleAttributes` byte (typically bits 8–15 of the upper 32 bits of the 8-byte entry on x86). Test with a bitwise AND:

$$
\text{protected} = \text{HandleAttributes} \;\&\; \text{0x02} \;\neq\; 0
$$

In Volatility, this is exposed in the `handles` plugin output under the `HandleFlags` or `GrantedAccess` columns depending on plugin version. In live tools (Process Explorer → lower pane → handle properties), the `Protect from Close` attribute is shown directly.

**Malware Family (1 mark):** **Mimikatz** (specifically the `sekurlsa::minidump` and `lsadump` modules) is the canonical example. It opens `lsass.exe` with `HANDLE_PROTECT_FROM_CLOSE` set so that security products cannot release the handle and block credential dumping. The technique is also seen in **banking trojans**, **ransomware** (to lock the encryption-key file), and various **rootkits**.

#### (b) Live Triage — Three Suspicious Open File Handles — 7 Marks

**(i) `C:\Windows\Temp\lsass.dmp` (2.5 marks):**
- **Forensic significance:** `lsass.dmp` is a process-memory snapshot of the Local Security Authority Subsystem Service. It contains **hashed and sometimes clear-text credentials** for all users that have logged in. A process (other than the OS debugger) holding this file open is extremely suspicious.
- **Likely technique:** **Credential dumping**, most likely by **Mimikatz** or **ProcDump** invoked with admin privileges. The attacker has read the memory of `lsass.exe` to a file and is now in the process of exfiltrating or parsing it.

**(ii) `C:\Users\Public\keylog.txt` (2.5 marks):**
- **Forensic significance:** A *plain-text* file named `keylog.txt` inside the world-writable `Public` folder is a strong indicator of **keylogger activity**. The presence of an open write handle means the process is *actively appending* keystrokes to the file in real time.
- **Likely technique:** **Keylogging** — either a user-mode keylogger hooking the keyboard input pipeline, or a kernel-level filter driver. The fact that the file is in `C:\Users\Public` is consistent with the attacker choosing the most accessible, least-suspicious location.

**(iii) `C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts` (2 marks):**
- **Forensic significance:** The `hosts` file is a classic **persistence / redirection** target. Malware opens it with a write handle to insert entries that redirect domain name resolution (e.g., banking sites → attacker-controlled IPs).
- **Likely technique:** **DNS poisoning / hosts-file hijack**, a long-standing malware technique used by **trojans** (e.g., banking trojans, adware) and **red-team toolkits**. The process holding this handle is the one actively modifying system name resolution.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part B — Question B)**
> 1. **Mentioning only "suspicious" without naming the technique** — partial marks only. Always pair each suspicious path with a *named attack technique* (credential dumping, keylogging, DNS poisoning).
> 2. **Conflating "open file" with "file exists on disk"** — the *open handle* is the volatile evidence that the process is *currently* reading or writing the file. The forensic value is the *actuality*, not merely existence.
> 3. **Forgetting the anti-forensics component in part (a)** — examiners explicitly test for the link between `HANDLE_PROTECT_FROM_CLOSE` and **Mimikatz**-style defence evasion. A generic answer ("malware sometimes does this") loses at least 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Open File Handlers**

- A **file handle** is a per-process *index/reference* to an open kernel object (file, key, section, socket, mutant). It is **not** the file itself.
- The handle is created when the file is opened and destroyed when closed. It is **purely volatile** — recovered only from a **memory image**.
- In **Windows**, handles live in the `_HANDLE_TABLE` of the `_EPROCESS`, indexed by the 32-bit handle value.
- The `_HANDLE_TABLE_ENTRY` is **8 bytes on x86** and **16 bytes on x64**. The **low 3 bits** of the object pointer are repurposed as `UsualFlags`.
- The `TableCode` field encodes both the **table level** (lowest 2 bits) and the **base pointer** to the table data. Always mask with `& 0x3` to extract the level.
- The handle value's **low 2 bits** are reserved for level validation; the actual array index is `handle >> 2`.
- For **Level 0** tables, the maximum number of handles is **512 on x86** and **1024 on x64**.
- `ObjectTypeIndex == 0x03` (on Windows 7) is the typical *File* object indicator — confirm against the build's `ObTypeIndexTable`.
- `HandleAttributes & 0x02` flags `HANDLE_PROTECT_FROM_CLOSE` — a classic **anti-forensics** technique (Mimikatz, ransomware, banking trojans).
- **Volatility** `handles` plugin: `vol.py -f mem.raw --profile=Win7SP1x64 handles -p <PID>` — output includes handle value, process, object type, name, and granted access.
- **Live tools** for handle enumeration: Sysinternals **Handle.exe**, **Process Explorer**, **Process Monitor**; for memory images, **Volatility** / **Rekall**.
- On **Linux**, the equivalent is a **file descriptor (fd)**: `task_struct → files_struct → fdtable → fd[] → struct file`. Standard fds `0`, `1`, `2` are stdin/stdout/stderr.
- Forensic **value** of open file handles:
  1. Live evidence of file access (stronger than timestamps).
  2. Reveals **deleted-but-still-open** files (unlinked from directory, still held by a process).
  3. Identifies **memory-mapped executables** — sign of process injection.
  4. Detects **locked critical files** (logs, key files, `lsass.dmp`, `ntds.dit`).
  5. Surfaces **anti-forensics flag** (`HANDLE_PROTECT_FROM_CLOSE`).
- Always **acquire memory first** in live triage — handles vanish on shutdown.
- The most exam-tested formula is the **five-step handle resolution**: (1) level, (2) base pointer, (3) index, (4) byte offset, (5) absolute address.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Hacking Top Ten and Reconnaissance Tools, Network Forensics: The OSI Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCCT602 - CYBER FORENSICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** The Hacking Top Ten and Reconnaissance Tools, Network Forensics: The OSI Model # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The OWASP Top Ten (The Hacking Top Ten) ### Formal Definition The **OWASP Top Ten** is a standard awareness document published by the **Open Web Application Security Project (OWASP)** that lists the ten most critical web application security risks. It is derived from a broad consensus of security practitioners and is updated periodically (currently the 2021 edition). It serves as a foundational reference for application security and penetration testing methodology. ### Conceptual Analogy Imagine a bank vault with ten known weaknesses, identified by master safecrackers after studying thousands of break-ins. A bank manager cannot claim "we are safe" without first checking whether the vault has been protected against these specific, well-documented weak points. The **OWASP Top Ten is that master list of common break-in methods** for web applications — knowing it tells both attackers and defenders where the most common battlegrounds lie. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The 2021 OWASP Top Ten list reorganized categories from the 2017 list, merging injection-style flaws and emphasizing insecure design as a new category. ### The OWASP Top Ten (2021 Edition) — Quick Reference | Rank | Vulnerability | Core Idea | |------|---------------|-----------| | A01 | **Broken Access Control** | Users can act outside their intended permissions | | A02 | **Cryptographic Failures** | Weak/missing encryption of sensitive data | | A03 | **Injection** | Untrusted data is sent to an interpreter (SQL, LDAP, OS cmd) | | A04 | **Insecure Design** | Flaws in design patterns, not in implementation | | A05 | **Security Misconfiguration** | Default configs, open cloud storage, verbose errors | | A06 | **Vulnerable & Outdated Components** | Using libraries with known CVEs | | A07 | **Identification & Authentication Failures** | Weak login, credential stuffing, session hijack | | A08 | **Software & Data Integrity Failures** | Trusting unverified updates, insecure CI/CD pipelines | | A09 | **Security Logging & Monitoring Failures** | Insufficient audit trails, no alerting | | A10 | **Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)** | Server is tricked into fetching remote resources | --- ## 1.2 Reconnaissance Tools ### Formal Definition **Reconnaissance** (in the cyber-attack lifecycle, also called *footprinting* or *information gathering*) is the **first phase of ethical hacking / penetration testing** in which the tester collects as much information as possible about the target — its IP addresses, open ports, running services, operating system, and human/employee information — before launching any exploitation attempt. ### Conceptual Analogy Think of a detective arriving at a neighborhood before a planned heist investigation. He maps the streets, counts the houses, identifies which have lights on, which have dogs, where the cameras point, and which residents leave for work at 8 AM. **Reconnaissance is exactly that — mapping the digital neighborhood of the target** before any actual attack. In forensics, the *same* tools are used defensively to understand what an attacker would have seen. ### Categories of Reconnaissance | Type | Mode | Example | |------|------|---------| | **Passive Reconnaissance** | Without directly touching the target | WHOIS, Google Dorking, Shodan, social media OSINT | | **Active Reconnaissance** | Directly probing the target | Nmap port scan, ping sweeps, banner grabbing | ### Common Reconnaissance Tools (Forensic & Offensive Relevance) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Tools Mandated by the KTU 2024 Syllabus (PCCCT602 Module 3):** > 1. **Nmap** — Network Mapper, for port scanning & service detection > 2. **Netcat (nc)** — The "Swiss Army knife" of TCP/UDP > 3. **Wireshark** — Packet-level protocol analyzer > 4. **Tcpdump** — Command-line packet sniffer > 5. **Maltego** — OSINT & relationship mapping > 6. **theHarvester** — Email/subdomain OSINT > 7. **Shodan** — Search engine for internet-connected devices > 8. **Recon-ng** — Web reconnaissance framework --- ## 1.3 Network Forensics and the OSI Model ### Formal Definition **Network Forensics** is the sub-discipline of digital forensics that involves **capturing, recording, and analyzing network traffic** to investigate security incidents, reconstruct events, identify attackers, and gather legally admissible evidence. The **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Model**, defined by the **ISO/IEC 7498-1** standard, is a **7-layer conceptual framework** that standardizes the functions of a telecommunication or computing system into abstract layers. ### Conceptual Analogy Picture a postal system. When you mail a letter: - You write the **content** (Application layer) - You choose a **language** for the content (Presentation layer) - You **maintain a conversation** with the recipient (Session layer) - You **address** the letter with sender/receiver info (Transport layer) - You choose a **route** between cities (Network layer) - You hand it to a **local courier** (Data Link layer) - The courier uses **physical roads** (Physical layer) > **Forensic insight:** When an investigator traces a cyber-attack, they need to know **at which postal stage** tampering occurred. The OSI model gives them that map. ### The 7 Layers (Bottom-Up) | # | Layer | Unit of Data | Key Protocols (Forensic Interest) | |---|-------|--------------|-----------------------------------| | 7 | **Application** | Data / Message | HTTP, HTTPS, DNS, FTP, SMTP, SNMP | | 6 | **Presentation** | Data | TLS/SSL, MIME, ASCII, JPEG | | 5 | **Session** | Data | NetBIOS, RPC, PPTP, SIP | | 4 | **Transport** | Segment (TCP) / Datagram (UDP) | TCP, UDP, port numbers (0–65535) | | 3 | **Network** | Packet | IPv4, IPv6, ICMP, IPsec, ARP | | 2 | **Data Link** | Frame | Ethernet (MAC), Wi-Fi (802.11), PPP | | 1 | **Physical** | Bits | Cables, fiber, radio waves, hubs | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mnemonic (Bottom→Up):** **"Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away"** > **Mnemonic (Top→Down):** **"All People Seem To Need Data Processing"** ### Why the OSI Model Matters in Forensics 1. **Layered evidence collection** — different attacks leave evidence at different layers. 2. **Tool mapping** — Wireshark dissects by layer; Nmap operates mainly at Layers 3 & 4. 3. **Attack attribution** — MAC (Layer 2) ties to hardware; IP (Layer 3) ties to network; port (Layer 4) ties to service. 4. **Chain of custody** — captures can be indexed by layer for court admissibility. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The OWASP Top Ten — Operational Anatomy ### A01: Broken Access Control - **What it is:** Failure to enforce *who* can do *what* (e.g., a regular user modifying `user_id=1` in the URL to access admin data). - **Forensic trail:** Access logs showing privilege-escalation requests, HTTP 200 responses to forbidden URLs. ### A03: Injection (SQLi as the canonical example) - **What it is:** User input is concatenated into a command/query without sanitization. - **Canonical attack string:** `' OR '1'='1` — closes a string, adds a boolean tautology. - **Forensic trail:** SQL error messages in HTTP responses, anomalous `'` characters in log fields. ### A07: Identification & Authentication Failures - **Credential stuffing** uses leaked username/password pairs against many sites. - **Forensic trail:** Multiple failed logins from geographically distributed IPs (low-and-slow pattern). ### A10: SSRF (Server-Side Request Forgery) - **What it is:** Attacker makes the *server* fetch an internal URL (e.g., `http://169.254.169.254/...` — the cloud metadata service). - **Forensic trail:** Outbound HTTP requests from the server to private RFC-1918 addresses. --- ## 2.2 Reconnaissance — Phased Methodology ### Phase 1: Footprinting (Passive) - **WHOIS lookup** → registrant email, name servers, creation date. - **DNS enumeration** (`dig`, `nslookup`) → A, MX, TXT, NS records. - **Google Dorking** → `site:example.com filetype:pdf`, `intitle:"index of"`. - **Shodan** → exposed IoT devices, default credentials. ### Phase 2: Scanning (Active) - **Ping sweep** → identifies live hosts. - **Port scan** → identifies open services. - **Service/version detection** → identifies software + version. - **OS fingerprinting** → identifies the operating system. ### Phase 3: Enumeration - Extracts user lists, shares, SNMP data, routing tables. ### Phase 4: Vulnerability Mapping - Cross-reference findings with **CVE** database, **NVD**, **CVSS** scores. --- ## 2.3 OSI Model in Forensics — Layer-by-Layer Evidence | Layer | Evidence Captured | Forensic Tool | Attack Examples | |-------|-------------------|---------------|-----------------| | 7 Application | HTTP headers, URLs, payloads | Wireshark, Burp Suite | SQLi, XSS, CSRF | | 6 Presentation | Encrypted streams (TLS), certificates | Wireshark (with keys), ssldump | Downgrade attacks | | 5 Session | Session tokens, RPC IDs | Wireshark | Session hijacking | | 4 Transport | TCP flags, port numbers, sequence numbers | Wireshark, tcpdump | SYN flood, port scan | | 3 Network | IP src/dst, TTL, ICMP types | tcpdump, Nmap | IP spoofing, ping of death | | 2 Data Link | MAC addresses, ARP tables | Wireshark, ARPwatch | ARP poisoning, MAC flooding | | 1 Physical | Signal timing, cable taps | Hardware tap, TDR | Wiretapping, EMI | --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > Cyber Forensics is largely *conceptual and procedural*, not heavily formula-driven. The few quantitative anchors that appear in KTU questions are listed below. ### 2.4.1 Port Number Ranges (Layer 4 — critical for scans) | Range | Class | Examples | |-------|-------|----------| | 0 – 1023 | **Well-known / System ports** | 80 (HTTP), 443 (HTTPS), 22 (SSH), 21 (FTP), 25 (SMTP), 53 (DNS) | | 1024 – 49151 | **Registered / User ports** | 3306 (MySQL), 8080 (HTTP-alt) | | 49152 – 65535 | **Dynamic / Ephemeral ports** | Allocated by OS for client connections | ### 2.4.2 TCP Three-Way Handshake (the heartbeat of forensic timing) $$\text{SYN} \rightarrow \text{SYN-ACK} \rightarrow \text{ACK}$$ - Total packets before data = **3** - Forensically, a **half-open connection** (SYN sent, no SYN-ACK returned) is the fingerprint of a **SYN scan** (`-sS` in Nmap). ### 2.4.3 Nmap Scan Types (most-tested) | Nmap Flag | Scan Name | Forensic Signature | |-----------|-----------|--------------------| | `-sS` | SYN (half-open) scan | No completed handshake in logs | | `-sT` | TCP connect scan | Full handshake, appears in app logs | | `-sU` | UDP scan | ICMP "port unreachable" replies | | `-sN` | NULL scan | No flags set | | `-sF` | FIN scan | Only FIN flag set | | `-sX` | Xmas scan | FIN+PSH+URG flags set (lights up "like a tree") | | `-O` | OS detection | TCP/IP stack fingerprinting | | `-sV` | Version detection | Banner grabbing | | `-A` | Aggressive | OS + version + scripts + traceroute | ### 2.4.4 CVSS Severity Bands (used in vulnerability mapping) | Score Range | Severity | |-------------|----------| | 0.0 | None | | 0.1 – 3.9 | Low | | 4.0 – 6.9 | Medium | | 7.0 – 8.9 | High | | 9.0 – 10.0 | Critical | ### 2.4.5 Subnet & Address Counts (for network range footprinting) - Hosts per subnet of prefix `/n`: $$H(n) = 2^{(32 - n)} - 2$$ - The **-2** subtracts the **network address** and the **broadcast address**. - Example: `/24` → $2^8 - 2 = 254$ usable hosts. ### 2.4.6 Encryption Strength Anchor - **Symmetric key (AES):** key lengths of **128, 192, 256 bits** are considered secure. - **RSA:** keys below **2048 bits** are deprecated as of 2023+ NIST guidance. ### 2.4.7 Packet Capture Sizing (PCAP math) $$\text{PCAP size (bytes)} \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ \text{Header}_i + \text{Payload}_i \right]$$ - Typical Ethernet MTU = **1500 bytes**; with header = **1518 bytes** (or 1522 with VLAN tag). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example — Subnet Calculation for a Recon Scan **Problem (typical KTU 3-mark question type):** A forensic analyst is given the IP block `192.168.10.0/26`. Calculate the number of usable hosts, the network address, the broadcast address, and the first/last usable host. ### Step 1 — Determine the prefix The `/26` means **26 bits** are used for the network portion, leaving **6 bits** for hosts. ### Step 2 — Apply the host formula $$H = 2^{(32-26)} - 2 = 2^{6} - 2 = 64 - 2 = 62 \text{ usable hosts}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute the subnet mask in decimal The mask has **26 leading 1-bits**: $$\text{11111111.11111111.11111111.11000000}_{2}$$ $$\Rightarrow 255.255.255.192$$ ### Step 4 — Derive network and broadcast addresses The 4th octet increments in blocks of **64** ($2^6 = 64$) because the last 6 bits are host bits: - Network address: `192.168.10.0` - Broadcast address: `192.168.10.63` - First usable host: `192.168.10.1` - Last usable host: `192.168.10.62` ### Step 5 — Forensic interpretation When scanning `/26` with Nmap, an analyst should expect to see **at most 62 live hosts** in the host-discovery phase. If 63 are observed, the analyst can infer a **rogue device or IP spoofing** is present — a strong incident-response signal. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping the OWASP Top Ten to OSI Layers and Recon Tools | OWASP Risk | OSI Layer (Primary) | Typical Recon / Attack Tool | Forensic Evidence to Capture | |------------|--------------------|------------------------------|------------------------------| | A01 Broken Access Control | 7 (HTTP verbs, RBAC) | Burp Suite, OWASP ZAP | Access logs with privilege-escalation URLs | | A02 Cryptographic Failures | 6 (TLS) | testssl.sh, sslyze | Expired/self-signed certs in pcap | | A03 Injection | 7 (SQL/LDAP/CMD) | SQLMap, Nmap scripts | Quoted strings, DB error patterns | | A04 Insecure Design | 7 (logic flow) | Manual code review, threat modeling | Missing rate-limiter, no CAPTCHA | | A05 Security Misconfiguration | 7 + 4 (open ports) | Nmap, Nikto | Directory listing, default creds | | A06 Vulnerable Components | 7 (libraries) | Retire.js, Snyk, Dependency-Check | Outdated library banner in HTTP | | A07 Auth Failures | 7 + 5 (sessions) | Hydra, Burp Intruder | Brute-force pattern in logs | | A08 Integrity Failures | 7 (CI/CD, updates) | Sigcheck, manual review | Unsigned update acceptance | | A09 Logging/Monitoring Failures | 7 (audit) | Log analysis tools (Splunk, ELK) | Absence of logs around incident time | | A10 SSRF | 7 (outbound HTTP) | SSRFmap, manual | Outbound HTTP to RFC-1918 / metadata IPs | --- ## 3.3 Worked Example — Nmap Scan to OSI Evidence Mapping **Problem:** A security analyst runs `nmap -sS -O -sV 10.0.0.5` and receives: ``` PORT STATE SERVICE VERSION 22/tcp open ssh OpenSSH 8.2p1 80/tcp open http Apache httpd 2.4.41 443/tcp open https Apache httpd 2.4.41 (SSL) OS: Linux 4.15 - 5.6 ``` **Step-by-step forensic interpretation:** ### Step 1 — Decode the scan flags - `-sS` = **SYN scan** (Layer 4, half-open). Forensically: the target's connection logs will *not* show a completed handshake. - `-O` = **OS fingerprinting** (Layer 3 + 4) by analyzing TCP ISN, TTL, window size. - `-sV` = **Version detection** (Layer 7) by probing and reading banners. ### Step 2 — Translate to OSI evidence | Output | OSI Layer | Forensic Meaning | |--------|-----------|------------------| | `22/tcp open ssh` | Layer 4 (TCP) + Layer 7 (SSH) | SSH service reachable; potential brute-force surface | | `OpenSSH 8.2p1` | Layer 7 banner | Version disclosed → cross-check CVE database | | `Apache httpd 2.4.41` | Layer 7 banner | Known CVEs (e.g., CVE-2021-41773 path traversal) | | `Linux 4.15 - 5.6` | Layer 3/4 fingerprint | OS family inferred from TCP/IP stack behavior | | `443/tcp open https (SSL)` | Layer 6 (TLS) | Encrypted; full pcap needs server private key to decrypt | ### Step 3 — Cross-reference with OWASP Top Ten - `Apache 2.4.41` → maps to **A06 Vulnerable Components** (this version is affected by path-traversal CVEs). - `http (port 80)` + `https (port 443)` open together → possible **A02 Cryptographic Failures** if HTTP forces no redirect. ### Step 4 — Build a forensic report entry - **Time of scan**, **source IP** of scanner (attribution). - **Hash of output** for chain of custody. - **Suggested remediation** matched to OWASP A02 + A06. --- ## 3.4 The OSI Encapsulation / Decapsulation Derivation **The fundamental process by which data flows through the OSI model:** ### Sending Side (Encapsulation) 1. **Layer 7–5** — User data is created (PDU = *Data*). 2. **Layer 4** — Transport header (TCP/UDP) is prepended → PDU becomes a **Segment** (or *Datagram* for UDP). 3. **Layer 3** — Network header (IP) is prepended → PDU becomes a **Packet**. 4. **Layer 2** — Data-Link header (MAC) + trailer (FCS) are added → PDU becomes a **Frame**. 5. **Layer 1** — Frame is converted to **Bits** and transmitted on the physical medium. ### Receiving Side (Decapsulation) The reverse — each layer strips its header, exposes the payload to the next-higher layer. ### Forensic Equation of Total On-Wire Size For a single HTTP request: $$S_{\text{wire}} = H_{\text{eth}} + H_{\text{IP}} + H_{\text{TCP}} + P_{\text{HTTP}}$$ Where: - $H_{\text{eth}} = 14$ bytes (Ethernet header, no VLAN) - $H_{\text{IP}} = 20$ bytes (IPv4, no options) - $H_{\text{TCP}} = 20$ bytes (TCP, no options) - $P_{\text{HTTP}}$ = application payload $$S_{\text{wire}} = 14 + 20 + 20 + P_{\text{HTTP}} = 54 + P_{\text{HTTP}} \text{ bytes}$$ > **Forensic use:** if a pcap shows a frame larger than **1518 bytes** (standard Ethernet MTU + header), the analyst suspects **jumbo frames** or **malformed/fragmented traffic** — a common indicator of evasion attempts. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 OSI 7-Layer Model with Forensic Touchpoints ```mermaid flowchart TB L7["LAYER 7 APPLICATION

Explain any three in brief.** **Model Answer:** The OWASP Top Ten (2021): 1. A01 Broken Access Control 2. A02 Cryptographic Failures 3. A03 Injection 4. A04 Insecure Design 5. A05 Security Misconfiguration 6. A06 Vulnerable & Outdated Components 7. A07 Identification & Authentication Failures 8. A08 Software & Data Integrity Failures 9. A09 Security Logging & Monitoring Failures 10. A10 Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) **Brief explanations:** - **A01 Broken Access Control:** Users can act outside their intended permissions — e.g., a normal user editing `/admin/deleteUser?id=5` and succeeding. Defense: enforce role-based access control on every request. - **A03 Injection:** Untrusted input is sent to a command interpreter. Example: `' OR '1'='1` in a login form that bypasses SQL WHERE clause. Defense: parameterized queries, input validation. - **A07 Identification & Authentication Failures:** Weak passwords, no MFA, credential stuffing. Defense: enforce strong passwords, MFA, rate-limit attempts, and use secure session cookies. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, Cyber Forensics]** **Differentiate between active and passive reconnaissance with two examples each.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Passive Reconnaissance | Active Reconnaissance | |--------|------------------------|------------------------| | **Touches the target?** | No — uses third-party sources | Yes — directly probes the target | | **Risk of detection** | Negligible | High; can trigger IDS/IPS | | **Examples** | 1. WHOIS lookup of domain 2. Google Dorking for exposed files | 1. Nmap SYN scan (`-sS`) 2. Nmap version detection (`-sV`) | Passive recon gathers information from public records, search engines, and DNS databases without sending packets to the target, making it nearly invisible. Active recon sends packets directly to the target (e.g., pings, SYN scans, banner grabs) and can be logged by the target's firewalls and IDS — making it louder but more detailed. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PCCCT602, Module 3]** **(a)** Explain the OSI 7-layer model in detail with the function and example protocols of each layer. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Describe the network forensics process. How does the OSI model help in evidence collection at each layer? *(7 marks)* --- #### Part (a) — Model Solution The **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) model** is a 7-layer reference model standardized by **ISO/IEC 7498-1**. It abstracts the communication functions of a computing system into discrete layers. | # | Layer | Function | Example Protocols / Tech | PDU | |---|-------|----------|--------------------------|-----| | 7 | **Application** | Interface to user / network services | HTTP, HTTPS, DNS, FTP, SMTP, SNMP | Data | | 6 | **Presentation** | Translation, encryption, compression | TLS, SSL, MIME, JPEG, ASCII | Data | | 5 | **Session** | Manages sessions between applications | NetBIOS, RPC, SIP, PPTP | Data | | 4 | **Transport** | End-to-end delivery, port addressing | TCP, UDP | Segment / Datagram | | 3 | **Network** | Logical addressing & routing | IPv4, IPv6, ICMP, IPsec, ARP | Packet | | 2 | **Data Link** | Physical addressing, error detection on a link | Ethernet, Wi-Fi 802.11, PPP, MAC | Frame | | 1 | **Physical** | Transmission of raw bits on a medium | Cables, fiber, radio, hubs, repeaters | Bits | **Communication model:** Each layer serves the layer above it and is served by the layer below. Data flows down on the sender (encapsulation) and up on the receiver (decapsulation). **Example:** A browser request to a web server passes: - App → (HTTP GET created) → Presentation (TLS encrypt) → Transport (TCP, port 443) → Network (IP header) → Data Link (Ethernet, MAC) → Physical (bits on cable). --- #### Part (b) — Model Solution **Network Forensics Process:** 1. **Identification** — Detect that an incident has occurred (alerts from IDS, SIEM). 2. **Preservation** — Capture live traffic using tap/SPAN, save as PCAP with hash. 3. **Collection** — Store with chain of custody (timestamps, hashes, signatures). 4. **Examination** — Filter, decode, and extract relevant packets. 5. **Analysis** — Reconstruct the timeline, identify source/destination IPs, ports, payloads. 6. **Presentation** — Produce a court-admissible report. **How the OSI model aids evidence collection:** | Layer | Forensic Evidence | Tool | |-------|-------------------|------| | 7 Application | HTTP URLs, headers, payloads (XSS, SQLi strings) | Wireshark, Burp Suite | | 6 Presentation | TLS versions, cert validity, cipher suites | ssldump, Wireshark with keys | | 5 Session | Session tokens, RPC IDs | Wireshark | | 4 Transport | TCP flags, port numbers, sequence numbers | Tcpdump, Wireshark | | 3 Network | IP src/dst, TTL, ICMP codes | Tcpdump, Nmap | | 2 Data Link | MAC addresses, ARP mappings | Wireshark, ARPwatch | | 1 Physical | Signal integrity, EMI | Hardware tap, oscilloscope | **Conclusion:** The OSI model gives a forensic analyst a *systematic map*. Instead of searching blindly, the investigator examines each layer in turn — much like a doctor checking each body system — to localize where an attack occurred and gather court-admissible evidence. --- ### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, PCCCT602, Module 3]** **(a)** Explain any five categories of the OWASP Top Ten (2021) with a real-world example of each. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Discuss the following reconnaissance tools with their forensic use: Nmap, Netcat, Wireshark, Maltego, theHarvester. *(7 marks)* --- #### Part (a) — Model Solution **1. A01 Broken Access Control** — A user changes the URL from `/myOrders?id=100` to `/myOrders?id=101` and views another user's data because the server does not re-check ownership. Defense: enforce ownership at the database query level. **2. A02 Cryptographic Failures** — A website transmits passwords in plaintext HTTP, and an attacker on the same Wi-Fi captures them with Wireshark. Defense: enforce HTTPS with TLS 1.3 and HSTS. **3. A03 Injection** — Login form accepts `' OR '1'='1` as username, which appends to the SQL `SELECT * FROM users WHERE name='' OR '1'='1' AND pass='...'` and bypasses authentication. Defense: parameterized queries. **4. A05 Security Misconfiguration** — Default admin/admin credentials left on a router, leading to full network compromise. Defense: change defaults, harden configs, disable unused services. **5. A07 Identification & Authentication Failures** — No account lockout, allowing an attacker to try 10,000 passwords in a credential-stuffing attack. Defense: MFA, account lockout, CAPTCHA, rate limiting. **6. A10 SSRF** — A web app fetches user-supplied URLs to display previews. Attacker supplies `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/` to read cloud IAM credentials. Defense: allowlist outbound URLs, block private IP ranges, use IMDSv2. --- #### Part (b) — Model Solution | Tool | Category | Purpose | Forensic Use | |------|----------|---------|--------------| | **Nmap** | Active scanner | Port scan, service/version detection, OS fingerprinting | Generates the network footprint of a target; flags like `-sS` reveal half-open scans in pcaps | | **Netcat (nc)** | TCP/UDP utility | Reads/writes raw data over network connections; banner grabbing, port listening | Used defensively to set up a listener and capture a reverse shell's output; evidence of C2 channels | | **Wireshark** | Packet analyzer | Captures & decodes traffic at all 7 OSI layers | Primary forensic tool — produces PCAP files with full protocol dissection, court-admissible evidence | | **Maltego** | OSINT tool | Mines and links public data: emails, domains, social profiles | Builds a relationship graph of attacker infrastructure; passive evidence for attribution | | **theHarvester** | OSINT tool | Scrapes emails, subdomains, hosts from public sources (Bing, LinkedIn, Shodan) | Finds exposed employee emails → phishing surface; helps attribute campaigns | **Key forensic principle:** Tools like Nmap and Netcat leave *fingerprints* (e.g., default Nmap scans send specific TCP window sizes) — defenders can identify them in logs. Wireshark is the *gold standard* for evidence-grade packet capture, while Maltego and theHarvester are *non-intrusive* and useful for the early intelligence-gathering phase. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on This Topic** > 1. **OWASP vs OSSTMM confusion:** Students often mix OWASP Top Ten with the OSSTMM (Open Source Security Testing Methodology Manual) — they are *not* the same. OWASP is web-focused; OSSTMM covers broader security testing. > 2. **Confusing OSI with TCP/IP model:** The TCP/IP model has only **4 layers** (Application, Transport, Internet, Link). The OSI has **7**. Examiners love this distinction. > 3. **Memorizing the 2017 list instead of 2021:** The 2021 list **renamed** and **merged** categories. E.g., "XXE" and "XSS" were merged into A03 Injection. Using outdated lists loses marks. > 4. **Forgetting the port number ranges:** Examiners frequently ask "What is the well-known port range?" — answer: **0–1023**. > 5. **Omitting chain of custody:** In any forensic question, the examiner expects you to mention **integrity (hash)**, **timestamps**, and **chain of custody** — even in 3-mark answers. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Yield for KTU 2024** - ✅ **OWASP Top Ten (2021):** Memorize the 10 categories **in order** (A01 to A10). Focus on A01, A02, A03, A07, and A10 — these are the most frequently tested. - ✅ **OSI Model — 7 layers in order:** Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical. Know **PDU names** at each layer (Data → Segment/Datagram → Packet → Frame → Bits). - ✅ **Mnemonics:** "All People Seem To Need Data Processing" (top→down) and "Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away" (bottom→up). - ✅ **TCP vs UDP:** TCP = connection-oriented, reliable, 3-way handshake. UDP = connectionless, fast, no handshake. - ✅ **Port ranges:** Well-known 0–1023, Registered 1024–49151, Dynamic 49152–65535. **HTTP = 80, HTTPS = 443, SSH = 22, FTP = 21, DNS = 53**. - ✅ **Recon tools:** Nmap (scanning), Netcat (TCP/UDP swiss-army), Wireshark (packet analysis), Maltego (OSINT graph), theHarvester (email/subdomain mining), Shodan (IoT exposure). - ✅ **Nmap scan types and forensic fingerprints:** `-sS` (SYN/half-open), `-sT` (full connect), `-sU` (UDP), `-sX` (Xmas), `-sF` (FIN), `-sN` (NULL), `-O` (OS), `-sV` (version), `-A` (aggressive). - ✅ **Active vs Passive Recon:** Passive = no contact (WHOIS, Google Dork). Active = direct probing (Nmap, ping sweep). Passive is stealthier; active is more detailed. - ✅ **Forensic process:** Identification → Preservation → Collection → Examination → Analysis → Presentation. - ✅ **Chain of custody:** Always hash evidence (MD5/SHA-256), log timestamps, and maintain an unbroken trail. - ✅ **Network Forensics & OSI:** Each attack leaves evidence at a specific layer. Wireshark dissects by layer; Nmap operates mainly at Layers 3–4; physical-layer evidence requires hardware taps. - ✅ **CVSS bands:** Low (0.1–3.9), Medium (4.0–6.9), High (7.0–8.9), Critical (9.0–10.0). - ✅ **Subnet math:** Usable hosts = $2^{(32-n)} - 2$ for prefix `/n`. Always subtract network + broadcast. - ✅ **A02 vs A07:** A02 = data-at-rest/in-transit unprotected (encryption). A07 = authentication mechanism broken (passwords, sessions). Don't confuse them. - ✅ **A04 Insecure Design** is a *design-phase* flaw, distinct from implementation bugs. Threat modeling mitigates it. - ✅ **A10 SSRF** is a 2021 newcomer; the classic target is the cloud metadata service at `169.254.169.254`.

topics.title: Forensic Footprints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT602 - CYBER FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Forensic Footprints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Footprints** are the persistent, recoverable digital artifacts, traces, or residual data left behind on digital media, networks, applications, or systems as a direct or indirect result of user activity, malicious intrusion, or system operation. These artifacts constitute admissible evidence in cyber forensic investigations and are crucial for reconstructing events, attributing actions to users, and establishing timelines.

In the context of **Cyber Forensics**, a forensic footprint is anything that proves a user, device, or application *interacted* with a system. Unlike physical fingerprints, forensic footprints are often **latent (hidden)**, **volatile (transient)**, or **persistent (long-lasting)**, and may reside in the slack space, unallocated clusters, RAM, or even metadata of files.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Sand Beach

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Walking on a Wet Sand Beach**
> Imagine a pristine wet sand beach. Every time you (a user/attacker) walk across it, you leave footprints. These footprints tell a story:
> - **Size & Depth** → Which operating system or device was used.
> - **Direction** → The sequence of actions (browsing history).
> - **Distance between prints** → Time intervals between actions (timestamps).
> - **Disturbed sand (drag marks)** → Data transfers, uploads, or downloads.
>
> Even after you leave, an investigator (forensic analyst) can examine the beach and reconstruct your entire journey, the time you spent, and the objects you touched. Digital systems are the *beach*, and forensic footprints are the *impressions in the metadata and storage layers*.

## 1.3 Core Categories of Forensic Footprints

Forensic footprints are broadly classified into the following **KTU Board Exam standard categories**:

1. **Online / Internet Footprints** — Email, Web Browsing, Social Media, Cloud Storage.
2. **System Footprints** — Operating System artifacts (Registry, Event Logs, Prefetch, Recycle Bin).
3. **Network Footprints** — Firewall logs, IDS/IPS logs, Router logs, Packet Captures.
4. **Application Footprints** — Database logs, Application cache, Recent file lists.
5. **Physical / Peripheral Footprints** — Printer spool files, USB device history, External storage artifacts.
6. **Mobile Footprints** — Call Detail Records (CDR), SMS, GPS, App data.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics in Forensic Investigations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following metrics are universally accepted in **digital forensic investigations**:
> - **Order of Volatility (OOV):** Registers → Cache → RAM → Network State → Disk → Remote Logs → Off-line Storage.
> - **Time Standard:** **Coordinated Universal Time (UTC)** is the mandatory standard for timestamping evidence.
> - **Hashing Standards:** **MD5 (128-bit)**, **SHA-1 (160-bit)**, and **SHA-256 (256-bit)** for evidence integrity verification.
> - **Evidence Standard:** **ACPO (Association of Chief Police Officers)** Principle 1: No action should change data held on a digital device.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theory Behind Forensic Footprint Generation

Every digital interaction follows a deterministic process, leaving artifacts. Let us analyze this systematically.

### 2.1.1 The Footprint Generation Lifecycle

- **Step 1 — User Action (Trigger):** The user performs an action (e.g., opens a browser, sends an email, plugs in a USB).
- **Step 2 — Application Layer Capture:** The application (browser, mail client, OS service) creates user-space artifacts (cache files, log entries, configuration changes).
- **Step 3 — Kernel/OS Layer Persistence:** The Operating System records the action in:
  - **Windows Registry** (e.g., `UserAssist`, `MUICache`, `RecentDocs`).
  - **Event Logs** (`Security.evtx`, `System.evtx`, `Application.evtx`).
  - **Prefetch Files** (`C:\Windows\Prefetch\`).
  - **Link Files (LNK)** and **Jump Lists**.
- **Step 4 — File System Layer Imprint:** The filesystem (NTFS, FAT32, ext4) modifies:
  - **$MFT (Master File Table)** entries.
  - **$STANDARD_INFORMATION** and **$FILE_NAME** attributes (timestamps: `$SI`, `$FN`).
  - **$LogFile** and **$UsnJrnl` (Update Sequence Number Journal)**.
- **Step 5 — Network Layer Echo:** Network devices (routers, firewalls) record source/destination IPs, MAC addresses, and ports.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter?**
> Even if a user *deletes* a file, the footprint persists in the **unallocated space**, **slack space**, **file system journals**, and **swap/page files**. This is the *forensic paradox*: deletion is rarely true erasure.

## 2.2 Critical Windows Artifacts (KTU High-Yield)

| Artifact | Location | Information Revealed | Forensic Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Registry — UserAssist** | `NTUSER.DAT\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\UserAssist` | Executed programs with ROT-13 encoded timestamps | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Registry — RecentDocs** | `NTUSER.DAT\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\RecentDocs` | Recently opened documents (last 150) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Registry — Run/RunOnce** | `HKLM/HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run` | Persistence mechanisms (malware indicators) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Prefetch Files** | `C:\Windows\Prefetch\*.pf` | Program execution (up to 1024 entries, last 8 runs) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **LNK Files** | `C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Windows\Recent\` | Target path, MAC address, timestamps of accessed files | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Event Logs** | `C:\Windows\System32\winevt\Logs\` | Logon events (4624), Process creation (4688), USB usage | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Recycle Bin** | `$Recycle.bin\$I` (metadata) and `$R` (content) | Original path, deletion time, file size | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **$MFT** | `C:\$MFT` | Every file/folder, `$SI` & `$FN` timestamps (Timestomping detection) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **$UsnJrnl** | `C:\$Extend\$UsnJrnl:$J` | File system change journal (creation, modification, rename) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Shimcache** | `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\AppCompatCache` | Program execution with full path (anti-forensics resistant) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Amcache** | `C:\Windows\AppCompat\Programs\Amcache.hve` | Program execution, SHA-1 hash, install/uninstall times | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

## 2.3 Email Forensics Footprint Analysis

Email artifacts are the **most common** KTU exam topic. The footprint resides in **headers** and **client databases**.

### 2.3.1 Email Header Forensics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An email header is a **layered protocol artifact**. It contains routing information added by each Mail Transfer Agent (MTA) it passes through. The forensic examiner reads the header **bottom-up** to trace the email's true origin.

**Header Forensics Formula (Conceptual):**
$$
\text{True Originator} = \text{Bottom-most "Received:" header} \rightarrow \text{Originating IP}
$$
$$
\text{Authentication} = f(\text{SPF}, \text{DKIM}, \text{DMARC}) \in \{\text{Pass}, \text{Fail}, \text{SoftFail}\}
$$

### 2.3.2 Email Client Artifacts

| Client | Footprint Location | Forensic Data |
|---|---|---|
| **Microsoft Outlook (PST/OST)** | `%LOCALAPPDATA%\Microsoft\Outlook\` | Email body, attachments, contacts, calendar |
| **Mozilla Thunderbird (MBOX)** | `%APPDATA%\Thunderbird\Profiles\` | MBOX files (plaintext email storage) |
| **Webmail (Gmail/Outlook.com)** | Server-side; client browser cache | HTML files, attachments, local storage (IndexedDB) |

## 2.4 Web Browser Forensics Footprint

Every web activity leaves **four primary artifacts**:

1. **History** — `places.sqlite` (Firefox), `History` (Chrome), `WebCacheV01.dat` (Edge/IE).
2. **Cache** — Downloaded images, scripts, HTML (e.g., `Chrome Cache`, `thumbnails_*.db`).
3. **Cookies** — `cookies.sqlite` (Firefox), `Cookies` (Chrome, encrypted on v80+).
4. **Form Auto-fill / Saved Passwords** — `logins.json` (encrypted), `Login Data` DB.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Browser forensics is pivotal in **child exploitation cases**, **insider threat investigations**, and **corporate espionage** where attackers use legitimate web services for command-and-control (C2) operations.

## 2.5 Mobile & Network Footprints (Brief)

- **Android:** `data/data/<package>/databases/`, `/sdcard/Android/data/`, ADB logs, `tombstones`.
- **iOS:** `BackupManifest.db`, `Keychain`, `SMS.db`, `CallHistory.storedata`.
- **Network:** PCAP files, NetFlow records, DNS query logs, ARP tables.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Forensic Footprints

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Purpose / Unit | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Order of Volatility (OOV)** | $\text{Registers} < \text{Cache} < \text{RAM} < \text{Disk} < \text{Offline}$ | Defines evidence collection priority | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Hashing for Integrity** | $H(\text{file}) \rightarrow \text{MD5/SHA-1/SHA-256}$ | Verifies evidence non-repudiation | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Email Hop Count** | $\text{Hops} = \vert \text{Received Header Count} - 1 \vert$ | Number of MTAs traversed | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Timestamp Zones (NTFS)** | $\Delta t = \vert t_{\$SI} - t_{\$FN} \vert$ | Detects timestomping (anomaly) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **MAC Vendor Lookup** | $\text{OUI}(MAC) \rightarrow \text{Manufacturer}$ | Identifies device make/origin | ⭐⭐ |
| **Logon Event ID** | $\text{EID} = 4624 \ (\text{Success}), \ 4625 \ (\text{Fail})$ | Windows authentication footprint | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Process Creation ID** | $\text{EID} = 4688$ | New process footprint (Sysmon) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **USB Device Event ID** | $\text{EID} \in \{2003, 2100, \text{SetupAPI}.txt\}$ | Removable media footprint | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Cookie Expiry** | $t_{\text{expire}} = t_{\text{created}} + \text{TTL}_{\text{Set-Cookie}}$ | Tracks user session timeline | ⭐⭐ |
| **Browser Cache Age** | $t_{\text{age}} = t_{\text{now}} - t_{\text{Last-Modified}}$ | Time since page was fetched | ⭐⭐ |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: NTFS Timestamp Anomaly Detection (Timestomping)

In NTFS, every file has **two sets of timestamps**: `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` (`$SI`) and `$FILE_NAME` (`$FN`). When a user (or malware) uses tools like `timestomp.exe` to alter timestamps, they typically modify `$SI` (visible via the OS), but `$FN` is harder to alter. This forms the basis of **forensic detection of timestomping**.

Let us derive the anomaly detection logic step-by-step.

### Step 1 — Define the four MACb times

For any file $f$, the NTFS filesystem stores:
$$
f_{\$SI} = \{ M_{SI}, A_{SI}, C_{SI}, B_{SI} \}
$$
$$
f_{\$FN} = \{ M_{FN}, A_{FN}, C_{FN}, B_{FN} \}
$$
where:
- $M$ = Modified time
- $A$ = Accessed time
- $C$ = Creation (MFT entry modification) time
- $B$ = Birth (file creation) time

### Step 2 — Establish the integrity invariant

Under **normal, untampered** conditions, the following invariant holds:
$$
M_{FN} \geq C_{FN} = B_{FN}
$$
and
$$
C_{SI} \geq B_{SI}
$$
The `$FN` timestamps are set by the **filesystem driver at MFT entry creation** and are not easily user-modifiable.

### Step 3 — Compute the anomaly deltas

Define the timestamp deltas:
$$
\Delta t_{MC} = M_{SI} - C_{SI}
$$
$$
\Delta t_{FB} = C_{FN} - B_{FN}
$$
$$
\Delta t_{MS} = M_{SI} - M_{FN}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the anomaly condition

A file is **suspicious of timestomping** if:
$$
(M_{SI} < B_{FN}) \ \lor \ (C_{FN} \neq B_{FN}) \ \lor \ (\Delta t_{MS} \gg 0)
$$

### Step 5 — Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Example**
> Consider a file `secret.docx` with the following NTFS timestamps extracted by the EnCase forensic tool:
> - `$SI`: Modified = `2024-01-15 10:30:00 UTC`, Created = `2024-03-20 14:00:00 UTC`
> - `$FN`: Modified = `2024-01-15 10:30:00 UTC`, Birth = `2024-03-20 14:00:00 UTC`
>
> An investigator suspects timestomping because the user claims the file was created on `2024-01-15` (to support an alibi). Let us verify:
>
> **Compute the SI anomaly:**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \Delta t_{MC}^{SI} &= M_{SI} - C_{SI} \\
> &= \text{2024-01-15 10:30:00} - \text{2024-03-20 14:00:00} \\
> &= -65 \text{ days, } 3 \text{ hours, } 30 \text{ minutes}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
