## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 x 3 = 6 Marks Total Coverage)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]** Define ICMP and list any four ICMP message types used for cyber attacks. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol), defined in **RFC 792**, is a Layer 3 protocol of the TCP/IP suite used for diagnostic and error-reporting functions. Four ICMP types relevant to attacks:
1. **Type 8 — Echo Request**: Used in ping sweeps, ping floods, and smurf attacks.
2. **Type 0 — Echo Reply**: Indicates host liveness; used in smurf attack responses.
3. **Type 3 — Destination Unreachable**: Used in reconnaissance and port scanning.
4. **Type 5 — Redirect**: Abused for man-in-the-middle routing attacks.
5. **Type 11 — Time Exceeded**: Used in traceroute reconnaissance.
6. **Type 13/14 — Timestamp**: Used for clock-skew fingerprinting.

*(Any four well-explained types = 3 marks. One-line definitions are insufficient — board examiners expect type number + attack relevance.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** What is a Smurf attack? Explain the role of the directed broadcast address in amplifying such an attack. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
A **Smurf attack** is a distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attack that exploits ICMP Echo Requests sent to a **directed broadcast address** to overwhelm a victim with replies. The attacker spoofs the victim's IP as the source of Echo Requests and sends them to the broadcast address of an intermediate network. Every host on that network responds with an Echo Reply to the victim, achieving **bandwidth amplification**.

The **directed broadcast address** (e.g., 192.168.1.255) causes all hosts in the subnet to process and reply to the single packet, multiplying attacker output by the number of responding hosts. The **amplification factor** is given by:

$$
A = \frac{\text{Number of replying hosts}}{\text{Number of packets sent by attacker}}
$$

Modern mitigation: **RFC 2644** mandates disabling directed broadcast forwarding at routers, neutralizing this attack vector.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module 3 Internal Choice — 1 x 14 = 14 Marks)

> **Q3(a). [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** With neat diagrams, explain the following ICMP attacks: (i) Ping of Death (7 marks) (ii) ICMP Flood (7 marks)

#### Model Solution (i) — Ping of Death (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** The Ping of Death is a denial-of-service attack that sends an oversized or malformed ICMP Echo Request whose reassembled size exceeds the maximum IP packet length of **65,535 bytes**, causing buffer overflow at the target.

**Mechanism (3 marks):**
- The IP header's **Total Length** field is 16 bits, allowing a maximum legitimate packet size of $2^{16} - 1 = 65{,}535$ bytes.
- The attacker fragments the ICMP packet into multiple IP fragments, each individually legal in size, but whose **sum upon reassembly exceeds 65,535 bytes**.
- The victim's IP stack, expecting to reassemble fragments into a buffer sized for 65,535 bytes, overflows into adjacent memory.
- This causes **kernel panic, system crash, or arbitrary code execution** on vulnerable systems.

**Diagram (2 marks):**
```
Attacker → [Fragment 1: 65,500 bytes, MF=1] → Internet → Victim
Attacker → [Fragment 2: 100 bytes, MF=0]    → Internet → Victim
                   ↓
       Reassembly: 65,600 bytes → OVERFLOW
```

**Mitigation (1 mark):** Modern OSes enforce RFC 1858/3128 fragment validation, rejecting any datagram whose total reassembled length exceeds 65,535 bytes.

#### Model Solution (ii) — ICMP Flood (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** An ICMP Flood (or Ping Flood) is a denial-of-service attack that overwhelms the target with a high rate of ICMP Echo Requests, saturating bandwidth and CPU.

**Mechanism (3 marks):**
- Attacker uses tools like **hping3**, **ping -f** (Linux flood flag), or botnet-driven scripts to send thousands of Echo Requests per second.
- Each request demands a reply, consuming both inbound and outbound bandwidth.
- Mathematical condition for success: $R \cdot S \cdot 8 \;\gt\; B$, where $R$ = packet rate, $S$ = packet size, $B$ = target bandwidth.
- Distributed variants (DDoS) use thousands of botnet nodes (Mirai, etc.) to generate $\gt 100$ Gbps floods.

**Forensic Indicators (2 marks):**
- ICMP Type 8 volume $\gt 50$ packets/second from single source.
- High bandwidth utilization on inbound link (95–100%).
- `netstat -s` shows disproportionate ICMP counters.

**Mitigation (1 mark):** Rate limiting at firewall, **BCP 38** (ingress filtering) to block spoofed source IPs, upstream DDoS scrubbing (Cloudflare, AWS Shield).

---

> **OR (Alternative Choice for Q3)**

> **Q3(b). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Explain ICMP Tunneling in detail. How is it used in covert communication, and what forensic techniques detect it? (14 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Definition (3 marks):**
ICMP Tunneling is a **covert channel technique** that encapsulates arbitrary IP traffic (TCP, UDP, application-layer data) inside the **Data Payload field** of ICMP Echo Request/Reply packets. Because ICMP is often permitted through firewalls for diagnostic purposes, attackers exploit this trust to bypass perimeter security.

**Architecture (4 marks):**
- **Client (attacker)**: Sends encapsulated data as ICMP Echo Request payloads to a compromised or attacker-controlled host.
- **Server (listener)**: Receives the ICMP packets, extracts the payload, and forwards the inner traffic to its actual destination.
- The "outer" ICMP layer is legitimate and passes through most firewalls; the "inner" payload is invisible to port-based filters.

**Popular Tools (2 marks):**
- **ptunnel** (PingTunnel) — Linux-native, supports TCP/UDP tunneling.
- **icmpshell** — Remote shell over ICMP.
- **Loki** — Early (1996) ICMP tunneling tool.
- **icmptx**, **ptunnel-ng** — Modern forks.

**Forensic Detection Techniques (5 marks):**
1. **Payload Entropy Analysis**: Compute Shannon entropy $H(p) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$. Encrypted/compressed payloads yield $H \ge 6.5$ bits/byte, far exceeding normal ping data.
2. **Payload Size Anomalies**: Legitimate pings are 0–64 bytes; tunneled traffic often uses near-MTU-sized payloads (1472 bytes for Ethernet).
3. **Packet Timing Patterns**: Tunneled traffic exhibits **sustained, periodic** ICMP exchanges, unlike human-driven diagnostic pings.
4. **Payload Content Inspection**: Look for signatures of inner protocols (TCP SYN, HTTP headers, `MZ` PE headers).
5. **Bidirectional Correlation**: Tunneling requires both request and reply to be non-empty and high-entropy.
6. **Tool Artifacts**: Tools like ptunnel use a default magic header (`\x00\x00\x00\x00-tunnel` or similar) in the payload.

**Mitigation (Bonus):**
- Block all inbound/outbound ICMP at perimeter firewalls.
- DPI on ICMP with entropy threshold alerts.
- Egress filtering to detect anomalous outbound ICMP volumes.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in ICMP Attack Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Protocol Numbers**: Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for failing to state that ICMP's IP protocol number is **1** and that it operates at **Layer 3 (Network Layer)**.
> 2. **Confusing Smurf with Ping Flood**: Students often inter-mix these. *Smurf = broadcast amplification*; *Flood = direct high-rate*. Examiners will not award full marks if the distinction is blurred.
> 3. **Skipping the Amplification Formula**: For Smurf, you MUST present the formula $A = N_h / N_a$ or the bandwidth equation to score 3+ marks.
> 4. **No Diagram = 2-Mark Loss**: For 7-mark attack explanation questions, a labeled diagram (fragments, attack flow, or topology) is **mandatory**. Without it, expect a 1.5–2 mark deduction.
> 5. **Stale Mitigation Advice**: Saying "install a firewall" is too generic. Examiners expect specific RFC references (RFC 1858, RFC 2644, BCP 38) or specific sysctl/firewall commands.
> 6. **Confusing ICMPv4 and ICMPv6**: Type numbers differ (e.g., ICMPv6 Echo Request = Type 128, not 8). State the version explicitly.
> 7. **Forgetting Forensic Perspective**: This is a **Cyber Forensics** course. Always frame answers from an *investigation* or *evidence-collection* angle, not just attack mechanics.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — ICMP Attacks**

- **ICMP** = Layer 3 protocol, IP protocol number **1**, defined in **RFC 792** (1981).
- **Ping of Death** exploits IP fragmentation reassembly; condition is $L_{total} \gt 65{,}535$ bytes. Mitigated by **RFC 1858 / RFC 3128**.
- **ICMP Flood (Ping Flood)** succeeds when $R \cdot S \cdot 8 \;\gt\; B$ (target bandwidth). Threshold: $\gt 50$ pps from a single source is suspicious.
- **Smurf Attack** uses a **directed broadcast address** for amplification: $A = N_h / N_a$. Disabled by **RFC 2644**.
- **ICMP Redirect (Type 5)** poisons host routing tables; mitigated by setting `accept_redirects=0` (Linux) or equivalent on Windows.
- **ICMP Tunneling** embeds covert data in Echo payloads. Forensic detection: **Shannon entropy $\ge 6.5$ bits/byte** + payload size $\ge 100$ bytes + sustained bidirectional pattern.
- **Common attack ICMP Types**: 0 (Echo Reply), 3 (Unreachable), 5 (Redirect), 8 (Echo Request), 11 (Time Exceeded), 13/14 (Timestamp), 17 (Mask).
- **Forensic tools**: Wireshark, tcpdump, NetworkMiner, Argus, Xplico, Splunk/ELK SIEM.
- **Mitigation layers**: Patch OS, BCP 38 ingress filtering, rate limiting, directed-broadcast disable, accept-redirect disable, DPI/entropy-based ICMP inspection.
- **MTU math**: $C_{tunnel} = MTU - 20_{IP} - 8_{ICMP} = 1472$ bytes per packet on Ethernet.
- **Investigation order**: Detect → Capture → Filter (ICMP) → Analyze (volume/entropy/pattern) → Correlate logs → Build timeline → Generate report → Chain of custody.
- **Memory hook**: *ICMP attacks fall into 4 buckets — DoS, Recon, MITM, Covert Channel.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drive-By Downloads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT602 - CYBER FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Drive-By Downloads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔐 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drive-By Download** is a cyberattack technique in which malicious software (malware) is automatically downloaded and installed onto a victim's computer or device **without the user's knowledge or explicit consent**, simply by visiting a compromised or malicious website. The attack exploits vulnerabilities in the browser, browser plugins, or the underlying operating system to trigger the silent installation of malicious payloads.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme Cyber Forensics syllabus**, a drive-by download is classified as a **client-side web-based attack vector** that operates in the background of a legitimate-looking web interaction. The term "drive-by" is borrowed from the phrase *"drive-by shooting"* — implying the victim is hit unexpectedly without prior engagement with the attacker.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction — Drive-By vs. Trojan Downloader:**
> - **Drive-By Download:** Exploits a software vulnerability automatically when a page is loaded. The user does **not** click anything malicious.
> - **Trojan Downloader:** Requires the user to actively download and execute a disguised malicious file.
> 
> The defining feature of a drive-by is its **zero-interaction** or **single-click** nature.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Trojan Website Bakery

Imagine you walk past a bakery on a busy street. You glance at the display window (you "visit" the website). While you are simply looking at the pastries:

- A hidden spray nozzle behind the glass (**exploit code** embedded in the webpage) silently sprays an invisible mist (**malicious payload**) onto your face.
- You inhale it without realizing it (**silent exploitation of browser vulnerability**).
- Hours later, you fall ill (**malware activates and establishes persistence on your system**).
- The bakery looked perfectly clean from the outside — it was either **compromised** by criminals or **deliberately set up** as a trap.

This is precisely how a **drive-by download** operates in the digital world:

| Real-World Analogy Component | Drive-By Download Equivalent |
|------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Walking past the bakery | Loading a webpage in the browser |
| Hidden spray nozzle | Hidden `<iframe>`, JavaScript, or exploit code |
| Invisible mist | Malware payload (RAT, ransomware, spyware) |
| Falling ill later | Malware execution & persistence |
| Clean-looking bakery | Legitimate but compromised website |

---

## 1.3 Categorization of Drive-By Downloads

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Drive-By Downloads are classified into **two major categories** per KTU syllabus standards:

### **A. Malicious Drive-By Downloads**
- The attacker **intentionally hosts** a website engineered to exploit visitors.
- Common in phishing campaigns and malicious landing pages.
- Often paired with **Exploit Kits (EKs)** such as **Angler, Neutrino, RIG, or Magnitude**.

### **B. Non-Malicious (Compromised) Drive-By Downloads**
- A **legitimate, trusted website** has been compromised through SQL injection, XSS, or server-side vulnerabilities.
- The attacker injects malicious code into the legitimate site's HTML/JS.
- Examples: The 2018 **Ticketmaster breach** via a compromised chatbot script; the famous **2017 Newegg attack** via injected Magecart skimming code.
- **Forensic Challenge:** Attribution is harder because the victim was on a trusted domain.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Industry Statistics

> [!NOTE]
> - **~70%** of malicious websites used for drive-by attacks are hosted on **legitimate, compromised servers** (Source: Symantec ISTR).
> - The **average time-to-exploit** after visiting a malicious page is less than **60 seconds**.
> - Java and Adobe Flash were historically the most exploited plugins — together responsible for **over 80%** of exploit kit payloads before their deprecation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🧠 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Anatomy of a Drive-By Attack

A drive-by download attack is not a single event — it is a **multi-stage pipeline**. Each stage performs a specific function in the kill chain.

### 🔹 Stage 1 — Reconnaissance & Lure (Traffic Generation)

The attacker must first lure victims to the malicious landing page. Common delivery channels include:

- **Malvertising** — Malicious advertisements injected into legitimate ad networks (e.g., Yahoo!, Spotify, and the London Stock Exchange have all served malvertising).
- **SEO Poisoning** — Manipulating search engine results to rank malicious pages for trending queries.
- **Compromised Legitimate Sites** — Injecting `<iframe>` tags pointing to exploit kit servers.
- **Spam Email Links** — Hyperlinks disguised as invoices, delivery notices, or social media alerts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Forensic Indicator:** Lure URLs are often URL-shortened (bit.ly, t.co) or use **typosquatting** domains (e.g., `g00gle.com` instead of `google.com`).

### 🔹 Stage 2 — Landing Page Execution (Client-Side Vulnerability Probing)

Once the victim's browser loads the malicious page:

1. The page contains **obfuscated JavaScript** or a hidden `<iframe src="http://attacker.com/exploit">`.
2. A **fingerprinting script** (e.g., `PluginDetect.js`) runs silently to enumerate:
   - Browser vendor and version
   - Operating system (Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, iOS)
   - Installed plugins (Java, Flash, PDF readers, Silverlight)
   - Screen resolution, timezone, and user-agent strings
3. The collected fingerprint is forwarded to a **C2 (Command-and-Control) server** or a **cryptographic payload selector** that picks the appropriate exploit.

### 🔹 Stage 3 — Exploit Delivery (Exploit Kit Invocation)

The matched exploit is served. Historical exploit kits such as **Angler EK** maintained a portfolio of exploits, including:

- **CVE-2014-6332** — Windows OLE Automation Remote Code Execution (Internet Explorer)
- **CVE-2015-5119** — Adobe Flash `ByteArray` Use-After-Free
- **CVE-2016-0189** — Internet Explorer JScript `Array.sort` UAF
- **CVE-2018-8174** — Internet Explorer VBScript Engine (used by FIN7)

### 🔹 Stage 4 — Payload Execution (Shellcode & Dropper)

After successful exploitation:

1. The exploit drops a **shellcode stub** into memory.
2. The shellcode reaches out to a secondary URL to fetch the **final payload** — typically a banking Trojan, ransomware, RAT, or cryptominer.
3. A **dropper or loader** writes the payload to disk, often under a masquerading name (e.g., `svchost.exe` in `%APPDATA%`).
4. **Persistence mechanisms** are installed — registry `Run` keys, scheduled tasks, or services.

### 🔹 Stage 5 — Post-Exploitation (C2 Communication)

The malware contacts its C2 server (via HTTPS, Tor, or DNS tunneling) and awaits commands — data exfiltration, lateral movement, or ransomware deployment.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Stage — Attacker Mindset

| Stage | Attacker's Goal | Why It Matters for Forensics |
|-------|----------------|------------------------------|
| Reconnaissance | Maximize victim count | Helps trace traffic logs and ad-network logs |
| Probing | Avoid wasting exploits | Creates fingerprint artifacts in browser cache |
| Exploit Delivery | Bypass browser sandbox | Leaves memory artifacts; rarely hits disk |
| Payload Execution | Establish persistence | Creates registry, file-system, and event-log traces |
| C2 Communication | Maintain control | Generates network traffic for SOC/NDR analysis |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Analytical Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Forensic Metrics for Drive-By Download Analysis:**

| Metric / Concept | Symbol / Formula | Description |
|------------------|------------------|-------------|
| Infection Probability | $P(I) = P(V) \times P(E \mid V) \times P(P \mid E)$ | Probability of infection = probability of visiting vulnerable site × probability of exploit success × probability of payload execution |
| Time-to-Compromise | $T_c = T_{load} + T_{fp} + T_{exp} + T_{exec}$ | Total compromise time = page load + fingerprinting + exploit + execution |
| Exploit Success Rate | $\rho = \frac{N_{comp}}{N_{visits}}$ | Ratio of compromised systems to total page visits |
| Sandbox Evasion Likelihood | $S_{ev} = 1 - P(\text{detection})$ | Inverse of the probability that the sandbox flags the exploit |
| Browser Exploit Surface | $\Sigma_{browser} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot v_i$ | Weighted sum of vulnerabilities across $n$ browser components, with weights $w_i$ and CVE severity scores $v_i$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical pipes used in symbols like $P(E \mid V)$ use the LaTeX `\mid` delimiter to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Forensic Utility

In cybercrime investigations, drive-by download analysis is used for:

1. **Network Forensics** — Reconstructing victim sessions from proxy/firewall logs to identify the initial lure URL.
2. **Memory Forensics** — Using Volatility to detect **shellcode injections** (e.g., `malfind` plugin identifies injected RWX pages).
3. **Browser Forensics** — Examining **HTTP cache, cookies, history, and Local Storage** in Chrome, Firefox, Edge, and Safari SQLite databases.
4. **Reverse Engineering** — Decoding obfuscated JavaScript using **JSDetox, de4js, or AST injection parsers**.
5. **Indicator of Compromise (IoC) Sharing** — Submitting extracted domains, hashes, and YARA rules to **VirusTotal, MISP, and AlienVault OTX**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Forensic Deduction

## 3.1 Case Scenario Setup (Forensic Walkthrough)

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A corporate SOC analyst receives an alert that **Workstation-07 (IP: 10.10.45.87)** made an outbound HTTPS connection to `185.244.25.91` at **03:14 UTC**. A subsequent **process injection event** was flagged by EDR. You are tasked with determining whether the alert represents a **drive-by download incident** and reconstructing the attack chain.

---

### 🔍 Step 1 — Establishing the Initial Vector from Network Logs

From the **proxy server (Squid) logs**, the analyst extracts the following HTTP access records:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HTTP Log Entry:} \quad & \text{10.10.45.87} \rightarrow \text{www.legalnews-portal.com} \quad \text{GET /} \\
& \text{Referer: https://www.google.com/search?q=tax+filing+2024} \\
& \text{User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64)} \\
& \text{Time: 2024-09-12 03:11:42 UTC}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Analytical Interpretation:**
- The victim **visited a news portal** after a Google search — a normal, expected behavior pattern.
- This rules out **active user-initiated malware downloads** and supports a **drive-by hypothesis**.

---

### 🔍 Step 2 — Detecting the Exploit Page Response

Deep packet inspection of the response from `www.legalnews-portal.com` reveals an injected obfuscated `<iframe>`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Injected Snippet:} \quad & \text{<iframe src="https://185.244.25.91/landing.php?id=8a7f" } \\
& \text{width="0" height="0" frameborder="0"></iframe>
\end{aligned}
$$

**Analytical Interpretation:**
- A **0×0 hidden iframe** is a textbook signature of a drive-by landing page.
- The iframe points to a **PHP-based exploit kit landing endpoint**, which is dynamically fingerprinted.
- The originating site `legalnews-portal.com` is a **compromised legitimate site** (Non-Malicious Drive-By category).

---

### 🔍 Step 3 — Calculating Infection Probability

Using the metric $P(I) = P(V) \times P(E \mid V) \times P(P \mid E)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(V) &= 0.95 \quad \text{(vulnerable site visited — confirmed)} \\
P(E \mid V) &= 0.72 \quad \text{(unpatched IE/Chrome with Flash — from EDR asset data)} \\
P(P \mid E) &= 0.88 \quad \text{(payload server live and responding)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(I) &= 0.95 \times 0.72 \times 0.88 \\
P(I) &= 0.6019 \approx 60.2\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Forensic Conclusion:** A **60.2% probability of compromise** justifies escalating the alert to a **full incident response** rather than dismissing it as a false positive.

---

### 🔍 Step 4 — Memory Forensics Verification

The analyst acquires a memory dump of Workstation-07 using `winpmem` and runs the **Volatility `malfind` plugin**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Process:} \quad & \text{chrome.exe (PID 4012)} \\
\text{Flagged Region:} \quad & \text{0x1f4a0000 - 0x1f4a1000} \quad \text{(RWX page)} \\
\text{Content:} \quad & \text{4D 5A 90 00 03 00 00 00 ... (MZ Header)} \\
\text{Interpretation:} \quad & \text{PE executable injected into browser memory}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Confirmation:** The presence of an **MZ header inside the chrome.exe process memory** confirms that an exploit successfully dropped shellcode into the browser process — a definitive indicator of drive-by exploitation.

---

### 🔍 Step 5 — Persistence Mechanism Identification

Registry analysis reveals the following persistence entry:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Registry Key:} \quad & \text{HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run} \\
\text{Value Name:} \quad & \text{"WindowsUpdateHelper"} \\
\text{Value Data:} \quad & \text{"C:\Users\jdoe\AppData\Roaming\wuauserv.exe"}
\end{aligned}
$$

This masquerading file path (named after the legitimate `wuauserv` Windows Update service) confirms **persistence was established** after the drive-by execution.

---

### 🔍 Step 6 — Calculating Time-to-Compromise

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{load} &= 2.4 \, \text{s} \quad \text{(HTML page load)} \\
T_{fp} &= 1.8 \, \text{s} \quad \text{(browser fingerprinting)} \\
T_{exp} &= 0.6 \, \text{s} \quad \text{(CVE-2018-8174 VBScript exploit)} \\
T_{exec} &= 3.2 \, \text{s} \quad \text{(payload download + execution)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_c &= 2.4 + 1.8 + 0.6 + 3.2 \\
T_c &= 8.0 \, \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The entire compromise occurred in **under 10 seconds** — a classic drive-by execution window. SOCs typically alert on sessions exceeding this threshold for suspicious outbound beacons.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix for Classifying Drive-By Incidents

| Forensic Evidence | Drive-By Signature | Weight (0–1) |
|-------------------|--------------------|--------------|
| Hidden 0×0 iframe in HTML response | **Strong indicator** | 0.85 |
| MZ header in browser process memory | **Definitive indicator** | 0.95 |
| Obfuscated JavaScript with eval/unescape | **Strong indicator** | 0.75 |
| No user download in browser history | **Supports drive-by hypothesis** | 0.70 |
| Registry Run key masquerading as system process | **Post-exploitation indicator** | 0.90 |
| Outbound C2 beacon within 60s of page load | **High correlation** | 0.80 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📊 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Drive-By Attack Kill Chain

This diagram maps the **complete lifecycle of a drive-by download attack**, from the attacker’s infrastructure to victim-side persistence and C2 communication.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Attacker Infrastructure] --> B[Compromise Legitimate Site via SQLi or XSS]
    A --> C[Register Malicious Domain and Host Exploit Kit]
    B --> D[Inject Hidden Iframe into Legitimate Page]
    C --> E[Exploit Kit Landing Page]
    D --> E
    F[Victim Visits Legitimate Site via Search or Ad] --> G[Browser Loads HTML and Fetches Iframe]
    G --> H[Browser Fingerprinting Script]
    H --> I[Fingerprint Sent to Exploit Kit]
    I --> J{Match Known Vulnerability}
    J --> K[CVE 2018 8174 VBScript]
    J --> L[CVE 2014 6332 OLE Automation]
    J --> M[CVE 2015 5119 Flash UAF]
    K --> N[Shellcode Dropped in Memory]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Payload Downloaded from CDN]
    O --> P[Dropper Writes to AppData]
    P --> Q[Registry Persistence Installed]
    Q --> R[C2 Beacon Initiated over HTTPS or Tor]
    R --> S[Attacker Gains Full Control or Deploys Ransomware]
```

---

## 4.2 Forensic Investigation Architecture

This diagram illustrates the **forensic investigative workflow** used by a SOC/DFIR team to reconstruct a drive-by incident.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Network Logs] --> D1[Log Aggregator SIEM]
    A2[Endpoint EDR Telemetry] --> D1
    A3[Memory Dump Volatility] --> D1
    A4[Browser Cache SQLite] --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Indicator Extraction]
    E1 --> E2[URL Analysis and Reputation Lookup]
    E1 --> E3[Hash Lookup on VirusTotal]
    E1 --> E4[JavaScript Deobfuscation]
    E2 --> F1[Timeline Reconstruction]
    E3 --> F1
    E4 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Final Incident Report with IoC Sharing]
```

---

## 4.3 Anatomical Decomposition of an Exploit Kit

This block diagram maps the **internal modules of a classic exploit kit** such as Angler, Neutrino, or RIG.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X1[Landing Module] --> X2[Fingerprint Engine]
    X2 --> X3[Exploit Selector]
    X3 --> X4[Browser Exploits Module]
    X3 --> X5[Plugin Exploits Module]
    X4 --> X6[Shellcode Generator]
    X5 --> X6
    X6 --> X7[Payload Delivery Module]
    X7 --> X8[Banking Trojan]
    X7 --> X9[Ransomware]
    X7 --> X10[RAT]
    X7 --> X11[Cryptominer]
    X6 --> X12[Anti Forensic Module]
    X12 --> X13[Sandbox Evasion]
    X12 --> X14[VM Detection]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)**

### **Q1. Define drive-by download attacks. How are they different from traditional malware downloads?** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **drive-by download** is a cyberattack in which malicious software is automatically downloaded and installed on a victim's system **without any explicit user action or awareness**, simply by visiting a compromised or malicious website. The attack exploits vulnerabilities in the browser, its plugins, or the OS to silently execute a malicious payload.

| Aspect | Drive-By Download | Traditional Malware Download |
|--------|-------------------|------------------------------|
| User Interaction | None (or single click on legitimate site) | Explicit download & execution |
| Delivery Vector | Compromised websites, malvertising | Email attachments, P2P, USB |
| Attack Surface | Browser/Plugin vulnerabilities | Social engineering |
| Detection Difficulty | **Very High** (silent) | Moderate (requires user awareness) |

**(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for differentiation table)**

---

### **Q2. List and briefly explain any THREE common delivery vectors used in drive-by download attacks.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Three common delivery vectors are:**

1. **Malvertising (Malicious Advertising):** Cybercriminals inject malicious code into legitimate online ad networks. When users load a page containing the malicious ad, the payload is delivered automatically.

2. **Compromised Legitimate Websites:** Attackers exploit CMS vulnerabilities (WordPress, Joomla) or inject `<iframe>` tags pointing to malicious exploit kit servers, infecting visitors of otherwise trusted sites.

3. **Exploit Kits:** Pre-packaged toolkits like Angler, Neutrino, or RIG automate the fingerprinting and exploitation process, serving browser/plugin exploits tailored to each victim.

**(3 marks — 1 mark per correctly explained vector)**

---

## **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)**

---

### **🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**With a neat diagram, explain the complete kill chain of a drive-by download attack. Identify each stage and the forensic evidence left at each stage.**

#### **Model Answer:**

> **Kill Chain of a Drive-By Download Attack:**

```
Stage 1: Reconnaissance & Lure (Traffic Generation)
Stage 2: Landing Page (Hidden Iframe / Obfuscated JS)
Stage 3: Browser Fingerprinting
Stage 4: Exploit Delivery via Exploit Kit
Stage 5: Shellcode Injection into Browser Memory
Stage 6: Payload Download & Execution
Stage 7: Persistence (Registry / Scheduled Tasks)
Stage 8: C2 Communication & Post-Exploitation
```

**Forensic Evidence at Each Stage:**

| Stage | Forensic Evidence |
|-------|-------------------|
| Stage 1 | DNS query logs, proxy logs, URL-shortener redirects |
| Stage 2 | HTML source with hidden `<iframe>`, obfuscated JS in browser cache |
| Stage 3 | PluginDetect.js artifacts, HTTP traffic to fingerprint endpoint |
| Stage 4 | Exploit kit landing pages preserved in cache, exploit signatures in IDS logs |
| Stage 5 | MZ header in browser process memory (Volatility `malfind`) |
| Stage 6 | Suspicious binaries in `%APPDATA%`, `%TEMP%` |
| Stage 7 | Registry `Run` keys, scheduled tasks, services |
| Stage 8 | Outbound HTTPS beacons, TLS SNI entries, Tor traffic patterns |

**(7 marks — 3 marks for diagram, 4 marks for stage-wise forensic evidence mapping)**

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Discuss in detail the role of Exploit Kits (EKs) in drive-by download attacks. Name any four famous Exploit Kits and explain their working.**

#### **Model Answer:**

> **Role of Exploit Kits:** Exploit Kits are **automated frameworks** that streamline the entire drive-by attack pipeline — from fingerprinting victims, to selecting appropriate exploits, to delivering final payloads. They eliminate the need for attackers to manually craft exploits for each victim, making large-scale attacks feasible and highly profitable.

**Four Famous Exploit Kits:**

1. **Angler EK** (Active 2014–2016): Pioneered **fileless malware delivery** by injecting payloads directly into memory without writing to disk. Used **CVE-2015-5119** and **CVE-2014-6332** as primary exploits.

2. **Neutrino EK** (Active 2013–2017): A lightweight, Russian-developed kit known for **cryptographic payload selection** to evade sandboxing. Used **CVE-2016-0189** (IE JScript) for exploitation.

3. **RIG EK** (Active 2014–2019): Distributed primarily via **malvertising on adult and streaming sites**. Leveraged **CVE-2018-8174** (IE VBScript) and was a leading distributor of **GandCrab ransomware**.

4. **Magnitude EK** (Active 2014–2020): Specialized in **Asian geofencing**, primarily targeting users in South Korea, Taiwan, and Japan. Used **Flash Player and Silverlight exploits**.

**Working Principle:** EK architecture typically follows a layered modular design:
- **Landing Page:** Fingerprints victim.
- **Exploit Selector:** Picks compatible exploit.
- **Exploits Module:** Delivers browser/plugin exploit.
- **Payload Module:** Drops final malware.

**(7 marks — 2 marks for role explanation, 5 marks for the four EKs with working details)**

---

### **🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the forensic investigation methodology for a suspected drive-by download incident. Include memory, network, and browser forensics techniques.**

#### **Model Answer:**

> **Forensic Investigation Methodology:**

**Phase 1 — Network Forensics:**
- Extract **proxy logs, DNS logs, and NetFlow** records to identify the initial compromised URL.
- Analyze **TLS SNI fields** and certificate metadata to trace C2 infrastructure.
- Correlate **firewall logs** with **IDS/IPS alerts** for known exploit kit signatures.

**Phase 2 — Memory Forensics (Most Critical):**
- Acquire RAM image using **FTK Imager** or **winpmem**.
- Run **Volatility** with plugins:
  - `pslist`, `pstree` — to identify suspicious browser child processes.
  - `malfind` — to detect **RWX memory regions** containing injected PE headers.
  - `netscan` — to find anomalous connections from browser processes.

**Phase 3 — Browser Forensics:**
- Parse **Chrome** (`History`, `Cookies`, `Cache`, `Login Data`) and **Firefox** (`places.sqlite`, `formhistory.sqlite`) SQLite databases.
- Extract **obfuscated JavaScript** from cached responses and decode using **JSDetox**.
- Look for **hidden iframe signatures** in cached HTML.

**Phase 4 — Endpoint Forensics:**
- Examine **Registry Run keys**, scheduled tasks, and WMI event subscriptions.
- Search for **unusual binaries** in `%APPDATA%`, `%LOCALAPPDATA%`, and `%TEMP%`.
- Hash suspicious files and query **VirusTotal / MISP** for known IoCs.

**Phase 5 — Correlation & Reporting:**
- Build a **timeline of compromise** using **Plaso/log2timeline**.
- Generate **YARA rules** for proactive detection across the enterprise.
- Share IoCs via **STIX/TAXII** feeds.

**(7 marks — full marks awarded for covering all five phases with relevant tools)**

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**What are the prevention and mitigation strategies against drive-by download attacks? Suggest best practices for end-users and enterprises.**

#### **Model Answer:**

> **Prevention & Mitigation Strategies:**

**For End-Users:**
1. **Keep browsers and plugins updated** — disable or remove outdated plugins (Flash, Java).
2. **Use script blockers** such as **uBlock Origin** or **NoScript** to prevent unauthorized JavaScript execution.
3. **Enable browser sandboxing** and use **Chromium-based browsers** with Site Isolation enabled.
4. **Avoid clicking suspicious ads** — use reputable ad-blockers.
5. **Deploy reputable endpoint security** with web filtering and behavior-based detection.

**For Enterprises:**
1. **Patch Management:** Implement a strict **patch management policy** with SLAs for critical CVEs.
2. **Browser Hardening:** Enforce **Group Policies** to disable outdated plugins and restrict script execution.
3. **Network Segmentation:** Isolate internet-facing workloads and use **proxy filtering** with URL reputation databases.
4. **Email & Web Filtering:** Deploy **Secure Web Gateways (SWG)** with real-time threat intelligence integration.
5. **Endpoint Detection & Response (EDR):** Use EDR solutions with **memory injection detection** (e.g., CrowdStrike Falcon, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint).
6. **Security Awareness Training:** Conduct phishing & malvertising awareness programs quarterly.
7. **DNS Sinkholing:** Use services like **Cisco Umbrella** or **Quad9** to block known malicious domains.

**(7 marks — 3 marks for end-user strategies, 4 marks for enterprise-level strategies)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse drive-by downloads with phishing.** Phishing requires user interaction (clicking a link); drive-by exploits happen passively.
> 2. **Do NOT omit the memory forensics step.** Many students focus only on disk analysis. Examiners specifically award marks for **Volatility-based memory analysis** in drive-by questions.
> 3. **Always mention exploit kits by name** (Angler, Neutrino, RIG, Magnitude) — generic answers without specific EK references lose marks.
> 4. **Avoid confusing "drive-by" with "drive-by download as a service"** (DBaaS) — modern cloud-based EKs operate on subscription models; mentioning this shows advanced understanding.
> 5. **Always draw a labeled diagram** in 7-mark questions; missing diagrams cost **1.5 to 2 marks** per KTU valuation patterns.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ Drive-by download = **silent malware installation** without explicit user consent.
- ✅ Two categories: **Malicious** (attacker-hosted) vs **Non-Malicious** (compromised legitimate sites).
- ✅ **Exploit Kits** (Angler, Neutrino, RIG, Magnitude) are the backbone of automated drive-by attacks.
- ✅ Hidden `<iframe>` with **0×0 dimensions** is a classic signature in HTML source.
- ✅ Memory forensics is **critical** — MZ headers in browser process memory = definitive proof of exploitation.
- ✅ Persistence indicators: Registry `Run` keys, scheduled tasks, masquerading binaries in `%APPDATA%`.
- ✅ **Average time-to-compromise is under 10 seconds** — SOC alerts should be tuned to this threshold.
- ✅ Browser cache, history, cookies, and LocalStorage are **primary browser forensic artifacts**.
- ✅ Tools to remember: **Volatility** (memory), **Wireshark** (network), **JSDetox** (JS deobfuscation), **MISP/VirusTotal** (IoC sharing).
- ✅ Mitigation = **patching, browser hardening, script blockers, EDR, and security awareness training**.
- ✅ Attackers prefer **malvertising & SEO poisoning** for high-volume victim acquisition.
- ✅ Forensic timeline reconstruction using **Plaso/log2timeline** is the gold standard for incident reporting.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Forensic Analysis Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT602 - CYBER FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Network Forensic Analysis Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 3 - Network Forensic Analysis Tools

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Network Forensic Analysis Tools (NFATs)** are specialized hardware and software systems designed to **capture, record, store, reconstruct, analyze, and interpret** network traffic data for the purpose of investigating security incidents, detecting intrusions, identifying anomalous behavior, and producing legally admissible digital evidence of network-based activities.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCCCT602 - Module 3)**, Network Forensic Analysis Tools constitute the operational backbone of *post-incident* and *real-time* network investigations. They operate at **OSI Layers 2 through 7**, parsing frames, packets, segments, and application-layer payloads to reconstruct communication sessions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU module specifically categorizes NFATs into **Hardware Tools** (e.g., dedicated tap appliances, hardware protocol analyzers) and **Software Tools** (e.g., Wireshark, tcpdump, Nmap, Snort, NetworkMiner). Students must understand the *capture*, *analysis*, and *reporting* phases of these tools.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **highway toll booth system** that does more than just collect money:

- **Cameras on every lane** = packet capture devices (NICs in promiscuous mode)
- **The toll database** = the storage/PCAP repository
- **The control room officer** = the analysis engine that flags suspicious vehicles
- **The traffic replay monitor** = the session reconstruction module

Just as investigators can rewind CCTV footage to trace a getaway car, NFATs let cyber investigators rewind the "digital highway" to see exactly which IP sent malicious traffic, when, and what payload it carried. The **evidence must be tamper-proof** — hence cryptographic hash chaining (SHA-256/MD5) is applied to every captured packet.

### 1.3 Key Metrics in Network Forensics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Used in KTU Board Examinations:**
> - **Throughput (Gbps)** — sustained capture rate of the tool
> - **Packet Loss Rate (%)** — dropped packets / total packets
> - **Mean Time to Detect (MTTD)** — time between intrusion and detection
> - **Mean Time to Respond (MTTR)** — time to contain incident
> - **Storage Capacity (TB/PB)** — archival depth of captured traffic
> - **False Positive Rate (FPR)** — incorrect alert percentage

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of NFATs

Network Forensic Analysis Tools follow a **four-phase operational pipeline**:

1. **Capture Phase** — Raw bits are intercepted from the wire using:
   - **Network TAPs (Test Access Points)** — passive, full-duplex hardware splitters
   - **SPAN/Mirror Ports** — switch-configured copies of traffic
   - **Inline sensors** — devices placed physically in the data path

2. **Storage Phase** — Captured traffic is written to disk in standardized formats:
   - **PCAP / PCAP-NG** (libpcap format)
   - **NetFlow v5/v9 / IPFIX** (flow records)
   - **SPLUNK / ELK** indexed logs

3. **Analysis Phase** — Pattern matching, protocol decoding, and anomaly detection are applied using:
   - **Signature-based engines** (Snort, Suricata)
   - **Statistical anomaly engines** (baseline deviation)
   - **Stateful protocol analysis**

4. **Reporting Phase** — Forensic chain-of-custody documents, timeline reconstruction, and **SHA-256 hash values** are generated for courtroom admissibility.

## 2.2 Classification of NFATs

### A. Software-Based Tools

| Tool | Function | Layer | Open Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wireshark** | Deep packet inspection & reconstruction | 2-7 | Yes |
| **tcpdump** | Command-line packet capture | 2-4 | Yes |
| **Nmap** | Network mapping & port scanning | 3-4 | Yes |
| **Snort** | Signature-based IDS/IPS | 3-7 | Yes |
| **Suricata** | Multi-threaded IDS/IPS | 3-7 | Yes |
| **NetworkMiner** | Passive host & file extraction | 2-7 | Yes (Free) |
| **Xplico** | Application-layer content reconstruction | 7 | Yes |
| **Argus** | Bidirectional flow auditing | 3-4 | Yes |
| **Tcpflow** | TCP stream reassembly | 4 | Yes |
| **Splunk** | SIEM-based log correlation | 7 | Commercial |

### B. Hardware-Based Tools

- **NetWitness / RSA Security Analytics** — enterprise-grade NFAT appliance
- **Solera Networks DS** — deep packet capture (DPC) appliance
- **Niksun NetVCR** — full-packet capture appliance
- **cPacket cVu** — 100 Gbps capture appliance
- **Hardware TAPs** — optical/electrical splitters from Garland, Datacom Systems

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULAS FOR KTU BOARD EXAMS** — Memorize the table below; questions frequently derive numerical values from these relations.

| Formula | Symbol Meaning | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| $S = R \times T \times P$ | $S$ = Storage, $R$ = capture rate, $T$ = time, $P$ = avg packet size | Disk sizing for full-packet capture |
| $L_{pct} = \dfrac{N_{dropped}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | Packet loss percentage | Verifying forensic integrity |
| $H_{integrity} = \text{SHA-256}(P_{i} \,\vert\vert\, H_{i-1})$ | Cryptographic chain hash | Tamper-evident evidence |
| $D_{precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $TP$ = true positives, $FP$ = false positives | Detection quality |
| $R_{recall} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $FN$ = false negatives | Coverage of detection |
| $F1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{D \cdot R}{D + R}$ | Harmonic mean of precision & recall | Tool accuracy metric |
| $T_{reconstruct} = \dfrac{N_{packets}}{C_{rate}}$ | Time to reconstruct a session | Investigation SLA |

**Key Boundary Conditions (KTU Emphasis):**
- **Wireshark libpcap** maximum theoretical capture: **1 Gbps** (default ring buffer)
- **tcpdump** linear write throughput: limited by **disk I/O** (typically 200-500 MB/s on SSD)
- **Promiscuous mode** requirement: NIC must bypass MAC filtering to capture all frames
- **PCAP-NG file** supports multi-interface, nanosecond timestamps, and comments
- **NetFlow v5** records 1 flow entry per 7-tuple: **{SrcIP, DstIP, SrcPort, DstPort, Protocol, ToS, InputIF}**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Incident Response Teams (CSIRTs)** use Wireshark for malware C2 (Command & Control) traffic analysis
- **Law Enforcement (CBI, Interpol)** rely on NFAT appliances for evidence admissible under **Section 65B of Indian Evidence Act**
- **Telecom operators** deploy NFATs at 5G core for lawful interception compliance
- **Data centers** use NetFlow analyzers to detect **DDoS attacks** and **data exfiltration**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Storage Requirement for Full-Packet Capture

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **frequently-asked 7-mark derivation** in KTU University Examinations. Students are often given link bandwidth and asked to compute total storage for a retention window.

### Problem Statement

A network link operates at **$R$ Gbps** with an **average packet size $P$ bytes** captured continuously for **$T$ hours**. Derive the total storage $S$ required assuming no compression.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Convert the link rate to bytes per second:**

$$
R_{Bps} = \dfrac{R \times 10^{9}}{8} \; \text{bytes/second}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* $1 \text{ Gbps} = 10^9$ bits per second; divide by $8$ to convert bits to bytes.

**Step 2 — Express the capture time in seconds:**

$$
T_{sec} = T \times 3600
$$

> *Conversion logic:* $1 \text{ hour} = 3600 \text{ seconds}$.

**Step 3 — Compute total packets captured:**

$$
N_{packets} = \dfrac{R_{Bps} \times T_{sec}}{P}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* dividing total bytes by per-packet size yields packet count.

**Step 4 — Compute total storage required (uncompressed):**

$$
\boxed{S = R_{Bps} \times T_{sec} = \dfrac{R \times 10^{9} \times T \times 3600}{8} \; \text{bytes}}
$$

> *Note:* This assumes **full-packet capture**. If only headers are stored, replace $P$ with the header-size constant.

---

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

A SOC captures traffic on a **1 Gbps** link continuously for **24 hours** with an **average packet size of 800 bytes**. Compute total storage in **TB**.

**Given:**
- $R = 1$ Gbps
- $T = 24$ hours
- $P = 800$ bytes (used only if computing packet count; not needed for pure storage)

**Step 1:** Compute bytes per second:

$$
R_{Bps} = \dfrac{1 \times 10^{9}}{8} = 1.25 \times 10^{8} \; \text{B/s}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute total seconds:

$$
T_{sec} = 24 \times 3600 = 86400 \; \text{s}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute total bytes:

$$
S = 1.25 \times 10^{8} \times 86400 = 1.08 \times 10^{13} \; \text{bytes}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to Terabytes ($1$ TB $= 2^{40}$ bytes $\approx 1.0995 \times 10^{12}$ bytes):

$$
S = \dfrac{1.08 \times 10^{13}}{1.0995 \times 10^{12}} \approx 9.82 \; \text{TB}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $S \approx 9.82$ TB of uncompressed storage is required.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Cryptographic Chain Hash for Evidence Integrity

To prevent tampering, forensic tools chain-hash packets using:

$$
H_{i} = \text{SHA-256}(P_{i} \oplus H_{i-1})
$$

where:
- $P_{i}$ = i-th packet payload
- $H_{i-1}$ = hash of the previous packet
- $H_{0}$ = predefined initialization vector
- $\oplus$ = concatenation operator

**Verification property:** If any packet $P_{j}$ is altered, $H_{j}$ changes, and the chain divergence propagates to $H_{n}$ — making tampering **mathematically detectable** with probability $1 - 2^{-256}$ per bit.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Tool Selection Decision

| Forensic Requirement | Recommended Tool | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Deep packet decoding | Wireshark | GUI + 3000+ dissectors |
| Live capture via SSH | tcpdump | CLI, low overhead |
| Asset discovery | Nmap | Active probing, OS fingerprinting |
| IDS / real-time alerting | Snort / Suricata | Signature matching, inline deployment |
| Passive file carving | NetworkMiner | Reconstructs transferred files without write |
| Flow telemetry | Argus / nfdump | Low-volume summary records |
| Enterprise SIEM | Splunk / ELK | Log correlation, dashboards |
| 10 Gbps+ full capture | Niksun / cPacket | Hardware-accelerated write |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Network Forensic Analysis Pipeline (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Network Link under surveillance] --> B[Hardware TAP or SPAN Port]
    B --> C[Capture Engine - tcpdump or libpcap]
    C --> D[PCAP Storage - Disk Array or NFS]
    D --> E[Analysis Engine - Wireshark or Suricata]
    E --> F[Protocol Decoder - TCP, HTTP, DNS, TLS]
    F --> G[Anomaly Detector - Statistical and Signature]
    G --> H{Threat Identified?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Alert and Incident Response]
    H -- No --> J[Archive and Baseline Update]
    I --> K[Forensic Report with SHA-256 Hash Chain]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Legal Chain of Custody Documentation]
```

## 4.2 Tool Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Network Forensic Analysis Tools] --> B[Software Tools]
    A --> C[Hardware Tools]
    B --> D[Packet Capture - tcpdump, Wireshark, Tcpflow]
    B --> E[Network Mapping - Nmap, Hping3, Masscan]
    B --> F[IDS and IPS - Snort, Suricata, Bro Zeek]
    B --> G[Flow Analysis - Argus, nfdump, SiLK]
    B --> H[SIEM Platforms - Splunk, ELK Stack, IBM QRadar]
    C --> I[TAP Devices - Optical and Electrical Splitters]
    C --> J[DPC Appliances - Niksun, NetWitness, cPacket]
    C --> K[Lawful Interception Platforms - Utimaco, Verint]
```

## 4.3 Network Forensic Investigation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1 - Identify Scope of Incident] --> P2[Step 2 - Secure the Network TAP and SPAN Configuration]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 - Capture PCAP files with chain hashing]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 - Filter traffic by IP, Port, and Protocol]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 - Reconstruct TCP sessions and extract files]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 - Apply IDS rules and signature matching]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7 - Correlate with logs from SIEM]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8 - Generate Forensic Report with Timeline]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9 - Preserve Chain of Custody for Court]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define Network Forensic Analysis Tools (NFATs). List any four examples.**

**Model Answer:**
Network Forensic Analysis Tools are software/hardware systems used to **capture, store, analyze, and reconstruct network traffic** for investigating cyber incidents and producing legally admissible evidence.
Examples: **Wireshark, tcpdump, Snort, NetworkMiner**. *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for examples)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**Differentiate between a Network TAP and a SPAN port. Why is TAP preferred for forensic capture?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Network TAP | SPAN Port |
|---|---|---|
| Nature | Passive hardware splitter | Switch software feature |
| Failure mode | Fail-open (passes traffic) | Fails silently |
| Full-duplex | Yes, dedicated TX and RX | Single direction mirrored |
| Packet integrity | No alteration | May drop/error under load |
| Forensic reliability | **High** | Lower |

> **TAP is preferred** because it provides **lossless, full-duplex, deterministic capture** with no risk of switch CPU saturation. *(3 marks)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each - Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks] Discuss the architecture of Network Forensic Analysis Tools with a neat block diagram. Explain the role of the capture, storage, and analysis phases.

**Model Answer:**

The NFAT architecture is divided into **four sequential phases**:

1. **Capture Phase:** Raw network frames are intercepted through **Network TAPs or SPAN ports**. The NIC operates in **promiscuous mode**, and a capture library (**libpcap on Linux**, **WinPcap/Npcap on Windows**) reads frames directly from the kernel. Frame metadata (timestamp, length, interface index) is appended.

2. **Storage Phase:** Captured frames are persisted in **PCAP/PCAP-NG** format. For long-term retention, files are typically rotated (e.g., 100 MB chunks) and indexed by **flow tuples** for fast retrieval. **Cryptographic chain hashes** are computed at write-time to ensure tamper-evidence.

3. **Analysis Phase:** A **protocol decoder** (eireshark's dissectors, or Suricata's parser) interprets the frame structure across all 7 OSI layers. **Signature-based detection** (Snort rules) and **statistical anomaly detection** are applied. **TCP stream reassembly** reconstructs sessions for payload-level inspection.

4. **Reporting Phase:** The investigator exports **filtered PCAPs**, generates **timeline visualizations**, and produces a **chain-of-custody document** with SHA-256 hashes for each evidence file. *(7 marks — full marks for diagram + 4 phase explanations)*

#### (b) [7 Marks] A network link operates at 2.5 Gbps and is monitored continuously for 8 hours. If the average packet size is 750 bytes, compute (i) total packets captured, and (ii) total storage in TB.

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $R = 2.5$ Gbps, $T = 8$ hours, $P = 750$ bytes.

**Step 1 — Bytes per second:**

$$
R_{Bps} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{9}}{8} = 3.125 \times 10^{8} \; \text{B/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Total seconds:**

$$
T_{sec} = 8 \times 3600 = 28800 \; \text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Total bytes captured:**

$$
S = 3.125 \times 10^{8} \times 28800 = 9.0 \times 10^{12} \; \text{bytes}
$$

**Step 4 — Total packets captured:**

$$
N_{packets} = \dfrac{9.0 \times 10^{12}}{750} = 1.2 \times 10^{10} \; \text{packets}
$$

**Step 5 — Storage in TB** ($1$ TB $\approx 1.0995 \times 10^{12}$ bytes):

$$
S_{TB} = \dfrac{9.0 \times 10^{12}}{1.0995 \times 10^{12}} \approx 8.185 \; \text{TB}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $N_{packets} = 1.2 \times 10^{10}$ packets, $S \approx 8.19$ TB. *(7 marks: 2 for setup, 3 for calculation, 2 for final conversion)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the working of Wireshark and tcpdump as packet capture tools. Compare their features.

**Model Answer:**

**Wireshark** is a **GUI-based, cross-platform packet analyzer** built on the **libpcap** library. It provides:
- **Real-time capture** from live interfaces
- **3,000+ protocol dissectors** (HTTP/2, QUIC, MQTT, gRPC)
- **Display filters** (e.g., `http.request.uri contains "password"`)
- **Follow TCP/UDP/HTTP streams** for session reconstruction
- **VoIP call reconstruction** and **SSL/TLS decryption** (with key log)
- **Statistics & I/O graphs** for traffic pattern analysis

**tcpdump** is a **command-line packet capture tool** optimized for **headless servers and SSH sessions**. It:
- Uses **Berkeley Packet Filter (BPF)** syntax for filtering
- Outputs in **PCAP, PCAP-NG, or ASCII** formats
- Has **minimal CPU/memory footprint**
- Can write rotated files (`-w capture_%Y%m%d_%H%M%S.pcap`)
- Is **scriptable** in bash/Python pipelines

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Wireshark | tcpdump |
|---|---|---|
| Interface | GUI | CLI |
| Memory | High | Low |
| Filtering | Display + capture filters | Capture filters only (BPF) |
| Protocol dissection | 3000+ | Limited |
| Use case | Deep investigation | Live capture, servers |
| Export formats | 20+ | PCAP/PCAP-NG |

*(7 marks: 3 for Wireshark, 2 for tcpdump, 2 for comparison)*

#### (b) [7 Marks] Describe the role of Snort and Suricata as Intrusion Detection Systems. Write a sample Snort rule to detect an ICMP ping sweep.

**Model Answer:**

**Snort** is an open-source signature-based NIDS/NIPS by Cisco. It operates in **sniffer**, **packet logger**, or **NIDS** mode. It uses **rule-based pattern matching** to identify malicious traffic from a community-curated ruleset.

**Suricata** is a high-performance, multi-threaded IDS/IPS developed by OISF. It supports **multi-threaded packet processing**, **Lua scripting**, and **EVE JSON output** for SIEM integration.

**Sample Snort rule to detect ICMP ping sweep:**

```
alert icmp $EXTERNAL_NET any -> $HOME_NET any \
  (msg:"ICMP Ping Sweep Detected"; \
   itype:8; \
   detection_filter:track by_src, count 20, seconds 5; \
   sid:1000001; rev:1;)
```

> **Rule Explanation:**
> - `alert` — generate alert action
> - `icmp ... any -> $HOME_NET any` — match any ICMP from external to internal
> - `itype:8` — match ICMP Echo Request (ping)
> - `detection_filter` — trigger only if **20 pings in 5 seconds** from same source (sweep pattern)
> - `sid:1000001` — unique signature ID; `rev:1` — revision number

*(7 marks: 2 for Snort, 2 for Suricata, 3 for rule + explanation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students frequently **confuse SPAN ports with TAPs** — remember: TAPs are **hardware** and **fail-open**; SPAN is **software** and can drop packets.
> - In derivation problems, do **not forget the conversion $1 \text{ Gbps} = 10^9 / 8$ B/s** — this is the most common 1-mark deduction.
> - For **Snort rules**, students often miss the `detection_filter` clause and write a rule that triggers on a **single ping**, which is incorrect for sweep detection.
> - In storage calculations, **1 TB = $2^{40}$ bytes, NOT $10^{12}$ bytes** — examiners check this distinction.
> - Always mention **chain-of-custody** and **SHA-256 hashing** when discussing forensic admissibility — it's a high-weight concept under NEP 2020 outcomes.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **NFAT = capture + storage + analysis + reporting** — the four-phase pipeline.
- **TAP > SPAN** for forensic integrity; TAPs provide **lossless full-duplex** capture.
- **Promiscuous mode** is mandatory on the capture NIC to bypass MAC filtering.
- **Wireshark** = GUI deep analysis; **tcpdump** = CLI live capture; both rely on **libpcap**.
- **Snort / Suricata** are signature-based NIDS; Suricata is multi-threaded.
- **Nmap** is for **active scanning**; **NetworkMiner** is for **passive** host profiling.
- **Storage formula:** $S = \dfrac{R \times 10^{9} \times T \times 3600}{8}$ bytes for full-packet capture.
- **$1$ TB = $2^{40}$ bytes = $1.0995 \times 10^{12}$ bytes** — use this exact conversion.
- **Chain hash** $H_i = \text{SHA-256}(P_i \oplus H_{i-1})$ guarantees tamper-evidence.
- **NetFlow 7-tuple** = SrcIP, DstIP, SrcPort, DstPort, Protocol, ToS, InputIF.
- **Forensic admissibility** under Indian law requires **Section 65B** certificate + hash values.
- **PCAP-NG** supports multi-interface, nanosecond timestamps, and file comments — preferred modern format.
- **F1-score** is the standard accuracy metric combining precision and recall for IDS evaluation.
- **MTTD and MTTR** are the two key SLA metrics used in SOC operations.
- Always cite **chain-of-custody** in your exam answers — NEP 2020 emphasizes **constitutional and legal compliance** in engineering.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Log Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT602 - CYBER FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Network Log Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Network Log Analysis** is the systematic process of collecting, normalizing, parsing, correlating, and interpreting records (logs) generated by network devices, security appliances, operating systems, and applications to detect malicious activity, reconstruct security incidents, support forensic investigations, and ensure regulatory compliance.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> Network Log Analysis is defined under Module 3 of PCCCT602 (Cyber Forensics) as the process of examining network-based event records to identify Indicators of Compromise (IoCs), reconstruct attack timelines, and produce legally admissible evidence for incident response and post-breach forensic investigation.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, Network Log Analysis falls under the following **Course Outcomes (COs)**:
- **CO3:** Apply forensic investigation techniques on network-based evidence and logs.
- **CO4:** Analyze security incidents using log correlation and event reconstruction.

The discipline operates on three foundational pillars:
1. **Collection** – Capturing logs from heterogeneous sources (firewalls, routers, IDS/IPS, servers, endpoints).
2. **Normalization** – Converting vendor-specific log formats into a unified, machine-parseable schema (e.g., CEF – Common Event Format, LEEF – Log Event Extended Format).
3. **Analysis & Correlation** – Applying rule-based, statistical, and AI/ML techniques to identify patterns indicative of intrusion, data exfiltration, lateral movement, or policy violations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The CCTV Control Room**
> Imagine a large shopping mall with **hundreds of CCTV cameras**, **entry-exit logs**, **POS transaction records**, and **employee biometric logs**. A security supervisor in a control room reviews all these streams *continuously* to detect shoplifting, fraud, or suspicious behavior. The supervisor does not watch every frame — instead, he looks for *patterns* (a person entering, lingering, then leaving without a purchase receipt).

**Network Log Analysis is the digital equivalent of this control room:**
- **CCTV Cameras = Network Devices** (firewalls, IDS, routers) that record every packet, flow, and event.
- **Entry-Exit Register = Authentication Logs** (Active Directory, RADIUS, SSH logs).
- **POS Transactions = Application/Web Server Logs** (Apache/Nginx access logs, database audit trails).
- **Security Supervisor = SOC Analyst / Forensic Investigator** using SIEM tools (Splunk, ELK, QRadar).

Just as the supervisor *correlates* "person entered at 10:15 PM + unusual behavior + no purchase + left at 10:45 PM" to flag shoplifting, a forensic analyst *correlates* "failed login attempts at 02:00 AM + privilege escalation event + outbound connection to suspicious IP" to detect a **brute-force attack followed by data exfiltration**.

## 1.3 Why Network Log Analysis Matters in Cyber Forensics

| **Aspect**              | **Significance in Forensics**                                                                 |
|--------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Evidence Source**      | Logs provide **non-repudiable, time-stamped** records of network activity.                  |
| **Incident Reconstruction** | Enables timeline reconstruction of attacker TTPs (Tactics, Techniques, Procedures).       |
| **Compliance**           | Mandated by **PCI-DSS, HIPAA, GDPR, ISO 27001, SOC 2, NIST 800-92** for audit trails.      |
| **Threat Detection**     | Identifies IoCs, anomalies, and zero-day exploitation patterns.                              |
| **Legal Admissibility**  | Logs with proper chain-of-custody and integrity hashing (SHA-256) are court-admissible.    |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics**
> - **Industry Standard Log Retention:** **6 months hot + 1 year cold** (PCI-DSS mandates 1 year).
> - **NIST Recommendation:** NIST SP 800-92 (Guide to Computer Security Log Management) is the de-facto standard.
> - **Average Enterprise Log Volume:** **10–15 TB/day** (Gartner 2023 estimate for large enterprises).

## 1.4 Types of Network Logs

Network logs can be broadly classified into the following categories:

| **Category**              | **Examples**                                                              | **Forensic Value**                          |
|---------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------|
| **Network Device Logs**   | Router/Switch syslogs, NetFlow, sFlow, IPFIX                              | Traffic flow, routing changes               |
| **Security Appliance Logs** | Firewall (Cisco ASA, pfSense), IDS/IPS (Snort, Suricata)                | Blocked/allowed connections, signatures     |
| **Operating System Logs** | Windows Event Logs, Linux syslog (/var/log/), macOS unified log          | Authentication, process execution           |
| **Application Logs**      | Web server (Apache, Nginx, IIS), Database (MySQL, MSSQL), Email (SMTP)  | User activity, data access                  |
| **Authentication Logs**   | Active Directory, RADIUS, TACACS+, Kerberos, SSH                         | Login attempts, privilege changes           |
| **DNS Logs**              | BIND, Microsoft DNS, dnsmasq                                             | Domain resolution, C2 beaconing              |
| **DHCP Logs**             | ISC DHCP, Windows DHCP Server                                            | IP-MAC assignments, rogue devices           |
| **Proxy/VPN Logs**        | Squid, Blue Coat, OpenVPN, WireGuard                                     | Web browsing, tunneled traffic              |
| **Endpoint Detection (EDR)** | CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Carbon Black                                 | Process trees, file modifications           |
| **Cloud Logs**            | AWS CloudTrail, Azure Activity Log, GCP Cloud Audit Logs                | API calls, resource provisioning            |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Network Log Analysis Lifecycle (NIST SP 800-92 Aligned)

The **NIST SP 800-92** framework defines a six-phase log management lifecycle. KTU examiners frequently test this in Part A questions.

### Phase 1: Log Generation
- Logs are generated by **sources** (devices, applications, OS).
- Each source has a **log format** (syslog RFC 5424, Windows XML Event Log, JSON, CEF).
- **Syslog Severity Levels (RFC 5424):**

| **Code** | **Severity**      | **Description**                          |
|----------|-------------------|------------------------------------------|
| 0        | Emergency         | System is unusable                      |
| 1        | Alert             | Immediate action required               |
| 2        | Critical          | Critical conditions                     |
| 3        | Error             | Error conditions                        |
| 4        | Warning           | Warning conditions                      |
| 5        | Notice            | Normal but significant condition        |
| 6        | Informational     | Informational messages                  |
| 7        | Debug             | Debug-level messages                    |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exams, remember: **Lower numerical value = Higher severity.** Emergency (0) is the most severe.

### Phase 2: Log Storage & Protection
- Logs must be stored with **integrity** (write-once media, WORM drives, cryptographic hashing).
- **Chain of Custody** must be maintained from the moment of collection.
- Common storage: **SIEM databases, ELK stack, cloud storage (S3 with object lock).**

### Phase 3: Log Analysis
This is the **core forensic activity** and comprises:

#### A. **Parsing & Normalization**
- Converting heterogeneous formats (syslog, XML, JSON, CSV) into a unified schema.
- Example fields: `timestamp, source_ip, dest_ip, action, user, event_id, severity`.

#### B. **Correlation**
Combining events from multiple sources to identify related activities.

**Types of Correlation:**

| **Type**                  | **Description**                                                          | **Example**                                                        |
|---------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Rule-Based**            | Predefined correlation rules (e.g., "5 failed logins + 1 success")      | Brute force detection                                             |
| **Statistical**           | Threshold-based anomaly detection                                       | Unusual outbound data volume > 3σ from baseline                   |
| **Temporal**              | Time-window based event sequencing                                      | Login → Privilege escalation within 60 seconds                    |
| **Behavioral (UEBA)**     | User & Entity Behavior Analytics — baseline deviation                  | User accessing server at 03:00 AM when they normally work 9–5     |
| **Cross-Log**             | Correlation across heterogeneous sources                                | Firewall block + IDS alert + AV quarantine on same host           |

#### C. **Anomaly Detection**
- **Signature-based** (known-bad): Matches against IoC feeds (STIX/TAXII).
- **Behavioral-based** (anomaly): Statistical deviation from baseline.

### Phase 4: Log Review & Reporting
- Manual review by analysts for **false positive reduction**.
- Generating executive summaries and technical incident reports.

### Phase 5: Log Retention & Disposal
- Retention policy aligned with regulatory mandates.
- Secure disposal using **NIST SP 800-88** (Clear, Purge, Destroy).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept**                 | **Formula / Definition**                                                                 | **Unit / Notes**                          |
|-----------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| **Log Volume Estimation**   | $V_{daily} = N_{devices} \times E_{events/device/day} \times S_{avg-event-size}$       | Bytes/day                                 |
| **Syslog Severity**         | $S_{value} \in \{0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7\}$ where $0 = \text{Emergency}$                        | RFC 5424                                  |
| **Log Integrity (SHA-256)** | $H_{log} = \text{SHA-256}(L_{content})$                                                  | 256-bit hash, hex-encoded                 |
| **Z-Score Anomaly Score**   | $Z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$                                                            | $\vert Z \vert > 3$ indicates anomaly   |
| **Entropy (Shannon)**       | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i) \log_{2} P(x_i)$                                         | Bits/symbol — detects encrypted C2        |
| **Benford's Law (First Digit)** | $P(d) = \log_{10}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{d}\right),\ d \in \{1,\dots,9\}$              | Detects data fabrication in logs          |
| **Base-Rate Fallacy**       | $\text{PPV} = \dfrac{\text{Sens} \times P}{\text{Sens} \times P + (1-\text{Spec}) \times (1-P)}$ | Bayesian — low PPV even at high accuracy |
| **Pareto (80/20 Rule)**     | $80\%$ of security events originate from $20\%$ of sources                               | Log triage prioritization                 |
| **Log Retention (PCI-DSS)** | $R \geq 365$ days with $\geq 90$ days immediately accessible                            | Compliance metric                         |
| **MTTD (Mean Time to Detect)** | $\text{MTTD} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(T_{detect,i} - T_{occur,i})$                  | SOC efficiency metric                     |
| **MTTR (Mean Time to Respond)** | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(T_{resolve,i} - T_{detect,i})$               | IR efficiency metric                      |
| **False Positive Rate**     | $\text{FPR} = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN}$                                                       | ROC analysis                              |
| **Precision**               | $\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$                                                 | SIEM tuning metric                        |
| **Recall (Sensitivity)**    | $\text{Recall} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$                                                    | Detection coverage metric                 |
| **F1-Score**                | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ | Harmonic mean                            |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Always use `\vert` instead of `|` in formulas to prevent markdown table breakage.

## 2.3 Common Log Formats — Deep Dive

### 2.3.1 Syslog (RFC 5424)
Syslog is the **de-facto standard** for UNIX/Linux and network device logging.

**Structure:**
```
<PRI>VERSION TIMESTAMP HOSTNAME APP-NAME PROCID MSGID STRUCTURED-DATA MSG
```

**Example:**
```
<165>1 2024-12-15T10:23:45.003Z myrouter sshd 1234 - - Failed password for invalid user admin from 192.168.1.50
```

**PRI Decoding:**
- PRI = Facility × 8 + Severity
- Example: PRI 165 = Facility 20 (local4) + Severity 5 (Notice)
- Formula: $F = \lfloor \text{PRI} / 8 \rfloor,\ S = \text{PRI} \mod 8$

### 2.3.2 Windows Event Log
Three primary logs:
1. **System** — OS-level events (driver failures, service starts).
2. **Application** — Application-generated events.
3. **Security** — Authentication, authorization, audit policy changes.

**Key Event IDs for Forensics:**

| **Event ID** | **Description**                                    | **Forensic Significance**           |
|--------------|----------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| 4624         | Successful logon                                   | Valid authentication tracking         |
| 4625         | Failed logon                                       | Brute-force detection                |
| 4648         | Logon with explicit credentials                   | Lateral movement, Pass-the-Hash      |
| 4720         | User account created                               | Persistence / privilege escalation  |
| 4732         | Member added to security-enabled local group      | Privilege escalation                 |
| 1102         | Audit log cleared                                   | Anti-forensics, evidence tampering  |
| 4688         | Process created                                     | Execution tracking                   |
| 4697         | Service installed                                   | Persistence mechanism                |
| 7045         | Service installed (alternative)                   | Persistence mechanism                |

### 2.3.3 Web Server Logs
**Apache Combined Log Format:**
```
127.0.0.1 - frank [10/Oct/2024:13:55:36 -0700] "GET /index.html HTTP/1.1" 200 2326 "http://example.com" "Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0)"
```

**Fields:** IP, ident, user, timestamp, request method/path/HTTP version, status code, bytes, referrer, user-agent.

### 2.3.4 NetFlow (Cisco)
- **NetFlow v5/v9**, **IPFIX** (IETF standard).
- Records: `src_ip, dst_ip, src_port, dst_port, protocol, packets, bytes, start_time, end_time, TCP_flags, TOS, input_if, output_if, next_hop, src_AS, dst_AS, src_mask, dst_mask`.

## 2.4 Log Analysis Methodology (KTU Board Pattern)

**The PICERL Methodology (SANS Institute):**

| **Phase**            | **Activity**                                              | **KTU Relevance**                  |
|----------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| **P**reparation      | Tooling, baseline, log sources inventory                 | 3-mark question                     |
| **I**dentification   | Detect anomalies, trigger IR                             | 7-mark question                     |
| **C**ontainment      | Isolate affected systems, preserve logs                  | 7-mark question                     |
| **E**radication      | Remove threat, patch vulnerabilities                     | Application-level question          |
| **R**ecovery         | Restore services, monitor                                | Application-level question          |
| **L**essons Learned  | Post-incident report, improve detection                  | Module-level takeaway               |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PICERL vs NIST (800-61):** NIST uses 4 phases (Preparation, Detection & Analysis, Containment/Eradication/Recovery, Post-Incident Activity). SANS uses 6. KTU often expects students to **state the phases in order** — memorize both.
