> **Print this checklist and revise 10 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **Web Attack Tests** = Offensive (Pen Testing) + Defensive (Forensic Reconstruction).
- ✅ **OWASP Top 10 (2021)** is the industry standard — memorize codes A01 to A10.
- ✅ **CVSS v3.1 Base Score** = round_up(min(Impact + Exploitability, 10)), range **0.0–10.0**.
- ✅ **SQLi prevention** = parameterized queries, stored procedures, input validation, least-privilege DB accounts.
- ✅ **XSS prevention** = output encoding, Content-Security-Policy headers, HttpOnly cookies.
- ✅ **CSRF prevention** = anti-CSRF tokens, SameSite=Strict cookies, Origin header validation.
- ✅ **LFI/RFI prevention** = allow-list of files, disable `allow_url_include` in PHP.
- ✅ **Brute-force keyspace** = $K^{L}$; entropy = $L \log_{2} K$ bits.
- ✅ **Chain of Custody** = SHA-256 hashing, write-blockers, signed logs, time-synchronized (UTC) evidence.
- ✅ **Forensic evidence sources** = WAF logs, web server logs, DB audit logs, memory dumps, network pcap, file system mtime.
- ✅ **Memory forensics** = Volatility plugin (`vol.py -f mem.dmp windows.netscan`).
- ✅ **Disk forensics** = Autopsy, EnCase, FTK Imager; never work on original evidence.
- ✅ **Log retention** = PCI-DSS mandates **90 days** online + **1 year** archived.
- ✅ **CERT-In (India)** mandates incident reporting within **6 hours** of detection.
- ✅ **Tools to remember** = Burp Suite, OWASP ZAP, Nmap, Nikto, SQLMap, Wireshark, Splunk, Volatility, Autopsy, YARA.
- ✅ **Frameworks** = PTES (testing), OWASP Testing Guide, NIST SP 800-115 (technical testing), ISO 27001 (governance).
- ✅ **Forensic Principle:** *Never modify the original evidence — always work on copies.*
- ✅ **Golden Rule:** *If a vulnerability is not in the OWASP Top 10, justify with a CVSS score.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU examiners love asking *"Compare SQLi vs. XSS"* or *"Differentiate penetration testing vs. vulnerability scanning."* Keep crisp 3-point comparisons ready: *Scope, Depth, Automation level, Reporting depth, Legal implications.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Penetration Testing, Mobile Device Forensics and Internet of Anything, Cloud Forensics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT602 - CYBER FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Penetration Testing, Mobile Device Forensics and Internet of Anything, Cloud Forensics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Penetration Testing

### Formal Academic Definition
**Penetration Testing** (commonly called **Pen Testing** or **Ethical Hacking**) is a systematic, authorized, and methodical simulation of a cyber-attack against a computer system, network, or web application to evaluate its security posture by safely exploiting vulnerabilities. It is a controlled, goal-oriented security assessment governed by an explicit **Rules of Engagement (RoE)** document, scope definition, and a formal **Non-Disclosure Agreement (NDA)** between the tester (often called a *Red Team*) and the system owner.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Penetration testing is a **proactive** security assessment — its outcome is not a "yes/no secure" answer, but a **prioritized risk register** with proof-of-concept (PoC) exploits, business impact ratings, and remediation roadmaps.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Ethical Burglar"
Imagine hiring a **licensed, bonded locksmith** to break into your own house. You give them the keys, the blueprints, the alarm codes (or none of it, depending on the test type), and you say: *"Try to get in, take what you can, and tell me exactly how you did it so I can fix the locks."* That locksmith is your penetration tester. They don't *steal* anything — they *prove* it could be stolen, and they hand you a report. The burglar alarm test is a **Vulnerability Assessment**; the actual break-in simulation is a **Penetration Test**.

### The Three Modes of Penetration Testing

| Mode | What the Tester Knows | Real-World Analogy | Cost / Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Black-Box Testing** | Zero internal knowledge — external attacker view | A stranger casing your house from the street | High (most realistic, most time-consuming) |
| **White-Box Testing** | Full source code, network diagrams, credentials | You give the locksmith the full blueprint and all keys | Lowest (fastest, deepest coverage) |
| **Grey-Box Testing** | Partial knowledge (e.g., a logged-in low-privilege user account) | A delivery guy who has been inside once before | Medium (best ROI for most engagements) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pen Test vs. Red Team Engagement:** A penetration test is *time-boxed* (usually 1–4 weeks) and *scope-limited*. A **Red Team engagement** is a longer (months), *goal-based* adversarial simulation (e.g., "exfiltrate the CEO's email") where the defenders (Blue Team) don't know it's happening.

---

## 1.2 Mobile Device Forensics

### Formal Academic Definition
**Mobile Device Forensics** is a specialized branch of digital forensics that involves the **acquisition, preservation, analysis, and reporting** of digital evidence recovered from mobile devices such as smartphones, tablets, smartwatches, vehicle infotainment systems, and IoT wearables, while maintaining the **forensic integrity** and **chain of custody** of the data in a manner admissible in a court of law.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Hook:** A mobile device is essentially a **federation of evidence sources** — call logs, SMS, GPS, app data (sandboxed databases like SQLite), cloud-synced artifacts, baseband processor data, and even accelerometer/gyroscope logs. The forensic examiner must treat each as a separate evidentiary lane.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Witness Who Knows Too Much"
A mobile phone is like a **digital witness** that sits in your pocket 24×7. It knows:
- **Where you went** (GPS history, cell tower logs, Wi-Fi associations)
- **Who you talked to** (call logs, contacts, messaging apps)
- **What you saw** (photos, videos, screenshots)
- **What you typed** (keyboard caches, autofill, clipboard)
- **What you bought** (Apple Wallet / Google Pay tokens)

The mobile forensic examiner is the **interpreter** who reads the phone's memory in a way that does not alter the witness's testimony. Pulling the battery mid-examination is the equivalent of "threatening the witness."

### Evidence Categories on a Mobile Device
1. **User Data** — contacts, SMS/MMS, call logs, photos, videos, documents
2. **Application Data** — WhatsApp, Telegram, Signal databases, browser history
3. **System Data** — device logs, network connections, installed apps, OS version
4. **Location Data** — GPS coordinates, cell tower IDs (CID/LAC), Wi-Fi BSSIDs
5. **Cloud-Referenced Data** — iCloud, Google Drive cached tokens and synced artifacts
6. **Deleted / Carved Data** — unallocated space, SQLite `WAL` files, cache remnants

---

## 1.3 Internet of Things (IoT) Forensics — "Internet of Anything"

### Formal Academic Definition
**Internet of Things (IoT) Forensics**, sometimes referred to as **Internet of Anything (IoA) Forensics** in next-generation KTU syllabi, is the discipline of identifying, acquiring, preserving, analyzing, and presenting digital evidence from a **highly distributed, heterogeneous, resource-constrained, and often headless** ecosystem of networked "smart" devices, sensors, actuators, gateways, and their associated cloud backends.

> [!NOTE]
> **Terminology Note:** The phrase "Internet of Anything" is a 2024-syllabus evolution of "Internet of Things" — it broadens scope to include **people** (IoP / wearables), **processes** (smart manufacturing), **data** (smart analytics), and **things** (sensors) under one unified forensic umbrella. The acronym **IoT** is retained for the technical domain.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Crime Scene Spread Across 50 Houses"
Traditional computer forensics is investigating **one crime scene** (a single laptop). IoT forensics is investigating a **crime where the victim's refrigerator in house #1, the smart doorbell in house #2, the fitness watch on house #3, and the cloud server in another country are all witnesses** — and they speak different languages, have different memories, and some only wake up when you ask the right question. You have to correlate timestamps across time zones, protocols (Zigbee, BLE, MQTT, CoAP), and jurisdictions.

### The Four Pillars of IoT Forensics

| Pillar | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Device-Level Forensics** | Examining the constrained node itself | A hacked smart thermostat |
| **Network-Level Forensics** | Capturing traffic between device and gateway | Zigbee traffic, MQTT brokers |
| **Cloud/Backend Forensics** | Investigating the server side | AWS IoT Core logs, Azure IoT Hub telemetry |
| **Cross-Layer Correlation** | Stitching evidence across all three layers | Correlating doorbell timestamp ↔ cloud upload ↔ phone notification |

---

## 1.4 Cloud Forensics

### Formal Academic Definition
**Cloud Forensics** is a sub-discipline of digital forensics that deals with the **identification, collection, preservation, examination, and analysis** of digital evidence residing in **cloud computing environments** (public, private, hybrid, or community clouds) delivered through service models such as **Infrastructure-as-a-Service (IaaS)**, **Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS)**, and **Software-as-a-Service (SaaS)**, while respecting the multi-jurisdictional, multi-tenancy, and elastic nature of the cloud.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Anchor:** Unlike traditional forensics where the investigator has **physical possession** of the storage media, cloud forensics operates under a model of **logical possession** — the data may physically reside in a data center in a different legal jurisdiction (e.g., AWS `eu-west-1` in Ireland) and is shared with other tenants on the same physical hardware.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Locked Filing Cabinet in a Foreign Country"
Imagine you stored all your company secrets in a **safety deposit box** in a Swiss bank. The bank owns the box, controls the key, has its own rules about who can open it, and the laws of Switzerland apply. You (the customer) can *logically* access it, but the bank is the **custodian**. Now imagine the police need to investigate. They can't just break in — they have to go through **Mutual Legal Assistance Treaties (MLAT)**, request logs from the bank, and depend on the bank's cooperation. **That is cloud forensics.**

### The Three Service-Model Forensics Matrix

| Service Model | Customer Controls | Provider Controls | Forensic Challenge |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IaaS** (AWS EC2, Azure VM) | OS, applications, data | Hypervisor, physical host, storage fabric | Volatile memory of VMs, ephemeral instances |
| **PaaS** (Heroku, App Engine) | Application code and data | Runtime, OS, middleware, infrastructure | Logging visibility gaps |
| **SaaS** (Gmail, Office 365) | User behavior and uploaded data | All backend infrastructure | API-only access, no disk imaging possible |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Terminology Hook — The "Five Pillars" of Cloud Forensics (per the 2024 syllabus)**: **Identification, Preservation, Collection, Examination, Analysis** — with an additional sixth pillar **Presentation** when the report goes to a court of record.

---

## Physical Constants, Standards & Metrics in Cyber Forensics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Bold Constants & Standards:**
> - **NIST SP 800-115** — *Technical Guide to Information Security Testing and Assessment* (the gold standard for penetration testing methodology).
> - **OWASP Testing Guide v4.2** — *Web application penetration testing bible.*
> - **PTES (Penetration Testing Execution Standard)** — *Industry-standard pen-test lifecycle.*
> - **ISO/IEC 27043:2015** — *International standard for incident investigation and digital forensics.*
> - **ACPO (UK Association of Chief Police Officers) Principles** — *5 principles of digital evidence handling.*
> - **RFC 3227** — *Guidelines for Evidence Collection and Archiving.*
> - **CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System) v3.1** — *0.0 to 10.0 severity scoring for pen-test findings.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Penetration Testing — The Five Phases (PTES Lifecycle)

The Penetration Testing Execution Standard defines a **five-phase** methodology. Each phase has specific deliverables and tool chains.

### Phase 1: Intelligence Gathering (Reconnaissance)
The goal is to collect as much information as possible about the target **before launching any active scan**.

**Passive Reconnaissance** (target is unaware):
- **OSINT (Open-Source Intelligence)** — WHOIS, DNS records, Shodan, Censys, Google Dorking
- **Social Media Profiling** — LinkedIn, GitHub commit history, employee enumeration
- **Certificate Transparency Logs** — crt.sh to find subdomains

**Active Reconnaissance** (target may detect):
- **Network Sweeps** — Nmap ping sweeps, masscan
- **Service Enumeration** — Nmap version detection (`-sV`)
- **Banner Grabbing** — Netcat, Nmap NSE scripts

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** "Differentiate between active and passive reconnaissance" is a recurring 7-mark question. The key distinction: *passive = no packet leaves your machine to the target; active = direct interaction with the target's systems.*

### Phase 2: Threat Modeling & Vulnerability Analysis
Using recon data, the tester builds a **threat model** mapping:
- **Assets** — what is valuable (PII, payment data, intellectual property)
- **Threat Actors** — who would attack (insider, competitor, nation-state, script kiddie)
- **Attack Surfaces** — entry points (web, mobile, network, physical, human)
- **Vulnerabilities** — weaknesses (CVE-listed, zero-day, misconfiguration)

**Vulnerability Scanning Tools:**
- **Nessus**, **Qualys**, **OpenVAS** — automated network vulnerability scanners
- **Burp Suite**, **OWASP ZAP** — web application scanners
- **Nikto** — web server misconfiguration scanner

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vulnerability Assessment ≠ Penetration Test.** A VA produces a *list* of vulnerabilities; a pen test *exploits* them to prove business impact.

### Phase 3: Exploitation (Gaining Access)
This is the "break-in" phase. The tester uses vulnerabilities to gain unauthorized access.

**Common Exploitation Frameworks:**
- **Metasploit Framework** — the de-facto standard, with ~2000+ exploits
- **Cobalt Strike** — commercial red-team framework with advanced C2 (Command & Control)
- **SQLmap** — automated SQL injection
- **Hydra / John the Ripper** — password brute-forcing

**Exploitation Categories:**
1. **Network Exploits** — buffer overflows, protocol-level attacks (SMB, RDP, SSH)
2. **Web Exploits** — SQLi, XSS, CSRF, SSRF, IDOR, RCE, LFI/RFI
3. **Wireless Exploits** — WPA2 KRACK, Evil Twin, WPS Pixie Dust
4. **Social Engineering** — phishing, vishing, USB drop, pretexting
5. **Physical Access** — bad-USB, lockpicking, tailgating

### Phase 4: Post-Exploitation (Maintaining Access & Escalation)
Once inside, the tester:
- **Escalates Privileges** — local privesc (Linux: SUID binaries, Windows: UAC bypass, token impersonation)
- **Pivots** — uses compromised host as a jump box to reach internal networks
- **Establishes Persistence** — backdoors, scheduled tasks, cron jobs, rootkits
- **Lateral Movement** — Pass-the-Hash, Pass-the-Ticket, NTLM relay
- **Data Exfiltration** — demonstrates *what* an attacker could steal (the actual goal of the test)

> [!NOTE]
> **Golden Rule of Post-Exploitation:** **Do not destroy data.** A real attacker would; a *professional* penetration tester documents the path and exits cleanly.

### Phase 5: Reporting & Remediation
The final deliverable is a **penetration test report**, which typically contains:
1. **Executive Summary** — non-technical risk overview for the C-suite
2. **Methodology** — what was tested and how
3. **Findings (vulnerabilities)** — each with: description, risk rating, PoC screenshot, business impact, remediation
4. **Risk Rating Matrix** — Critical / High / Medium / Low / Informational
5. **Remediation Roadmap** — prioritized fix list
6. **Appendices** — raw scan outputs, tool versions, full PoC code

---

## 2.2 CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System) — The KTU High-Yield Formula

The **CVSS v3.1** score is computed from three metric groups. KTU examiners love this formula.

### Base Score Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Impact} &= 6.42 \times \left(1 - \left((1 - C_I) \times (1 - C_A) \times (1 - C_S)\right)\right) \\
\text{Exploitability} &= 8.22 \times A_V \times A_C \times A_I \times A_U \\
\text{BaseScore} &= \begin{cases} \text{roundUp}(\min(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability},\ 10)) & \text{if Impact} > 0 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $C_I, C_A, C_S$ = Confidentiality, Availability, Integrity impact (each 0.0, 0.22, 0.56, or 0.0)
- $A_V, A_C, A_I, A_U$ = Attack Vector, Complexity, Integrity impact, User interaction (each 0.2 to 0.85)

### Severity Rating Scale

| CVSS Score | Severity | Color Code |
|---|---|---|
| **0.0** | None | Grey |
| **0.1 – 3.9** | Low | Yellow |
| **4.0 – 6.9** | Medium | Orange |
| **7.0 – 8.9** | High | Red |
| **9.0 – 10.0** | Critical | Dark Red |

---

## 2.3 Mobile Device Forensics — Acquisition Pyramid

The **forensic acquisition pyramid** defines four levels of increasing invasiveness and data depth:

```
                    [Level 4: Cloud Acquisition]
                  /         (e.g., iCloud, Google)  \
                 /   Cross-device synced artifacts    \
              [Level 3: Physical / Chip-Off]
            /     [JTA, ISP, eMMC dump]                \
          /     Bypasses locks, requires expertise      \
      [Level 2: File System (Logical)]
    /    [File-level access via ADB, iTunes backup]     \
   /     Sees app data, SQLite databases, media         \
[Level 1: Manual (User-Level)]
Direct on-screen viewing, screenshots, photos
```

### The Logical vs. Physical Acquisition Distinction

| Aspect | Logical Acquisition | Physical Acquisition |
|---|---|---|
| **Method** | File-system level copy via API | Bit-for-bit copy of flash memory |
| **Deleted Data** | Not recovered | Recovered from unallocated space |
| **Tools** | Cellebrite UFED Logical, XRY Logical, iTunes backup | Cellebrite UFED Physical, XRY Physical, Chip-off |
| **Time** | Minutes to hours | Hours to days |
| **Skill** | Low–Medium | High (chip-off requires soldering) |

---

## 2.4 IoT Forensics — The Three-Layer Model

IoT forensics operates across three layers, each with distinct challenges:

### Layer 1: Perception / Device Layer
- **Sensors and actuators** — generate raw data
- **Challenges** — limited storage (often 32–256 KB), no screen, proprietary firmware
- **Forensic Tools** — JTAG, UART, SPI flash readers, Chip-off

### Layer 2: Network / Communication Layer
- **Gateways, brokers, RF protocols** — transports data
- **Protocols** — MQTT, CoAP, Zigbee, Z-Wave, BLE, LoRaWAN, 6LoWPAN
- **Forensic Action** — packet capture on gateway (tcpdump, Wireshark), broker log analysis

### Layer 3: Application / Cloud Layer
- **Cloud backends, analytics, dashboards** — stores processed data
- **Forensic Action** — subpoena / MLAT request to cloud provider, REST API log extraction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The IoT Forensics Chain-of-Custody Problem:** Evidence in IoT is **distributed, ephemeral, and non-replicable**. A sensor may sample data every 100 ms and overwrite its buffer in 30 seconds. The forensic window is tiny.

---

## 2.5 Cloud Forensics — Challenges Matrix

Cloud forensics has **six primary challenges** (per Ruan et al., 2011 — the foundational KTU-referenced paper):

| # | Challenge | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Data Volatility** | Cloud data is elastic and ephemeral — VM instances can be terminated and disks wiped in minutes. |
| 2 | **Multi-tenancy** | Multiple customers share the same physical hardware. Risk of cross-tenant data leakage. |
| 3 | **Jurisdictional Issues** | Data may reside in a different country with different privacy laws (GDPR, IT Act 2000). |
| 4 | **Lack of Physical Access** | Investigator cannot physically seize the server or disk. |
| 5 | **Chain of Custody** | SLA-bound response times; logs may roll over before preservation. |
| 6 | **Decentralization** | Single "incident" may span 12 services in 3 regions. |

### Service-Model Forensics Capability Matrix

| Forensic Action | IaaS | PaaS | SaaS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Disk Imaging** | Possible (snapshot the volume) | Limited (depends on platform) | **Not possible** |
| **Memory Dump** | Possible (with admin rights) | Not possible | Not possible |
| **Log Access** | Full (syslog, app logs) | Partial (platform logs only) | Limited (audit logs via API) |
| **Network Forensics** | VPC flow logs, packet capture | Limited | Not possible |
| **Legal Request** | CSP SLA response | CSP SLA response | CSP SLA response |

---

## 2.6 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA / CHEAT SHEET

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **CVSS Base Score** | $\text{roundUp}(\min(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability},\ 10))$ | Risk rating of a pen-test finding |
| **CVSS Severity** | Score ranges 0.0–10.0 → None/Low/Medium/High/Critical | Categorization of vulnerabilities |
| **Risk** | $\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$ | Risk-based prioritization |
| **Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE)** | $\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}$ | Cost-benefit analysis for pen-test investment |
| **Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)** | $\text{SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor}$ | Per-incident loss |
| **Annualized Rate of Occurrence (ARO)** | Estimated yearly frequency of threat | Threat frequency |
| **Forensic Hash Verification** | $\text{SHA-256(evidence)} = \text{SHA-256(original)}$ | Chain of custody integrity |
| **Carrier Signal Strength (RSSI to Distance)** | $\text{FSPL (dB)} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)$ | Estimating device distance from access point |
| **Hash Collision Probability (Birthday)** | $P(\text{collision}) \approx 1 - e^{-n^2 / (2 \times 2^k)}$ | MD5/SHA-1 vulnerability assessment |
| **Moore's Law (Storage)** | $\text{Storage} \times 2 \text{ every 18 months}$ | Reasoning about evidence scale |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where $\vert$ appears in math (e.g., $\vert x \vert$), always use the LaTeX command \vert or \mid to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.**

---

## 2.7 Engineering Real-World Utility

**Penetration Testing** is a **mandatory regulatory requirement** in:
- **PCI-DSS v4.0** (Payment Card Industry) — quarterly external + annual internal pen tests
- **HIPAA** (US healthcare) — risk analysis required
- **RBI Cyber Security Framework** (Indian banking) — mandatory for all scheduled banks
- **ISO 27001 Annex A.12.6.1** — independent pen testing recommended

**Mobile Forensics** is critical in:
- **Criminal investigations** — drug trafficking, kidnapping, homicide (location history is a "silent witness")
- **Corporate eDiscovery** — employee misconduct, IP theft via personal devices (BYOD)
- **Counter-terrorism** — analyzing encrypted chat apps

**IoT Forensics** is essential for:
- **Smart-home crime scenes** — Alexa/Google Home recordings, smart doorbell video
- **Industrial espionage** — compromised SCADA/ICS sensors
- **Autonomous vehicle accidents** — Tesla/vehicle data recorders

**Cloud Forensics** is foundational for:
- **Cross-border cybercrime** — email investigations in Office 365
- **Ransomware response** — recovering data from compromised cloud storage
- **Insider threat** — SaaS audit log analysis

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: CVSS Base Score — Worked Example

**Problem:** A vulnerability in Apache Log4j (Log4Shell) has the following CVSS v3.1 metrics:
- Attack Vector: Network (AV:N = 0.85)
- Attack Complexity: Low (AC:L = 0.77)
- Privileges Required: None (PR:N = 0.85)
- User Interaction: None (UI:N = 0.85)
- Confidentiality Impact: High (C:H = 0.56)
- Integrity Impact: High (I:H = 0.56)
- Availability Impact: High (A:H = 0.56)

**Step 1 — Compute the Impact Sub-Score (ISS):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ISS} &= 1 - \left[(1 - C_I) \times (1 - C_A) \times (1 - C_S)\right] \\
&= 1 - \left[(1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0.56) \times (1 - 0.56)\right] \\
&= 1 - \left[0.44 \times 0.44 \times 0.44\right] \\
&= 1 - \left[0.085184\right] \\
&= 0.914816
\end{aligned}
$$

> Here, $C_I = 0.56$ (Confidentiality High), $C_A = 0.56$ (Integrity High), $C_S = 0.56$ (Availability High).

**Step 2 — Compute the Impact:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Impact} &= 6.42 \times \text{ISS} \\
&= 6.42 \times 0.914816 \\
&= 5.8731
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Exploitability Sub-Score:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Exploitability} &= 8.22 \times A_V \times A_C \times P_R \times U_I \\
&= 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85 \\
&= 8.22 \times 0.4734 \\
&= 3.887
\end{aligned}
$$

> Where $A_V = 0.85$ (Network), $A_C = 0.77$ (Low), $P_R = 0.85$ (None), $U_I = 0.85$ (None).

**Step 4 — Compute the Base Score:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BaseScore} &= \text{roundUp}\left(\min(\text{Impact} + \text{Exploitability},\ 10)\right) \\
&= \text{roundUp}\left(\min(5.8731 + 3.887,\ 10)\right) \\
&= \text{roundUp}\left(\min(9.7601,\ 10)\right) \\
&= \text{roundUp}(9.7601) \\
&= 10.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The vulnerability receives a CVSS score of **10.0 (Critical)** — the maximum possible. This matches the real-world Log4Shell classification.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE) for Pen-Test Investment

**Problem:** A bank is considering a ₹50 lakh penetration test. The asset value is ₹10 crore, the exposure factor for a breach is 30%, and the ARO (annual rate of occurrence) is estimated at 0.5 (one breach every 2 years). Should they invest?

**Step 1 — Compute the Single Loss Expectancy (SLE):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SLE} &= \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor} \\
&= \text{₹}10{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.30 \\
&= \text{₹}3{,}00{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is the expected loss from a single breach event — three crore rupees.

**Step 2 — Compute the Annualized Loss Expectancy (ALE):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALE} &= \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO} \\
&= \text{₹}3{,}00{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 \\
&= \text{₹}1{,}50{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

> The bank is expected to lose ₹1.5 crore per year *without* a pen test.

**Step 3 — Compute the Return on Security Investment (ROSI):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ROSI} &= \frac{\text{ALE} - \text{Cost of Control}}{\text{Cost of Control}} \\
&= \frac{\text{₹}1{,}50{,}00{,}000 - \text{₹}50{,}00{,}000}{\text{₹}50{,}00{,}000} \\
&= \frac{\text{₹}1{,}00{,}00{,}000}{\text{₹}50{,}00{,}000} \\
&= 2.0 = 200\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> Every ₹1 invested in the pen test returns ₹2 in expected avoided losses.

**Decision:** Invest in the pen test.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Forensic Image Hash Chain Verification

**Problem:** An investigator takes a SHA-256 hash of an evidence disk before and after acquisition. The original hash is `a3f5...c9d2` and the post-acquisition hash is `a3f5...c9d2`. Is the evidence admissible?

**Step 1 — Original Hash:**

$$
H_1 = \text{SHA-256}(\text{original.IMG}) = a3f5b2e1c4d9f8a7b6e5d4c3b2a1f0e9d8c7b6a5f4e3d2c1b0a9f8e7d6c5b4a3 \text{ (truncated to } a3f5...c9d2\text{)}
$$

**Step 2 — Acquired Image Hash:**

$$
H_2 = \text{SHA-256}(\text{acquired.IMG}) = a3f5b2e1c4d9f8a7b6e5d4c3b2a1f0e9d8c7b6a5f4e3d2c1b0a9f8e7d6c5b4a3 \text{ (truncated to } a3f5...c9d2\text{)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Integrity Check} &= H_1 \stackrel{?}{=} H_2 \\
a3f5\ldots c9d2 &\stackrel{\checkmark}{=} a3f5\ldots c9d2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** Identical. The acquisition is **bit-for-bit** verified. The evidence is **admissible** under Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act (now Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam 2023) and ACPO Principle 1.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hash Collision Probability** for SHA-256 is astronomically small — $2^{-128}$ — so a match is cryptographic proof of identical content.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) for Mobile/IoT Forensics

**Problem:** A Wi-Fi access point logged a client device with an RSSI of $-60$ dBm at $2.4$ GHz. Estimate the maximum distance.

**Step 1 — Recall FSPL Formula:**

$$
\text{FSPL (dB)} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)
$$

> Where $d$ = distance in meters, $f$ = frequency in Hz, $c$ = $3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

**Step 2 — Substitute RSSI as approximation for FSPL:**

For an estimate, assume $\text{FSPL} \approx 60$ dB (the RSSI magnitude).

**Step 3 — Compute the constant term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
20\log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right) &= 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi}{3 \times 10^8}\right) \\
&= 20\log_{10}(4.189 \times 10^{-8}) \\
&= 20 \times (-7.378) \\
&= -147.56
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $20\log_{10}(f)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
20\log_{10}(2.4 \times 10^9) &= 20 \times 9.380 \\
&= 187.60
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $20\log_{10}(d)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
60 &= 20\log_{10}(d) + 187.60 - 147.56 \\
60 &= 20\log_{10}(d) + 40.04 \\
20\log_{10}(d) &= 19.96 \\
\log_{10}(d) &= 0.998 \\
d &\approx 10^{0.998} \approx 9.95 \text{ meters}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The device was approximately **10 meters** from the access point — within the same room or adjacent room.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Cloud Forensic SLA Response Time Compliance Check

**Problem:** An incident occurs in AWS at 14:00:00 UTC. The forensic team requests logs at 14:05:00 UTC. AWS SLA guarantees log preservation request response within 4 hours for `us-east-1`. Will the evidence survive?

**Step 1 — Identify the SLA window:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{deadline}} &= T_{\text{request}} + T_{\text{SLA}} \\
&= 14{:}05{:}00 + 4\text{ hours} \\
&= 18{:}05{:}00 \text{ UTC}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the log retention window:**

AWS CloudTrail logs are retained for **90 days** by default, but the log **availability** depends on the account-level configuration. If a S3 lifecycle policy deletes logs after 7 days:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{retention deadline}} &= T_{\text{incident}} + 7\text{ days} \\
&= 14{:}00{:}00 \text{ (Day 0)} + 7\text{ days} \\
&= 14{:}00{:}00 \text{ (Day 7)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{deadline}} = 18{:}05{:}00 \text{ (same day)} &< T_{\text{retention deadline}} = 14{:}00{:}00 \text{ (Day 7)} \\
\Rightarrow \text{Evidence is preserved if requested within SLA.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The forensic team has ample time — but the **real** risk is *not* the SLA, but the **S3 lifecycle policy** auto-deleting the logs at Day 7. Best practice: **request preservation immediately** and **freeze the S3 lifecycle policy** for the investigation.

```mermaid
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8

    classDef cloudPhase fill:#8b4513,stroke:#5a2d0c,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff,font-weight:bold
    classDef cloudSub fill:#fdf0e0,stroke:#8b4513,stroke-width:1px,color:#5a2d0c
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Step **P3 (CSP Legal Request)** is the most time-consuming — it may take 24 hours to 90 days depending on jurisdiction and the CSP's legal process (subpoena, court order, MLAT).

---

## 4.5 Comparative Block Diagram: Forensics Across All Four Domains

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PEN["PENETRATION TESTING"]
        PEN1["Target: Network / App"]:::penNode
        PEN2["Output: Vulnerability List + PoC"]:::penNode
    end

    subgraph MOB["MOBILE FORENSICS"]
        MOB1["Target: Smartphone / Tablet"]:::mobNode
        MOB2["Output: Call Log, SMS, App Data"]:::mobNode
    end

    subgraph IOT["IoT FORENSICS"]
        IOT1["Target: Sensor / Gateway / Cloud"]:::iotNode
        IOT2["Output: Telemetry Logs, RF Captures"]:::iotNode
    end

    subgraph CLD["CLOUD FORENSICS"]
        CLD1["Target: IaaS / PaaS / SaaS"]:::cldNode
        CLD2["Output: API Logs, Snapshots, VM Dumps"]:::cldNode
    end

    PEN1 --> PEN2
    MOB1 --> MOB2
    IOT1 --> IOT2
    CLD1 --> CLD2

    PEN --> MOB
    MOB --> IOT
    IOT --> CLD

    classDef penNode fill:#1a3d6d,stroke:#0a2540,color:#ffffff
    classDef mobNode fill:#2c5f2d,stroke:#1a3d1a,color:#ffffff
    classDef iotNode fill:#5d3a8e,stroke:#2d1a4e,color:#ffffff
    classDef cldNode fill:#8b4513,stroke:#5a2d0c,color:#ffffff
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The arrows between subgraphs show the **modern convergence** — a single investigation may span all four domains (e.g., a phishing attack originates in email → SaaS cloud, is delivered to a mobile device, triggers an IoT smart-home action, and exfiltrates via the corporate IaaS network).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Penetration Testing and Vulnerability Assessment. List any four phases of penetration testing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Vulnerability Assessment | Penetration Testing |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Identify *all* known vulnerabilities | Identify *exploitable* vulnerabilities |
| **Depth** | Surface-level scan, automated | Deep, manual exploitation |
| **Output** | List of CVEs with severity scores | Exploited paths with business impact |
| **Frequency** | Continuous / quarterly | Annual / bi-annual |
| **Approach** | Breadth-first | Depth-first |

**Four phases of penetration testing (PTES):**
1. **Intelligence Gathering (Reconnaissance)** — collect target information
2. **Threat Modeling** — map assets, threats, attack surfaces
3. **Exploitation** — use vulnerabilities to gain access
4. **Reporting** — document findings, risk, and remediation

> **Mark split:** 1 mark for table comparison + 2 marks for phases.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Explain the concept of Cloud Forensics. List any three challenges in cloud forensics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Cloud Forensics** is the application of digital forensic principles to data, applications, and infrastructure hosted on cloud computing platforms. It involves the identification, preservation, collection, examination, and analysis of digital evidence across **IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS** service models delivered over public, private, hybrid, or community clouds.

**Three challenges:**
1. **Data Volatility** — elastic cloud resources can be terminated and wiped in minutes.
2. **Lack of Physical Access** — investigator cannot seize the physical disk/server.
3. **Multi-Jurisdictional Issues** — data in foreign data centers is subject to foreign laws (GDPR, US CLOUD Act).
4. **Multi-tenancy** — evidence may be co-mingled with other tenants on the same hardware.
5. **Chain of Custody Gaps** — CSP SLA response times may exceed log retention windows.

> **Mark split:** 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for challenges (with brief explanation).

---

## PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 MARKS, MODULE INTERNAL CHOICE)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **five phases of penetration testing** as defined by PTES, with the tools used in each phase. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A company wants to evaluate the security of its public-facing web application. Design a **grey-box penetration test plan** covering scope, methodology, deliverables, and legal considerations. **(7 marks)**

---

### **SOLUTION — Question A**

#### (a) Five Phases of PTES (7 marks)

**Phase 1 — Intelligence Gathering (Reconnaissance):**
The tester collects information about the target to plan the attack.

- **Passive:** WHOIS, DNS enumeration, Shodan, Google Dorking, social media OSINT
- **Active:** Nmap ping sweep (`nmap -sn 10.0.0.0/24`), service version detection (`nmap -sV`), banner grabbing
- **Tools:** Maltego, recon-ng, SpiderFoot, theHarvester

**Phase 2 — Threat Modeling & Vulnerability Analysis:**
The tester maps the attack surface and prioritizes targets.
- Identify **assets, threat actors, attack vectors, vulnerabilities**
- Run automated vulnerability scanners: **Nessus, Qualys, OpenVAS, Nikto, OWASP ZAP**
- Cross-reference findings with **CVE / NVD database** and CVSS scores

**Phase 3 — Exploitation:**
The tester attempts to *exploit* identified vulnerabilities.
- **Network exploits:** Metasploit, Cobalt Strike
- **Web exploits:** Burp Suite, SQLmap, XSStrike
- **Wireless:** Aircrack-ng, Wifite
- **Social engineering:** GoPhish, SET (Social Engineering Toolkit)

**Phase 4 — Post-Exploitation:**
After gaining initial access, the tester escalates and demonstrates impact.
- **Privilege escalation:** LinPEAS, WinPEAS, PowerUp
- **Lateral movement:** CrackMapExec, BloodHound
- **Persistence:** scheduled tasks, cron, rootkits
- **Data exfiltration:** demonstration of sensitive data extraction (without actual theft)

**Phase 5 — Reporting:**
The final deliverable is a comprehensive report.
- **Executive summary** for management
- **Technical findings** with PoC, CVSS, business impact
- **Remediation roadmap** with prioritized fixes
- **Appendices:** raw outputs, tool versions, evidence hashes

> **Mark allocation (7 marks):** ~1.4 marks per phase (definition + tools).

---

#### (b) Grey-Box Web Application Penetration Test Plan (7 marks)

**1. Scope Definition (2 marks):**
- **In-scope:** `https://app.company.com` — login portal, user dashboard, payment module, REST API endpoints
- **Out-of-scope:** production database direct access, third-party payment gateway, denial-of-service testing, social engineering of employees
- **Tester credentials:** one standard user account + one admin user account (provided by client)

**2. Methodology (2 marks):**
- **Standards followed:** OWASP Testing Guide v4.2, NIST SP 800-115, PTES
- **Test type:** Grey-box — tester has valid user credentials
- **Approach:** manual testing + automated scanning (Burp Suite Pro + OWASP ZAP)
- **Test areas:** authentication, session management, access control, input validation, business logic, cryptographic storage, error handling
- **Duration:** 10 working days (2 days recon, 3 days testing, 2 days exploitation, 1 day reporting, 2 days re-test after fixes)

**3. Deliverables (1.5 marks):**
- Daily progress report to the security point-of-contact
- Vulnerability findings with: title, description, affected URL, CVSS v3.1 score, PoC screenshots, business impact, remediation steps
- Final report in PDF (executive + technical sections) and JSON export for SIEM ingestion
- Re-test report after remediation

**4. Legal & Compliance Considerations (1.5 marks):**
- **Signed Master Service Agreement (MSA)** and **Statement of Work (SOW)** before any testing
- **Rules of Engagement (RoE)** document specifying: scope, testing window, emergency contacts, allowed techniques
- **Non-Disclosure Agreement (NDA)** — confidentiality of findings
- **Authorization letter** — explicit written permission from the CEO/CTO
- Compliance with: **IT Act 2000 / Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023**, **CERT-In Directions 2022** (incident reporting), **GDPR** (if EU users), and the **client's acceptable use policy**
- **Data handling:** no real customer data to be exfiltrated; use test data only; secure destruction of findings post-delivery

> **Mark allocation:** Scope (2) + Methodology (2) + Deliverables (1.5) + Legal (1.5)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **three-layer model of IoT forensics** with the challenges at each layer. List four tools used in IoT forensic investigations. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** A suspect's mobile phone is seized during a narcotics investigation. The phone is locked with a 6-digit PIN. Describe the **complete forensic acquisition process** — from device isolation to chain-of-custody documentation — covering the tools and techniques used. **(7 marks)**

---

### **SOLUTION — Question B**

#### (a) Three-Layer Model of IoT Forensics (7 marks)

**Layer 1 — Perception / Device Layer:**
- **Components:** sensors, actuators, embedded microcontrollers (e.g., ESP32, Arduino, Raspberry Pi)
- **Forensic evidence:** firmware, sensor logs, EEPROM/flash data
- **Challenges:** **constrained resources** (32–256 KB RAM), no display, proprietary firmware, **volatile memory** cleared on power loss, no standardized file system
- **Acquisition methods:** JTAG, UART serial console, SPI flash chip-off, ISP (In-System Programming)

**Layer 2 — Network / Communication Layer:**
- **Components:** gateways, brokers, RF modules
- **Protocols:** Zigbee, Z-Wave, BLE, LoRaWAN, MQTT, CoAP, 6LoWPAN
- **Forensic evidence:** packet captures, broker logs, association tables
- **Challenges:** **encrypted radio traffic** (WPA2, BLE pairing), proprietary protocols, **lack of timestamps** in some protocols, **time synchronization** across heterogeneous devices

**Layer 3 — Cloud / Application Layer:**
- **Components:** IoT cloud platforms (AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub, Google Cloud IoT)
- **Forensic evidence:** telemetry streams, device shadow states, rule engine logs, user activity logs
- **Challenges:** **jurisdictional barriers** for data access, **multi-tenancy** of cloud storage, **API rate limits**, dependency on CSP's cooperation, **SLA delays**

**Four Tools Used in IoT Forensics (from anywhere in the model):**
1. **Wireshark** — packet capture and protocol analysis
2. **tcpdump** — command-line packet capture on gateways
3. **Cellebrite UFED / XRY** — mobile-side evidence (when phone is the IoT controller)
4. **Binwalk** — firmware extraction and analysis
5. **Foremost / Scalpel** — file carving from raw flash dumps
6. **Volatility** — memory analysis of gateways running Linux

> **Mark allocation (7 marks):** ~1.5 marks per layer + 1 mark for tools.

---

#### (b) Mobile Forensic Acquisition of a PIN-Locked Phone (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Scene Safety & Legal Authority (0.5 marks):**
- Obtain **search warrant** under Section 165 CrPC / Section 27 of the Indian Telegraph Act (or applicable law)
- Wear anti-static gloves; photograph the device in situ (screen, surroundings)

**Step 2 — Network Isolation (0.5 marks):**
- Place the device in a **Faraday bag** (or activate airplane mode within 30 seconds) to **prevent remote wipe** via iCloud / Android Device Manager
- Document: time of isolation, signal bars before isolation, battery percentage

**Step 3 — Device Documentation (0.5 marks):**
- Photograph: make, model, IMEI, serial number, condition of screen
- Record: time of seizure, location, seizing officer, witness present
- Fill **evidence bag tag** with unique case number

**Step 4 — Bypass / Unlock Attempt (1.5 marks):**
- **Try the PIN provided by the suspect** first (if offered)
- If the device is locked, options depend on OS and iOS/Android version:
  - **iOS:** use **Cellebrite UFED** or **GrayKey** for authorized unlock; for older iOS, use the **UFED Physical Analyzer** with the lock-down exploit
  - **Android:** use **Cellebrite UFED** for chipset exploit; on older Android (pre-5.0), use **ADB debugging** if USB debugging is enabled
- **Document every attempt** — failed attempts may erase the device (Android 5+ enforces Factory Reset Protection after 10 failed attempts)

**Step 5 — Acquisition (1.5 marks):**
- **Logical acquisition** using Cellebrite UFED — extracts SMS, call logs, contacts, photos, app data (WhatsApp, etc.)
- **File-system acquisition** — deeper access via Cellebrite Advanced Logical
- **Physical acquisition** (preferred) — bit-for-bit copy of flash via Cellebrite Physical Analyzer or chip-off
- **Hash the image** with SHA-256 immediately after acquisition:

$$
H_{\text{image}} = \text{SHA-256}(\text{acquired\_phone.img})
$$

- Re-hash after analysis to verify integrity

**Step 6 — Analysis (1.5 marks):**
- Parse the image with **Cellebrite Physical Analyzer**, **Autopsy**, or **Magnet AXIOM**
- Recover: call logs, SMS/MMS, contacts, photos/videos, app databases (SQLite), location history, browser history, deleted files (from unallocated space), WhatsApp `msgstore.db` (with decryption key)
- **Timeline analysis** — correlate events with the narcotics activity timeline

**Step 7 — Chain of Custody Documentation (1 mark):**
- Maintain a **chain-of-custody form** with: case number, exhibit number, date/time, person handling, action performed, hash before and after
- Sign and witness at every transfer
- Store the original device in a **secure evidence locker** with controlled access
- Generate the **final forensic report** with: methodology, tools (with version), findings, interpretations, supporting screenshots, hash values, and examiner's signature

> **Mark allocation:** Steps 1–4 (3 marks) + Steps 5–6 (3 marks) + Step 7 (1 mark)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "Vulnerability Assessment" with "Penetration Testing"** — they are *not* the same. VA = list, PT = exploit + impact.
> 2. **Skipping legal/authorization discussion** in pen-test plans — under IT Act 2000 (now BNS 2023), unauthorized access is a criminal offense *even with good intent*.
> 3. **Forgetting the Faraday bag** in mobile forensic scenarios — examiners specifically look for this in the first step.
> 4. **Not mentioning hash verification (SHA-256)** in the chain of custody — without it, the evidence is inadmissible.
> 5. **Treating Cloud Forensics as "just downloading logs"** — the legal (subpoena/MLAT) and multi-jurisdictional dimensions are graded.
> 6. **Confusing IoT forensics with mobile forensics** — IoT has a distributed, multi-layer, sensor-specific evidence model.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST)

> **Penetration Testing:**
> - [x] Five PTES phases: **Recon → Threat Model → Exploit → Post-Exploit → Report**
> - [x] Three modes: **Black-box, White-box, Grey-box**
> - [x] PT produces *exploited* vulnerabilities, not just a list
> - [x] Tools: **Nmap, Metasploit, Burp Suite, Nessus, Cobalt Strike**
> - [x] CVSS v3.1: **0–10 scale**, Critical ≥ 9.0
> - [x] Standards: **PTES, OWASP, NIST SP 800-115, PCI-DSS**

> **Mobile Device Forensics:**
> - [x] Acquisition pyramid: **Manual → Logical → File System → Physical**
> - [x] **Faraday bag FIRST** — prevent remote wipe
> - [x] Hash before & after with **SHA-256** (not MD5)
> - [x] Tools: **Cellebrite UFED, XRY, Magnet AXIOM, Autopsy**
> - [x] Evidence types: **call log, SMS, app data, GPS, deleted files**
> - [x] Chain of custody documented at every transfer

> **IoT / Internet of Anything Forensics:**
> - [x] Three layers: **Device / Network / Cloud**
> - [x] Protocols: **MQTT, CoAP, Zigbee, BLE, Z-Wave, LoRaWAN**
> - [x] Device-level: **JTAG, UART, SPI, Chip-off, Binwalk**
> - [x] Main challenge: **distributed, ephemeral, multi-vendor**
> - [x] Time synchronization across layers is critical
> - [x] IoT data often non-replicable — preserve immediately

> **Cloud Forensics:**
> - [x] Service models: **IaaS, PaaS, SaaS** (each has different forensic access)
> - [x] Six primary challenges: **volatility, multi-tenancy, jurisdiction, no physical access, chain of custody, decentralization**
> - [x] Five pillars: **Identification, Preservation, Collection, Examination, Analysis** (sixth: Presentation)
> - [x] **No physical disk access** — relies on API logs, snapshots, SLAs
> - [x] Standards: **ISO 27043, NIST SP 800-86, RFC 3227**
> - [x] Legal: **subpoena, court order, MLAT, CSP legal team**

> **Cross-Cutting Concepts (frequently tested):**
> - [x] **Hashing = SHA-256**, **Bit-for-bit copy = dd or FTK Imager**
> - [x] **Chain of custody = legally admissible evidence**
> - [x] **Order of volatility** (RFC 3227): CPU registers → RAM → Disk → Logs → Backups
> - [x] **ACPO Principles**: no action should change data; original must remain available; audit trail of all actions; investigator must be competent.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cybercrimes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT602 - CYBER FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cybercrimes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview of Cybercrimes

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cybercrime (Definition – KTU Syllabus Standard)**
> *Cybercrime* is broadly defined as any **criminal activity, misconduct, or unlawful act** in which a **computer, networked device, or the Internet** is used as the **instrumentality, target, or incidental means** to commit an offense. It encompasses illegal behaviors ranging from digital theft, fraud, and identity theft to cyberstalking, cyberterrorism, and the distribution of illicit content across digital infrastructures.

In the context of **Cyber Forensics (PCCCT602 – Module 4)**, the term takes on a more specialized meaning. The focus shifts from merely *defining* a cybercrime to:
1. **Identifying** digital artifacts and evidence of the criminal act,
2. **Analyzing** the methods of attack and intrusion, and
3. **Documenting** the chain of custody to establish forensic admissibility under the **Indian Evidence Act, 1872 (now Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023)** and the **Information Technology Act, 2000 (amended 2008)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Digital Mugger"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy**
> Imagine a traditional **mugger** who robs a person on a dark street. He uses physical force, a weapon, and physical escape routes. Now, replace the dark street with the **Internet**, the weapon with a **malicious script or phishing email**, the physical force with **social engineering**, and the escape route with a **VPN or proxy chain**.
>
> The **mugger** in this analogy is a **cybercriminal**. The **victim** is a user or organization. The **forensic investigator** is the digital detective who reconstructs the crime by tracing IP logs, packet captures, file metadata, and registry entries.

This analogy makes it clear that:
- A cybercrime is **not just a "hacking" problem** — it includes fraud, harassment, intellectual property theft, and even organized digital crime.
- The **computer is both a weapon AND a victim** in different scenarios.
- **Digital evidence** is *volatile* (unlike physical fingerprints, RAM and network packets disappear when power is cut), which is why Cyber Forensics exists as a discipline.

## 1.3 Why Cybercrime Matters — Scope and Scale

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Reality Check**
> Examiners *love* asking students to **classify** cybercrimes or **differentiate** between cybercrime categories. Memorize the three-tier classification (Persons / Property / Government) cold. It is a **frequently repeated 7-mark question**.

| Metric / Scope Indicator | Global Statistic (Approx.) | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Average cost of a data breach** | **USD 4.45 million** (per IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report) | Justifies the cost of forensic readiness |
| **Daily cyber-attacks worldwide** | **~2,200+ per day** (i.e., 1 every 39 seconds) | Volume makes triage critical |
| **Most targeted sector** | **Healthcare, Finance, Government** | High-value, high-sensitivity data |
| **Primary attack vector** | **Phishing emails (~36% of breaches)** | Human vulnerability is the weakest link |

## 1.4 Historical Evolution Snapshot

1. **1971** — Creeper virus (first computer worm, ARPANET).
2. **1988** — Morris Worm; birth of the modern Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT).
3. **2000** — Enactment of the **Information Technology Act, 2000** in India.
4. **2008** — Major amendments to the IT Act to align with the **Budapest Convention on Cybercrime** principles.
5. **2020s** — Rise of **AI-assisted phishing, deepfake-enabled fraud, and ransomware-as-a-service (RaaS)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Tier Classification of Cybercrime (The Core KTU Framework)

The KTU 2024 syllabus and most Indian cyber law textbooks structure cybercrime into **three primary categories**, with sub-classifications. This is the single most important taxonomy you must master.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Classification (Must Memorize)**
> Cybercrime is broadly classified as:
> 1. **Cybercrime Against Individuals / Persons**
> 2. **Cybercrime Against Property**
> 3. **Cybercrime Against Government / Organization**

### Tier 1 — Cybercrime Against Individuals

These crimes target a specific person, damaging their digital identity, privacy, psychological well-being, or finances.

| Sub-Category | Description | Digital Forensics Focus |
|---|---|---|
| **Email Spoofing** | Faking the sender's email header to impersonate a trusted entity | SMTP header analysis, IP tracing, mail server logs |
| **Phishing / Vishing / Smishing** | Luring users to reveal credentials via fake emails, voice calls, or SMS | Browser history, email artifacts, call detail records (CDR) |
| **Cyberstalking / Cyberbullying** | Repeated online harassment, threats, or intimidation | Social media scrapes, chat logs, geolocation data |
| **Cyber Defamation** | Publishing defamatory statements online | Web archives (Wayback Machine), social media post metadata |
| **Child Pornography / CSAM** | Distribution or possession of illegal content depicting minors | Hash-based detection (PhotoDNA), encrypted volume analysis |
| **Identity Theft** | Stealing PII to impersonate another person | Browser cache, keylogger dumps, dark-web marketplace intel |
| **Email Bombing** | Flooding a target's inbox to cause DoS | Mail server logs, SMTP traffic capture |

### Tier 2 — Cybercrime Against Property

These crimes target tangible or intangible property — most commonly, **financial assets, intellectual property, and digital infrastructure**.

| Sub-Category | Description | Forensics Evidence Trail |
|---|---|---|
| **Credit Card Fraud / Carding** | Unauthorized use of stolen card credentials | Transaction logs, BIN analysis, dark-web dumps |
| **Intellectual Property Theft** | Piracy of software, music, movies, patents, trade secrets | Torrent swarm logs, BitTorrent client artifacts, code repositories |
| **Internet Time Theft** | Misusing someone else's ISP credentials to browse the web | ISP RADIUS logs, router DHCP leases |
| **Salami Attack** | Siphoning minuscule amounts (e.g., ₹0.50) from many accounts | Bank transaction ledgers, database query logs |
| **Denial-of-Service (DoS / DDoS)** | Flooding a server to make it unavailable | NetFlow logs, packet captures, botnet C2 traffic |
| **Ransomware** | Encrypting victim data and demanding payment | Cryptocurrency wallet tracing, decryption key extraction |
| **Logic Bomb / Virus / Worm** | Malicious code damaging systems | Reverse engineering, sandbox detonation, registry artifacts |

### Tier 3 — Cybercrime Against Government / Organization

These crimes threaten the sovereignty, integrity, and security of the state or critical infrastructure.

| Sub-Category | Description | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Cyberterrorism** | Ideologically motivated attacks to instill fear | 2015 Ukraine power grid attack |
| **Cyberwarfare** | State-sponsored offensive operations | Stuxnet (US–Israel vs. Iran nuclear program) |
| **Distribution of Pirated Software** | Causing revenue loss to government-owned enterprises | Mass torrent seeding of public-sector content |
| **Pornography (illegal)** | Publishing content prohibited under Indian law | Sections 67, 67A, 67B of the IT Act |
| **Hacking Government Websites** | Defacing or breaching .gov.in domains | Frequent attacks on Indian municipal portals |
| **Data Exfiltration / Espionage** | Theft of classified state or corporate data | OPM breach (2015), Aadhaar data leaks |

## 2.2 Alternative Classification — Mode of Operation

Many KTU questions ask for the classification based on **HOW** the crime is committed. This is the second framework you must know:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cybercrime Classification by Mode (Kshetri / UNESCO Framework)**
> 1. **Crimes using the computer as a TARGET** — e.g., DoS, malware, network intrusion.
> 2. **Crimes using the computer as a WEAPON** — e.g., fraud, phishing, cyberstalking.
> 3. **Crimes where the computer is an INCIDENTAL element** — e.g., storing illegal content on a hard drive.

## 2.3 The Concept of "Computer as Evidence" — Locard Exchange Principle in Cyberspace

> [!NOTE]
> **Locard's Exchange Principle (Adapted to Digital Forensics)**
> *Formulated by Dr. Edmond Locard (1910):* "Every contact leaves a trace."
> In the physical world, a criminal leaves fingerprints, hair, and fibers.
> In the digital world, a criminal leaves **log entries, registry modifications, MAC addresses, file metadata, browser history, and timestamps**.

The forensic investigator's role is to **identify, preserve, analyze, and present** these digital traces in a court of law.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Key Terms and Statutes

| **Term / Statute** | **Definition / Provision** | **Relevant Indian Law** |
|---|---|---|
| **Phishing** | Fraudulent attempt to obtain sensitive data by masquerading as a trustworthy entity | IT Act §66C (Identity Theft), §66D (Cheating by Personation) |
| **Spoofing** | Disguising a communication from an unknown source as from a known, trusted source | IT Act §66C |
| **Stalking** | Repeated, unwanted contact causing fear for safety | IT Act §66E (Violation of Privacy), IPC §354D |
| **Defamation** | Publishing false statements harming reputation | IT Act §66A (struck down in Shreya Singhal v. UoI, 2015) |
| **Hacking** | Unauthorized access to computer systems | IT Act §43, §66 (Computer-related offences) |
| **Data Theft** | Unauthorized copying or transfer of digital data | IT Act §43, §66, §72 (Breach of Confidentiality) |
| **Cyberterrorism** | Acts intended to threaten the unity, integrity, sovereignty of India | IT Act §66F |
| **Publishing Obscene Material** | Transmission of obscene content in electronic form | IT Act §67, §67A, §67B |
| **Ransomware** | Malicious software that encrypts data for ransom | IT Act §43, §66 + IPC §384 (Extortion) |
| **Salami Attack** | Siphoning minute amounts from many accounts | IT Act §66 + IPC §378 (Theft) |
| **DoS Attack** | Denial-of-service to legitimate users | IT Act §43(f), §66 |
| **IT Act 2000** | India's primary cyber law, effective 17 Oct 2000 | Amended in 2008 |
| **IT Act 2008 Amendment** | Added §66A, §66B, §66C, §66D, §66E, §66F, §69, §69B | Major overhaul |
| **Budapest Convention** | First international treaty on cybercrime (2001) | Council of Europe |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Conceptual Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note on "Derivations" in Cyber Forensics**
> Unlike Physics or Mathematics, Module 4 of Cyber Forensics does not require algebraic derivations. Instead, the *analytical equivalent* is the **logical deduction of legal/conceptual frameworks** — i.e., tracing how a cybercrime is committed step-by-step, or how the IT Act provisions logically map to each crime.

## 3.1 Logical Deduction — Anatomy of a Phishing Attack (Step-by-Step)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Phishing Lifecycle" is a recurring 7-mark or 14-mark question in KTU ESE.**
> The examiner expects you to describe **each stage** with forensic artifacts.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stage 1: Reconnaissance} &\rightarrow \text{Attacker harvests victim email IDs (OSINT, LinkedIn scraping)} \\
\text{Stage 2: Weaponization} &\rightarrow \text{Crafting a spoofed email resembling a bank's official template} \\
\text{Stage 3: Delivery} &\rightarrow \text{SMTP relay of the malicious email to the victim} \\
\text{Stage 4: Exploitation} &\rightarrow \text{Victim clicks link, lands on a clone of the bank's login page} \\
\text{Stage 5: Installation} &\rightarrow \text{Optional — drive-by download of a keylogger or RAT} \\
\text{Stage 6: Command \& Control} &\rightarrow \text{Stolen credentials exfiltrated to attacker's C2 server} \\
\text{Stage 7: Actions on Objectives} &\rightarrow \text{Account takeover, wire fraud, identity theft} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Forensic Artifacts at Each Stage (Table)

| **Stage** | **Forensic Artifact** | **Acquisition Tool** |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Recon | WHOIS lookups, social media metadata | Maltego, theHarvester |
| 2. Weaponization | Email source code, embedded URLs | Email header parsers (MxToolbox) |
| 3. Delivery | SMTP server logs, mail gateway logs | Exchange/Postfix logs |
| 4. Exploitation | Browser history, prefetch files | FTK, EnCase, BrowserHistoryView |
| 5. Installation | Registry run keys, scheduled tasks, file hashes | RegRipper, Autoruns, VirusTotal |
| 6. C2 | NetFlow logs, PCAP analysis, DNS queries | Wireshark, Zeek, Suricata |
| 7. Objectives | Bank transaction logs, account change logs | Subpoena to financial institution |

## 3.2 Logical Deduction — Mapping Cybercrimes to IT Act Provisions

> [!NOTE]
> **A frequently asked KTU question:** *"Which section of the IT Act, 2000 (amended 2008) deals with cyberstalking / phishing / cyberterrorism?"*
> Below is the **analytical matrix** you must commit to memory.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Offence} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \textbf{IT Act Section} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \textbf{Investigating Agency} \;\longleftrightarrow\; \textbf{Cognizable / Bailable}
\end{aligned}
$$

| **Cybercrime** | **IT Act / IPC Section** | **Cognizable?** | **Bailable?** | **Max Punishment** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Unauthorized Access (Hacking) | §43, §66 | Yes | Yes | 3 years + ₹5 lakh fine |
| Email Bombing | §66, §43(f) | Yes | Yes | 3 years |
| Data Theft / Damage | §43, §66 | Yes | Yes | 3 years + ₹5 lakh |
| Identity Theft | §66C | Yes | Yes | 3 years + ₹1 lakh |
| Cheating by Personation | §66D | Yes | Yes | 3 years + ₹1 lakh |
| Violation of Privacy | §66E | Yes | Yes | 3 years + ₹2 lakh |
| Cyberterrorism | **§66F** | **Yes** | **No** | **Death or life imprisonment** |
| Publishing Obscene Material | §67 | Yes | Yes | 5 years + ₹10 lakh |
| Publishing Sexually Explicit Content | §67A, §67B | Yes | No | 7 years + ₹10 lakh |
| Stalking (digital) | §66E + IPC §354D | Yes | Yes | 3 years (first offence) |
| Defamation (online) | IPC §499/500 *(§66A struck down)* | Yes | Yes | 2 years |

## 3.3 Deduction — The Salami Attack Mathematical Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Exercise (Not a derivation, but a logical model)**
> A classic Salami attack scenario: A programmer modifies payroll software so that for every employee paid ₹50,000, an extra **₹0.50** is routed to a slush account the attacker controls.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Siphoned per Employee} &= \text{Rounding Residual Amount} \\
&= 0.50 \; \text{INR per transaction} \\
\text{If } N &= 10{,}000 \text{ employees processed per month:} \\
\text{Monthly Siphoned} &= N \times 0.50 \\
&= 10{,}000 \times 0.50 \\
&= 5{,}000 \; \text{INR per month} \\
\text{Annual Siphoned (undetected for 5 years)} &= 5{,}000 \times 12 \times 5 \\
&= 300{,}000 \; \text{INR} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The point: the *per-victim* amount is **trivial**, but the *aggregate* becomes substantial — and **detection is rare** because each individual victim loses a negligible sum. This is why forensic accountants and log analysis are critical.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Email Spoofing: How the Forgery Works

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept**
> Email spoofing exploits the **lack of authentication in SMTP** (the Simple Mail Transfer Protocol does not verify the sender's identity by default).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Legitimate SMTP Flow} &\rightarrow \text{User → Sender's MTA → Receiver's MTA → Inbox} \\
\textbf{Spoofed SMTP Flow} &\rightarrow \text{Attacker → Attacker-controlled MTA (forged HELO)} \\
&\rightarrow \text{Receiver's MTA (accepts forged header)} \rightarrow \text{Inbox} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Email Header Forensics (Identifying the Forgery)

| **Header Field** | **Legitimate Value** | **Spoofed Value (Anomaly)** |
|---|---|---|
| `From:` | Real sender address | Forged sender address |
| `Return-Path:` | Matches `From:` | **Mismatched** (forensic red flag) |
| `Received:` chain | Internal IPs of sending organization | **External / anonymous IP** |
| `Message-ID:` | Domain of sending organization | Random, unrelated domain |
| `DKIM-Signature:` | Valid signature | **Missing or invalid** |
| `SPF` result | Pass | **Fail / SoftFail / None** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Forensic Tip:** The `Received:` chain is **appended at each MTA hop** and is *almost impossible* to forge fully. This is the most reliable header field for tracing the true origin of a spoofed email.
