# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Confidence of Attribution under Three Independent Log Sources

### Problem
A forensic investigator has three independent corroborating pieces of evidence that a particular attacker IP performed the breach:
- ISP RADIUS log: $p_1 = 0.55$
- Switch CAM-table log: $p_2 = 0.70$
- Web-server authentication log: $p_3 = 0.65$

Find the combined confidence of attribution $C$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the model.**  
Since the three sources are *independent* and *complementary* (any one of them could, by itself, partially implicate the attacker), the parallel-evidence OR-fusion model applies:

$$
C(x) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}\bigl(1 - p_i(x)\bigr)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $n = 3$.**

$$
C(x) = 1 - (1 - 0.55)(1 - 0.70)(1 - 0.65)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each complement.**

$$
1 - 0.55 = 0.45, \quad 1 - 0.70 = 0.30, \quad 1 - 0.65 = 0.35
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply the complements.**

$$
0.45 \times 0.30 = 0.135
$$
$$
0.135 \times 0.35 = 0.04725
$$

**Step 5 — Subtract from 1 to get the combined confidence.**

$$
C(x) = 1 - 0.04725 = 0.95275 \approx 95.28\%
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**  
There is approximately a **$95.28\%$** chance that the suspect IP is the true attacker. This is well above the $90\%$ civil-court "balance of probabilities" threshold and approaches the "beyond reasonable doubt" criminal threshold when combined with corroborating physical evidence (e.g., CCTV).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "OR-fusion"?** Because each *miss* of the attacker in one log source is *independent* of a miss in another. The probability of *missing* in **all** sources is the product of individual miss-probabilities. Subtraction from 1 gives the probability of being *caught* by at least one source.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Magnetic-Recovery Probability Decay Across Eraser Passes

### Problem
A single overwrite pass recovers the previous bit with probability $p_0 = 0.10$ (i.e., 10% chance the old magnetic domain is dominant enough to read). After $k$ independent passes with random patterns, what is the recovery probability $p_k$? How many passes are needed to push $p_k$ below $10^{-6}$?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recurrence relation.**  
Each new pass multiplies the residual "old-data dominance" by the single-pass factor $p_0$, because the new pattern overwrites the old dominant direction with a random pattern that has only a $p_0$ chance of leaving the old orientation readable:

$$
p_k = p_0 \cdot p_{k-1}, \qquad p_1 = p_0
$$

**Step 2 — Closed form by induction.**  
Repeated substitution yields:

$$
p_k = p_0^k
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the constraint $p_k < 10^{-6}$ with $p_0 = 0.10$.**

$$
(0.10)^k < 10^{-6}
$$

**Step 4 — Take $\log_{10}$ of both sides.**

$$
k \cdot \log_{10}(0.10) < -6
$$
$$
k \cdot (-1) < -6
$$
$$
k > 6
$$

**Step 5 — Minimum integer solution.**  
The smallest integer $k$ is $k = 7$. This is precisely why the **Schneier 7-pass** and **DoD 7-pass** standards are considered mathematically sufficient for magnetic media.

**Step 6 — Sanity check with the Gutmann 35-pass standard.**  
For $k = 35$:

$$
p_{35} = (0.10)^{35} = 10^{-35}
$$

This is astronomically small — overkill for modern drives but designed for legacy MFM/RLL encoding schemes. Modern drives (since $\sim 2005$) with PRML/EPRML channels typically require only **1 to 3** passes (per NIST SP 800-88).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Counter-Measures vs. Attacker Techniques

| Attacker Technique | What the Attacker Alters | Primary Forensic Counter-Measure | Secondary Counter-Measure |
|--------------------|--------------------------|----------------------------------|---------------------------|
| Eraser (wipe a file) | File data sectors | Carve unallocated clusters; examine MFT-resident resident data | Check `$LogFile` and `$UsnJrnl` for prior file references |
| Timestomp on `cmd.exe` | `$STANDARD_INFORMATION.M` | Compare with `$FILE_NAME.M` | Verify against prefetch file `C:\Windows\Prefetch\CMD.EXE-*.pf` |
| Event-log clearing (1102) | `Security` event log | Check `WinEVT` for Event-ID **104** (log cleared) in `System` log | Inspect raw `.evtx` file in `$MFT` for zeroed clusters |
| ARP spoofing | ARP cache | Monitor gratuitous ARP rate via IDS (Snort rule `arp_spoof`) | Static ARP entries on critical hosts |
| DNS cache poisoning | Resolver cache | DNSSEC validation, randomised source port | Compare authoritative vs. recursive TTLs |
| Email spoofing | `From:` header | SPF, DKIM, DMARC triplet check | Inspect `Received:` chain hop-by-hop |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Do not confuse the *clearing* of an event log (which leaves a sentinel Event-ID 104) with the *deletion* of the `.evtx` file (which leaves an MFT deletion entry). They are two distinct forensic artefacts.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Detecting a Timestomp with MFT Attributes

**Scenario.** A file `report.docx` on an NTFS volume has the following timestamps:

- `$STANDARD_INFORMATION.M` (Modified): 2024-08-15 10:30:00
- `$FILE_NAME.M` (Modified): 2024-01-04 14:12:00

**Inference.**

$$
\Delta t = \vert \text{2024-08-15 10:30:00} - \text{2024-01-04 14:12:00} \vert
$$

$$
\Delta t = 223 \text{ days, } 20 \text{ hours, } 18 \text{ minutes}
$$

This is a **strong indicator of timestomping** because the two timestamps — which should normally differ by at most a few seconds — diverge by over **7 months**. The attacker manipulated `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` but did not have raw-disk access to modify the *immutable* `$FILE_NAME` attribute (the OS protects `$FILE_NAME` from user-mode tampering).

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
*With a neat block diagram, explain the working of the **Eraser** tool as an anti-forensic utility. List any **two** sanitisation standards supported by it.**

### Model Answer (3 Marks)
Eraser is a Windows-based secure file-erasure tool that *overwrites* the sectors of a target file with deterministic and pseudo-random bit patterns so that the original data cannot be recovered by forensic means.  
**Working pipeline (block diagram):**

```
Target Selection -> Algorithm Pick -> PRNG -> k Passes Overwrite -> Verify -> Wipe MFT Entry
```

**Two supported standards (any two):**
1. **DoD 5220.22-M** (3-pass / 7-pass)
2. **Gutmann Method** (35-pass)
3. **Schneier 7-Pass Method**
4. **RCMP TSSIT OPS-II** (4-pass)

*(Diagram 1.5 marks, standards 1 mark each = 0.5 each → 3 marks total.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**  
*Differentiate between **Trail Obfuscation** and **Spoofing** in the context of cyber forensics. Give **one** example of each.**

### Model Answer (3 Marks)

| Aspect | Trail Obfuscation | Spoofing |
|--------|-------------------|----------|
| Definition | Destroying or camouflaging *what happened* (logs, files, timestamps). | Forging *who* performed an action (IP, MAC, email, DNS). |
| Target of attack | Evidence trail | Identity attribute |
| Forensic counter | File carving, journal analysis, slack-space recovery | Cross-correlation of router, switch, ISP, and authentication logs |
| Example | Clearing Windows Security event log using `wevtutil cl Security` | Sending an email with forged `From: ceo@company.com` header |

*(1 mark definition each, 0.5 mark example each = 3 marks.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts of **7 marks** each. Internal choice is provided within the same question (i.e., between Option A and Option B, the student answers *one*).

---

### Option A — Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **Eraser** tool in detail. With a neat diagram, describe the **DoD 5220.22-M 3-pass** sanitisation algorithm. Show mathematically why **$k = 7$** passes are sufficient to drive the residual-recovery probability below $10^{-6}$ for a single-pass recovery factor of $p_0 = 0.10$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **Trail Obfuscation** techniques used by attackers. Explain **timestomping**, **NTFS alternate data streams**, and **log wiping** with their forensic detection methods. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution A(a) — 7 Marks
1. **Definition (1.5 marks):** Eraser is a Windows secure-erasure utility that overwrites file sectors with deterministic and pseudo-random patterns, supporting standards such as DoD 5220.22-M, Gutmann, Schneier, and RCMP TSSIT OPS-II.
2. **DoD 3-pass diagram (2.5 marks):** The block diagram must show three sequential passes (zeros, ones, random) with a final verify read-back. Award full marks for: `Pass 1: 0x00` → `Pass 2: 0xFF` → `Pass 3: Random` → `Verify`.
3. **Mathematical proof (3 marks):**

$$
p_k = p_0^k
$$

For $p_0 = 0.10$ and $p_k < 10^{-6}$:

$$
(0.10)^k < 10^{-6}
$$
$$
k \log_{10}(0.10) < -6
$$
$$
k \cdot (-1) < -6 \implies k > 6
$$
$$
k_{\min} = 7
$$

This is the mathematical justification for the **DoD 7-pass** extension. Full marks require the closed-form recurrence, the log step, and the integer-rounding reasoning.

### Model Solution A(b) — 7 Marks
1. **Timestomping (2.5 marks):** Definition, tools (`timestomp.exe`, `SetMACE`), detection via the $M_{SI} \ne M_{FN}$ mismatch.
2. **Alternate Data Streams (2 marks):** Hidden data in `file.txt:secret`; listing with `dir /R`; forensic detection by scanning the raw NTFS attribute `$DATA` with names other than the default.
3. **Log wiping (1.5 marks):** Methods (`wevtutil cl`, `cat /dev/null > /var/log/syslog`, `metasploit clearev`); forensic detection via Event-ID **104** sentinel in Windows, or journal-block reference still present in `$LogFile`.
4. **Examiner's pitfall (1 mark):** Always check the *system* log for a "log was cleared" event even when the *security* log is empty.

---

### Option B — Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 4]**

**(a)** With neat block diagrams, explain the **seven major variants of spoofing** and the trust attribute each one forges. Discuss any **three** forensic detection methods in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A forensic investigator has three independent corroborating evidence sources implicating an attacker. The individual probabilities are $p_1 = 0.55$, $p_2 = 0.70$, $p_3 = 0.65$. Compute the **combined confidence of attribution** using the parallel-evidence OR-fusion model and interpret the result. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution B(a) — 7 Marks
1. **Enumeration (3 marks):** List the seven variants — IP, MAC, ARP, DNS, Email, VoIP/Caller-ID, GPS — and identify the trust attribute each one forges (1 mark for the table, 2 marks for the brief block diagram showing attacker → forged attribute → victim).
2. **Three detection methods (3 marks):**
   - **IP spoof detection (1 mark):** Use TTL value mismatches — the attacker cannot easily replicate the *original* TTL of the trusted host; the hop-count through `traceroute` will differ.
   - **Email spoof detection (1 mark):** SPF + DKIM + DMARC triplet validation against the purported sender's domain DNS records.
   - **ARP spoof detection (1 mark):** Deploy Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) on managed switches; alert on gratuitous ARP rate.
3. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Spoofing can never be 100% eliminated, but the cost of forging multiple layers simultaneously (e.g., IP + MAC + behavioural) makes practical attribution feasible.

### Model Solution B(b) — 7 Marks
1. **Statement of formula (2 marks):**

$$
C(x) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - p_i(x))
$$

2. **Substitution (1.5 marks):**

$$
C(x) = 1 - (1 - 0.55)(1 - 0.70)(1 - 0.65)
$$

3. **Step-by-step arithmetic (2.5 marks):**

$$
1 - 0.55 = 0.45
$$
$$
1 - 0.70 = 0.30
$$
$$
1 - 0.65 = 0.35
$$
$$
0.45 \times 0.30 \times 0.35 = 0.04725
$$
$$
C(x) = 1 - 0.04725 = 0.95275
$$

4. **Interpretation (1 mark):** Approximately **$95.3\%$** combined confidence of attribution. This exceeds the civil-court threshold of *balance of probabilities* ($> 50\%$) and approaches the criminal standard of *beyond reasonable doubt* when supported by independent physical evidence.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "anti-forensics" with "counter-forensics"** — these are *not* synonyms in KTU answer keys. Anti-forensics is *preventive*; counter-forensics is *destructive*.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the `$FILE_NAME` attribute** when explaining timestomp detection. Most students describe the *attack* but omit the *forensic counter-indicator*, which is worth **2–3 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 3. **Writing `|` inside markdown tables** — the symbol breaks the table parser, and the model answer renders as a malformed page. Use $\vert$ in LaTeX.
> 4. **Mixing up DoD 3-pass and DoD 7-pass** — these are *two different* sanitisation levels of the *same* standard. Be explicit.
> 5. **In derivation questions**, students often write only the final answer ($k = 7$) without showing the log-step — and lose 2 of 3 marks. Always show the algebraic transition.
> 6. **For Spoofing**, examiners expect the student to identify *which OSI layer* is being spoofed and *which trust attribute* is forged. A vague answer like "the attacker changed his address" gets 1 of 7 marks.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- **Eraser** is a *secure file-wipe tool* on Windows that overwrites sectors to defeat forensic recovery. It supports DoD 5220.22-M (3/7 pass), Gutmann (35 pass), Schneier (7 pass), and RCMP TSSIT OPS-II (4 pass) standards.
- **Magnetic-recovery probability decays exponentially**: $p_k = p_0^k$. With $p_0 = 0.10$, **7 passes** drop $p_k$ below $10^{-6}$.
- **SSDs defeat classical overwrite** because of wear-leveling; chip-off and `SECURITY ERASE UNIT` are required.
- **NIST SP 800-88** defines three sanitisation levels: **Clear**, **Purge**, **Destroy**.
- **Trail Obfuscation** is the umbrella term for *evidence-destruction techniques*: log wiping, timestomping, ADS hiding, steganography, history purging.
- **Timestomping detection**: compare `$STANDARD_INFORMATION.M$` with `$FILE_NAME.M$`. A large $\Delta t$ is a strong indicator.
- **Event-log clearing** leaves Event-ID **104** in the `System` log and an MFT deletion entry for the `.evtx` file.
- **NTFS Alternate Data Streams (ADS)** can hide data invisible to `dir`; list with `dir /R` or forensic tools (FTK, Autopsy, X-Ways).
- **Spoofing** is identity forgery. Variants: IP, MAC, ARP, DNS, Email, VoIP, GPS. Each operates at a specific OSI layer.
- **IP spoof detection**: TTL mismatch, IP-ID sequence anomalies, hop-count analysis via traceroute.
- **Email spoof detection**: SPF, DKIM, DMARC triplet validation; analyse the `Received:` header chain hop-by-hop.
- **ARP spoof detection**: gratuitous-ARP rate monitoring, Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI), static ARP entries.
- **DNS spoof detection**: DNSSEC validation, randomised source port, TTL anomaly.
- **Confidence of attribution** uses the **OR-fusion** formula: $C(x) = 1 - \prod(1 - p_i)$. Three independent sources at $p = 0.6$ each give $C \approx 0.936$.
- **Locard's Exchange Principle** is the philosophical foundation of forensics; anti-forensics tries to violate it; counter-forensics tries to *amplify the residual signal*.
- **Differentiate**: Spoofing lies about *who*; Obfuscation lies about *what happened*; Eraser destroys the *physical substrate* of evidence.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Modification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT602 - CYBER FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data Modification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the discipline of **Cyber Forensics**, the term **Data Modification** refers to any unauthorized, intentional, or accidental alteration of digital information, metadata, file contents, timestamps, or system logs that compromises the original state, integrity, and evidentiary value of digital artifacts. It encompasses both **malicious tampering** (anti-forensic sabotage) and **incidental corruption** (storage failure, transmission errors).

According to the standard forensic triad — **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA)** — Data Modification is a direct violation of the **Integrity** pillar. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCCT602 – Cyber Forensics)** syllabus, Data Modification is studied as a sub-domain of digital evidence handling, where forensic examiners must:

- Detect unauthorized changes in storage media.
- Reconstruct the original state of data using **hashing algorithms** (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256).
- Establish **chain of custody** proof that data has not been altered between seizure and court presentation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Data Modification is treated as a critical forensic concept because it directly impacts the **admissibility of digital evidence** under Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act (now Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you received a **sealed legal contract** in a tamper-proof envelope. The envelope has a **unique wax seal** and a **photograph** attached showing its original contents. When you open it later, you notice:
- The wax seal is **broken and re-stamped** (tampering with metadata).
- A paragraph has been **whitened out and retyped** (file content modification).
- The **date on the document** has been changed (timestamp modification).
- A **page has been swapped** with a forged one (file replacement).

In the digital world, **Data Modification** is the cyber equivalent of these actions. Forensic investigators act like detectives who, using the **photograph (hash value)** and the **seal pattern (digital signature)**, can detect every change — no matter how small — and reconstruct the original.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning in KTU Context |
|------|------------------------|
| **Bit Rot** | Gradual silent corruption of stored bits over time. |
| **Data Tampering** | Malicious intentional modification of file contents. |
| **Metadata Spoofing** | Falsifying file properties like author, timestamps. |
| **File Slack Alteration** | Modifying unallocated space within a file cluster. |
| **Anti-Forensics** | Techniques used by attackers to defeat forensic analysis. |
| **Hash Collision** | Two distinct inputs producing the same hash digest. |

> [!NOTE]
> A core principle in Cyber Forensics is the **Locard Exchange Principle** — every contact leaves a trace. Data Modification, even when successful, leaves behind forensic artifacts that trained investigators can detect.

---

## 1.4 Scope of Data Modification in KTU Module 4

The KTU 2024 syllabus for **PCCCT602 Module 4** positions Data Modification within the following sub-domains:

1. **File-level modifications** — content, headers, footers, embedded objects.
2. **Metadata-level modifications** — EXIF, XMP, Office document properties.
3. **Filesystem-level modifications** — MAC (Modified, Accessed, Created) timestamps, inode data.
4. **Log-level modifications** — Windows Event Logs, Syslog, application logs.
5. **Anti-forensic techniques** — steganography, data hiding, log wiping, timestamp shifting.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Data Modification Attack

A Data Modification event typically follows a structured **Kill Chain**:

### Step 1 — Reconnaissance
The attacker identifies the target file system, identifies forensic tools in use, and locates the file of interest (e.g., log file, image, document).

### Step 2 — Privilege Escalation
Attacker gains **write access** to the target file, often through:
- Exploiting OS vulnerabilities.
- Abusing legitimate administrative tools (e.g., `PowerShell`, `sed`, `dd`).
- Insider threats with valid credentials.

### Step 3 — Modification Execution
The alteration is performed using techniques such as:
- **Direct binary editing** (hex editors like `HxD`, `WinHex`).
- **Programmatic writing** (Python, Perl, or shell scripts).
- **Metadata stripping tools** (ExifTool, MAT2).
- **Log cleaners** (Metasploit's `clearev`, `wipe` utilities).

### Step 4 — Anti-Forensic Concealment
The attacker attempts to mask the modification by:
- Resetting MAC timestamps to original values (**timestomping**).
- Altering log entries to remove traces.
- Embedding data inside other files (**steganography**).

### Step 5 — Persistence or Exfiltration
The modified data is then used to mislead investigators, frame an innocent party, or hide exfiltrated content.

---

## 2.2 Categories of Data Modification

### A. Content Modification
Altering the actual bytes of a file. Detected by **cryptographic hash comparison** (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256, SHA-3).

### B. Metadata Modification
Changing file properties such as:
- File name and extension.
- Author/Owner name.
- MAC timestamps.
- GPS coordinates in images.
- Document revision history.

### C. Filesystem-Level Modification
Altering filesystem structures:
- Master File Table (MFT) entries (NTFS).
- inode metadata (ext4, XFS).
- Directory entries.

### D. Application-Level Modification
- Database record tampering.
- Email header forgery.
- Browser history manipulation.

### E. Log Modification
- Deleting event log entries.
- Modifying log timestamps.
- Log injection (adding false entries).

---

## 2.3 The Forensic Hash Function (Why and How)

A **cryptographic hash function** $H$ maps an arbitrary-length input message $M$ to a fixed-length output digest $d$:

$$H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$$

Where $n$ is the digest length in bits.

### Properties Required (KTU High-Yield):

1. **Determinism:** Same input always produces same output.
2. **Pre-image resistance:** Given $d$, finding $M$ is computationally infeasible.
3. **Collision resistance:** Finding $M_1 \neq M_2$ such that $H(M_1) = H(M_2)$ is infeasible.
4. **Avalanche effect:** A 1-bit change in $M$ causes ~50% bits of $d$ to change.

### Avalanche Effect (Bit Difference Ratio)

The **Bit Difference Ratio (BDR)** between two digests $d_1$ and $d_2$ is computed as:

$$
\text{BDR} = \frac{\text{HammingDistance}(d_1, d_2)}{n}
$$

A cryptographically strong hash yields $\text{BDR} \approx 0.5$.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description | Typical Use |
|---------|----------------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Hash Digest Length (bits)** | $n = 128$ (MD5), $160$ (SHA-1), $256$ (SHA-256) | Output bit length | Algorithm selection |
| **Avalanche BDR** | $\text{BDR} = \frac{\text{Hamming}(d_1, d_2)}{n}$ | Measures sensitivity to input change | Hash quality test |
| **Birthday Attack Probability** | $P \approx 1 - e^{-k^2 / (2 \cdot 2^n)}$ | Collision probability after $k$ trials | MD5 weakness proof |
| **NTFS MACE Timestamps** | $\text{MAC} = \{\text{Modified}, \text{Accessed}, \text{Changed}, \text{Entry Modified}\}$ | $8 \times 4 = 32$ fields per file | Timeline reconstruction |
| **Steganographic Capacity** | $C = W \times H \times 3 \div 8$ (bytes) for 24-bit LSB | Max hidden data in an RGB image | Anti-forensic analysis |
| **Log Integrity Score** | $L_{\text{int}} = 1 - \frac{\text{Tampered Entries}}{\text{Total Entries}}$ | Log trustworthiness metric | Audit & compliance |
| **Data Slack Space** | $\text{Slack} = \text{Cluster Size} - \text{File Size}$ | Hidden bytes in last cluster | Forensic carving |
| **Parity Bit (RAID-5)** | $P = d_1 \oplus d_2 \oplus \ldots \oplus d_k$ | XOR-based recovery | Storage forensics |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Reminder:** In the table above, the symbol $\vert$ has been written as $\mid$ inside math expressions. When transcribing, **never use the raw pipe character** in markdown tables to avoid breaking the table.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Data Modification analysis is foundational in:

- **Incident Response:** Detecting tampering during breach investigation.
- **Legal Proceedings:** Validating evidence integrity for court.
- **Compliance Auditing:** Ensuring logs meet ISO 27001, GDPR, RBI, SEBI standards.
- **Insider Threat Detection:** Identifying rogue employees altering records.
- **Malware Analysis:** Understanding self-modifying code and polymorphic viruses.

---

## 2.6 Chain of Custody Hashing Protocol

A standard forensic workflow for tamper detection is:

$$
H_0 = H(\text{Original File})
$$

$$
H_t = H(\text{Current File at time } t)
$$

$$
\text{Integrity} = \begin{cases} \text{INTACT} & \text{if } H_0 = H_t \\ \text{TAMPERED} & \text{if } H_0 \neq H_t \end{cases}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For this to be legally defensible, both $H_0$ and $H_t$ must be computed using **FIPS-validated algorithms** on a **forensically clean workstation** with documented **chain of custody**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bit Difference Ratio (BDR) of MD5 with 1-bit Flip

Let us consider a forensic scenario where an attacker flips **one bit** of an original file $F$ to produce $F'$. We wish to demonstrate that the **MD5 hash** exhibits the **avalanche effect**.

### Setup

Let $F$ be a 16-byte file (for clarity):
$$
F = \texttt{0x4A 0x6F 0x68 0x6E 0x20 0x44 0x6F 0x65 0x20 0x32 0x30 0x32 0x34 0x21 0x21 0x21}
$$

The first byte $0x4A = 01001010_2$ is flipped at bit position 5 to become $0x5A = 01011010_2$. Thus:

$$
F' = \texttt{0x5A 0x6F 0x68 0x6E 0x20 0x44 0x6F 0x65 0x20 0x32 0x30 0x32 0x34 0x21 0x21 0x21}
$$

### Step 1 — Compute MD5(F) (illustrative digest)

For a real forensic case, MD5 produces a 128-bit digest. Let us assume:

$$
H(F) = 5D41402A BC4B2A76 B9719D91 17D44C4F \quad (\text{hex})
$$

### Step 2 — Compute MD5(F')

The avalanche effect of MD5 ensures a drastically different digest:

$$
H(F') = 7E5B8A0F 1C49AC3E BD4F76A2 1AB09E13 \quad (\text{hex})
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Binary

$$
H(F)_{\text{bin}} = 01011101 \, 01000001 \, 01000000 \, 00101010 \ldots
$$

$$
H(F')_{\text{bin}} = 01111110 \, 01011011 \, 10001010 \, 00001111 \ldots
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Hamming Distance

Count bit positions where the two digests differ. For MD5, a single 1-bit input change typically causes **~64 bits** (out of 128) to flip.

$$
\text{Hamming}(H(F), H(F')) = 64
$$

### Step 5 — Compute BDR

$$
\text{BDR} = \frac{\text{Hamming}(H(F), H(F'))}{n} = \frac{64}{128} = 0.5
$$

### Step 6 — Interpretation

A BDR of **0.5** (50%) confirms strong avalanche behavior. For any cryptographically valid hash, the expected BDR lies in the range:

$$
0.4 \leq \text{BDR} \leq 0.6
$$

Anything below 0.3 or above 0.7 indicates a **broken hash function**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in forensics?** If a hash function has poor avalanche, an attacker can flip bits in a file with minimal changes to the digest — making tampering **invisible** to hash comparison. This is why MD5 is considered **cryptographically broken** and SHA-256 is preferred.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Birthday Attack Collision Probability for MD5

The **birthday paradox** gives the probability of finding a hash collision after $k$ trials:

$$
P(k) \approx 1 - e^{-k^2 / (2 \cdot 2^n)}
$$

### Step 1 — Define Parameters

For MD5, $n = 128$ bits. To achieve $P = 0.5$ (50% chance of finding a collision):

$$
0.5 = 1 - e^{-k^2 / (2 \cdot 2^{128})}
$$

### Step 2 — Solve Algebraically

$$
e^{-k^2 / (2^{129})} = 0.5
$$

$$
-\frac{k^2}{2^{129}} = \ln(0.5) = -0.693
$$

$$
k^2 = 0.693 \times 2^{129}
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate

$$
k = \sqrt{0.693 \times 2^{129}} = 2^{64} \times \sqrt{0.693}
$$

$$
k \approx 0.832 \times 2^{64} \approx 1.7 \times 10^{19}
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation

An attacker needs approximately $2^{64}$ MD5 computations to find a collision with **50% probability**. While this number is large, modern GPU clusters achieve this in **hours**, making MD5 unsuitable for forensic evidence hashing.

For **SHA-256** ($n = 256$):

$$
k \approx 0.832 \times 2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}
$$

This is computationally infeasible with current technology.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Detection Techniques vs. Modification Type

| Modification Type | Detection Tool | Detection Method | Hash Required? |
|-------------------|----------------|------------------|----------------|
| **File Content Edit** | Autopsy, X-Ways, EnCase | Diff comparison + hash | Yes (SHA-256) |
| **MAC Timestamp Change** | Plaso, log2timeline, Sleuth Kit | Timeline analysis + $MFT$ parsing | No |
| **Metadata Stripping** | ExifTool, FOCA | EXIF/XMP comparison | No |
| **Log Entry Deletion** | LogParser, Kibana, Splunk | Gap detection in sequence | Yes (signed logs) |
| **Log Entry Insertion** | SIEM correlation | Anomaly detection | Yes |
| **File Deletion** | PhotoRec, Recuva, Foremost | Carving + slack space | Optional |
| **Steganography** | Stegdetect, binwalk, zsteg | Statistical LSB analysis | No |
| **Anti-Forensic Wiping** | TestDisk, photorec | Carving + entropy check | No |
| **Database Tampering** | MySQL binlog, pgAudit | Transaction log replay | Yes |
| **Email Header Forgery** | MessageHeader Analyzer, EmailTracker | SPF/DKIM/DMARC check | Optional |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Steganographic LSB Capacity

For a 24-bit RGB image of dimensions $W \times H$, the **Least Significant Bit (LSB)** steganography capacity is:

### Step 1 — Total Bits Available

$$
\text{Total LSB bits} = W \times H \times 3
$$

(Since each pixel has 3 channels: R, G, B, and 1 LSB per channel.)

### Step 2 — Convert to Bytes

$$
C_{\text{bytes}} = \frac{W \times H \times 3}{8}
$$

### Step 3 — Practical Capacity (with Header Overhead)

Subtract $\sim 10\%$ for header and synchronization:

$$
C_{\text{effective}} = 0.9 \times \frac{W \times H \times 3}{8}
$$

### Step 4 — Example Calculation

For a $1920 \times 1080$ full HD image:

$$
C_{\text{effective}} = 0.9 \times \frac{1920 \times 1080 \times 3}{8} = 0.9 \times 777600 = 699840 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
C_{\text{effective}} \approx 683 \text{ KB}
$$

This is enough to hide a **small text file, a malicious script, or a keylogger payload** — a major anti-forensic concern.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Forensic Investigation Workflow for Data Modification Cases

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incident Detection] --> B[Secure the Scene]
    B --> C[Acquire Bit-Stream Image]
    C --> D[Compute Initial Hash H0 SHA-256]
    D --> E[Load into Forensic Workstation]
    E --> F[Run File System Parser]
    F --> G{Anomaly Detected?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Compare H0 vs Current Hash Ht]
    G -->|No| I[Generate Baseline Timeline]
    H --> J{H0 equals Ht?}
    J -->|No| K[Mark as TAMPERED]
    J -->|Yes| L[Mark as INTACT]
    K --> M[Isolate Modified File]
    L --> I
    M --> N[Run Log Analysis]
    N --> O[Run Metadata Analysis ExifTool]
    O --> P[Run Slack Space Carving]
    P --> Q[Identify Anti-Forensic Technique]
    Q --> R[Reconstruct Original State]
    R --> S[Document Chain of Custody]
    S --> T[Generate Forensic Report]
    I --> T
    T --> U[Court Presentation]
```

## 4.2 Data Modification Attack Chain (Anti-Forensic Kill Chain)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Reconnaissance] --> A2[Privilege Escalation]
    A2 --> A3[Identify Target File]
    A3 --> A4[Modify Content Bytes]
    A4 --> A5[Modify Metadata EXIF MFT]
    A5 --> A6[Timestomp Reset MAC]
    A6 --> A7[Wipe or Forge Logs]
    A7 --> A8[Embed in Stego Container]
    A8 --> A9[Exit and Cover Tracks]
```

## 4.3 Hash Comparison Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start Verification] --> X[Load Original Hash H0]
    X --> Y[Recompute Hash Ht on Suspect File]
    Y --> Z{H0 equals Ht?}
    Z -->|Yes| P1[File is INTACT and Admissible]
    Z -->|No| P2[File is TAMPERED]
    P2 --> P3[Run Forgery Analysis]
    P3 --> P4[Check for Timestomping]
    P3 --> P5[Check for Steganography]
    P3 --> P6[Check for File Substitution]
    P4 --> P7[Document Findings in Report]
    P5 --> P7
    P6 --> P7
```

## 4.4 Data Modification Categories — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Data Modification] --> C1[Content Level]
    ROOT --> C2[Metadata Level]
    ROOT --> C3[Filesystem Level]
    ROOT --> C4[Log Level]
    ROOT --> C5[Application Level]
    C1 --> S1[Byte editing]
    C1 --> S2[Header injection]
    C1 --> S3[Macro injection]
    C2 --> M1[EXIF stripping]
    C2 --> M2[Author spoofing]
    C2 --> M3[GPS faking]
    C3 --> F1[MFT tampering]
    C3 --> F2[inode modification]
    C3 --> F3[Timestamp shifting]
    C4 --> L1[Log deletion]
    C4 --> L2[Log injection]
    C4 --> L3[Log timestomp]
    C5 --> A1[DB record edit]
    A1 --> A2[Email forgery]
    A2 --> A3[Browser history edit]
```

## 4.5 Anti-Forensic Technique → Detection Counter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Steganography LSB] --> D1[Binwalk Stegdetect]
    T2[Timestomping] --> D2[Plaso log2timeline]
    T3[Log Wiping Metasploit] --> D3[SIEM Correlation]
    T4[File Wiping DoD 5220] --> D4[Magnetic Force Microscopy]
    T5[Encryption Ransomware] --> D5[Memory Forensics Volatility]
    T6[MAC Spoofing] --> D6[Network Forensics Wireshark]
    T7[Registry Hiding] --> D7[Registry Hive Analysis]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric and prefixed (A1, T1, D1, etc.) to avoid syntax conflicts. Labels are plain uppercase text — no bold, italics, or HTML inside the quoted labels.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define Data Modification in the context of Cyber Forensics. List any two techniques used by attackers to alter digital evidence.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (2 Marks):** Data Modification in Cyber Forensics refers to any unauthorized or intentional alteration of digital data, metadata, timestamps, or log files that compromises the **integrity** of the original information. It directly violates the **CIA Triad's Integrity pillar** and can render evidence inadmissible under **Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act / Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam 2023**.

**Two Techniques (1 Mark):**

1. **Timestomping** — Modifying the MAC (Modified, Accessed, Created) timestamps of files to mislead forensic timeline analysis.
2. **Log Wiping** — Using tools like `Metasploit's clearev` or `wevtutil cl` to erase or alter Windows Event Logs, hiding attack traces.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Explain the role of cryptographic hash functions in detecting Data Modification. Why is SHA-256 preferred over MD5 in forensic investigations?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Role of Hash (2 Marks):** Cryptographic hash functions like MD5, SHA-1, and SHA-256 produce a **unique fixed-length fingerprint** (digest) of a file. Any modification — even a single bit flip — produces a **completely different digest** due to the **avalanche effect**. By comparing the hash of the original (acquired) file with the hash of the current file, investigators can definitively detect tampering.

**Why SHA-256 (1 Mark):** MD5 (128-bit) is **cryptographically broken** — collisions can be generated in hours using modern hardware. SHA-256 (256-bit) provides **stronger collision resistance** ($\approx 2^{128}$ operations to break), making it the **FIPS-validated** and legally defensible standard for forensic hashing.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Note:** As per the 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern, Part B carries 14 marks with **internal choice** between two full questions. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

#### (a) Discuss in detail the various categories of Data Modification that a forensic examiner may encounter during a digital investigation. (7 Marks)

#### (b) With suitable diagrams and example calculations, explain how the **Birthday Attack** probability applies to MD5 and SHA-256, and state why MD5 is no longer accepted in court. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Content Modification (2 Marks):**
Direct alteration of file bytes — e.g., changing a financial record from $1,000 to $100,000 in a spreadsheet, injecting a macro into a Word document, or editing a photograph to remove incriminating detail. Detected via **hash comparison** and **diff tools**.

**2. Metadata Modification (1 Mark):**
Falsifying EXIF data (camera model, GPS), Office document properties (author, last modified by), or PDF metadata. Detected using **ExifTool** and **FOCA** (Fingerprinting Organizations with Collected Archives).

**3. Filesystem-Level Modification (1 Mark):**
Tampering with NTFS Master File Table (MFT) entries, ext4 inodes, or directory timestamps. Includes **timestomping** (changing MAC times to match pre-incident state).

**4. Log Modification (1 Mark):**
Deletion, modification, or injection of entries in Windows Event Logs, Syslog, or application logs (Apache, IIS, firewall logs). Detected via **gap detection** in sequence numbers and **SIEM correlation**.

**5. Application-Level Modification (1 Mark):**
Database record tampering (SQL UPDATE without audit), email header forgery (SPF/DKIM bypass), browser history manipulation, or registry hive modification on Windows.

**6. Anti-Forensic Concealment (1 Mark):**
Steganography, data hiding in slack space, alternate data streams (ADS), or encryption with key destruction — all aimed at making recovery impossible.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — The Birthday Attack Formula (2 Marks):**

The probability of finding a hash collision after $k$ trials against an $n$-bit hash is:

$$
P(k) \approx 1 - e^{-k^2 / (2 \cdot 2^n)}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for 50% Collision Probability (2 Marks):**

Setting $P(k) = 0.5$:

$$
0.5 = 1 - e^{-k^2 / (2^{n+1})}
$$

$$
e^{-k^2 / 2^{n+1}} = 0.5
$$

$$
-\frac{k^2}{2^{n+1}} = \ln(0.5) = -0.6931
$$

$$
k = 0.8324 \times 2^{(n+1)/2}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply to MD5, n = 128 (1 Mark):**

$$
k = 0.8324 \times 2^{64.5} \approx 2.3 \times 10^{19} \text{ trials}
$$

Modern GPU clusters (e.g., 1000× RTX 4090 at $10^{11}$ hashes/sec) achieve this in **~hours**.

**Step 4 — Apply to SHA-256, n = 256 (1 Mark):**

$$
k = 0.8324 \times 2^{128.5} \approx 4.8 \times 10^{38} \text{ trials}
$$

This is **computationally infeasible** even with all current global computing power.

**Step 5 — Why MD5 is Court-Rejected (1 Mark):**
Because chosen-prefix collisions have been **practically demonstrated** (e.g., Flame malware 2012, MD5 chosen-prefix attack by Stevens 2012), an attacker can produce two **different files with the same MD5 hash**. This destroys the **non-repudiation** property, and **Indian courts (and most jurisdictions globally) no longer accept MD5** as the sole integrity proof. SHA-256 is **FIPS 140-2/3 validated** and is the de-facto forensic standard.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

#### (a) Explain the concept of **Timestomping** as an anti-forensic technique. Describe how an investigator can detect timestomping using timeline analysis and NTFS MACE timestamps. (7 Marks)

#### (b) Discuss **Steganography** as a Data Modification tool. With a worked example, compute the LSB-based steganographic capacity of a $1920 \times 1080$ RGB image, and explain how investigators detect hidden data. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Definition of Timestomping (2 Marks):**
Timestomping is the anti-forensic technique of **modifying the MAC (Modified, Accessed, Created) timestamps** of a file to mislead forensic timeline reconstruction. Tools like `timestomp` (Metasploit), `touch -t` (Linux), and `SetFileTime` (Windows API) allow attackers to set arbitrary timestamps, including those predating the incident.

**2. Why Attackers Use It (1 Mark):**
To make a malicious file appear as if it was created/modified **before** the breach (planted evidence), or to align timestamps with a forged alibi, thereby breaking the forensic narrative.

**3. The NTFS MACE Concept (1 Mark):**
NTFS stores **four timestamps** in the MFT entry (and two in the $STANDARD_INFORMATION attribute, two in the $FILE_NAME attribute):
- **Modified** (M)
- **Accessed** (A)
- **Changed** (MFT entry itself) (C)
- **Entry Modified** (E)

Attackers typically modify only the $SI attribute, leaving $FN unchanged. This **mismatch is a smoking gun**.

**4. Detection Using Plaso/log2timeline (2 Marks):**
The **Plaso** (Python-based) framework correlates timestamps from MFT, $LogFile, $UsnJrnl:$J, and event logs. A forensic timeline (e.g., in **Timesketch**) reveals **inconsistencies**, such as:
- $SI Modified > $FN Entry Modified (impossible sequence).
- File modification time **earlier** than file creation time.
- A burst of files with identical timestamps from different sources.

**5. Counter-Forensic Tools (1 Mark):**
- **ExifTool** for image metadata.
- **MFTExplorer** for NTFS MFT browsing.
- **KAPE** (Kroll Artifact Parser and Extractor) for rapid triage.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**1. Steganography as a Modification Tool (2 Marks):**
Steganography is the practice of **hiding data within another file** (cover) such that the presence of the hidden data is imperceptible. Common types:
- **Image Steganography** (LSB, DCT, wavelet).
- **Audio Steganography** (spectral LSB).
- **Document Steganography** (whitespace, zero-width characters).
- **File-in-file** (embedded ZIP, polyglot files).

It is a **Data Modification technique** because the cover file's bytes are altered to embed the secret.

**2. LSB Capacity Formula (1 Mark):**

$$
C_{\text{bytes}} = \frac{W \times H \times 3}{8}
$$

**3. Worked Example for 1920×1080 (2 Marks):**

$$
C_{\text{bytes}} = \frac{1920 \times 1080 \times 3}{8} = \frac{6220800}{8} = 777600 \text{ bytes} \approx 759 \text{ KB}
$$

Accounting for $\sim 10\%$ header overhead:

$$
C_{\text{effective}} = 0.9 \times 777600 = 699840 \text{ bytes} \approx 683 \text{ KB}
$$

This can hide a **small script, keylogger, or exfiltrated database**.

**4. Detection Methods (2 Marks):**
- **Statistical analysis** (chi-square test on LSB distribution).
- **Visual analysis** (bit-plane visualization).
- **Tool-based detection** (`binwalk` for embedded files, `zsteg` for PNG/BMP, `stegdetect` for JPEG).
- **Hash comparison** between original (known) and suspect cover image.
- **Entropy mapping** to find suspicious regions.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Data Modification Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing MD5 with SHA-1 digest lengths** — MD5 is **128 bits**, SHA-1 is **160 bits**, SHA-256 is **256 bits**. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Forgetting the Avalanche Effect** — Any hash question without mention of avalanche is marked incomplete. Always state: *"A 1-bit input change causes ~50% bits of digest to change."*
> 3. **Using raw `|` in math tables** — Markdown tables break on pipe characters. Use $\mid$ or $\vert$ in LaTeX.
> 4. **Omitting the chain of custody** — A hash alone is not enough; examiners expect a mention of **bit-stream imaging + write-blocker + hash + chain of custody**.
> 5. **Not stating the legal basis** — Indian forensic answers should reference **Section 65B / Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam 2023** and **IT Act 2000/2008** for full marks.
> 6. **Birthday Attack formula misapplication** — The exponent is $-(k^2)/(2 \cdot 2^n)$, not $-k/2^n$. A common error that costs 2 marks.
> 7. **Forgetting MACE vs MFT mismatch** — A classic timestomping detection question: always mention the **$SI vs $FN attribute mismatch** trick.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Data Modification** is a violation of the **Integrity** pillar of the **CIA Triad**.
- **MD5 (128-bit)** is broken and **not court-admissible**; use **SHA-256** or **SHA-3**.
- **Avalanche Effect** is the cornerstone of hash-based tamper detection (BDR ≈ 0.5).
- **Birthday Attack** probability: $P(k) \approx 1 - e^{-k^2/(2 \cdot 2^n)}$ — yields $2^{n/2}$ trials for 50% collision.
- **NTFS MACE** timestamps (Modified, Accessed, Changed, Entry-Modified) are stored in **$STANDARD_INFORMATION** and **$FILE_NAME** — mismatches reveal **timestomping**.
- **Steganography LSB capacity** in a 24-bit RGB image: $C = (W \times H \times 3) / 8$ bytes.
- **Anti-forensic techniques** include: timestomping, log wiping, steganography, encryption, file wiping (DoD 5220.22-M).
- **Detection tools**: Autopsy, X-Ways, EnCase, Plaso, ExifTool, binwalk, Volatility, KAPE.
- **Legal Framework (India)**: **Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam 2023 (Section 63)**, **IT Act 2000/2008 (Sections 65, 66, 66F)**, and **Indian Evidence Act 1872 (Section 65B)** for admissibility of electronic records.
- **Chain of Custody** is mandatory — every transfer of evidence must be logged with hash, timestamp, and handler signature.
- **Forensic soundness** demands **write-blockers**, **bit-stream imaging**, and **FIPS-validated hashing** at every stage.
- **Log integrity** is verified by SIEM correlation, sequence gap analysis, and signed log forwarding (syslog-sign, WEF).
- **Mermaid diagrams** in KTU answers should remain **simple, text-based, and academic** — avoid emojis or HTML tags inside nodes.
- **Mathematical presentation**: Use `$$` for display equations, `$` for inline, with **one blank line before and after** every display block.
- **Markdown tables**: Never use `|` inside cell math — substitute with $\mid$ or $\vert$ to avoid rendering breakage.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study: Timestamp, Encryption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT602 - CYBER FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Study: Timestamp, Encryption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 4: Case Study — Timestamp, Encryption

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Timestamp** in cyber forensics refers to the metadata-encoded time markers attached to a digital artifact (file, log entry, registry key, email header, or network packet) that record **when** a specific event occurred or **when** a file property was altered. In legal and investigative contexts, timestamps form the chronological backbone — the *timeline of truth* — of any digital investigation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Encryption** in a forensic context refers to the application of cryptographic algorithms (symmetric ciphers such as AES-256, DES, 3DES, or asymmetric schemes such as RSA-2048, ECC) to render digital evidence unreadable to unauthorized parties. Forensic encryption analysis involves **identification, classification, preservation, and (where lawful) decryption** of such protected artifacts without altering the original data.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Timestamp Analogy — The Postmark on a Sealed Letter:**
Imagine a forensic investigator walks into a crime scene and finds ten sealed letters scattered on a desk. Each letter has a **postmark** stamped on the envelope showing the date and city of mailing. Even though the letters are sealed (and you cannot read the content yet), the postmarks alone tell a story — *Letter A was mailed from Kochi on 12-March, Letter B from Chennai on 14-March, but Letter C's postmark is smudged and unreadable*. A forensic analyst treats digital timestamps exactly like these postmarks: they reveal *when* a file was created, modified, or accessed, even if the *content* is sealed (encrypted) or deleted.

**Encryption Analogy — The Tamper-Proof Safe with a Key Registry:**
Think of an encrypted file as a **bank vault**. The vault (ciphertext) is impenetrable to the investigator. The forensic examiner's job is to:
1. Identify the *type* of vault (which cipher was used).
2. Locate the *key* (recovery key, escrow, or memory dump).
3. Validate that opening the vault did not disturb its contents (integrity hash before and after).

### Core Metric Standards in Timestamp Forensics

| Metric | Standard Value | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Unix Epoch Base** | **1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC** | Reference point for POSIX time |
| **Windows FILETIME Base** | **1601-01-01 00:00:00 UTC** | 100-nanosecond intervals |
| **NTFS $STANDARD_INFORMATION** | UTC stored, local displayed | First source of MAC times |
| **NTFS $FILE_NAME** | UTC stored in MFT entry | Second, more reliable source |
| **FAT32 Timestamp Resolution** | 2-second (last modify), 10-ms (create) | DOS-derived |
| **exFAT Timestamp Resolution** | 10-millisecond | Modern flash-friendly FS |
| **GPT Timestamp Resolution** | **100-nanosecond** | EFI specification |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Students must remember that *the same event on a Windows NTFS volume generates **two pairs** of MAC times*: one in `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` (writable, can be altered by user-mode tools) and another in `$FILE_NAME` (MFT-resident, only modifiable by the OS kernel). Comparing the two is a classic **timestamp anti-forensic detection technique**.

### Core Definition — Encryption in Forensics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Digital Encryption Forensics** is the disciplined application of cryptographic reverse-engineering, key-recovery protocols, and side-channel analysis to lawfully extract plaintext from encrypted digital evidence while maintaining the **chain of custody** and the **forensic soundness** of the original artifact.

### Why These Two Topics Are Coupled in a Case Study

A realistic KTU-style case study almost never examines *only* timestamps or *only* encryption in isolation. A live forensic scenario will commonly contain:
- An **encrypted container** (VeraCrypt volume, BitLocker drive) whose **creation timestamp** is the only clue to *when* the suspect began concealing data.
- A **log file** with **GMT vs. local time confusion** that misplaces a suspect's alibi by 5 hours 30 minutes (the IST offset).
- An **email** whose PGP signature was applied *before* the file's last modified time, suggesting **pre-staging of evidence**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of File Timestamps (MAC + E + B)

A Windows forensic examiner must be fluent in **seven** distinct time values, not just three:

1. **M — Modified Time:** The timestamp of the *last write* to the file's *data content*.
2. **A — Accessed Time:** The timestamp of the *last read* (read = open, copy, or execute — *not* just metadata read).
3. **C — Creation Time (Birth Time):** The timestamp of the file's *initial creation* in its current location.
4. **E — Entry Modified Time (MFT Entry):** The timestamp of the *last change* to the file's *metadata attributes* (permissions, rename, owner).
5. **B — Birth Time (macOS/BSD):** Equivalent to Windows *Creation*, retained through copies and moves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Mandate:** When answering a 14-mark case study on timestamps, the KTU examiner expects the student to **explicitly label** which `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` vs. `$FILE_NAME` value is being cited. Marks are deducted if the candidate conflates them.

## 2.2 Timestamp Conversion Formulas (KTU Cheat Sheet)

| Conversion | Formula / Rule | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Windows FILETIME → Unix Epoch** | $\text{Unix} = \dfrac{\text{FILETIME} - 116444736000000000}{10000000}$ | Parsing `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` |
| **Hex Little-Endian → Decimal** | Reverse byte order, then convert base-16 → base-10 | Raw MFT analysis |
| **Local Time → UTC** | $\text{UTC} = \text{LocalTime} - \text{Bias}_{\text{TZ}}$ | Log correlation across zones |
| **Epoch Seconds → Human Time** | $\text{Date} = \text{epoch} + \text{1970-01-01}$ | Tool output (e.g., Sleuth Kit) |
| **NIST NTP Skew Detection** | $\Delta t = \vert t_{\text{local}} - t_{\text{NIST}} \vert$ | Validating suspect's clock |

## 2.3 The Three Encryption Categories Encountered in Case Studies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners test students on **three encryption layers** that are routinely encountered during a real seizure:

### Layer 1 — Full-Disk Encryption (FDE)
- **Examples:** BitLocker, FileVault, VeraCrypt full-disk, LUKS (Linux Unified Key Setup), Symantec PGP WDE.
- **Forensic Implication:** The *entire* file system is opaque. The investigator must capture the **encryption key in volatile memory (RAM)** before the suspect machine powers down, or rely on a **recovery key escrow** (Microsoft Account, Active Directory escrow, TPM-bound key).

### Layer 2 — File / Container-Level Encryption
- **Examples:** VeraCrypt hidden volume, 7-Zip AES-256, OpenSSL-encrypted ZIP, PGP/GPG single file.
- **Forensic Implication:** The file system itself is readable; only specific container files are sealed. **The container's timestamps** (created, modified) become primary investigative leads.

### Layer 3 — Application / Network Encryption
- **Examples:** TLS 1.3 sessions, Signal Protocol messages, HTTPS traffic, SSH-tunnelled transfers.
- **Forensic Implication:** Content is sealed in transit; investigator relies on **metadata (packet headers, SNI, certificate fingerprints)** and **endpoint artifacts** (browser history, cache).

## 2.4 The Forensic Encryption Workflow (5-Phase)

1. **Identification Phase:** Detect the encryption type using **file signatures** (magic bytes), **entropy analysis** (encrypted data has $\approx 7.99$ bits/byte entropy vs. $\approx 4\text{–}5$ for compressed).
2. **Preservation Phase:** Image the source using **EnCase**, **FTK Imager**, or **dd**; compute the **SHA-256** hash of the original and the working copy; verify with **MD5 + SHA-1** dual hash.
3. **Key Recovery Phase:** Search for keys in **memory dumps**, **page files**, **hibernation files**, **Windows Credential Manager**, or **browser key stores**.
4. **Decryption Phase:** Use **Elcomsoft Forensic Disk Decryptor**, **Passware Kit Forensic**, or **libguestfs** for Linux.
5. **Verification Phase:** Re-hash decrypted output and compare to the *expected* hash if known.

## 2.5 The Shannon Entropy Test for Encrypted Content

A high-yield KTU formula is the **Shannon Entropy** calculation, used to determine whether a file is encrypted, compressed, or random:

$$
H(X) = - \sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \cdot \log_2 p(x_i)
$$

Where:
- $H(X)$ is the entropy in **bits per symbol**.
- $p(x_i)$ is the probability of byte value $x_i$ occurring in the file.
- The maximum value is $H_{\max} = \log_2(256) = 8$ bits/byte for a uniform distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU Exams:**
> - $H < 5.0$ → likely plaintext or weakly structured.
> - $5.0 \le H < 7.0$ → compressed (e.g., ZIP, RAR, JPEG).
> - $H \ge 7.5$ → **strong encryption or true random** (suspicious!).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Timestamp + Encryption analysis is foundational in:
- **Incident Response (IR):** Reconstructing a kill-chain timeline.
- **e-Discovery:** Producing court-admissible email timelines.
- **Insider Threat Detection:** Correlating file creation with off-hours VPN logins.
- **Ransomware Investigation:** Verifying the *last modified* timestamp on encrypted files to estimate the encryption completion time, then computing ransom-payment window.
- **GDPR / DPDPA Compliance Audits:** Demonstrating *when* personal data was last accessed and *how* it was protected at rest.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1 — Converting an NTFS FILETIME to a Human-Readable Date

> [!NOTE]
> **Case Study Input:** A raw MFT entry shows the `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` Modification timestamp in little-endian hex as:
> **`0x 01 D5 4D 7F 8E F2 DA 01`**

### Step 1 — Reverse the Byte Order (Little-Endian → Big-Endian)

The Windows FILETIME structure is stored in **little-endian** byte order, so we must reverse the 8 bytes:

$$
\text{Raw Hex} = \texttt{01 D5 4D 7F 8E F2 DA 01}
$$

$$
\text{Reversed} = \texttt{01 DA F2 8E 7F 4D D5 01}
$$

### Step 2 — Convert Hexadecimal to Decimal

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Decimal Value} &= 0\text{x}01\text{DAF28E7F4DD501} \\
&= 1 \times 16^{15} + 13 \times 16^{14} + 10 \times 16^{13} + 15 \times 16^{12} \\
&\quad + 2 \times 16^{11} + 8 \times 16^{10} + 14 \times 16^{9} + 7 \times 16^{8} \\
&\quad + 4 \times 16^{7} + 13 \times 16^{6} + 5 \times 16^{5} + 1 \times 16^{0}
\end{aligned}
$$

Computing term-by-term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= 7.2057 \times 10^{18} + 2.2310 \times 10^{18} + 1.7556 \times 10^{18} + 1.0995 \times 10^{18} \\
&\quad + 3.5180 \times 10^{17} + 1.1259 \times 10^{17} + 3.6700 \times 10^{15} + 1.8000 \times 10^{14} \\
&\quad + 2.1470 \times 10^{14} + 1.6540 \times 10^{13} + 5.2429 \times 10^{12} + 1 \\
&= 133509312000000000 \;\; \text{(FILETIME ticks, 100-ns intervals)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Subtract the Windows Epoch Offset

The Windows epoch starts at **1601-01-01 00:00:00 UTC**. The difference between this and the Unix epoch (**1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC**) in 100-nanosecond intervals is:

$$
\text{Offset} = 116{,}444{,}736{,}000{,}000{,}000
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Unix Ticks} &= 133{,}509{,}312{,}000{,}000{,}000 - 116{,}444{,}736{,}000{,}000{,}000 \\
&= 17{,}064{,}576{,}000{,}000{,}000 \;\; \text{(100-ns ticks since 1601)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert 100-ns Ticks to Seconds

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Unix Seconds} &= \dfrac{17{,}064{,}576{,}000{,}000{,}000}{10{,}000{,}000} \\
&= 1{,}706{,}457{,}600 \;\; \text{seconds since 1970-01-01 UTC}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert Epoch Seconds to a Human Date

Using the standard epoch conversion (or the `date -d @1706457600` command):

$$
\text{Date} = \text{1970-01-01} + 1{,}706{,}457{,}600 \text{ seconds} = \text{2024-01-29 00:00:00 UTC}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forensic Conclusion:** The file's `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` Modification time is **2024-01-29 00:00:00 UTC**. If the suspect's machine was set to IST (UTC + 5:30), the *displayed* local time would be **2024-01-29 05:30:00**. This 5h 30m offset is the most common mistake in KTU answers.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2 — Shannon Entropy of a Suspect File

> [!NOTE]
> **Case Study Input:** A 1 KB binary file `secret.bin` is seized from a suspect's USB. Byte frequency count yields 8 distinct byte values, each occurring 128 times. Compute the entropy and classify the file.

### Step 1 — Compute the Probability of Each Byte

$$
p(x_i) = \dfrac{128}{1024} = \dfrac{1}{8} = 0.125
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Shannon Formula

$$
H(X) = - \sum_{i=1}^{8} p(x_i) \cdot \log_2 p(x_i)
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(X) &= - 8 \times \left(0.125 \times \log_2 0.125\right) \\
&= - 8 \times \left(0.125 \times (-3)\right) \\
&= - 8 \times (-0.375) \\
&= +3.0 \;\; \text{bits/byte}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Interpret the Result

Since $H = 3.0$ bits/byte is **well below 5.0**, the file is **NOT encrypted** and **NOT random**. It is most likely:
- A low-entropy structured file (e.g., a log file with repetitive ASCII content), or
- A file containing repeated patterns (e.g., a padded file to obscure size).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Students often *assume* an unusual file is encrypted. A forensic examiner must **always** confirm with entropy, magic bytes, and contextual metadata before declaring encryption.

## 3.3 Worked Derivation #3 — Hash Verification After Decryption

> [!NOTE]
> **Case Study Input:** An encrypted file `evidence.zip` is decrypted to `evidence_decrypted.txt`. The forensic image has hash `SHA-256: 9F86D081...7C4` and the decrypted output has hash `SHA-256: 9F86D081...7C4`. Confirm forensic soundness.

The verification rule is:

$$
\text{Verdict} = \begin{cases} \text{Sound} & \text{if } H_{\text{decrypted}} = H_{\text{expected}} \\ \text{Tampered} & \text{if } H_{\text{decrypted}} \neq H_{\text{expected}} \end{cases}
$$

Since $9\text{F86D081...7C4} = 9\text{F86D081...7C4}$, the verdict is **Sound** — the decrypted output matches the original, proving **forensic integrity** was preserved throughout the chain of custody.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — KTU High-Yield Timestamp × Encryption Traps

| # | Trap in Case Study | Correct Forensically Sound Action | Marks at Stake |
| :-- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Suspect's PC shows local IST time, examiner reports it as UTC | Convert: $\text{UTC} = \text{IST} - 5{:}30$ | 2 |
| 2 | Examiner cites only `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` modification time | Must compare with `$FILE_NAME` to detect timestomping | 3 |
| 3 | Encrypted file assumed guilty without hash comparison | Always compute **SHA-256** of original ciphertext | 2 |
| 4 | Decryption done on the *original* evidence | Always decrypt on a **working copy**; preserve original | 4 |
| 5 | Time zone of email server ignored | Identify server TZ (often GMT) and convert to examiner TZ | 2 |
| 6 | Mistaking compressed for encrypted | Compute entropy; ZIP entropy is $\approx 7.8$, not $7.99$ | 2 |
| 7 | BitLocker key discarded with RAM shutdown | Capture memory first; otherwise escalate to escrow | 3 |
| 8 | `$MFT` Entry Modified confused with data Modified | State explicitly which attribute changed | 2 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — Timestamp Forensic Acquisition Flow

This mermaid diagram maps the operational pipeline an examiner follows from the moment a powered-on suspect machine is encountered to the final timeline correlation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Scene Arrival: Powered-On Suspect Machine] --> B[Document On-Scene State]
    B --> C[Acquire Volatile Memory via FTK Imager]
    C --> D[Acquire Live Filesystem Image]
    D --> E[Parse $MFT for $STANDARD_INFORMATION]
    E --> F[Parse $MFT for $FILE_NAME]
    F --> G{Compare SI vs FN MAC times}
    G -->|Mismatch Detected| H[Flag Timestomping Suspect]
    G -->|Match Confirmed| I[Build Master Timeline UTC]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Convert UTC to Local Time IST UTC 5:30]
    J --> K[Correlate with Event Logs and Network Captures]
    K --> L[Final Court-Admissible Timeline Report]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The **diamond-shaped decision node** `G` is the heart of timestamp forensics. A mismatch between `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` and `$FILE_NAME` for the *same* file is a **direct forensic indicator of anti-forensic tool use** (e.g., `timestomp.exe`, `SetMACE`, `NirSoft BulkFileChanger`).

## 4.2 Diagram B — Encryption Forensic Decision Tree

This mermaid diagram captures the investigator's decision-making process when encrypted evidence is encountered.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Suspect Media Seized] --> B[Compute SHA-256 of Original Image]
    B --> C[Run Entropy Analysis on File Clusters]
    C --> D{Entropy greater than 7.5}
    D -->|No| E[File is Plaintext or Compressed]
    D -->|Yes| F[Run File Signature Magic Bytes Check]
    F --> G{Signature Identified}
    G -->|BitLocker Header| H[Acquire BitLocker Recovery Key]
    G -->|VeraCrypt Header| I[Check for Hidden Volume Magic]
    G -->|PGP / GPG Block| J[Search Local Keyrings]
    G -->|None Identified| K[Custom Cipher Unknown]
    H --> L[Decrypt on Working Copy Only]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> M[Consult Cryptanalysis Lab]
    L --> N[Re-hash Decrypted Output]
    M --> N
    N --> O{Hash Matches Expected}
    O -->|Yes| P[Forensically Sound Evidence]
    O -->|No| Q[Chain of Custody Broken Alert]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The **entropy threshold of 7.5** is the operational decision boundary. Below it, the file is treated as accessible evidence; above it, the encryption-response protocol is triggered. This is the same threshold used by the tool `ent` (Unix) and the Windows `bulk_extractor` forensics tool.

## 4.3 Diagram C — Timestamp-Anti-Forensic Detection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[File Under Examination] --> B[Read SI Modified Time]
    A --> C[Read FN Modified Time]
    B --> D{Difference more than 1 sec}
    C --> D
    D -->|Yes| E[SI has been tampered]
    D -->|No| F[Times Consistent - Likely Genuine]
    E --> G[Cross-check with Event Log 4663]
    E --> H[Cross-check with $LogFile Journal]
    G --> I[Confirm Anti-Forensic Activity]
    H --> I
    F --> J[Add to Master Timeline]
    I --> J
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students stop at the `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` value. The KTU 2024 examiner explicitly tests whether the student **systematically compares** the two MFT-resident sources. Failing to do so costs a full 3-mark segment in a 14-mark question.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

### Q1. Differentiate between `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` and `$FILE_NAME` timestamps in an NTFS Master File Table. Why is the distinction critical in a timestamp forgery case?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Attribute | `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` (SI) | `$FILE_NAME` (FN) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Writability** | User-mode writable; can be modified by tools like `timestomp` | Kernel-only writable; protected by the OS |
| **Update Trigger** | Changes on data write, attribute read, or rename | Changes only on rename or parent-directory move |
| **Use in Forensics** | First-glance MAC times; often manipulated | **Reliable witness** for forgery detection |

> **Examiner Note (1 mark):** A forensic examiner compares SI vs. FN — if they disagree for the *same* event, the SI has been altered by an anti-forensic tool. This is the **gold standard for timestomp detection**.

### Q2. A suspect file has Shannon Entropy $H = 7.96$ bits/byte and a magic-byte signature of `D0 CF 11 E0 A1 B1 1A E1`. What does this tell you about its state?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Entropy 7.96** is *above* the 7.5 threshold, indicating **random or strongly encrypted** content (1 mark).
- **Magic bytes `D0 CF 11 E0 A1 B1 1A E1`** are the **OLE Compound File signature**, identifying the file as a **Microsoft Office legacy document** (.doc, .xls) (1 mark).
- **Verdict:** The original Office document has been **encrypted** (likely with Microsoft Office's in-built AES-128/256 password protection or a 7-Zip AES layer). The examiner must acquire the decryption password or recovery key before extracting content (1 mark).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

### Question A (14 Marks) — Timestamp Case Study

**Scenario:** A cyber forensic team is investigating a suspected data exfiltration case at a Kochi-based IT firm. The seized workstation is running Windows 10 with NTFS. A sensitive file `ClientData.xlsx` is found in `D:\Projects\2024\Q1\`. The MFT entry for the file shows the following raw values (little-endian hex):

| Attribute | `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` (hex) | `$FILE_NAME` (hex) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Modified | `01 D5 4D 7F 8E F2 DA 01` | `01 D5 4D 7F 8E 12 DC 01` |
| Accessed | `01 D5 4D 7F 8E F2 DA 01` | `01 D5 4D 7F 8E 12 DC 01` |
| Created | `01 D5 4D 7F 8E F2 DA 01` | `01 D5 4D 7F 8E 12 DC 01` |
| MFT Entry Modified | `01 D5 4D 7F 8E F2 DA 01` | `01 D5 4D 7F 8E 12 DC 01` |

The machine's clock is set to IST (UTC + 5:30). The investigator notes the file was last accessed 3 hours before the firm's 22:00 IST shutdown protocol.

#### Part (a) — Decode both SI and FN timestamps. Identify whether tampering is evident. (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Convert SI Hex to FILETIME Integer**

Reverse byte order of `01 D5 4D 7F 8E F2 DA 01`:

$$
\text{SI Integer} = 0\text{x}01\text{DAF28E7F4DD501} = 133{,}509{,}312{,}000{,}000{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Unix Epoch**

$$
\text{SI Unix} = \dfrac{133{,}509{,}312{,}000{,}000{,}000 - 116{,}444{,}736{,}000{,}000{,}000}{10{,}000{,}000}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= \dfrac{17{,}064{,}576{,}000{,}000{,}000}{10{,}000{,}000} \\
&= 1{,}706{,}457{,}600 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Decode to UTC**

$$
\text{SI Date UTC} = \text{2024-01-29 00:00:00 UTC}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to IST**

$$
\text{SI Date IST} = \text{2024-01-29 05:30:00 IST}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert FN Hex to FILETIME Integer**

Reverse byte order of `01 D5 4D 7F 8E 12 DC 01`:

$$
\text{FN Integer} = 0\text{x}01\text{DC128E7F4DD501} = 133{,}485{,}312{,}000{,}000{,}000
$$

**Step 6 — Convert FN to Unix Epoch and Date**

$$
\text{FN Unix} = \dfrac{133{,}485{,}312{,}000{,}000{,}000 - 116{,}444{,}736{,}000{,}000{,}000}{10{,}000{,}000} = 1{,}704{,}057{,}600
$$

$$
\text{FN Date UTC} = \text{2024-01-26 12:00:00 UTC}
$$

$$
\text{FN Date IST} = \text{2024-01-26 17:30:00 IST}
$$

**Step 7 — Tampering Verdict**

$$
\Delta T_{\text{Mod}} = \text{SI} - \text{FN} = 3 \text{ days} \; 12 \text{ hours}
$$

> **Conclusion (7 marks awarded):** A **3-day 12-hour discrepancy** between SI and FN modification times is **statistically impossible** in a clean system. The SI timestamps have been **forensically tampered** using an anti-forensic tool such as `SetMACE` or `timestomp`. The **FN timestamps are the reliable witness** and point to the original event time of **2024-01-26 17:30 IST**.

#### Part (b) — What investigative steps should the examiner follow to preserve and present this timestamp evidence in a court of law? (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Bit-stream Image the Disk (1 mark)**
Use `FTK Imager` or `dd` to create a forensic image. Compute the **SHA-256 hash** of the source and the working copy; both hashes must match for chain of custody.

**Step 2 — Parse the `$MFT` Using Sleuth Kit / Autopsy (1 mark)**
Run `fls -m / -r C: > mft_body.txt` to dump all MFT entries. Use `istat` to display individual file attributes for `ClientData.xlsx`.

**Step 3 — Corroborate with Event Logs (1 mark)**
Cross-reference Event ID **4663** (file system access attempts) and **4660** (file deletion) from the Windows Security log. These are written by the kernel and **cannot be back-dated** by user-mode tools.

**Step 4 — Corroborate with `$LogFile` and `$UsnJrnl` (1 mark)**
The `NTFS $LogFile` records metadata transactions. The `USN Journal` (`$J`) records file system changes. Both provide **independent, OS-managed** timestamps that confirm the original MFT Entry Modified value.

**Step 5 — Document the Tool Chain (1 mark)**
Maintain a detailed log of: tool name, version, hash of executable, parameters used, date/time of execution, and operator name. This is required under **Section 65B of the Indian Evidence Act, 1872 (now Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023)** for electronic evidence admissibility.

**Step 6 — Generate an Examiner's Report (1 mark)**
Produce a written report containing: (a) the source media description, (b) the acquisition hash, (c) the tool chain, (d) the timeline reconstruction with both SI and FN values side-by-side, and (e) the conclusion of anti-forensic activity.

**Step 7 — Preserve Original Evidence (1 mark)**
The original disk image is stored in a write-protected safe; all analysis is performed on **forensic copies**. This guarantees the original remains admissible in court.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> In timestamp questions, students frequently **fail to convert UTC to IST** (or vice versa) and lose 2 marks. A second common error is to **present SI timestamps without comparing them to FN**, missing the entire forensic point of the question (cost: 3 marks). Always: (1) identify which timestamp source you are citing, (2) state the time zone explicitly, and (3) compare both sources.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Encryption Case Study *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**Scenario:** A corporate espionage investigation yields a 16 GB USB drive from a departing employee. The drive contains a single 16 GB file named `archive.vc` with the **VeraCrypt volume header signature** at offset 0. The entropy of the file body is $H = 7.987$ bits/byte. The employee is uncooperative and refuses to provide the password. The forensic team has access to the employee's previous office desktop, which was imaged during a routine audit 6 weeks ago.

#### Part (a) — Construct a complete forensic decryption strategy. Justify each step with reference to chain-of-custody and forensic soundness. (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Verify the File Signature and Entropy (1 mark)**
Confirm the VeraCrypt header (magic bytes `VERA` at offset 0) and re-compute entropy using **NIST SP 800-22** test suite to validate that the body is encrypted (not merely pseudo-random).

**Step 2 — Acquire Volatile Memory from the Previous Desktop (1 mark)**
The 6-week-old image may contain a **page file** or **hibernation file** (`hiberfil.sys`, `pagefile.sys`, `swapfile.sys`) where VeraCrypt keys or master-key fragments may still reside. Use `Volatility` with the `mimikatz` plugin or `Bulk Extractor` to scan for high-entropy blobs of 64 bytes (typical AES-256 key length).

**Step 3 — Check for VeraCrypt Rescue Disk / Key Files (1 mark)**
Search the previous desktop image for `VeraCrypt Rescue Disk.iso`, `VERACRYPT_KEYFILE`, or any `.key` files in `%APPDATA%\VeraCrypt\`.

**Step 4 — Mount the Volume on a Forensic Workstation (1 mark)**
Use VeraCrypt on a *write-blocked, read-only* forensic workstation (running a clean Linux forensic distribution such as **Caine** or **Paladin**). Mount with the `-m=nokernelcrypto` and `-r` flags to ensure no write operations reach the encrypted volume.

**Step 5 — Attempt Key Derivation Using Known Attack Surfaces (1 mark)**
- **Dictionary attack** using `hashcat -m 13721` (VeraCrypt mode) with corporate policy password lists.
- **Brute-force** if the password length is estimated $< 8$ characters.

**Step 6 — If Decryption Succeeds, Verify Integrity (1 mark)**
Compute SHA-256 of the decrypted volume contents. Compare against any known file hashes from the prior audit image. Document the entire process in a **chain-of-custody log** with timestamps and operator signatures.

**Step 7 — If Decryption Fails, Document Inability and Seal Evidence (1 mark)**
Produce a written statement of inability, store the encrypted volume in a tamper-evident bag, and escalate to the legal team for **court-ordered disclosure** of the encryption password (per **Section 165 Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023**).

#### Part (b) — The decrypted volume contains an inner hidden VeraCrypt volume. How does this change the forensic approach? Discuss the legal and technical implications. (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Recognize the Hidden Volume Indicator (1 mark)**
A VeraCrypt *hidden volume* is identifiable by the absence of a second header in the host file but with entropy patterns suggesting duplicate allocation. The examiner must be **aware** that mounting the outer volume with the *wrong* password may **damage the hidden volume** if data is written.

**Step 2 — Apply the *Write-Block* Principle Strictly (1 mark)**
**Never** mount the outer volume in *writable* mode. The forensic mount must use the `--readonly` flag equivalent (in VeraCrypt: open as read-only, do not provide the outer password for a writable mount).

**Step 3 — Document the Plausible Deniability Aspect (1 mark)**
The mere existence of a hidden volume may constitute *intentional* anti-forensic design. The examiner should not be misled into assuming the outer volume contents are the *complete* evidence set; the inner volume may contain the *actual* exfiltrated data.

**Step 4 — Decrypt the Hidden Volume Using a Separate Password (1 mark)**
If the suspect eventually provides the *hidden volume* password, mount it via VeraCrypt's *hidden volume* option. If not, attempt the same recovery vectors as in Part (a): memory dumps, key files, brute force on the inner volume header (offset 65536, size 512 bytes).

**Step 5 — Legal Implications — Right Against Self-Incrimination (1 mark)**
In India, the **right against self-incrimination (Article 20(3) of the Constitution)** may protect the suspect from being compelled to disclose the hidden volume password, depending on evolving jurisprudence. Compare with the **UK R v. S and A** (2019) case where decryption orders were upheld.

**Step 6 — Produce a Layered Evidence Map (1 mark)**
The final report must show **three distinct evidence layers**:
- Layer 1: The encrypted container (file `archive.vc`).
- Layer 2: The outer (decoy) volume contents.
- Layer 3: The hidden volume contents (if decrypted).

**Step 7 — Maintain the Hash Chain Across All Layers (1 mark)**
Compute and record SHA-256 at each layer transition. The final evidence integrity depends on the unbroken chain: Original Media → Image → Container → Outer Volume → Hidden Volume → Plaintext.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> In encryption case studies, students often **forget to specify the tool used and its version**, costing 1 mark per missing tool. The examiner also penalizes answers that **decrypt on the original evidence** instead of a working copy. Always state: (1) tool + version, (2) read-only mount, (3) hash verification at every stage.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Two timestamp sources on NTFS:** Always cite `$STANDARD_INFORMATION` *and* `$FILE_NAME`; their **disagreement is the smoking gun** of timestomping. ✅
- **Windows FILETIME base:** **1601-01-01 UTC**, expressed in **100-nanosecond intervals**. The conversion formula is $\text{Unix} = (\text{FILETIME} - 116{,}444{,}736{,}000{,}000{,}000) / 10{,}000{,}000$. ✅
- **Time zone handling is non-negotiable:** Always state whether your timestamp is **UTC, IST, GMT, or local**; document the offset ($\text{IST} = \text{UTC} + 5{:}30$). ✅
- **Shannon Entropy rule of thumb:** $H < 5.0$ plaintext, $5.0 \le H < 7.5$ compressed, $H \ge 7.5$ encrypted or random. ✅
- **Three encryption layers:** Full-Disk, File/Container, and Application. Each demands a different forensic response. ✅
- **Always decrypt on a working copy.** The original evidence is **never** altered. ✅
- **Hash chain must be unbroken:** SHA-256 (or SHA-1 + MD5 dual) at every stage — original → image → decrypted output. ✅
- **Volatile data first:** RAM dump, page file, and hibernation file must be acquired **before** any disk imaging. ✅
- **Anti-forensic detection sources:** Windows Event ID **4663**, **4660**; NTFS **`$LogFile`**; **`$UsnJrnl:$J`**. These are kernel-managed and tamper-resistant. ✅
- **Legal compliance:** Indian evidence law (**Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam 2023, Section 63 + Schedule**) and the IT Act 2000/2008 amendments govern admissibility; document tool, version, hash, and operator for every step. ✅
- **Hidden volumes demand a *read-only* mount** of the outer container; writable mount risks destroying the inner volume. ✅
- **Never equate "encrypted" with "guilty"** — entropy + magic bytes + context are the three required checks. ✅

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study: VeraCrypt
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCCT602 - CYBER FORENSICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Study: VeraCrypt

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔐 CASE STUDY: VERACRYPT — A FORENSIC PERSPECTIVE

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**VeraCrypt** is a free, open-source, cross-platform disk-encryption utility that provides *on-the-fly encryption (OTFE)* of file containers, partitions, and entire system drives.
